I ask that you rule for non governmental agencies to be barred their use around critical and hazardous infrastructure. Thank you

I am a locomotive engineer with 21 years of service. Active rail yards are already dangerous enough without drones creating distractions overhead. Frontline railroad workers are the true experts on rail safety. These drones used for surveillance and operational testing create unnecessary hazards and security concerns. Railroad workers deserve to be consulted before these practices are normalized in critical infrastructure environments. Normalizing drones flying over the thousands of gallons of hazardous chemicals can't be allowed to happen, because us, as employees, won't know when we are seeing an act of terrorism in progress vs. a company official or MTO trying to conduct operations tests. Please restrict drone operations around transportation facilities!

This is not only a public hazard but an employee hazard. We have a company that is ridiculously infatuated with terminating employees so they can justify a one man crew. These drones, cameras, (in cabs, outside cabs, crew rooms, all over the yard, managers hiding in bushes, etc etc etc are creating a hostile environment. I’m to the point we’re I’m afraid to go to the bathroom at work in fear of cameras. As well as fear from camera in my hotel room. This has to stop!! It is nothing but an angry giant flexing its muscles against the common folk.

I disagree whole heartedly with the use of drone operations in our rail yards! As a switchman/ brakeman/ conductor/ & cut-back engineer, I work in these yards everyday. These drones are a major distraction from the safe work I am trying to preform. These things flying over your head divert your attention from the task at hand, and cause a very unsafe situation. Your focus is now on, What the heck is flying over me, and away from the move you are in the middle of making. Please rule these out of our rail yards!
Thank you,
Scott Lundberg

Re: Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03
Designation: Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility

To the Federal Aviation Administration:

I submit this public comment as a railroader with thirty (30) years of railroad experience and as someone who reviews railroad discipline cases, including cases where drone footage has been used by railroad management. I support the FAA’s effort to address unmanned aircraft operations near critical infrastructure, including rail yards, chemical facilities, intermodal facilities, and other transportation-related locations.

Active rail yards are already dangerous enough. Railroad employees work around moving equipment, switching operations, hazardous materials, close clearances, live tracks, plant facilities, vehicles, contractors, and constant radio communication. The margin for error is thin. Adding drones into that environment creates another distraction, another uncertainty, and another hazard for the employees doing the work on the ground.

This is not hypothetical. Railroad managers have used drones around active rail operations and near plant facilities while crews were working. I am aware of situations where drone use raised questions from local law enforcement because the drone was flown near or over protected or sensitive facilities. I have also reviewed discipline matters where drone footage became part of the Carrier’s case against employees. That creates a serious safety culture problem. Employees are no longer focused only on the movement, job briefing, radio communication, close clearance, track conditions, or hazards around them. They are also looking skyward, wondering whether they are being watched from above.

That is not safety. That is distraction.

Railroad workers are the true rail safety experts. The employees switching cars, riding equipment, protecting shoves, handling hazardous materials, and working inside industrial plants understand these risks in a way that cannot be captured from a parking lot, drone camera, or management office. Drones should not be normalized as an operational testing or surveillance tool inside active rail yards without strong federal limits, clear safety requirements, and meaningful input from the workers affected.

The FAA should also consider the security risk. Railroad workers operate in and around critical infrastructure every day. Many rail yards and customer facilities handle hazardous chemicals, fuel, pressure vessels, and other sensitive materials. When drones fly over these locations as a normal management practice, employees on the ground have no reasonable way to know whether the drone is authorized, unauthorized, careless, or something more serious. A drone over rail infrastructure or chemical facilities should not be treated as routine.

If the FAA adopts this rule, I urge the agency to make clear that rail facilities, active yards, hazardous material handling locations, intermodal facilities, and rail-served industrial plants are environments where drone activity presents both safety and security risks. The rule should not create a loophole where a facility operator or railroad can restrict outside drones while freely using its own drones over employees without safety controls.

Any authorized drone use in or around active rail operations should include advance notice to affected crews when possible; restrictions against flying directly over active switching operations, moving trains, employees, or hazardous material handling except for documented safety necessity; coordination with affected frontline employees; clear identification of the drone operator and purpose; and limits on using drones as routine discipline or operational testing tools.

A drone does not become safe simply because it is flown by railroad management. The risk to employees is the same if the drone creates distraction, confusion, or uncertainty during safety-sensitive work. Railroaders are trained to keep their eyes and attention on the movement, track, equipment, crew members, and hazards around them. They should not be forced to split that attention because a manager is flying a drone overhead.

For these reasons, I respectfully urge the FAA to adopt strong restrictions on drone operations around rail infrastructure and to include specific worker-safety protections before drones are permitted or normalized in active railroad operating environments.

Respectfully submitted,

Charles Piland

Only way SMART can get anything done is if they stand their ground, we need the mafia to take over unions again!! The way we are being used by the railroad for work and extra moves is atrocious. The union now doesn’t take employees into heart, they only do what benefits them, why? Because they get paid anyways. The railroad is no place for drones no we have enough to look out for and don’t need drones flying over out heads and worrying about them looking down on us.  It has nothing to do with safety and everything do with trying to get mud in trouble

Greetings, as a pilot that holds an instrument rating and a railroad worker, I am writing you to STRONGLY support the proposed rule of limiting the use of drones around fixed site facilities, particularly rail yards.

As both a railroad conductor entering my fifth year in the industry and an FAA-certificated pilot with an instrument rating, I understand how important safety culture, situational awareness, and disciplined operations are in transportation environments.

Over the past several years, I have personally witnessed railroad management increasingly using drones inside active rail yards and around moving trains. These drones are often not being used for their intended purpose of inspecting bridges, infrastructure, or other hard-to-reach locations. Instead, they are being used to hover over crews and switching operations in an attempt to identify minor rule violations for discipline purposes.

Active rail yards are already dangerous enough environments without additional distractions flying overhead. Railroad workers are constantly monitoring train movement, radio communication, hand signals, track conditions, and hazardous surroundings. Introducing drones into these environments creates unnecessary distractions and undermines operational focus during critical tasks.

As a pilot, I also find this troubling from a safety and security perspective. In aviation, unmanned aircraft operations are treated seriously because they can create hazards, confusion, and loss of situational awareness if misused. Railroad management has normalized drones flying around trains carrying thousands of gallons of hazardous materials, often in close proximity to crews on the ground. That should concern everyone.

There is also a broader security issue. If drones become commonplace over rail infrastructure, workers may no longer immediately recognize when unauthorized or malicious drone activity is occurring. In an era where railroads remain critical national infrastructure, that normalization creates unnecessary risk.

Front-line railroad workers are the true experts on rail safety because we are the ones working in these environments every day. Before drone operations become permanently normalized throughout the railroad industry, the FAA should listen carefully to the concerns of the men and women on the ground.

I strongly support additional FAA restrictions on non-essential drone operations around active rail facilities and moving trains, especially when those drones are being used for surveillance and disciplinary purposes rather than legitimate infrastructure inspection.
This would make it very unsafe for the employees because they would be distracted.

The Government Should Reexamine the Assumptions.

Commentary on a flawed plan. The probability that this theoretical reactive fix will yield the desired results is very low. Most of what I see as a “solution” is predicated on products developed by and lobbied for by a now-banned Chinese toy company. Unfortunately, managers at the FAA were beguiled by existing "solutions" built into products mandated by the CCP.  Some of these “solutions” could be used against the US of A and our allies.   

The FAA gave representatives of that same company seats on committees that assisted them in promulgating policy and rules that compromised the safety of the US NAS and put domestic drone manufacturers at an economic disadvantage.  That relationship between representatives of the now-banned Chinese company and the government bordered on the absurd. The FAA selected DJI to be an LAANC SSP until some of us raised concerns that the FAA was giving them the “keys to the domestic airspace kingdom.” Furthermore, in some instances, DJI company representatives had more power than duly elected US politicians. Even more troubling is the notion that someone from the government purportedly shared classified material with DJI company representatives.

I would encourage the Federal government to use this opportunity to conduct a forensic study, including a FARA investigation, of the regulatory process, since the involvement of any CCP-sponsored companies in federal rulemaking or policy development.

The safety of the US National Airspace System is at stake.

I am submitting this comment as a surveyor with Welch Comer Engineers, Inc., a civil engineering and surveying firm working in Idaho and Washington. Our survey work supports public infrastructure, private development, utilities, transportation, construction, boundary work, design, and public agency projects.

UAS technology has become a practical field tool for surveyors, not just an optional add-on. Drone-based imagery and lidar allow us to collect safer, more complete site data in less time than many traditional methods. We use UAS data for topographic mapping, design surfaces, construction documentation, corridor mapping, utility-related work, and other engineering deliverables. These projects often have tight windows based on weather, access, construction schedules, traffic control, crew availability, and client deadlines.

Because of that, the approval process matters. If an operation is technically allowed but requires unclear coordination, manual review, or waiting on a specific person to respond, the work may still not realistically be completed. For a survey crew, delayed approval can mean losing a weather window, missing site access, rescheduling staff, delaying design, or using slower and sometimes less safe methods.

I support protecting truly sensitive facilities from unsafe or unlawful drone activity. However, the final UAFR rule should separate legitimate security concerns from routine professional work performed by qualified operators. A lawful, insured, Part 107 survey operation using registered aircraft, Remote ID, and standard safety procedures should not be treated the same as an unknown or careless flight near a sensitive facility.

The current concept of allowing access only to “transition” through a Standard UAFR does not fit surveying and mapping. Survey flights are usually not pass-through operations. A mapping mission may require grid flights, cross-grid flights, repeated passes, low-altitude detail shots, lidar coverage, photo overlap, control verification, and sometimes hovering or circling a feature to fully document it.

The final rule should allow qualified operators to conduct non-transitory surveying, mapping, inspection, engineering, construction documentation, emergency repair, and public works flights within Standard UAFRs when the operation is lawful and meets objective safety requirements. Operators should be allowed to remain within the area for the minimum time reasonably necessary to complete the covered professional operation.

The FAA should make the process centralized, predictable, and fast. A LAANC-style system would make sense for Standard UAFR access and deviations. Operators should be able to submit notice or authorization through one FAA system, document Part 107 certification, aircraft registration, Remote ID compliance, operating area, time window, and purpose of flight, and receive near-real-time approval when the operation meets defined criteria.

If case-by-case review is required, the final rule should include clear deadlines, written reasons for denial, a way to correct or resubmit an application, and deemed approval if there is no response within the required time. For routine survey and mapping work, one week should be the maximum approval time. Without those protections, delay or non-response can function the same as a denial.

The rule should also clearly address contractor work. In engineering and utility projects, the asset owner is often not the drone operator. A utility, public agency, or private owner may hire an engineering firm, and that firm may use an internal survey crew or a separate drone pilot. The final rule should provide a simple pathway so the operator performing the flight can obtain approval or provide notice without confusion over whether the asset owner must be directly involved.

UAS surveying is now part of how many engineering and survey firms deliver accurate, efficient, and safer project data. The final UAFR rule should protect sensitive sites without unintentionally blocking routine professional operations that support infrastructure, utilities, construction, land development, transportation, and public works.

I respectfully ask the FAA to preserve fast, predictable, and low-friction access for qualified commercial UAS operators performing surveying, mapping, inspection, engineering, construction documentation, and public works operations within Standard UAFRs.

Respectfully submitted,

Thomas Tucker
Survey Technician
Welch Comer Engineers, Inc.

Subject: Support for the proposed Airspace Restriction Rule with Integrated Countermeasures
Dear I am writing to express my strong support for the proposed rule restricting the airspace up to 400 feet above Water and Wastewater facilities, with boundaries confined strictly within the facility’s proprietary lines. This measure is a critical step in ensuring the security, safety and privacy of these areas considering the global unrest and growing concerns and risks associated with Unauthorized Unmanned Aerial System (UAS) operations.
The prohibition of UAS operations within these designed airspace boundaries, except operators equipped with Remote Identification (Remote ID), is prudent and necessary approach. Most operators with Remote ID are identified, accountable and less prompt to unlawful or malicious drone activity.
But I advocate for the inclusion of counter measures within this regulation. This will allow Utility to use counter-drone technologies, such as system designed to capture or disable trespassing drones, will provide Utility the right to respond swiftly and effectively to the violation of their protected airspace and processes. Complementing these measures with authorized use of electronic frequency scrambling devices to interfere with video transmission and remote-control signals of unauthorized UAS will further enhance the capability to mitigate potential threats and prevent any trespassing.
Together, these provisions will empower Water and Wastewater Facility owners and operators to maintain control over their airspace, safeguard sensitive operations and protect the public and personnel from unintended disruptions or security breaches caused by unauthorized drone activity.
I urge the relevant authorities to carefully consider and adopt them in the final rule. The communities served by our sectors deserve those protections and it’s our duty to safeguard the effluent water of our process.
Thank you very much for your attention to this matter. And please do not hesitate to contact me if further discussion or clarification is required.

Sincerely,
Kouao-Eric Ekoue
Sr. Superintendent of Water Quality
Middlesex Water Company
Not only is a drone hovering over you while you are in the middle of a serious potentially dangerous job distracting but Union Pacific are using drones as a harassment and retaliation device!

To the Federal Aviation Administration, I am writing to bring to your attention a critical safety concern affecting railroad workers across the country. My name is David Rivera, and I work as a conductor on the extra board for Union Pacific in Tucson. I regularly work locals, yard assignments, and trains across routes in Arizona, New Mexico and Texas. Recently, we have experienced a significant conflict due to the use of drones by railroad management inside active railyards. These drones cause a serious distraction and interfere with our ability to maintain situational awareness—a critical factor when working with heavy equipment and hazardous, explosive, TIH, PIH chemicals and combustible material, and anything that can cause danger. It is the FAA's JOB and position to ensure that policies, procedures, and activities are safe for the American people. By not taking action against these unsafe drone operations, I feel that the agency is failing to uphold its mandate to protect critical infrastructure, you will not only fail the aviation field but you will now place yourself to blame for possible catastrophic consequences in Railway Freight.

Nevada Center for Applied Research (NCAR) COMMENT LOG
Document Description: FAA NPRM Section 2209, NCAR NV UASTS Comments
Index Page & Paragraph Comment
No.
1 General comment NCAR commends the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) for issuing the “Designation – Restrict the
Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility” NPRM. This NPRM
implementation of Section 2209 represents a major milestone on the protection of critical infrastructure
from unauthorized drone operations while ensuring that the proposed regulatory approach also allows for
the safe and scalable use of drones for commercial purposes.
NCAR has consistently emphasized that any framework governing low-altitude airspace must be designed
to permit authorized commercial drone operations, while at the same time protecting critical infrastructure
from potentially harmful or unauthorized activity. The objective of NCAR comments is to ensure that the
proposed rules both safeguard critical infrastructure and provide a pathway for safe and scalable
commercial drone use.
In addition, NCAR has provided input on the Department of Transportation’s Notice of Proposed
Rulemaking (NPRM) titled “Normalizing Unmanned Aircraft Systems Beyond Visual Line of Sight
Operations.” This NPRM addresses critical aspects related to expanding the operational capabilities of
unmanned aircraft systems.
NCAR thanks the FAA for the opportunity to comment on this NPRM and looks forward to continued
engagement as drone technologies and operations evolve.

“(c) The facility must have security monitoring.
4. Protective Security (§ 74.56)
(d) For purposes of paragraph (c) of this section, security monitoring must include the capability, either
directly or through a contracted service, to receive broadcast Remote Identification messages from
unmanned aircraft operating within or in close proximity to the requested unmanned aircraft flight
restriction, using equipment that is compatible with the requirements of part 89 of this chapter….”
The unmanned aircraft most likely to present a security or safety concern—clueless, careless, or criminal
operators who approach or enter a UAFR—may be non-cooperative and therefore may not broadcast
Remote Identification messages (or may broadcast inaccurate information). A monitoring requirement
limited to receiving broadcast Remote ID messages does not address non-cooperative, clueless, careless,
or criminal unmanned aircraft and may fail to provide the facility with meaningful situational awareness
within or near the UAFR.
Revise § 74.56(d) to require the fixed-site facility operator/proprietor with an active UAFR to have the
capability, either directly or through a contracted service, to detect unmanned aircraft operating within or in
close proximity to the requested unmanned aircraft flight restriction, including aircraft that do not
broadcast Remote Identification messages. Receipt of broadcast Remote Identification messages using
6

No.
equipment compatible with part 89 should be treated as one component of the overall monitoring
capability, not the sole required method.

The unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) industry has grown significantly over the past decade. What once seemed like a small recreational technology or personal hobby has now become an important tool in many commercial industries, including infrastructure inspection, emergency response, agriculture, security, and transportation. Because unmanned aircraft are now operating in more complex environments and closer to sensitive facilities, I support the FAA’s proposed rule to create flight restrictions near certain fixed-site facilities.

Manned aircraft are held to a high legal and safety standard, and it is reasonable that unmanned aircraft should also be subject to strong rules when their operation could affect public safety, property, national security, or critical infrastructure. This proposed rule would help create clearer boundaries for UAS operators and would make the industry more professional and accountable.

I believe these restrictions can help prevent careless or irresponsible drone use while still allowing responsible operators to continue using UAS technology in safe and productive ways. Clear rules also help legitimate commercial operators by giving the public and businesses more confidence in the industry. For these reasons, I am in favor of the proposed regulation.

I have both safety and security concerns with drones being allowed in rail yards. First they are a major distraction and could cause a serious injury if you get distracted while doing a safety sensitive job. Getting distracted while operating an RCO could lead you to not hit the right control. On top of this, non FCC compliant drones could interfere with RCO operations. How can railroad workers know if a drone is compliant without a clear look. Or how can they determine a compliant or non compliant drone if you allow one and not the other.

Next is railroad security. Since 9/11 we have been instructed to approach trespassers and report any suspicious activity. Now with drone technology, terrorists can now search for hazardous materials on rail cars and identify the most dangerous all from not being on the property of known to railroad security officials. They can see further into yards and remain totally anonymous. Just as drones have been used by Ukraine to attack Russia, terrorists can now carry an explosive device and place it next to hazardous materials all from a distance and possibly without interference from railroad officials.

Please Ban ALL drones from railroad property.

routine, lawful, low-altitude missions that the proposed rule risks foreclosing if implemented without
refinement. As a SLED-licensed investigator with 38 years of practice and a national-board obligation
through NCISS, I have a particular concern that the rule provide a clear credibility pathway for
accountable, vetted commercial operators rather than treating all unmanned-aircraft activity as
presumptively suspect. And as the administrator of a GPS monitoring center, I am keenly aware of the
cybersecurity, data-retention, and chain-of-custody implications of any new federally mandated
notification or tracking system - issues addressed directly in the enclosed comment.
The enclosed comment is offered constructively. It supports the FAA's implementation of section 2209
of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016 and the framework of proposed part 74, while
identifying eight specific areas where targeted refinements would better balance the protection of
critical infrastructure against the statutory public right of transit under 49 U.S.C. § 40103 and the
legitimate operational needs of Public Safety and accountable commercial aviators.
I am available to expand on any point in the enclosed comment, to provide additional operational detail
from county Emergency Management or SAR experience, or to coordinate further input through NCISS
or SCALI as the rulemaking proceeds. Thank you for your consideration and for the FAA's continued
work on the safe integration of unmanned aircraft into the National Airspace System.

Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03; RIN 2120-AL33
91 FR 24650 (May 6, 2026); Comments Due July 6, 2026
Dear Administrator and Docket Operations:
I respectfully submit these comments on the Federal Aviation Administration's (FAA) proposed rule to
establish a process for designating Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs) under proposed 14
CFR part 74. I am the owner of Alden Wheeler Detective Agency & GPS, a licensed South Carolina private
investigation firm whose service lines include drone-supported field operations (search and rescue,
hazardous-materials response support, surveillance, and infrastructure inspection), GPS monitoring, and
a Cyber Threat Assessment practice. My current operational fleet includes a OJI Matrice 30T, a Mavic 3T,
and a Chasing Ml Pro tethered surface platform, all flown under 14 CFR part 107 with appropriate
waivers when required.
I commend the FAA for working to implement section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security
Act of 2016 (FESSA), as amended, and for attempting to balance the legitimate protection of critical
infrastructure with the public right of transit under 49 U.S.C. § 40103. The proposed rule reflects
substantial coordination with the Sector Risk Management Agencies (SRMAs) and a clear effort to apply
restrictions only where necessary. The comments below are offered in support of that balance - not
against the rule, but to identify specific provisions that, as drafted, would impose disproportionate
Page 1 of 7

Comment of Alden Wheeler - Docket FAA-2026-4558
burdens on small commercial Part 107 operators, public-safety support providers, and licensed
investigators without meaningfully advancing the rule's safety and security objectives.
I. Summary of Comments
My principal points are as follows:
• Scope and cumulative footprint. A program contemplating more than 9,000 designated fixed
site facilities, layered onto existing controlled airspace, restricted areas, and Special Security
Instructions, risks an aggregate "patchwork" effect that materially reduces low-altitude airspace
available to compliant commercial and public-safety operators. The FAA should publish, before
any UAFR program goes live, a national low-altitude availability analysis and commit to revisiting
the rule if cumulative coverage in any region exceeds a defined threshold.
• Transit standard in§ 74.250 is unconstitutionally vague. The requirement that a Part 91, 107,
108, 135, or 137 operator transit a UAFR "in the shortest practicable time" provides no objective
metric, creates strict-liability enforcement exposure, and will chill lawful overflight. The FAA
should replace this with an objective standard tied to airspeed, altitude, or duration.
• Notification under§ 74.255 must be centralized. Requiring operators to notify a "site manager"
at each facility individually is unworkable when a single mission may transit multiple UAFRs. The
FAA should adopt a single FAA-hosted notification portal modeled on LAANC, with API access
and machine-readable acknowledgments.
• Public-safety, SAR, and exigent operations need an enumerated access pathway. Proposed§
74.250 does not clearly accommodate Public Safety operations conducted under 49 U.S.C. §
44807, Certificates of Waiver or Authorization (COA), or exigent-circumstances SAR and hazmat
support. Lives are lost in minutes; access cannot depend on a 5-business-day update cycle or
facility-by-facility permission.
• Licensed private investigators conducting lawful, court-authorized investigations should have
a defined credibility pathway. The rule asks (Question E) what information an operator should
provide to establish credibility. A state-licensed Pl with an active Part 107 certificate, registered
aircraft, and verifiable insurance is a known, accountable operator who can be vetted in the
same manner as Part 135 carriers.
• Remote ID is already mandatory and is sufficient identifying technology. Layering additional
broadcast or coordination requirements on lawful operators - while malicious actors will
simply disable Remote ID - punishes the wrong population. The FAA should resist proposals to
expand Remote ID obligations beyond 14 CFR part 89.
• Notice-and-comment on Special UAFRs must remain the rule, not the exception. The "good
cause" carve-out in§ 74.6 should be narrowly construed and accompanied by mandatory post
promulgation comment within 30 days, to preserve transparency.
Page 2 of7

Comment of Alden Wheeler - Docket FAA-2026-4558
• Sector eligibility criteria should be public and challengeable. Where SRMA security
assessments cannot be disclosed in full, the FAA should publish a redacted basis document for
each UAFR so commenters can meaningfully evaluate proportionality.
II. Detailed Comments
A. Cumulative Airspace Impact and the 9,000-Facility Scenario
The FAA's own Regulatory Impact Analysis contemplates a scenario of over 9,000 eligible fixed-site
facilities obtaining UAFRs, with annualized program costs of $21-$31 million. While the per-facility
lateral footprint is bounded by the property line and an altitude ceiling generally at 400 feet AGL, the
cumulative effect on Part 107 commercial operations - utility inspection, real-estate photography,
agricultural mapping, journalism, and the rapidly growing SAR and hazmat-support sectors - has not
been adequately quantified.
I recommend that the FAA, prior to issuing any final rule, publish a quantitative analysis of: (1) projected
cumulative UAFR coverage in representative urban, suburban, and rural counties at 5-year, 10-year, and
steady-state buildout; (2) the percentage of low-altitude (~ 400 ft AGL) airspace expected to be
restricted in each; and (3) the projected effect on Part 107 commercial revenue. Without that analysis,
the public cannot meaningfully evaluate whether the rule preserves "the public right of transit"
mandated by 49 U.S.C. § 40103(a)(2).
B. The "Shortest Practicable Time" Transit Standard(§ 74.250)
Proposed § 74.250 permits a Part 91, 107, 108, 135, or 137 operator to transit a Standard UAFR
provided the operator broadcasts Remote ID and transits "in the shortest practicable time." This phrase
is undefined. In an environment where a single enforcement action can result in suspension of a Part
107 certificate under FAA Order 2150.3C and civil penalties under 14 CFR part 13, vague terms invite
selective enforcement and discourage lawful flight in or near a UAFR even when transit is otherwise
permitted.
I recommend the FAA replace the phrase with an objective standard, such as:
• Direct routing at or above the operator's normal en-route ground speed for the aircraft class,
not to exceed a defined maximum dwell time (e.g., 90 seconds) within the UAFR; or
• A maximum lateral deviation from the great-circle transit path of, e.g., 200 feet, absent ATC
instruction or operational necessity (wind, obstacles, emergency).
Either approach would give operators an objective compliance target and give enforcement personnel a
defensible metric.
C. Notification Mechanics(§ 74.255)
Page 3 of 7

Comment of Alden Wheeler - Docket FAA-2026-4558
Requiring case-by-case notification to a designated site manager at each UAFR is operationally infeasible
for any mission that traverses more than one UAFR - a routine occurrence on linear-infrastructure
inspections (pipelines, rail corridors, transmission lines) and on SAR or hazmat operations that may cross
multiple energy and water-sector facilities in a single sortie.
I recommend a single, FAA-hosted notification mechanism modeled on the Low Altitude Authorization
and Notification Capability (LAANC). The system should: (1) accept machine-to-machine notifications; (2)
automatically route them to the registered site manager(s) of each UAFR transited; (3) return a digital
acknowledgment that satisfies the operator's recordkeeping obligation; and (4) be available 24/7 with
documented uptime targets. Building one durable national system is more efficient than requiring tens
of thousands of independent operator-to-facility communication channels.
D. Public-Safety, SAR, Hazmat, and Exigent-Circumstance Operations
Proposed§ 74.250 lists Parts 91, 107, 108, 135, and 137 as allowed-operation categories. It is silent or
unclear regarding: (1) Public Safety entities operating under a Certificate of Waiver or Authorization
(COA) or under 49 U.S.C. § 44807; (2) operators supporting state or county Emergency Management or
Hazmat teams on a contract basis; and (3) any operator responding to an exigent SAR or life-safety
emergency.
Lives can turn on minutes. A drowning, a wildland search, a chlorine release at a water-treatment plant,
a derailment with multiple casualties - in each of those scenarios, a five-business-day update cycle and
a static notification list are inadequate. I recommend the FAA add an explicit§ 74.250 paragraph that
authorizes exigent-circumstance access for:
• Aircraft operating in direct support of a federal, state, tribal, or local Public Safety entity actively
responding to a life-safety, SAR, or declared emergency incident, provided Remote ID is
broadcast and the incident commander or appropriate ATC facility is notified as soon as
practicable; and
• Aircraft operating under an active Public Safety COA or 49 U.S.C. § 44807 authorization, when
transiting in support of the authorized mission.
This pathway is consistent with the FAA's long-standing practice of authorizing Public Safety operations
in otherwise restricted airspace and would not undermine the rule's security purpose.
E. Credibility Pathway for Licensed Private Investigators and Small Commercial
Operators
The FAA expressly invites comment (Executive Summary, Section F) on what information an operator
should provide to establish credibility and demonstrate they are not a security threat. From the
perspective of a licensed investigative firm, the following identifiers are already verifiable through
existing federal and state systems and should be sufficient, individually or in combination:
Page 4 of 7

Comment of Alden Wheeler - Docket FAA-2026-4558
• An active 14 CFR part 107 Remote Pilot Certificate, with TSA Security Threat Assessment on file
(already required for issuance);
• An aircraft registered under 14 CFR part 47 or 48 with broadcast Remote ID per part 89;
• A state-issued private investigator license in good standing (verifiable through the issuing state
agency - in South Carolina, through SLED);
• Evidence of commercial aviation liability insurance at a defined minimum (e.g., $1,000,000 per
occurrence) naming the FAA or facility as additional certificate holder, where applicable;
• For Public Safety support contracts, a letter of designation from the contracting public agency.
Verification of these credentials should be a one-time enrollment in a national "Trusted Operator"
registry, not a per-facility, per-flight ordeal. Operators in the registry would be eligible to transit UAFRs
subject to the notification and Remote ID requirements above.
F. Remote ID Is Sufficient; Do Not Layer Additional Broadcast Mandates
The FAA expressly asks (§ 74.56 commentary) whether more stringent Remote ID requirements should
apply to operations within a UAFR beyond 14 CFR part 89. My answer is no, for two reasons. First, lawful
operators are already burdened with the cost and complexity of compliant Remote ID modules; the
marginal security benefit of imposing further broadcast requirements on this population is minimal.
Second, and more important, malicious operators - the population the rule purports to address - will
simply disable Remote ID, fly a non-compliant aircraft, or modify a compliant one. Adding requirements
that only the law-abiding will follow is a classic case of regulatory mismatch. Resources are better spent
on detection, attribution, and counter-UAS authority for the facilities that need it.
G. Special UAFRs (§ 74.6): Preserve Notice-and-Comment as the Default
I support the proposed framework for Special UAFRs as a transparent, durable alternative to repeated
short-term 14 CFR § 99.7 Special Security Instructions. I urge the FAA, however, to construe the "good
cause to forgo notice and comment" exception narrowly. Where good cause is invoked, the FAA should
commit to: (1) publishing a redacted basis document at promulgation; (2) opening a 30-day post
promulgation comment period; and (3) sunset review of every Special UAFR at year three of its five-year
term.
H. Sector-Specific Criteria Should Be Publicly Reviewable
The preamble correctly notes that classified threat information may limit the FAA's ability to publish
sector-specific eligibility criteria in full. Where that is the case, I recommend the FAA, in coordination
with the relevant SRMA, publish a redacted basis document at the NPRM stage for each requested
UAFR. Without some publicly reviewable articulation of the threat and the proposed remedy, the 30-day
comment period contemplated by§ 74.15 is reduced to a formality. The Administrative Procedure Act's
Page 5 of 7

Comment ofA lden Wheeler - Docket FAA-2026-4558
notice-and-comment obligations are not satisfied by an opportunity to comment on an undisclosed
factual record.
I. Obligation to Update(§ 74.20) and Cyber Considerations
The five-business-day update obligation in§ 74.20(a} is reasonable in concept, but the rule does not
address the cybersecurity of the system into which those updates are filed, nor the cybersecurity of the
notification system contemplated in§ 74.255. A centralized notification system that announces, in near
real time, that a particular drone is about to overfly a particular hardened facility is itself a target. I
recommend the FAA: (1) require the operating system to meet, at a minimum, Fed RAMP Moderate; (2)
restrict site-manager access to the minimum required information; (3) prohibit retention of operator
flight data beyond a defined period; and (4) subject the system to annual third-party penetration testing
with summary results published. As an operator who provides cyber threat assessment services, I view
this as a foreseeable and meaningful risk, not a theoretical one.
J. Environmental Review(§ 74.70)
I support a streamlined NEPA approach for UAFRs that, by design, do not authorize any new physical
activity. The principal environmental considerations - noise and wildlife disturbance - are unaffected
by adding a UAFR, because the rule restricts overflight rather than authorizing it. A categorical exclusion
with documented findings is appropriate for the typical UAFR. Where a particular facility raises unique
environmental considerations (e.g., overlap with a designated wilderness area or critical habitat}, an
Environmental Assessment should be required.
Ill. Direct Responses to Questions Posed by FAA
Question: What additional types of unmanned aircraft operations should FAA
allow through a UAFR?
In addition to Parts 91, 107, 108, 135, and 137: (a} operations under an active Public Safety COA or 49
U.S.C. § 44807 authorization; (b} operations by, or under direct contract to, a federal, state, tribal, or
local public-safety agency for SAR, hazmat, fire, or law-enforcement support; (c} operations by Trusted
Operator registrants (see Section 11.E above}; and (d} exigent-circumstance overflights tied to a
documented life-safety emergency.
Question: What is the economic impact to commercial UAS operators if they
cannot transit UAFRs?
For my agency specifically, projected revenue tosses from inability to transit critical-infrastructure UAFRs
on linear-corridor inspection, SAR support contracts, and insurance-related investigative work would
meaningfully impact a small-business gross margin, in addition to displaced indirect spending on aircraft,
batteries, training, insurance, and connectivity (Starlink}. Scaled across the Part 107 commercial
population, the aggregate effect of the 9,000-facility scenario is likely to be substantial. The FAA should
Page6of7

Comment of Alden Wheeler - Docket FAA-2026-4558
quantify this directly in the final Regulatory Impact Analysis, including impacts to small entities under
the Regulatory Flexibility Act.
Question: What information should an operator provide to establish credibility?
See Section 11.E above. In brief: Part 107 certificate (with TSA STA on file), aircraft registration and
Remote ID, state Pl license (where applicable), commercial aviation liability insurance, and - for Public
Safety contracts - a letter of designation from the contracting agency. These data are already collected
by federal and state systems; the FAA should leverage existing records rather than build duplicative
ones.
Question: Are additional technological or procedural requirements appropriate
(e.g., Remote ID, site coordination, law-enforcement coordination)?
Beyond the existing Remote ID broadcast requirement under 14 CFR part 89, the FAA should consider:
(a) a machine-readable LAANC-style notification with digital acknowledgment; (b) a Trusted Operator
registry; and (c) optional voluntary coordination with local law enforcement for sensitive missions. The
FAA should not impose additional broadcast or transponder requirements that exceed part 89, for the
reasons set out in Section 11.F.
IV. Conclusion
Section 2209 of FESSA reflects a legitimate congressional judgment that certain fixed-site facilities
deserve a defined process for restricting unmanned-aircraft overflight. The FAA's proposed rule is, on
the whole, a thoughtful implementation of that mandate. With the targeted refinements outlined above
- an objective transit standard, a centralized notification system, an enumerated Public Safety and
exigent-circumstance pathway, a Trusted Operator credibility track for state-licensed investigators and
small commercial operators, narrow construction of the Special UAFR good-cause exception, and
meaningful cybersecurity safeguards around the notification system - the final rule can fairly protect
critical infrastructure without unduly burdening the lawful commercial and public-safety operators on
whom communities increasingly rely.
I appreciate the opportunity to comment and am available to discuss any of the foregoing in greater
detail.
Respectfully submitted,
Alden Wheeler
Owner, Alden Wheeler Detective Agency & GPS
Part 107 Remote Pilot
Anderson, South Carolina

I commend the FAA for working to implement section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security

Re: Comments on Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03 – Designation – Restrict
the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility (NPRM)
Dear Federal Aviation Administration Rulemaking Team:
The Jackson County Port Authority, operating as Port Pascagoula, appreciates the opportunity to
provide comments on the Federal Aviation Administration's Notice of Proposed Rulemaking
concerning the designation of unmanned aircraft flight restrictions (UAFRs) around eligible
fixed-site facilities.
As Mississippi's largest port and one of the nation's leading strategic seaports, Port Pascagoula
serves as a critical component of the United States maritime transportation system. The Port
handles military cargo, energy products, breakbulk commodities, forest products, project cargo,
and other essential cargoes that support national defense, economic stability, and supply chain
resilience. As a Maritime Transportation Security Act (MTSA) regulated facility, Port
Pascagoula maintains approved Facility Security Plans, complies with all applicable Maritime
Security (MARSEC) requirements, and works closely with the U.S. Coast Guard, Department of
Homeland Security, federal law enforcement agencies, and industry partners to protect critical
infrastructure.
Port Pascagoula strongly supports the FAA's efforts to finally implement Section 2209 of the
FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016. Establishing a formal process for critical
infrastructure operators to request drone flight restrictions is an important and long-overdue step

toward addressing the growing risks posed by unauthorized unmanned aircraft systems operating
near sensitive facilities.
The threat posed by drones continues to evolve. Ports face increasing concerns regarding
unauthorized surveillance, intelligence gathering, operational disruption, cyber vulnerabilities,
and potential physical attacks against critical infrastructure. Recent incidents across the maritime
sector have demonstrated that unmanned aircraft can be used to collect sensitive information,
monitor vessel operations, and access areas that are otherwise secured from public view.
Port Pascagoula respectfully offers the following recommendations regarding the proposed rule:
Streamline Eligibility for MTSA-Regulated Maritime Facilities
Port Pascagoula strongly encourages the FAA to establish an expedited approval process for
maritime facilities already regulated under MTSA and operating under approved Facility
Security Plans.
Maritime facilities already undergo rigorous federal oversight and maintain extensive security
measures that have been reviewed and approved by the U.S. Coast Guard. Requiring ports to
recreate or duplicate security documentation for UAFR applications would create unnecessary
administrative burdens while providing little additional security value.
Accordingly, Port Pascagoula recommends that:
• MTSA-regulated facilities with approved Facility Security Plans automatically qualify to
apply for UAFRs;
• Existing Coast Guard security documentation be accepted as supporting evidence
whenever practical;
• Strategic seaports and nationally significant maritime infrastructure receive priority
review consideration.
Recognize Port Authorities as Eligible Governing Entities
Public port authorities are uniquely positioned to coordinate security across complex, multi-
tenant environments that include cargo terminals, energy facilities, rail infrastructure, industrial
operations, and maritime transportation facilities.

Port Pascagoula's jurisdiction includes multiple facilities and tenants operating under a unified
security framework. Requiring individual operators to seek separate flight restrictions could
create inconsistencies and administrative inefficiencies.
Port Pascagoula recommends that the FAA:
• Explicitly recognize port authorities as eligible applicants capable of submitting UAFR
requests on behalf of multiple facilities;
• Allow port authorities to coordinate applications covering public port property and
associated infrastructure;
• Clarify that individual tenant participation should not be required when a port authority is
acting within its statutory authority to protect critical infrastructure.
Allow Operational Flexibility Beyond Strict Property Boundaries
Ports operate in highly complex environments where security concerns often extend beyond
surveyed property lines.
Critical maritime infrastructure frequently includes waterways, navigation channels, rail
corridors, utility easements, roadways, and interconnected facilities that function as a single
operational system. Limiting UAFRs strictly to property boundaries may leave vulnerabilities
that could be exploited by malicious actors.
Port Pascagoula recommends that the FAA:
• Permit reasonable boundary extensions when justified by security requirements;
• Allow interconnected maritime and industrial facilities to be treated as a unified
operational area;
• Consider restrictions that account for critical waterways and navigation corridors adjacent
to secure facilities.
Improve Processing Capacity and Interagency Coordination
The FAA has acknowledged that a significant volume of applications may be received once the
rule is implemented.
To ensure timely and effective processing, Port Pascagoula recommends that the FAA:

• Expand coordination with the U.S. Coast Guard, Department of Homeland Security, and
other Sector Risk Management Agencies;
• Increase staffing and technical review capacity;
• Prioritize applications from facilities supporting national defense, energy security, and
critical supply chains;
• Develop efficient electronic application and notification systems that reduce
administrative burdens while preserving transparency.
Preserve Authorized Port Drone Operations
Port Pascagoula routinely utilizes unmanned aircraft systems for legitimate operational purposes,
including:
• Infrastructure inspections;
• Facility assessments;
• Emergency response;
• Environmental monitoring;
• Construction oversight;
• Waterside security operations.
The Port requests clarification that approved drone restrictions will not inadvertently limit
authorized operations conducted by port personnel, contractors, or government agencies acting
on behalf of the Port.
Port Pascagoula supports the FAA's consideration of Remote ID-based whitelisting systems and
encourages the development of efficient mechanisms allowing authorized operators to conduct
legitimate missions within restricted areas.
Continue Advancing Counter-UAS Authorities
While Port Pascagoula strongly supports the establishment of UAFRs, flight restrictions alone
will not deter malicious actors willing to violate federal law.
In early 2026, Port Pascagoula conducted drone detection evaluations utilizing advanced
counter-UAS technologies that identified multiple unauthorized drone operations in the vicinity
of critical maritime infrastructure. These evaluations reinforced the growing need for improved
detection capabilities and stronger coordination among federal, state, and local partners.

Accordingly, Port Pascagoula encourages continued federal efforts to:
• Expand authorized counter-UAS detection and mitigation authorities for critical
infrastructure operators;
• Increase federal training opportunities for authorized personnel;
• Improve coordination among FAA, DHS, DOJ, TSA, the U.S. Coast Guard, and other
stakeholders;
• Prioritize strategic seaports in future counter-UAS programs and initiatives.
Conclusion
Port Pascagoula plays a vital role in supporting national defense, energy security, economic
development, and global commerce. As drone technology continues to advance, critical
infrastructure operators must have practical and effective tools available to address emerging
threats while preserving the safety and efficiency of the National Airspace System.
Port Pascagoula appreciates the FAA's engagement with stakeholders and its efforts to establish
a framework for protecting critical infrastructure from unauthorized unmanned aircraft activity.
We look forward to continued collaboration with the FAA, DHS, TSA, and the U.S. Coast Guard
to ensure America's ports remain secure, resilient, and prepared to address evolving threats.
Thank you for your consideration of these comments.
Sincerely,
Joseph Powell
Director of Security
Port Pascagoula
Jackson County Port Authority
