While VDOT welcomes the federal protections afforded to our qualifying sites, VDOT also
recommends that the FAA adopt a holistic approach to critical infrastructure, and in particular
the security of transportation system, looking beyond the singular fixed site and considering
the impacts on the whole network of systems. The FAA should consider considering together all
critical infrastructure assets in a certain area, not looking at the various critical infrastructure
assets as mutually exclusive. As an example, within a 5-mile segment of highway right-of-way,
there may be several critical infrastructure assets (e.g.,cellular towers, electric power stations
and communication conjunction boxes, natural gas terminal) that all have separate perimeter
fencing and controlled access points. These are just a few examples of where critical
infrastructure nodes intersect and may not always necessarily be located on facilities that meet
the UAFR criteria stated in the proposed federal rule.
The transportation infrastructure regulations should allow for ability to protect the
infrastructure areas that VDOT owns because that would best promote the public interests in
safety and security without VDOT needing toprovide additional justification to FAA regarding
the need for imposing additional restrictions pursuant to safety and security requirements
already granted to VDOT under federal or state laws.
Absent clarification that the proposed rule does not prevent state transportation agencies from
using existing legal authority to protect people and property on the ground beyond qualifying
sites, the proposed rule may substantially diminishthe role of state transportation agencies in the
planning, coordination, and oversight of aviation activities that directly affect state-owned

While the FAA maintains exclusive authority over the safety and efficiency of the National
Airspace System, states retain independent sovereign authority and statutory responsibilities for
transportation planning, infrastructure investment, right-of-way management, public property
administration, emergency management, and stewardship of taxpayer-funded transportation
assets.
State transportation agencies collectively invest billions of dollars annually in airports,
transportation corridors, communications infrastructure, navigational facilities, and emerging
technologies. These investments create a legitimate state interest that extends beyond aviation
safety regulations and includes the protection, coordination, and management of publicly owned
transportation infrastructure.
The FAA should not adopt regulatory language that could be interpreted as preempting or
diminishing the authority of states to establish reasonable coordination requirements, operational
notification procedures, infrastructure protection measures, land-use considerations, or other
requirements necessary for the states to fulfill their statutory transportation responsibilities.
Federal authority over airspace operations and aviation safety has historically coexisted with
state authority over public property, transportation systems, and infrastructure management. The
final rule should preserve this balance and expressly recognize the continued role of state
transportation agencies as governmental stakeholders with independent responsibilities and
interests in aviation-related activities occurring within their jurisdictions.
Failure to preserve an appropriate state role risks the creation of conflicts between federal
aviation objectives and state transportation responsibilities, particularly where aviation activities
intersect with state-owned rights-of-way, transportation facilities, critical infrastructure,
emergency operations, and publicly funded transportation investments.
Without appropriate state-level coordination, unnecessary challenges may arise under this
proposed rule, as owners of fixed sites could apply directly to the FAA for the establishment of
UAFRs. Those UAFRs may conflict with existing state transportation processes and
infrastructure management practices, resulting in a complex and potentially overlapping
framework of restrictions that could inadvertently increase risks to the infrastructure that this rule
was designed to protect.
As stated above, VDOT is this state department of transportation and
has
safety under Title 33.2 of the Code of Virginia. Absent clarification of the proposed rule, the
FAA seems, in effect, to be removing or substantially limiting the ability of VDOT and other
similarly organized state transportation agencies to carry out these responsibilities when aviation
activities directly affect state-owned transportation infrastructure and assets.
VDOT stands ready to work directly with the FAA to reduce risks and improve the transparency,
consistency, and effectiveness of UAFRs across the country. A transportation-led coordination
process would align with existing transportation planning processes and authorized federal, state,
and local permitting activities, including right-of-entry requirements, limited-access controls,

1. FAA Proposed § 74.255 Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restriction Access Notification
VDOT could support a FAA Low Altitude Authorization and Notification Capability (LAANC)
or Special Government Interest (SGI) process if state property interests and ground-based safety
responsibilities remain intact, the process does not adversely impact state level operations, and

VDOT agrees that operations conducted by a U.S. government agency or law enforcement acting
within the scope of their legal authority and responding to an actual emergency or law

Allowing all UAS operations under 14 C.F.R.part 107, part 135, or part 137 that are not directly
related to the operations, maintenance, and safety of the transportation system within a UAFR
may have unintended consequences, including putting sites at risk or impairing

As a practical matter, facility owners have limited ability to determine during flight whether a

2. FAA Proposed § 74.100, Approvals and Denial (Subpart D)
VDOT supports the use of the Federal Register for the posting of approvals and denials of
UAFRs, with a comment period of at least 30 days. VDOT would prefer that FAA continue to

3. FAA Proposed § 74.160, Petitions to reconsider denial (Subpart E)
VDOT appreciates the need for expediency, but a 30-calendar day response to a denial

Require coordination between individual UAFRs and jurisdiction-wide airspace management programs.

The proposed rule is facility-centric. Individual facilities apply for individual UAFRs, and the airspace designations are published through FAA Order JO 7400.12. What the rule does not address is the coordination layer between a UAFR designation and the broader jurisdiction in which it sits.
In our assessment work, we consistently find that effective airspace management depends on jurisdictional awareness: the ability of a county sheriff's office, a municipal public safety department, or a regional coordination body to understand what is happening in the airspace above their jurisdiction and to integrate that picture into operational response. A UAFR designation changes the legal status of airspace in a jurisdiction. If the jurisdiction has no awareness of the designation, no integration with the detection and notification systems that serve the designated facility, and no coordination protocols with the facility's site manager, the UAFR creates a legal restriction without creating a governance layer to manage it.

The final rule should require UAFR applicants to document how the designation will be communicated to and coordinated with the relevant state or local law enforcement and emergency management agencies. It should also require that UAFR boundary data be published in machine-readable formats integrated with existing UAS flight planning infrastructure on the day any designation takes effect. Operators should never discover a UAFR through violation.

comments.commenter: Comment from Office of the Attorney General of the State of New York
comments.comment_text: The Office of the Attorney General of the State of New York respectfully requests that the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) provide a 30-day extension for public comments on the notice of proposed rulemaking titled "Designation: Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility" for the reasons stated in the attached letter. We respectfully request that any notice by the FAA concerning this request be published in the Federal Register no later than July 3, 2026.

Explicitly recognize passive, non-cooperative detection as meeting the detection capability requirement under proposed § 74.56.

Proposed § 74.56 requires UAFR applicants to demonstrate capability to receive and log Remote ID broadcasts from unmanned aircraft operating within or near the UAFR. This is a necessary but insufficient requirement. A malicious operator will not broadcast Remote ID. Remote ID-dependent detection provides no protection against the threat scenarios that most justify UAFR designations: deliberate surveillance of facility operations, payload delivery, and coordinated incursions.

Passive, non-cooperative detection systems that receive ambient signals without emitting, jamming, or interfering with any aircraft or communication system are the most practically deployable detection layer available to private-sector facility operators. These systems do not require FCC coordination, do not implicate jamming prohibition statutes, and can be deployed immediately without federal authorization.

The final rule should explicitly state that passive, non-cooperative detection capability meets and supplements the Remote ID reception requirement under § 74.56, and should clarify that operating such systems does not constitute "interference" under existing law. Without that clarification, facilities will remain uncertain whether deploying the most effective available detection layer is legally permissible, and many will forgo it. That uncertainty directly undermines the layered security standard the rule intends to require.

Mandate a fast-track authorization pathway for commercial operators.

The proposed rule creates restrictions with no corresponding authorization system. The practical consequence is that Part 107 commercial operators performing facility-invited work (infrastructure inspections, utility surveys, maintenance documentation) will have no reliable pathway to access UAFR airspace even when the facility has authorized them. The LAANC program demonstrated that the private sector can build and operate fast, reliable airspace authorization infrastructure under FAA authorization. A UAFR authorization platform requires more facility-level customization than LAANC's geographic zone model, but the architecture is achievable and the market will build it if the FAA requires it. The final rule should mandate that any active UAFR include a defined, FAA-authorized authorization pathway for compliant commercial operators and emergency services personnel. That pathway should provide near-real-time field verification, allow facilities to pre-register authorized operators by Remote ID, and produce a logged record of all authorized operations within the UAFR. Without this requirement, the rule effectively grounds the commercial operators that the facilities themselves depend on for maintenance and inspection.

I am currently a passenger locomotive engineer who's career began at Union Pacific in 2008, and spans 5 western states. I have several concerns about unmanned aircraft operations near railroad facilities.

First, a drone flying near switching operations is very distracting to a crew performing work that is extremely safety sensitive. A distraction at the wrong moment can cost a worker life or limb in a split second. I've witnessed first hand on several occasions where Union Pacific officials flying drones to "observe" switching operations have caused workers to pay more attention to the drone than the work they are doing, causing an unnecessarily dangerous high risk situation.

Second, I have valid concerns about terrorism. Freight rail hauls poisonous and toxic hazardous materials in very high volumes through population centers throughout the country. A drone attack on a tank car loaded with a toxic inhalation hazardous material in an urban area could kill or injure an entire city's population. There is no way to tell if a drone flying near a train car loaded with hazardous material is friend or foe from the ground, so no drone should be allowed anywhere near freight rail operations.

Lastly, something that I frequently experience as a passenger train engineer is rail fans using drones to film our trains as we operate them. They are small, fast, and very distracting. Operating a train with 200 passengers at high speed requires my full attention to do so safely. My passengers deserve my full attention and focus on getting them to their destination safely. A drone distracting me as I operate creates unnecessary danger to me, and my passengers.

The right thing to do for both the public and rail workers is to pass a rule banning drone operations near rail facilities and operations.

Thank you,
Jeremy Hansen
SLC, Utah

FPV Freedom Coalition comments on FAA 2209 NPRM
Reference:
Designation – Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site
Facility
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03
RIN 2120-AL33
Comments submitted by:
Dave Messina
President & CEO, FPV Freedom Coalition
dmessina@fpvfc.org
http://www.fpvfc.org
June 26, 2026
Summary
FPV Freedom Coalition is one of four FAA-recognized Community Based Organizations.
Since 2019, the FPV Freedom Coalition has advocated the FAA and provided safety
education for recreational multi-rotor sUAS flyers which are the largest cohort of sUAS
operators and pilots, numbering over 1.5 million flyers per the FAA’s estimates. This NPRM,
continues a FAA pattern, like the Beyond Visual Line of Sight NPRM where recreational
flyers are ignored or completely short-changed. Ignoring recreational operators is short-
sighted since the safety record of recreational operators flying multi-rotor, electric aircraft
is has zero fatalities.
The FAA’s continued lack of recognition and support of recreational sUAS flyers will have
long-term negative impact on the commercial, public safety and military sectors because
young recreational operators are the resource pool of future technicians, engineers,
programmers and pilots. School based drone programs across the country are growing and
may be forced to small, indoors operations to avoid ever more-restrictive regulations from
the FAA.
As always, we welcome hearing from the FAA and would be happy to provide additional
insight into our comments.
Issues:
1. As just stated in the summary, this NPRM has no provision for 44809 / Recreational
flyers. Operators with part 107, 91, and 135 can transit UAFRs but Recreational
flyers are not. Why? There is no record of safety or security issues. There are vague
references that the reason Recreational operators are omitted from this rule is that
FPVFC comments on 2209 NPRM.docx 1

FPV Freedom Coalition comments on FAA 2209 NPRM
they are less educated on aviation topics and therefore less safe. Recreational
operators using electric multi-rotors have been logging more flight-hours than any
other flyer or pilot of any aircraft without a fatality.
2. No data supporting the creation of this rule. New FAA rules and regulations are
supposed to be supported with data and rationale. The closest this NPRM comes to
this is in I. Executive Summary, A. Background where the following sentences state
there is a problem, but no data is presented to back up the claim:
a. “…The relatively low cost of highly capable UAS technology has triggered
hundreds of thousands of new operators [FPVFC note this is grossly
understated. There are 1.5 million recreational operators in the USA per the
FAA.] to enter the aviation community, some of whom have aviation
knowledge and experience and many others who do not. The proliferation of
these operations presents significant opportunities for innovation and growth
and represents a driving force in the aviation sector. As the scale and scope
of UAS activities has grown, some stakeholders have become concerned
about the safety and security implication of unmanned aircraft (UA) flying in
close proximity to certain type of facilities. [New paragraph] These concerns
led Congress to enact section 22-09 of the FAA Extension, Safety and
Security Act of 2016 (FESSA), directing FAA to create a system under which
operators or proprietors of certain fixed site facilities…”
b. No data is presented. What issues are we solving? Are we calming concerns
of Congressional Representatives TEN YEARS after this bill was passed?
There have been zero fatalities caused by electric, multi-rotor sUAS’s. What
issue is addressed?
3. Remote ID receiving station requirement is unnecessary and over-burdens UAFR
operator/proprietors. The NPRM and the Advisory Circular describe the requirement
where a FSF must establish not only a system to receive Remote ID broadcasts from
UAS transiting the FSF’s fixed site but also is expected to verify the UAS matches the
notification (Standard UAFR) or approval (Special UAFR) and log this verification of
Remote ID Broadcast signal against the notification or request for approval. This
requirement is highly overburdensome and creates a high risk of litigation by the
UAS operator against false violations as logged by the FSF.
4. The Advisory Circular [Subject: Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Air raft in Close
Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility], associated with this NPRM overreaches normal
Advisory Circulars in that while the AC is non-binding, there are numerous new and
more stringent rules and requirements meaning this Advisory Circular appears to be
reaching into rulemaking without the rulemaking process.
FPVFC comments on 2209 NPRM.docx 2

FPV Freedom Coalition comments on FAA 2209 NPRM
5. This NPRM follows the BVLOS NPRM in that it sets out requirements which are
costly and without sufficient data prompting such draconian restrictions. The
BVLOS NPRM is targeted to package delivery and effectively eliminates part 107 and
recreational operators from flying BVLOS. This 2029 NPRM follows that pattern of
being overly complex and costly. A likely result will be fewer sUAS operators. Is the
objective of the FAA to dramatically reduce the number of sUAS in the NAS?
Detail Comments:
Section: 74.70
Issue/Concern:
The FAA requires a 2209 application to comply with National Environmental Policy
Act of 1969. This appears to be an unreasonable requirement. The UAFR application must
provide information to the FAA on Historic or cultural resources under the National Historic
Preservation Act of 1966, The Presence of Tribal land Federally Recognized Tribes,
Properties protected under section 4(f) of the Department Transportation (Act 49 U.S.C
303), and more.
FPVFC Comment:
Section: Lateral Boundary 74.58
Issue/Concern:
The FAA requests comments regarding lateral boundaries of the UAFR.
FPVFC Comment:
FPVFC supports the UAFR boundary not to exceed the operator’s or proprietor’s property
boundary. Expanding UAFR beyond the property boundary would not make sense as the
rationale of the UAFR is to protect the property or assets on the UAFR.
Section: Protective Security, Section 74.56 (4)
Issue/Concern:
The FAA requires the UAFR applicant to receive Remote ID messages from unmanned
aircraft operating within or in close proximity to the UAFR.
FPVFC Comment:
Having the UAFR operator/proprietor be responsible for receiving Remote ID signals and
then reporting issues to law enforcement changes the role of the UAFR operator/proprietor
FPVFC comments on 2209 NPRM.docx 3

FPV Freedom Coalition comments on FAA 2209 NPRM
to that of law enforcement without the appropriate authority. This creates unnecessary
liability for the UAFR operator/proprietor. This requirement should be removed from the
2029 final rule.
With respect to the FAA requesting comments on any new Remote ID requirements to any
other pertinent information the FAA should consider when requiring Remote ID to be the
main methodology for making UAS conspicuous to FSFs. The FAA is delegating detection
of UAFR transit to the UAFR operator/proprietor and then is asking if there is additional
information or an alternative to gathering this information. FPVFC recommends this
requirement to police the transit of the UAFR should not fall to the UAFR
operator/proprietor. Is there a manned aircraft analog where property owners are required
to monitor and report violations of TFRs?
The FAA also seeks comments on the economic impact such requirements would have in
terms of cost to the fixed-site operator and market supply of RID sensing technology. The
FPVFC’s comments to this are: There is no problem for anyone in the USA procuring RID
receiving technology. The economic impact of this requirement comes from additional
insurance costs to cover potential legal liability costs from litigation brought by sUAS flyers
who have been incorrectly accused of wrongly transiting a UAFR.
Advisory Circular Comment: The UAFR Advisory Circular materially increases the
requirements of the UAFR operator/proprietor beyond the NPRM to the point where the FAA
is rulemaking via Advisory Circular. This is a violation of statute, and the requirements
should be removed from the Advisory Circular.
Section: Eligible Facilities, Section 74.54
Issue/Concern:
The FAA proposes to have an applicant be required to have protective security measures in
place that are commensurate with the site’s criticality to national security, home security,
aviation safety and protection of persons and property within the UAFR area.
FPVFC Comment:
The FPVFC’s recommendation is this requirement should be removed from the final rule.
For a Standard UAFR, we propose that no layers of protective security are the commiserate
level of required security. For a Special UAFR, the using Agency should be responsible for
establishing protective security levels and not the UAFR operator/proprietor.
FPVFC comments on 2209 NPRM.docx 4

FPV Freedom Coalition comments on FAA 2209 NPRM
Section: General, Section 74.50
Issue/Concern:
The Advisory Circular associated with the 2209 NPRM contains requirements which are
better described as rules rather than in a non-binding AC.
FPVFC Comment:
The FPVFC recommends the following requirements within the AC should be removed as
they appear to be rulemaking via AC. The AC appears to work around the notice and
comment process which is required by statute.
We recommend Section 3.5 Protective Security and Section 3.6 UAS Security and Incident
Response Plan both be removed from the Advisory Circular and be republished as an
addendum to the NPRM with a new window for public comment. Both of these sections
establish new regulations and rules which the Fixed Site Facility Operator/Proprietor are
required to comply with. Because Advisory Circulars are non-binding, how can these new
stipulations be taken seriously by FSF Operator/Proprietors? And, we assert the content of
these two sections constitute rules and regulations and therefore require promulgation
through the notice and comment process like the rest of the 2029 NPRM.
Section: Definitions, Section 74.1
Issue/Concern:
The FAA defines a Fixed site facility to mean a permanent structure, building or asset with
defined geographic boundaries.
FPVFC Comment:
The FPVFC applauds the FAA’s decision to not include mobile assets as Fixed Sites. While
this may appear obvious, the inclusion of mobile assets in recent NOTAMS has created a
situation where the only way to comply was to ground all sUAS.
Section: Definitions, Section 74.1
Issue/Concern:
The FAA identifies a FAA Order JO 7400.12 as included in the same docket as the 2029
NPRM.
FPVFC comments on 2209 NPRM.docx 5

FPV Freedom Coalition comments on FAA 2209 NPRM
FPVFC Comment:
The FAA Order JO 7400.12 in draft or final form appears not to have been published. The

Section: IV Overview of the Proposed Rule, B. What is a UAFR?
Issue/Concern:
The FAA notes that Special UAFRs remain subject to notice-and-comment rulemaking
unless good cause exists to forgo notice and comment thereby balancing protection of
critical national-security sites with the FAA’s mandate for safe and efficient NAS access.
FPVFC Comment:
It is the understanding of the FPVC that the notice-and-comment process may be forgone if
an emergency exists. Through 2025, the US Federal government has claimed emergencies
exist when it is difficult for the common man to understand the emergency. The FPVFC

Section: IV. Overview of the Proposed Rule, B. What is an UAFR?
Issue/Concern:
We believe these sentences may be inconsistent with other claims in the NPRM:
“Violations to Standard UAFRES may lead to civil enforcement actions, while violations of
Special UAFRs may lead to civil or criminal enforcement actions consistent with FAA’s

FPVFC comments on 2209 NPRM.docx 6

FPV Freedom Coalition comments on FAA 2209 NPRM
Section: E. FAA Evaluation, Approvals and Denial (Subparts D and E of Part 74)
1. Evaluation
Issue/Concern:
The FAA asks for comments on the possibility of an applicating having to gain a security

Where a Fixed site contains national security assets or intellectual property, a security

The following comments relate to draft Advisory Circular, Subject: Restrict the

Section: Section 7.2 What are the Operational Requirements? Remote ID
Issue/Concern:
This section includes these statements: “Consistent with requirements under 14 CFR Part
89, unless other authorized by the Administrator, UA operations within a UAFR will be
required to broadcast Remote ID. This underpins the FSF’s requirements to have
technology to sense Remote ID broadcast signals and will allow them to match the allowed
UA operations Remote ID serial number with the notification the FAF received via the UAFR
module notifying them of the allowed operations with requisite Remote ID serial number.
This is a crucial aspect for addressing security concerns over these sensitive facilities.”
FPVFC comments on 2209 NPRM.docx 8

FPV Freedom Coalition comments on FAA 2209 NPRM
FPVFC Comment:
As this is characterized as a “crucial aspect” for addressing security concerns, this section
needs to be formally added to the NPRM via a new window of comments. This content
rises to that of a new rule and if it remains in the Advisory Circular, it will result in a violation
of statute by the FAA of rulemaking outside the notice and comment process.
Next, this requirement for the FSF to match notified Remote ID serial numbers with
broadcast Remote ID serial numbers is nearly impossible and opens the FSF to litigation.
Both will result in widespread non-compliance. This AC-based rule is nearly impossible
because for transit of a Standard UAFR, the UAV operator must only notify the FSF. If this
notification is close to the time of transit, how could a FSF be responsible for matching
Remote ID serial numbers. In the case of Specialized UAFR, this becomes difficult, costly
and risky as any mistake could result in a criminal penalty of a UAV operator. Mistakes
would result in litigation of the FSF. This is a risk few FSF proprietors should take.
The FPVFC requests the FAA to scrap this section.
FPVFC comments on 2209 NPRM.docx 9

I work in the transportation industry for Union Pacific, I was working a local and was being tested at my home terminal and when I was being briefed by the manager at that terminal he told me to watch out for drone testing at my future pick ups in my route for the local job I was on that day.  Every time I went to a different industry my focus was blurred because of the drone testing.  The drone only has a top view and the testing can be easily miss judged by the distance between equipment and other hazards the employee is avoiding but the drone might not see it, I bring this up because after the test is performed (we) the employees don't find out about the test until days later and it's hard to fight your case at that point.

Please don’t add more restrictions for hard
Working professional drone operators. The DJI ban was bad enough. More restrictions will be horrible for this most beneficial industry.
Thank you.

This rulemaking is particularly significant given the documented increase in unauthorized drone activity across critical infrastructure sectors, including energy facilities. The absence of a clear, consistent framework has limited the ability of owners and operators to proactively mitigate these risks through airspace protections. DTM views the proposed UAFR construct as a foundational control that enhances deterrence, improves situational awareness, and establishes a regulatory mechanism for protecting facilities. At the same time, we recognize the FAA’s responsibility to balance security needs with the continued integration and safe operation of UAS within the National Airspace System (NAS), and we support this balanced approach.

As the FAA advances toward a final rule, it is essential that the framework remains practical, risk-informed, and operationally implementable for industry stakeholders. This includes ensuring clarity in eligibility criteria, timely processing of applications, and alignment with existing security and compliance obligations. DTM emphasizes that UAFRs should be viewed as an initial step, not a complete solution. While they provide an important deterrent and regulatory safeguard, additional clarity and progress on scalable, lawful counter-UAS capabilities will be necessary to fully address the evolving threat landscape facing critical infrastructure owners and operators.

Drone usage for operations testing should have never been implemented in the first place. I voiced my concerns to management and much like every other valid concern it was downplayed. I have been tested around 150 times in the last calendar year. Drones are the biggest distraction I have ever encountered, 22 year employee btw. Even when drones aren’t out we are consistently watching out for them creating distractions. Bnsf also does not stop you from moving when they see a serious rules violation. They let you keep working until you are finished with the industry, work or current moves being made. I have questioned them about this and the response was “we like to observe”. “Sometime we can’t observe because of a serious rules violation at the start of testing”. I for one feel harassed since drone testing has been implemented. 150 times in a year is excessive considering my peers are around 10-15 a year.

Comments Submitted re: Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity
to a Fixed Site Facility - FAA-2026-4558-0001. Submitted June 29, 2026.
My name is Ed Ruttledge, and I reside at the following address:
295 Church Street, SE #404
Salem, Oregon 97301
I am a semi-professional photographer. Retired, I provide photographic and video content
as an unpaid volunteer to government entities and non-profit organizations which have
included the following:
• Cascade Volcano Observatory (USGS)
• State of Oregon
• Tillamook Public Utility District
• The Tierra Del Mar Neighborhood Association
• The Vashon Island Nature Center
• Intercity Transit – Olympia, Washington
• The Civita Institute
I am a Certified Remote Pilot (#4234594) and presently have three small camera drones
registered with the FAA.
The primary purpose of my involvement with UAS devices has been to extend my
photographic capabilities. As technology advanced, it became possible to capture whole
new perspectives, especially in landscape and architectural photography by using small
UAS devices such as the Skydio 2+ and DJI Air3 I presently own. My considerable
photographic experience was in still photography. But my work with UAS devices
platforms has developed my interest in doing video work as well. While I have become a
skilled pilot of these UAV devices, flying a UAV is secondary to its function as a camera
platform.
There a myriad of issues raised by this proposed rule. I will focus on three topics in my
comments. I reserve my privilege to submit additional commentary within the comment
period. My comments regarding the proposed rules include the following:
• There needs to be a clear statement of Federal pre-emption - declaring UAS
airspace regulation to be an exclusive FAA function. This would preempt any state,
tribal, territorial, local or private entity rule that restricts UAS operations based on
proximity to critical infrastructure. Without such Federal pre-emption, we can
realistically expect to have a patchwork of regulations from a myriad of jurisdictions
or other entities impacting the operation of UAS devices within the NAS. Further,
notice to UAS pilots would become fragmented as would any waiver or
authorization procedures.

• Transit through a Standard UAFR should not be prohibited, especially if the device
broadcasts Remote ID, and moves through the Standard UFAR in the shortest
practicable time.
• Measurable care should be taken in determining what constitutes “critical
infrastructure.” Definitions need to be unambiguous. For example, what if
electrical substations and cell towers are included as “critical infrastructure?”
They are everywhere. Is the T-Mobile Gateway sitting on my windowsill, which
sends and receives 5G voice and data transmissions, a cell tower? What about the
series of undersea landing nodes that populate the California and Oregon
coastlines [and, which are usually underground and often purposefully obscured]?
Transit corridors crisscross every neighborhood. Even the definition of what is a
“railroad” could be debated. For example, is the Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) system in
Eugene, Oregon a “railroad?” Would the Rail Riders attraction in Tillamook County,
Oregon be a “railroad” or, perhaps, a linear “amusement park?”
Thank you for your consideration of the above in drafting the rule.

Drone photography has become one of the most important tools for making the very often hidden world of industrial animal agriculture visible to the public which people should have the basic right to look up and learn about. I sincerely hope the FAA will carefully consider the crucial public value of aerial photojournalism as it evaluates this proposal.

Hello,
I have 20 plus years of railroad service in transportation. I have been trained to report anything out of sync with my work environment. Boxes and containers placed haphazardly on or in railcars, mysterious containers on the right of way. This frontline defense is in addition to the multitasking of operating in the railroad setting; pointing and calling for switch handling, reviewing switching orders, looking out for poor walking conditions, wildlife and insects that may be lurking inside a railcar or underneath switch stand timbers, and keeping a monitoring eye on a massive influx of new hire trainees. As you can see my plate is full and my eyes are forever surveying the landscape before, beneath, and beside me.
I certainly, don't need another layer of distraction or another box to check off to see if the drone in the sky is friend or foe. Is it over by the PIH tank car because it's watching me or is this the first phase of a techno terrorism assault.
Regards,
Shawn Bruderly

comments.commenter: Comment from American Association of Port Authorities
comments.comment_text:
July 3, 2026
Docket Operations
U.S. Department of Transportation
1200 New Jersey Avenue, S.E., Room W58-213
Washington, D.C. 20590-0001
Desk Officer for Federal Aviation Administration
Office of Management and Budget
Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs
New Executive Office Building
725 17th Street, N.W.,
Room 10202
Washington, D.C. 20053
Re: Comments on Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03 – Designation – Restrict
the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
Dear Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) Office of Rulemaking,
The American Association of Port Authorities (AAPA) appreciates the opportunity to comment
on the FAA’s Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) concerning the designation of unmanned
aircraft flight restrictions (UAFRs) around eligible fixed site facilities. AAPA represents public
port authorities throughout the United States, including many of the nation’s largest strategic
seaports and critical infrastructure hubs.
AAPA applauds the FAA’s efforts to implement Section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and
Security Act of 2016. Establishing a process for eligible facilities to seek drone flight restrictions
is a meaningful step toward addressing the growing threat posed by unauthorized unmanned
aircraft systems (UAS) operating over critical infrastructure.
America's ports increasingly face drone-related vulnerabilities and operational disruptions. Ports
routinely handle hazardous materials, military cargo, cruise passengers, energy products,
chemical shipments, and essential consumer goods that are vital to national economic and
homeland security. Unauthorized drone activity in the vicinity of any of these facilities raises
safety, surveillance, and operational concerns. Moreover, the threat posed by attack drones is
particularly acute in the cruise sector, where a single vessel may carry several thousand
passengers and crew. A successful attack against a large passenger vessel could result in
catastrophic loss of life on a scale rivaling the casualties inflicted during previous terrorist
attacks, underscoring the urgent need for stronger protections for America’s seaports and
maritime gateways.
Strategic seaports are increasingly recognized as high value targets for foreign intelligence
collection, cyber-enabled surveillance, and disruptive activities involving UAS. Operational
disruptions at major ports can rapidly cascade throughout national and regional supply chains,
1

affecting fuel distribution, agricultural exports, manufacturing inputs, military readiness, and
consumer goods availability. Many ports also support Department of War and Department of
Transportation strategic sealift and military outload operations, further elevating their importance
to national security.
Because of these unique operational and national security responsibilities, ports warrant special
consideration within the FAA's implementation of Section 2209. The consequences of
unauthorized drone activity at a port may extend far beyond the immediate facility and impact
broader economic resilience, defense readiness, and homeland security objectives.
Given the sensitivity of their operations and proximity to large population centers, ports
represent some of the most mature and security-conscious critical infrastructure operators in the
nation. Accordingly, many port facilities are regulated under the Maritime Transportation
Security Act of 2002 (MTSA), maintain approved Facility Security Plans (FSPs), comply with
Maritime Security (MARSEC) requirements, and coordinate closely with the U.S. Coast Guard
(USCG) and federal law enforcement agencies.
AAPA strongly supports FAA’s recognition that fixed site facilities regulated under MTSA
explicitly qualify for UAFRs. We also appreciate the FAA’s acknowledgment that unmanned
aircraft may pose risks associated with surveillance, cyber intrusion, operational disruption, and
physical attacks against critical infrastructure.
AAPA respectfully offers the following recommendations and concerns regarding the proposed
rule:
Streamline Eligibility for Maritime Transportation Facilities: AAPA strongly encourages
FAA to establish an expedited or presumptive approval pathway for maritime facilities already
regulated under MTSA and operating under an active USCG-approved FSP or Alternate Security
Plan.
The NPRM appropriately recognizes that maritime transportation facilities regulated under
MTSA are eligible to request a UAFR. These facilities already undergo extensive federal security
oversight and maintain layered physical and cybersecurity protections. Ports should not be
required to duplicate extensive security demonstrations already validated through USCG
processes. Further, MTSA-regulated facilities already satisfy many of the security and
vulnerability assessment requirements contemplated by the NPRM. FSPs, Facility Security
Assessments, Alternate Security Programs, Area Maritime Security Committee participation, and
ongoing USCG oversight provide a robust security framework that FAA should leverage
wherever possible. Aligning UAFR eligibility with existing MTSA requirements would reduce
administrative burden, eliminate duplicative federal review, and advance broader interagency
efficiency objectives.
Accordingly, AAPA recommends that:
• MTSA-regulated facilities with approved FSPs automatically qualify to apply for a
UAFR;
2

• The FAA establish a streamlined review process for strategic seaports and maritime
critical infrastructure; and
• Existing USCG security documentation be accepted in lieu of duplicative FAA-specific
submissions where practicable.
Recognize Port Authorities as Governing Critical Infrastructure Operators: The NPRM
currently places substantial emphasis on individual site operators and property interests.
However, many public port authorities are governmental entities with statutory authority over
complex multi-tenant critical infrastructure environments.
Ports frequently contain multiple energy, cargo, and cruise terminals, chemical facilities, rail
operations, and federally regulated maritime facilities operating within a unified security
environment. Requiring each tenant individually to apply for separate UAFRs would create
inefficiency, inconsistency, and unnecessary administrative burdens.
AAPA recommends that FAA:
• Explicitly recognize port authorities as eligible applicants to apply for UAFRs covering
multiple facilities within port jurisdictional boundaries;
• Allow port authorities to coordinate UAFRs on behalf of tenants and operators; and
• Clarify that tenant objections or lack of participation should not prevent a port authority
from applying for a UAFR covering publicly governed port property.
As governmental entities responsible for the safety and security of maritime infrastructure, port
authorities should retain the ability to coordinate drone security measures across their jurisdiction
if they choose.
Allow Flexibility Beyond Strict Property-Line Boundaries: AAPA is concerned that the
proposed limitation restricting UAFR lateral boundaries strictly to property lines may create
significant operational and security gaps for ports and industrial waterfront facilities. Maritime
facilities are fundamentally different from many land-based critical infrastructure sites. Ports by
definition include waterside operational areas, including ship berths, mooring dolphins, vessel
loading zones, and navigation channels. These maritime assets operate as interconnected
facilities with rail corridors and public roadways in a unified security environment. As a result,
many ports contain noncontiguous parcels, with public roadways bisecting secure facilities,
shared intermodal corridors, or waterside operational areas. Connected energy and chemical
infrastructure can also be separated by easements or transportation corridors. Limiting UAFRs to
upland property boundaries may leave many of the assets most in need of protection uncovered.
For MTSA-regulated facilities, FAA should consider allowing applicants to define UAFR
boundaries consistent with the USCG-recognized security footprint reflected in approved FSPs
and related security documentation. Such an approach would better reflect the operational
realities of maritime facilities and ensure protection of waterside assets, vessel berths, and
adjacent security zones that are integral to port operations.
3

In addition, modern UAS platforms equipped with advanced optical zoom, thermal imaging, and
other surveillance capabilities may collect sensitive information while operating outside a
facility's strict property lines. FAA should provide applicants flexibility to justify reasonable
security buffer areas to address demonstrated surveillance vulnerabilities and operational risks.
AAPA encourages FAA to:
• Allow reasonable flexibility to extend UAFR boundaries beyond strict property lines
when justified by operational security considerations;
• Permit interconnected maritime and industrial facilities to be treated as a unified security
site where appropriate;
• Consider operational realities unique to ports and waterfront infrastructure such as flight
restrictions over critical navigation channels; and
• Accept USCG-recognized security footprints reflected in approved FSPs.
Address Vertical Boundary Limitations and Aerial Surveillance Risks: AAPA encourages
FAA to further evaluate whether the proposed vertical limitations adequately account for the
unique characteristics of maritime facilities, existing FAA operational authorities, and the
capabilities of modern UAS.
The NPRM generally limits UAFRs to 400 feet above ground level unless a facility contains
structures exceeding 300 feet in height. While AAPA appreciates FAA's effort to align UAFR
ceilings with existing small UAS operating limitations, this approach may not fully address the
security risks facing maritime facilities.
Many port facilities contain ship-to-shore cranes, container handling equipment, refinery
structures, loading arms, communications infrastructure, and other industrial assets that routinely
exceed 150 to 250 feet in height. Under 14 CFR § 107.51(b), small UAS may operate up to 400
feet above a structure's immediate uppermost limit. As a result, a drone operating near a 250-foot
ship-to-shore crane could potentially fly as high as 650 feet above ground level while remaining
compliant with existing FAA regulations.
AAPA appreciates the exception proposed in 14 § 74.60(c), which would allow the FAA to raise
a UAFR's altitude ceiling when a facility contains a structure exceeding 300 feet in height.
However, many maritime facilities contain critical infrastructure below that threshold.
Consequently, a port facility with a 250-foot ship-to-shore crane could face a circumstance where
drones are authorized to operate substantially above the proposed UAFR ceiling while still
maintaining the ability to observe terminal operations and other sensitive activities.
This issue is particularly significant because modern UAS platforms are increasingly capable of
conducting detailed surveillance from substantial distances and altitudes. High-resolution
cameras, advanced optical zoom capabilities, thermal imaging systems, wireless signal collection
technologies, and other commercially available tools may allow operators to observe cargo
operations, military vessel movements, security procedures, access control points, hazardous
material handling activities, and other sensitive operational information without physically
entering a facility's immediate perimeter.
4

Beyond traditional visual surveillance, UAS may also be capable of collecting information
regarding security personnel assignments and patrol patterns, operational movements, facility
vulnerabilities, and other indicators of security posture. Certain UAS platforms may also
facilitate cyber-enabled reconnaissance activities, including the collection of wireless network
information, signal interception, packet capture, or communications disruption attempts. In
addition, thermal imaging capabilities may reveal heat signatures associated with critical
infrastructure, refrigerated cargo, electrical substations, machinery, vessels, vehicles, or
personnel activity, potentially allowing UAS operators to identify active versus dormant
facilities, equipment, or operational areas. These concerns are particularly acute at Strategic
Seaports and other facilities supporting military deployments, defense logistics, energy
transportation, and critical supply chain operations, where collected information could have
significant homeland security or national defense implications.
From a security perspective, the risk is not solely the drone's physical presence over a facility,
but also its ability to collect intelligence regarding critical infrastructure operations. A drone
operating above or adjacent to a facility may be capable of observing many of the same activities
that would otherwise be protected by physical barriers, fencing, waterside security measures, and
restricted access controls. In many cases, the intelligence collection value of a drone may persist
even when the aircraft remains outside the proposed lateral or vertical boundaries of a UAFR.
Accordingly, AAPA recommends that FAA:
• Consider additional flexibility for facilities containing exceptionally tall industrial
structures, including ship-to-shore cranes, refinery infrastructure, and other maritime
assets;
• Allow applicants to demonstrate a need for expanded vertical protections based on
facility-specific security risks and infrastructure characteristics;
• Evaluate whether existing altitude exceptions could create unintended surveillance
opportunities that undermine the effectiveness of UAFRs; and
• Consider amending the altitude restriction proposed in 14 § 74.60 to be 400 feet above
the tallest structure on the facility.
Improve Coordination and Processing Capacity: The NPRM acknowledges that FAA expects
significant application volume and potential delays. AAPA shares concerns regarding whether
FAA possesses sufficient staffing and resources to review potentially thousands of applications in
a timely manner.
AAPA encourages FAA to:
• Expand interagency coordination with Sector Risk Management Agencies (SRMAs),
including USCG and the Department of Homeland Security (DHS);
• Increase reviewer staffing and technical expertise;
• Consider phased or prioritized implementation for nationally significant critical
infrastructure sectors, such as for those already maintaining FSPs;
• Develop proposed website-based notice mechanisms to streamline processing while
preserving meaningful public comment opportunities; and
5

• Prioritize review and approval of applications submitted by Strategic Seaports and other
nationally significant facilities supporting military deployments, defense logistics, and
strategic sealift operations.
Address Authorized Operations and Operational Flexibility: Ports routinely utilize drones
for infrastructure inspections, environmental monitoring, emergency response, waterside
surveillance, construction management, hazard assessments, and other legitimate operational
purposes. AAPA requests clarification that UAFRs will not unintentionally impede authorized
and beneficial port-related UAS operations.
AAPA supports FAA’s exploration of “whitelisting” Remote ID serial numbers and encourages
the development of scalable systems that allow approved operators to conduct legitimate
operations within UAFRs efficiently and securely. However, AAPA cautions that serial number-
based whitelisting alone may not provide sufficient operational flexibility for large port
complexes. At major ports, authorized drone flights may number in the hundreds or thousands
annually, and the inventory of approved aircraft may change frequently as drones are added,
replaced, retired, or assigned to different operational functions.
To preserve FAA’s ultimate authority over the National Airspace System while ensuring timely,
risk-based decision making, AAPA recommends that FAA develop localized or streamlined
mechanisms for approving authorized UAS operations within UAFRs at complex critical
infrastructure environments such as public port authorities. Such mechanisms could include
FAA-approved port UAS management plans, streamlined authorization procedures for trusted
operators, recurring authorization frameworks, or other scalable processes that recognize port
authorities as distinct from individual fixed-site operators.
Many port authorities already maintain mature drone airspace management practices, operational
experience, security coordination protocols, and technology-enabled situational awareness
capabilities. FAA should consider leveraging these capabilities by allowing port authorities to
coordinate authorized UAS operations across multiple facilities within a port-wide UAFR,
subject to FAA-approved parameters and appropriate reporting requirements. Quarterly reporting
on authorized flight activity, combined with timely notification of unauthorized incursions or
mitigation-related incidents, would provide FAA with continued oversight while avoiding
unnecessary administrative delays for routine, beneficial port operations.
AAPA also encourages FAA to provide flexibility for port authorities and maritime facilities to
satisfy any Remote ID monitoring, notification, or situational awareness requirements through
shared or port-wide capabilities. Many ports operate as multi-tenant environments with
numerous terminals and operators located within a common security framework. Requiring
duplicative systems for each facility could create unnecessary expense while reducing
operational efficiency. FAA should allow port authorities or other coordinating entities to
provide shared capabilities on behalf of multiple facilities where appropriate.
Protect Sensitive Security Information and Security Documentation: Maritime facility
applications for UAFRs may require submission of information already protected under federal
6

security programs, including FSPs, Facility Security Assessments, vulnerability analyses,
security system configurations, and related operational information.
AAPA requests that FAA clearly state in the final rule that Sensitive Security Information and
other protected security materials submitted in support of UAFR applications will receive
appropriate protection from public disclosure and will not be incorporated into public notices or
comment processes in a manner that could compromise facility security.
Continue Advancing Detection and Mitigation Authorities: While AAPA supports the
establishment of UAFRs, the NPRM itself acknowledges an important limitation: the proposal does not
independently authorize any additional drone detection or mitigation authorities.
While UAFRs represent an important step forward, they are most effective when paired with sufficient
detection and mitigation capabilities. Establishing restricted airspace without providing operators and law
enforcement adequate means to identify and respond to unauthorized incursions limits the practical
effectiveness of the program.
UAFRs are an important legal and airspace management tool, but they alone will not deter malicious
actors intentionally disregarding federal law. Ports continue to face real-world drone threats, including
surveillance concerns and operational vulnerabilities.
Accordingly, AAPA urges continued federal efforts to:
• Expand authorized counter-UAS detection and mitigation capabilities for critical
infrastructure;
• Accelerate the promulgation of approved drone mitigation technologies by federal
authorities, including as contemplated under the Safer Skies Act, so that ports and other
critical infrastructure operators have adequate lead time to research, test, procure, and
deploy mitigation systems appropriate for their individualized operating environments;
• Increase federal training capacity and available seats for authorized counter-UAS
operators;
• Strengthen interagency coordination among FAA, DHS, the Department of Justice, the
Transportation Security Administration (TSA), and USCG in countering UAS threats;
• Ensure strategic seaports are appropriately prioritized in future counter-UAS initiatives;
and
• Evaluate funding mechanisms and support programs for local law enforcement agencies
and public port authorities responsible for responding to drone threats affecting critical
infrastructure and strategic sealift operations.
America’s ports are foundational to national defense, supply chain resilience, energy security,
and economic stability. As drone technology evolves, maritime infrastructure operators require
practical, scalable, and coordinated tools to address emerging threats while preserving the
efficiency of the National Airspace System.
AAPA appreciates the FAA’s engagement with stakeholders and its efforts to establish a long-
overdue framework for protecting critical infrastructure from unauthorized UAS activity. We
look forward to continued collaboration with FAA, DHS, TSA, and USCG to ensure maritime
7

transportation facilities can effectively utilize this framework while recognizing the unique
operational realities of the port environment.
Thank you for considering these comments.
Sincerely,
Sang Yi
President & CEO
American Association of Port Authorities
8

CAFOs (Concentrated Animal Feeding Operations) should not be included in the restrictions of operating unmanned aircrafts. While CAFOs are fixed facility sites, it is in the public's interest to be able to see how large in scale CAFOs are as on-the-ground imaging cannot capture their scale accurately. In addition, CAFOs are typically well-hidden, out of the public's view - the public deserves to know just how vast CAFOs are so they can better understand the impact of land use, water use, and animal welfare concerns. Unmanned aircrafts allow for a non-invasive way to document CAFOs and to serve public interests.

I support a restriction on the operations of unmanned (Part 107) or manned aircraft (Part 103) in close proximity to a fixed site facility.   There needs to be a definitive, objective criteria to keep these types of vehicles a safe distance from fixed site facilities, such as power stations, wind turbine farms, transmission lines, bridges, AToN facilitates, ports, etc.   But, also, since Part 103 aircraft have very vague restrictions, and because there has been a monumental proliferation in Part 103 aircraft since Part 103 was instituted, particularly with powered paragliding activist, it would be nonsensical to restrict unmanned aircraft from these sites without also including manned aircraft that utilize Part 103 for their operations from these sites as well, because it is virtually the same problem except with a different category of aircraft.  Unmanned Aircraft (UAS) contemplated here only weight up to 55 lbs, yet unregistered Part 107 aircraft are operated unlicensed aviators in aircraft that can weigh upwards of 254 lbs (empty) -plus- the weight of fuel and persons on board, making these vehicles even more hazardous to the infrastructure and bystanders than any UAS contemplated here.   So, if the FAA adopts restrictions for UAS, it should seriously consider adopting the same rules for Part 103 operations.
Drone usage over agriculture facilities should not be restricted.

comments.commenter: Comment from International Association of Venue Managers
comments.comment_text:
June 30, 2026
Michelle Ferritto, Office of Rulemaking, Federal Aviation Administration
Docket Operations;
U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT)
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE, Room W58-213, West Building 5th Floor,
Washington, DC 20590-0001
Re: Notice of Proposed Rulemaking, Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned
Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility, Docket FAA-2026-4558
Dear Ms. Ferritto,
Thank you for this opportunity to provide comments regarding the FAA’s proposed rule to
establish criteria for unmanned aircraft flight restrictions (UAFR) to “critical infrastructure
facilities.”
Background:
The International Association of Venue Managers (IAVM) represents public assembly
venues, including auditoriums, arenas, convention centers, exhibit halls, stadiums,
performing arts centers, university complexes, and amphitheaters. Member venues attract
millions of patrons to an astonishing variety of events including sporting events, concerts,
conventions, conferences, and performance art.
The Threat:
While IAVM venue managers meticulously plan every detail of the events they host to
ensure the safety of our guests, a new threat has emerged that we cannot control - the use

of unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs or drones) in the airspace above outdoor events.
Venues rely on the assistance of law enforcement to police airspace above events, and
current law only allows law enforcement to take actions to detect, take control of, destroy,
or otherwise interfere with an unmanned aircraft in certain limited circumstances.
Commercial Facilities:
We appreciate the focus of the proposed rule on critical infrastructure. The commercial
facilities sector includes a wide range of sites that draw large crowds of people for
shopping, business, entertainment, or lodging. Venues where IAVM members work are part
of the “public assembly” subsector, for example, arenas and convention centers.
The proposed rule includes several criteria that venues must meet in order to obtain UAV
flight restrictions for their facilities. While we appreciate the risk to theme and
entertainment parks from UAVs, the proposed rule’s focus on that sector disregards the
many other types of commercial facilities and creates critical security gaps across the
sector. We urge the FAA to revise the rule’s threshold criteria as outlined below in order to
protect the entire sector.
Temporary v. Permanent:
The rule proposes that facilities must not be “a stadium or venue where the events may be
covered by temporary flight restrictions (TFRs).” First, we urge the agency to expand the
TFRs. Current temporary restrictions protect stadiums that host NFL, NCAA Division I
football, MLB, and major motor speedway events and seat over 30,000 people, and the FAA
now has authority to grant TFRs to other venues, but the 30,000-seat threshold remains.
The vast majority of IAVM members work at event venues that seat under 30,000 and don’t
qualify for TFRs. This highlights serious gaps in protections for event attendees that should
be addressed.
For the proposed rule, we understand this criteria is an attempt to focus the rule on
permanent, ongoing facilities and events like large theme parks. We also appreciate the
FAA’s position that TFRs are often more appropriate for events that have a defined start and
stop time, like concerts. However, we ask the FAA to consider the threat venues face and
allow for more flexibility and options for venues.
Venue operators face a dizzying and often conflicting array of federal, state, and local laws
and regulations regarding UAVs above their facilities. Different criteria across agencies,
unclear authorities, conflicting rules, and competing jurisdictions combine to create a
regulatory minefield. Under the current system, venue operators are forced to spend
limited time and resources applying, and re-applying, for various temporary restrictions.
Venues should have the option to apply for a permanent UAFR in order to provide

continuous protection to their visitors and staff and not spend time and money constantly
applying and reapplying for temporary restrictions. This would provide certainty, as
opposed to the ad hoc system of TFRs in place now, and it would increase visitor and staff
safety. We urge the FAA to remove this criteria from the final rule.
Annual Attendance:
The proposed rule sets a minimum annual attendance of 2.5 million in the previous year.
This is an extremely high threshold that only the most visited amusement parks in the
country will meet. The threshold immediately excludes a large portion of the sector from
even attempting to obtain a UAFR to protect their visitors and employees from airborne
threats.
For example, the famed outdoor concert venue Red Rocks Amphitheatre located near
Denver, CO had an annual attendance in 2025 of 1.75 million, making it the second most
attended venue in the United States, behind Madison Square Garden. While current local
and FAA rules generally require FAA approval for UAV flights above the amphitheater, the
venue should not be prohibited from applying for a UAFR under this proposed rule due to
an excessively high annual attendance threshold.
Sports fans attend outdoor events almost year-round in America. To cite just one example,
in 2025 Sporting KC, a Major League Soccer team located in Kansas City, KS drew an
average of just under 17,000 fans over 17 home games, for a total annual attendance of
around 282,000. Fans attending Sporting KC soccer games, concerts at Red Rocks, and
events at other outdoor venues across the country that host hundreds of thousands of
visitors a year deserve to be protected. We urge the FAA to lower the minimum annual
attendance threshold to 200,000.
Open to the Public:
The proposed rule sets a threshold of at least 120 days a year open to the public. We
understand this is an attempt to limit permanent UAFRs to facilities that are open a
significant portion of the year, compared to those open for shorter periods where flexible,
temporary flight restrictions might be more appropriate to balance protecting the public
with pilots’ rights to use open airspace.
However, the rule’s focus on large theme parks that are open nearly year-round in
comparison with events like state fairs that run for a few weeks misses a large number of
venues and events in between, such as amphitheaters that operate only during the
summer. We suggest lowering the threshold to 60 days in order to cover events that run
for at least two months.

I am writing to express my concern about any limitations of drone footage in the airspace in proximity to agricultural operations. It is in the public's interest to have as much transparency as possible of the highly secretive farming practices taking place in the U.S.

Thank you for your consideration.

Documentation of a company facility's regulations and processes should be transparent and made available to the public, especially when it concerns the population's food production, as it directly correlates to public health, safety, and lawful practices. Thusly, no restrictions should be made on unmanned aircrafts seeking to bring truth and shed light on the illegal and unnecessary cruel activity often exposed in these sites. Companies acting in opposition to their claims should be held accountable.

As a concerned citizen, I believe it is necessary to be cautious about any limitations of drone footage in the airspace near/above agricultural operations.

It's in the public's interest to have as much transparency as possible of the highly secretive farming practices taking place in the U.S. the public deserves to know where their food comes from and have it be accurate information. To restrict footage of farming operations is censorship. To control what is even allowed to be seen by the public is censorship.

Thank you for your consideration.

Public Comment on Docket No. FAA-2026-4558

Designation—Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
Notice No. 26-03 / 26-03A | RIN 2120-AL33

Submitted via regulations.gov, Docket FAA-2026-4558
Comment period

I am submitting this comment to urge the FAA to decline to establish food and agriculture sector eligibility criteria for Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs) under proposed 14 CFR Part 74 — or, at minimum, to draft any such criteria narrowly enough that they cannot be used to shield animal agriculture operations from public observation and documentation.

1. The statutory purpose does not support broad agricultural eligibility

Section 2209 and proposed § 74.56 require applicants to demonstrate that a restriction is necessary for aviation safety, protection of people or property on the ground, national security, or homeland security. The other 14 sectors named in NSM-22 — nuclear facilities, dams, chemical plants, defense sites — present concrete, well-documented physical or security vulnerabilities where an unmanned aircraft incident could cause catastrophic harm. A typical concentrated animal feeding operation (CAFO) or slaughter facility does not present a comparable profile. The FAA's own UAFR framework conditions eligibility on a "documented safety or security need," including evidence of existing drone activity near the facility and the consequences of an exploited vulnerability — not on a facility's preference for privacy or favorable public perception.

2. The rule should not become a vehicle for shielding facilities from transparency

Routine drone photography of animal agriculture sites overwhelmingly serves a different purpose than the threats this rule is designed to address: it documents animal welfare conditions, environmental discharges (such as manure lagoon runoff into waterways), and food safety practices that are otherwise extremely difficult for journalists, researchers, and the public to observe, since these facilities are typically located on private land with no public access points. If the FAA permits agricultural facilities to obtain UAFRs without requiring a genuine, documented security threat distinct from "unwanted observation," the rule will function as a no-fly zone for accountability journalism and animal welfare investigation rather than as a tool for aviation safety.

3. Recommended criteria, if the FAA proceeds

If the FAA does establish food and agriculture eligibility criteria, I urge it to require, at minimum:

A documented incident-based threshold. Applicants should be required to show specific instances of drones interfering with aviation safety (e.g., conflicts with agricultural aircraft under Part 137) or creating a demonstrable safety/security hazard — not simply that drone photography has occurred near the facility.
An explicit exclusion for newsgathering and investigative documentation. The rule should make clear that a UAFR may not be granted or enforced in a manner that restricts drone operations conducted for journalistic, research, or public-interest documentation purposes, consistent with the trusted-operator carve-outs already contemplated for Parts 91, 107, 108, 135, and 137.
A high evidentiary bar matched to the other 15 sectors. Eligibility criteria for food and agriculture facilities should require a showing of risk comparable in severity to that required of chemical plants or energy facilities, not a lower threshold justified by operational convenience or reputational concerns.

4. Conclusion

The FAA's mandate under Section 2209 is aviation safety and security — not the management of public perception of an industry. I respectfully ask the FAA to either omit food and agriculture eligibility criteria from the final rule, or adopt criteria narrow enough to prevent UAFRs from being used to suppress legitimate transparency into animal welfare, environmental compliance, and food production practices that are otherwise inaccessible to the public.

Thank you for the opportunity to comment.
You should allow drone usage over agricultural properties.
I OPPOSE any effort to restrict drone flights over animal agriculture facilities, as this is often the only way we know when abuse of animals, the environment, or workers takes place.

RE: Docket No. FAA-2026-4558 — Designation: Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
I’m a licensed Part 107 drone pilot writing to urge the FAA to exclude factory farms and other industrial animal agriculture facilities from the “Food and Agriculture” critical infrastructure sector eligible for Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs) — or, at minimum, to set a strict, evidence-based bar for any facility seeking one.
Section 2209 exists to protect sites where a drone poses a real safety or security threat: power plants, refineries, prisons, dams. A barn complex or manure lagoon doesn’t fit that category, and Congress removed the open-ended “other locations” catch-all in 2024 specifically to keep this rule narrow. The NPRM lists Food and Agriculture as an eligible sector but leaves its criteria blank — this comment urges the FAA to either drop the sector or write criteria that demand a documented threat, not just a desire to avoid scrutiny.
Licensed drone pilots are one of the only independent checks left on this industry. Ag-gag laws in many states already criminalize undercover ground investigation, even on public land. Legal drone flight in public airspace has become one of the few remaining lawful ways to document conditions invisible from the road: lagoon overflows, overcrowding, ventilation failures, and waste mismanagement.
This matters directly to consumers. Labels like “humane” and “free-range” are loosely regulated and rarely verified independently. Aerial documentation is often the only real check between marketing claims and reality — restricting it widens the information gap between producers and the people buying their products.
It matters for public health too. Outside documentation has repeatedly driven faster scrutiny of biosecurity failures during avian flu and other livestock disease events than industry self-reporting alone. If “biosecurity” becomes the default justification for airspace restriction, it should require actual evidence of a drone-related transmission or disruption risk — not just an assertion.
It matters for the environment. CAFOs are a major source of water pollution, and enforcement leans heavily on self-reporting and under-resourced inspections. Aerial observation has repeatedly caught lagoon breaches and unpermitted waste application invisible from ground level. Restricting drone access removes one of the few independent environmental checks this industry has.
And it matters for the animals raised in these facilities, whose treatment is visible to the public — when it’s visible at all — largely because of legal aerial documentation.
I ask the FAA to:
1.Exclude CAFOs and similar facilities from Food and Agriculture sector UAFR eligibility, absent documented drone-specific safety risk.
2.If the sector is retained, require rigorous criteria: documented vulnerability, exhausted self-help measures, and no reliance on unsupported biosecurity claims.
3.Protect Part 107 commercial transit rights within any granted UAFR, so notification requirements can’t be used to anticipate and block documentation flights.
4.Require enhanced public notice for agricultural UAFR applications, reaching regulators, journalists, and the public.
5.State explicitly that UAFR designation does not shield facilities from lawful environmental, public health, or animal welfare documentation.
The FAA’s job under Section 2209 is narrow: protect genuinely high-risk sites from real drone threats. Industrial animal agriculture doesn’t naturally belong in that category, and this rule shouldn’t become a tool for shielding it from the scrutiny a functioning food system depends on.
Respectfully submitted,
Jam Allison
Portland, OR​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

It erodes public trust to further reduce our food source transparency. We hope the FAA will carefully consider the public value of aerial photojournalism as it evaluates this proposal.

While the FAA proposal does not automatically restrict drone access to all agricultural facilities, it could create a pathway for some facilities to seek restrictions on aerial documentation. There is not doubt that responsible drone photography serves the public interest. Professional drone operators in the U.S. already operate within a framework of federal, state, and local regulations designed to promote safety. We Animals supports responsible drone operation and works with qualified drone operators who comply with applicable FAA requirements. Our concern is not that drone operators must meet safety and licensing standards.
