AirView Consulting appreciates the opportunity to comment on the Federal Aviation Administration's Notice of Proposed Rulemaking, "Designation: Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility" (Docket FAA-2026-4558).

AirView supports the FAA's objective of establishing a structured, transparent process for protecting facilities that present legitimate national security, public safety, and critical infrastructure concerns. A standardized framework provides greater consistency for facility operators while improving regulatory certainty for legitimate unmanned aircraft operators.

Based on our experience supporting public safety organizations, emergency management, Medical Reserve Corps operations, FAA regulatory compliance, unmanned aircraft integration, and multi-agency coordination, we respectfully offer the following recommendations:

1. Preserve Emergency Response Capability

The final rule should clearly recognize that authorized public safety operations—including law enforcement, fire service, emergency medical services, emergency management agencies, Medical Reserve Corps organizations, and other authorized disaster-response missions—may require timely access within or near designated restricted areas during emergencies. The final rule should provide a practical and predictable mechanism for rapid operational coordination when emergency response requires access.

2. Provide a Clear Process for Authorized Operators

The rule should establish transparent procedures allowing properly authorized operators to request and receive approval for legitimate operations near designated facilities. Predictable approval criteria will improve compliance while supporting lawful governmental, commercial, and public safety operations.

3. Maintain Narrow, Risk-Based Restrictions

Restrictions should remain limited to those reasonably necessary to address demonstrated security or safety risks. Narrowly tailored restrictions will better protect critical facilities while minimizing unnecessary impacts on legitimate unmanned aircraft operations.

4. Encourage Coordinated Situational Awareness

As unmanned aircraft become increasingly integrated into emergency response operations, coordinated situational awareness becomes increasingly important. Technologies and operational procedures that improve coordination among authorized responders—including shared operating picture capabilities such as Team Awareness Kit (TAK)—can improve safety, reduce confusion, and enhance operational effectiveness while supporting compliance with FAA requirements.

AirView Consulting supports the FAA's effort to establish a consistent national framework for protecting qualifying fixed-site facilities while continuing the safe integration of unmanned aircraft into the National Airspace System.

Thank you for the opportunity to provide these comments.
I oppose this regulation.

I believe FAA should not make it more difficult to use drones to document industrial animal agriculture facilities. I believe big Ag needs to be more transparent with the customers about the products we consume from their facilities. How they are treating animals is being deliberately hidden from the public, and allowing for drone footage would increase the welfare for the animals.

Seems strange to restrict drones in what will be largely desolate areas with minimal humans and property and damage can be minimal. Wouldn’t it make sense to completely shut down drones in cities and much wider circumferences around airports where they can actually cause damage first, especially given that literally yesterday a drone struck a plane in Newark.

Factory farms should not be considered a fixed site facility as the air space around factory farms should not be restricted. We, as consumers, have a right to know if a company is lying to us regarding how their animals are treated/held (e.g. if a company claims their eggs come from free-roaming chickens).

Drone photography has become one of the most important tools for making the often-hidden world of industrial animal agriculture visible. We hope the FAA will carefully consider the public value of aerial photojournalism as it evaluates this proposal.

While the FAA proposal does not automatically restrict drone access to all agricultural facilities, it could create a pathway for some facilities to seek restrictions on aerial documentation. There is not doubt that responsible drone photography serves the public interest. Professional drone operators in the U.S. already operate within a framework of federal, state, and local regulations designed to promote safety. We Animals supports responsible drone operation and works with qualified drone operators who comply with applicable FAA requirements. Our concern is not that drone operators must meet safety and licensing standards. Rather, it is that site-specific flight restrictions could prevent journalists and photographers from documenting matters of public interest.

comments.commenter: Comment from Bank Policy Institute & American Bankers Association
comments.organization: Bank Policy Institute & American Bankers Association
comments.comment_text:
June 30, 2026
Via Electronic Submission
Federal Aviation Administration
U.S. Department of Transportation
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Washington, DC 20590
Re: Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03; Designation--Restrict the Operation of Unmanned
Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
To Whom It May Concern:
The Bank Policy Institute1 and American Bankers Association2 appreciate the opportunity to
comment on the Federal Aviation Administration's proposed rule to restrict drone operations over
certain critical infrastructure sites. As the proposal notes, unmanned aircraft systems pose unique and
evolving threats to the critical physical infrastructure operated by financial institutions.3
The financial industry supports the FAA's proposed implementation of section 2209 because it
establishes a lawful, measured, and necessary process for restricting unmanned aircraft operations near
qualifying critical infrastructure sites. Congress directed the FAA to create a process under which
operators or proprietors of certain fixed-site facilities may request an unmanned aircraft flight
restriction.4 The proposal would do so through a new part 74 framework requiring applicants to show a
site-specific need grounded in aviation safety, protection of people and property on the ground,
national security, or homeland security.
Our members operate thousands of facilities that support the continuity, resilience, and security
of the financial services sector. Unauthorized unmanned aircraft activity near those sites can create
obvious and legitimate risks including: surveillance, hostile reconnaissance, operational disruption,
interference with emergency response, and threats to executives, employees, customers, and critical
business functions. This is also an area where cyber and physical security converge. Unauthorized
1 The Bank Policy Institute is a nonpartisan public policy, research, and advocacy group that represents universal
banks, regional banks, and the major foreign banks doing business in the United States. The Institute produces
academic research and analysis on regulatory and monetary policy topics, analyzes and comments on proposed
regulations, and represents the financial services industry with respect to cybersecurity, fraud, and other
information security issues. Business, Innovation, Technology and Security (“BITS”), BPI’s technology policy
division, provides an executive-level forum to discuss and promote current and emerging technology, foster
innovation, reduce fraud, and improve cybersecurity and risk management practices for the financial sector.
2 The American Bankers Association is the voice of the nation’s $26.1 trillion banking industry, which is composed
of small, regional and large banks that together employ over 2 million people, safeguard $20.5 trillion in deposits
and extend $13.7 trillion in loans.
3 Designation—Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility, 91 Fed. Reg.
24650, 24669 (proposed May 6, 2026).
4 Id. at 24650.
1

drone activity can enhance threat actor reconnaissance efforts for planning cyber intrusions, fraud,
extortion, and ransomware attacks.
The proposed rule addresses those risks in a sensible way. It does not impose a blanket
prohibition on drone operations. Instead, it creates a petition-based framework with facility-specific
justification, defined boundaries, FAA review, and limited exceptions for certain operations within a
designated unmanned aircraft flight restriction.
That balance matters. A designated restriction gives compliant operators clear notice of where
they cannot fly. It also helps corporate security teams and law enforcement distinguish authorized
activities from those that are suspicious or unlawful—strengthening preventive security before an
incident occurs. Similarly, we appreciate that the proposal provides other critical infrastructure sectors
we depend upon (e.g. energy, water, and telecommunications) with the same pathway to petition the
FAA for an unmanned aircraft flight restriction when circumstances warrant.
Financial sector criteria
The proposed criteria for the financial sector5 appropriately identify the facilities most
susceptible to unmanned aircraft risk. The NPRM would limit eligibility to fixed-site facilities that meet
the statutory definition of critical infrastructure and fall within identified sectors, a structure that helps
focus relief on facilities whose disruption could have a debilitating effect on security or national
economic security.
That approach fits the financial sector. Not every office or branch warrants a flight restriction.
But certain financial services facilities perform functions whose disruption could impair payment flows,
liquidity management, cash operations, data security, crisis response, or other core business functions.
A rule that ties eligibility to fixed-site critical infrastructure, rather than to an entire industry in the
abstract, gives the FAA an administrable standard and gives applicants a clear burden to meet.
Under the proposed rule, applicants would be required to submit information on existing
unmanned aircraft activity over a facility, the nature of the facility’s assets and associated vulnerabilities,
and risks due to unmanned aircraft. While this is not an unreasonable request, the sensitivity of this
information would require the FAA to implement robust data security measures to prevent improper
disclosure directly damaging to applicants.
Moreover, applicants should not have to wait until a critical unmanned aircraft incident has
already occurred at specific site to be granted an UAFR. It is also not clear whether the FAA would apply
some threshold to what it considered appropriate or inappropriate levels of activity given that not all
potential drone threats are equal. Instead, the FAA should permit applicants to use both site-specific
activity as well as evidence of actual or potential risks at similarly situated critical infrastructure facilities
to justify the placement of an UAFR at a financial institution-related site.
5 Id. at 24669.
2

Requested clarification
The FAA should clarify that a designation under proposed part 74 would not prohibit a financial
institution from using drones for its own lawful physical security purposes at an eligible financial services
facility, provided the institution complies with otherwise applicable FAA requirements. The preamble
already states that a UAFR would not itself authorize a geo-fence or other electromagnetic barrier6 and
that certain operations under parts 91, 107, 108, 135, and 137 may operate within a UAFR subject to
specified conditions, including Remote ID and notice requirements.7
A short clarification in proposed section 74.250 would avoid confusion. Financial institutions
may use drones for perimeter inspection, incident assessment, emergency response support, or other
security-related functions at covered facilities. The final rule should make clear that the UAFR
framework aims to deter unauthorized operations, not to disable lawful security operations conducted
by or for the protected facility itself. That clarification would reinforce the rule's security purpose and
reduce the risk that institutions decline useful and lawful protective measures because of regulatory
uncertainty.
Conclusion
We support the proposed rule and encourage the FAA to finalize it. The proposal implements
section 2209 through a clear and disciplined process that respects both security needs on the ground
and the FAA's responsibility to preserve safe and efficient use of the airspace. With the clarification
above, the rule will better protect financial services facilities and the broader critical infrastructure
ecosystem on which the banking system depends.
Sincerely,
/s/ Patrick Warren
Patrick Warren
Senior Vice President, Assistant General Counsel
Bank Policy Institute
/s/ Heather Wyson-Constantine
Heather Wyson-Constantine
Vice President, Cyber & Physical Security Policy
American Bankers Association
6 Id. at 24651.
7 Id. at 24652.
3

comments.commenter: Comment from American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials
comments.organization: American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials
comments.comment_text:
June 30, 2026
The Honorable Bryan Bedford
Administrator
Federal Aviation Administration
800 Independence Avenue, SW
Washington, DC 20591
Re: FAA’s Notice of Proposed Rulemaking on Designation: Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close
Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility (Docket No. FAA-2026-4558)
Dear Administrator Bedford:
The American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials (AASHTO) appreciates the opportunity to
provide input to the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) regarding the Designation: Restrict the Operation of
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) published in
the Federal Register on May 6, 2026. AASHTO is a nonprofit, nonpartisan association representing the state
transportation departments (state DOTs) in the 50 states, the District of Columbia, and Puerto Rico, with the mission to
support state DOTs in connecting America with the transportation system of today and tomorrow. AASHTO and the
state DOTs have a long history of successful partnership and collaboration with USDOT and its modal administrations
and given the state DOTs’ role in operating and maintaining transportation infrastructure, AASHTO is pleased to
provide the following input for consideration.
AASHTO supports implementation of Section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016 (PL 114-
190) through a process for designating unmanned aircraft flight restrictions near eligible fixed site facilities. This
approach appropriately balances the need to protect critical infrastructure with the continued safe and authorized use of
the national airspace. AASHTO also supports FAA’s approach of avoiding broad no-fly zones, instead providing
owners and operators of critical infrastructure with a defined pathway to request site-specific protections.
Managing access for vetted and known operators is especially important for state DOTs who rely on unmanned aircraft
to conduct maintenance, inspection, and hazard monitoring activities along transportation corridors, bridges, tunnels,
and other infrastructure that may fall within new or future restricted areas designated under this NPRM. AASHTO
further supports the FAA’s proposed burden of proof, which is necessary to prevent the over-designation of facilities
as critical infrastructure and to avoid unnecessary restrictions on unmanned aircraft activity. This approach
appropriately focuses on facilities whose disruption would have consequences beyond local impacts.
AASHTO appreciates FAA’s efforts to provide a framework that protects critical infrastructure while continuing to
support safe unmanned aircraft integration. We encourage FAA to engage with states DOTs as finalization and
implementation of this rule moves forward. If you have any questions, please contact Shayne Gill, AASHTO’s
Director of Multimodal Transportation at sgill@aashto.org or 202-624-3630.
Sincerely,
Russell R. McMurry, P.E.
President, American Association of State Highway and Transportation Officials
Commissioner, Georgia Department of Transportation
This is wrong and can not pass...no one is making sure these companies/farms are following the law but independent citizens and activists...!!!

As the InfraGard Dams Sector Chief for Region 8, I submit this comment to emphasize the
critical importance of protecting Dams Sector assets from unauthorized unmanned aircraft
systems (UAS) operations. The Dams Sector is one of the 16 critical infrastructure sectors and
includes dams, navigation locks, and levees. These assets provide essential services supporting
water supply, flood control, navigation, agriculture, energy generation, and protection of other
critical infrastructure.
1. Overview Section – Inclusion of Levees
The overview section appropriately highlights the importance of dams and locks but does not
adequately address the vital role of levees in our nation’s infrastructure. I recommend adding the
following language (or similar) to provide a more complete description of the Dams Sector:
Levees are essential man-made embankments designed to protect approximately 23 million
people and more than $2 trillion in infrastructure by containing rising river levels and blocking
coastal storm surges (National Levee Database, U.S. Army Corps of Engineers). By confining
water to specific channels, levees prevent the inundation of floodplains, enabling the
development of major urban centers and the cultivation of high-yield agricultural regions.
Beyond local protection, these structures safeguard vital national transportation and energy
networks in low-lying areas. For example, the Lafourche Basin Levee System in Louisiana helps
protect a nuclear power plant from inundation that could result in a catastrophic failure of the
reactor cooling system.
2. Eligibility Criteria – Dams
The proposed eligibility criteria for dams are unnecessarily stringent and may leave many critical
assets vulnerable to UAS incursions. High or significant hazard potential dams, as classified in
the National Inventory of Dams, along with all dams that have hydroelectric generation or
pumped hydro storage capability, should be eligible to apply for an Unmanned Aircraft Flight
Restriction (UAFR).
The significant costs associated with repairing damage to dams or their appurtenances —
whether from intentional or accidental UAS activity — strongly support broader eligibility. Even
lower-hazard dams can have substantial regional or national consequences if compromised.
3. Eligibility Criteria – Locks and Levees
The eligibility criteria for navigation locks are also too narrow. Any lock serving a navigation
function should be eligible for a UAFR, given their importance to commercial transportation and
supply chains.
Levees should be explicitly included in the acceptance criteria. Many levees directly protect
populations, agricultural lands, and assets belonging to multiple other critical infrastructure
sectors (e.g., energy, transportation systems, water and wastewater systems). Levees that provide
such protection should qualify for UAFR designation.
Dams, locks, and levees are integral to community water systems and collectively support
national resilience across sectors. Broadening eligibility for these assets would better align the
rule with the full scope and risk profile of the Dams Sector.
Thank you,
Kris Schartau
InfraGard Dams Sector Chief for Region 8
There should be restrictions, if not already in place, for any drones around critical infrastructure as they can be used for site surveillance prior to a security breach.

I’d like to express concern about any limitations of drone footage in the air space above or near agricultural, animal, and farming operations. It is the public’s right to know as much as possible regarding where our food is coming from and the practices involved.

I am a concerned citizen who likes to know what kind of food I'm buying. Transparency is incredibly important when it comes to the animal agriculture industry, which this rule will directly compromise. Drone photography around factory farms is crucial to ensure businesses are following laws and consumers are buying what they think they are buying. Do not allow facilities to apply for drone restriction over their facilities

My family and I are voting citizens of the United Stated based in Colorado and asking you not to restrict the use of drones in and around farms. It is imperative that citizens are able to view and understand what is happening on factory farms for food safety issues. It’s far too easy for large entities to hide dirty and illegal conditions. Do not restrict the use of drone photography around farms.

The use of drone technology in and around farms should NOT be restricted. This is the only way consumers get to see the reality of where their food comes from. This would be getting rid of any transparency whatsoever.

comments.commenter: Comment from The Longbow Group LLC
comments.comment_text:
July 1st, 2026
Submitted electronically via Federal eRulemaking Portal www.regulations.gov
TO: Federal Aviation Administration, Docket Operations M-30
RE: Comments on Notice of Proposed Rulemaking, Docket No. FAA-2026-4558,
Notice No. 26-03, RIN 2120-AL33
SUBJECT: Designation — Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close
Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility (Proposed 14 CFR Part 74)
I. INTRODUCTION AND COMMENTER IDENTITY
The LONGBOW Group (“LONGBOW”) respectfully submits these comments in
response to the Federal Aviation Administration’s Notice of Proposed Rulemaking
proposing 14 CFR Part 74, establishing a framework for Unmanned Aircraft Flight
Restrictions (UAFRs) around fixed-site critical infrastructure (Docket No. FAA-2026-
4558).
LONGBOW is a technology company developing PEGASUS GUARDIAN, a multi-
domain airspace operations platform designed to provide persistent situational
awareness, identity and access management, compliance monitoring, and evidence
documentation for unmanned aircraft operations in low-altitude airspace. PEGASUS
GUARDIAN is built on an integrated sensing architecture (Remote ID, radar, passive
RF, EO/IR, acoustic), a multi-domain common operating picture, and operator identity
and authorization workflows. PEGASUS GUARDIAN is designed to serve, among
others, critical infrastructure owners, law enforcement, federal agencies, and vetted UAS
operators.
LONGBOW has been an FAA-designated LAANC UAS Service Supplier (USS) since
2023, allowing us to provide structured low-altitude airspace authorization data to
operators through the FAA’s Low Altitude Authorization and Notification Capability. We
use this capability internally as part of our UAS research operations at our Unmanned
Systems Research and Technology Center (USRTC) at Fort Monroe and Downtown
Hampton, centrally located within the complex airspace of Hampton Roads. This
operational experience with the LAANC data model, facility grid architecture, and USS-
to-operator authorization workflow is directly applicable to UAFR implementation and
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558 | Page 1 of 9

informs several of the technical positions below. LONGBOW has active NASA Small
Business Innovation Research (SBIR) programs in low-altitude airspace management,
including programs in UAS subsystem risk assessment and trusted information
distribution for airspace operations. LONGBOW is also engaged with facility operators
in the Hampton Roads, Virginia region — including at least one facility that meets the
NPRM’s eligible critical infrastructure criteria — in planning real-world UAFR
operational deployments.
LONGBOW strongly supports the proposed Part 74 framework. Establishing defined,
chartable, verifiable, and legally enforceable airspace protection around critical
infrastructure is a necessary step in maturing the National Airspace System for scalable
UAS integration. The NPRM creates the correct structural foundation. These comments
identify specific areas where the final rule’s implementation framework — particularly
its data architecture, operator access workflow, and notification requirements — would
benefit from additional FAA guidance and minimum standards.
LONGBOW submits seven technical positions below, organized by the sections of the
NPRM to which they most directly apply.
II. POSITIONS
Position 1: Require Machine-Readable UAFR Data Publication
Regulatory context: The NPRM proposes that designated UAFRs be published in
FAA Order JO 7400.12 (incorporated by reference under §74.30) and noticed through
the Federal Register. The rule does not specify a structured data format, schema, or
machine-readable publication mechanism for UAFR boundaries.
LONGBOW’s position: The FAA should require that UAFR boundaries, vertical
limits, site identifiers, effective dates, expiration dates, designation status, and UAFR
type (standard vs. Special) be published in a machine-readable, structured format
accessible to UAS Service Suppliers (USS), UTM providers, and operational systems
such as PEGASUS GUARDIAN in near real time.
Rationale: Manual charting and NOTAM-based publication alone are insufficient for
real-time airspace enforcement in high-tempo BVLOS operations. A UAFR that is not
machine-readable in a structured format cannot be programmatically ingested into the
monitoring, deconfliction, and corridor authorization systems that the FAA
contemplates under the Part 108 framework. As the proposed rule itself acknowledges, a
UAFR has defined horizontal and vertical limits and may carry multiple operational
terms. These parameters must be available as structured data — not only graphical
depictions — for the operational infrastructure serving UAFR sites to function correctly.
EUROCAE ED-269 CHG 1:2025 (Minimum Operational Performance Standard for
UAS Geographical Zones) defines how geographic restriction zones must be structured
and published in machine-readable form, including required fields for zone type,
altitude limits, time windows, responsible authority, and applicable operator categories.
The companion standard EUROCAE ED-318:2024 (Technical Specification for
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558 | Page 2 of 9

Geographical Zones and U-Space Data Provision and Exchange) defines the machine-
readable data format and exchange protocol for publishing those zones to UTM service
providers — the more directly applicable specification for the data publication
architecture Position 1 recommends. Together, ED-269 CHG 1:2025 and ED-318:2024
have been adopted across European UTM implementations and demonstrate that a
standardized, machine-readable geographic restriction zone schema is both technically
feasible and operationally proven at scale. LONGBOW recommends the FAA use this
standards pair as the template for UAFR data publication rather than developing a novel
schema from scratch.
The FAA Data Exchange (LAANC infrastructure) already delivers structured airspace
authorization data to USS providers under interfaces conforming to ASTM F3548-21
(UAS Traffic Management USS Interoperability). LONGBOW recommends the FAA
extend the F3548-21 data model explicitly to include UAFR boundaries, leveraging the
existing USS ecosystem to propagate UAFR status to operators pre-flight. A UAFR data
layer structured as an extension of F3548-21 — rather than a parallel and potentially
conflicting specification — would enable immediate integration by the USS providers
already certified under LAANC.
As an FAA-designated LAANC USS since 2023, LONGBOW has direct operational
experience with the LAANC facility map and grid architecture. UAFR boundaries should
be published as a structured overlay that aligns with — and does not contradict — the
existing LAANC facility grid. Where a UAFR is established over or adjacent to a LAANC-
covered facility, the UAFR boundary geometry, vertical limits, and authorization status
must be expressed in the same coordinate and grid reference system as the surrounding
LAANC authorization surfaces. Misaligned or overlapping representations between
LAANC-authorized airspace and UAFR-restricted airspace will create operator
confusion, USS deconfliction failures, and legal ambiguity about which authorization
state governs a specific location and altitude. The LAANC model provides the correct
template: facility-anchored, grid-aligned, machine-readable, and propagated to
operators through USS providers in near real time. UAFR publication should be an
extension of that model, not a separate layer.
Minimum recommended specification: UAFR GeoJSON (RFC 7946) or ED-269-
aligned geometry with: site_id, uafr_type (standard/special), facility sector, horizontal
boundary, AGL floor and ceiling, effective_date, status (active/suspended/expired),
last_modified timestamp, LAANC facility_id cross-reference where applicable, and a
public-access API endpoint conforming to OpenAPI Specification 3.x.
Position 2: Establish a Standardized Digital Notification Interface for Facility
Operators
Regulatory context: The NPRM requires that operators seeking access to a standard
UAFR provide advance notification to the facility operator/proprietor. The proposed
rule does not define a standard data format, notification channel, response workflow, or
minimum acknowledgment timeline for this notification.
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558 | Page 3 of 9

LONGBOW’s position: This is the most operationally critical gap in the proposed
rule. The FAA should establish minimum standards — or mandate a standardized API —
for the facility notification workflow. Without a standard interface, each UAFR site will
develop an ad-hoc process, creating a fragmented, unscalable notification ecosystem
that fails operators conducting cross-facility or corridor operations.
Rationale: Under the proposed Part 108 framework, a single drone delivery route may
transit multiple UAFR sites. A national UAS logistics operator cannot manually contact
each facility by phone or email before each flight. The operational reality of scalable
BVLOS operations requires a notification mechanism that is:
1. Digital and structured — notification data (operator identity, aircraft
registration, Remote ID serials, certificate type, mission time window, approved
area) submitted and received in machine-readable form
2. Near real-time — time-sensitive operations cannot tolerate multi-hour or
same-business-day response windows
3. Auditable — the notification and any facility acknowledgment must be
timestamped and cryptographically durable for evidence and compliance
purposes
4. Revocable — the facility must be able to withdraw acknowledgment for a
specific mission window and have that state propagated to monitoring
infrastructure
LONGBOW recommends the FAA require UAFR sites above a threshold complexity
(e.g., facilities where BVLOS corridor operations are anticipated) to accept notifications
via a FAA-defined structured API, with a reference implementation made available
through the existing USS/UTM ecosystem. Smaller sites or lower-complexity UAFRs
could satisfy notification requirements through a web-based portal that generates a
machine-readable confirmation artifact.
Without this standard, the two most important UAFR compliance
functions — knowing who is supposed to be there and notifying the
facility when someone arrives — cannot be reliably automated,
correlated, or enforced.
Position 3: Define Minimum Performance Standards for Fixed-Site Remote ID
Monitoring Infrastructure
Regulatory context: The NPRM makes Remote ID operationally central to UAFR
compliance. Fixed-site applicants are expected to have the capability to receive Remote
ID broadcasts, either directly or through contracted services. The proposed rule does not
specify minimum coverage, receiver density, timestamping, retention, or health-
monitoring requirements for Remote ID sensing infrastructure at UAFR sites.
LONGBOW’s position: The FAA should establish minimum performance
characteristics for fixed-site Remote ID monitoring deployed to support UAFR
operations, including:
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558 | Page 4 of 9

• Coverage validation — a documented methodology for demonstrating that the
deployed receiver network achieves adequate ground-level and low-altitude
coverage within the designated UAFR boundary
• Timestamping precision — Remote ID messages used in compliance and
evidence workflows must carry traceable UTC timestamps to a defined precision
(LONGBOW recommends 100ms or better); fixed-site receivers should
synchronize to UTC using IEEE 1588-2019 Precision Time Protocol (PTP) (as
amended by IEEE 1588a/d/c/e through 2024) or equivalent, consistent with
evidence admissibility standards
• Retention duration — logged Remote ID messages should be retained for a
minimum period sufficient to support FAA annual reporting requirements and
law enforcement investigations (LONGBOW recommends 365 days minimum)
• Receiver health monitoring — UAFR sites should be able to demonstrate that
their Remote ID receiver network was operational and recording during any
period for which they assert compliance monitoring
ASTM F3411-22a (Standard Specification for Remote ID and Tracking), the technical
standard underlying FAA Part 89, defines the Remote ID broadcast message format and
minimum transmission intervals for UAS. It does not, however, address fixed-site
receiver requirements: coverage methodology, receiver density, timestamping precision,
retention duration, or health monitoring are outside the scope of F3411-22a. This gap is
intentional in the context of the existing rule — but it creates a critical deficiency for
UAFR compliance, where a fixed-site operator must be able to affirmatively
demonstrate what was observed, when, and with what fidelity. A UAFR compliance
record is only as valid as the completeness and integrity of the underlying sensing
record. Without minimum standards, UAFR sites may deploy insufficient sensing
infrastructure, creating gaps that undermine the evidentiary chain when violations
occur. For Special UAFRs where criminal penalties may attach, the sensing record must
meet a higher evidentiary bar.
LONGBOW’s PEGASUS GUARDIAN architecture already implements these
capabilities; we recommend the FAA adopt minimum standards that make them floor
requirements for all UAFR operational monitoring.
Position 4: Establish a Secure, Auditable Authorized-Operator Whitelist Model
Regulatory context: The NPRM envisions a process by which certain operators
establish their authorization status to operate within a specific UAFR. The proposed rule
does not specify how an operator’s authorization is recorded, maintained, updated, or
communicated to the UAFR site’s monitoring infrastructure.
LONGBOW’s position: The FAA should establish a secure, revocable, auditable
whitelist model that links operator identity, aircraft registration, Remote ID serials, and
certificate/permit type into a structured authorization record associated with a specific
UAFR site and mission scope.
Key design requirements:
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558 | Page 5 of 9

1. One-time credential submission — an operator should be able to submit
certificate credentials and Remote ID serial numbers once and reference a
credential record for subsequent missions, rather than resubmitting on each
notification
2. Revocability — the facility operator or FAA should be able to revoke a whitelist
entry with immediate effect, and that revocation should propagate to monitoring
infrastructure
3. API-based updates — whitelist records should be queryable and updateable via
a structured API to support real-time correlation against observed Remote ID
broadcasts
4. Trust scoring — the FAA should consider whether to allow UAFR sites to
maintain tiered trust levels based on operator history (e.g., repeated successful
prior missions vs. first contact)
5. Privacy protection — operator identity and certificate data held in whitelists
should be handled under appropriate privacy controls, with access limited to the
specific facility and authorized law enforcement
Rationale: The authorization problem is the core unsolved operational challenge in the
NPRM. The rule creates a clear legal distinction between authorized and unauthorized
operations, but does not define how that distinction is made machine-readable and
verifiable in real time. A monitoring system that cannot programmatically distinguish a
known whitelisted operator from an unknown intruder cannot serve the facility
notification, risk scoring, or evidence documentation functions the NPRM envisions.
Position 5: Define a Standard Evidence Package Schema
Regulatory context: The NPRM envisions that UAFR sites will be able to document
violations and provide information to law enforcement. The proposed rule does not
specify the content, format, or integrity requirements for UAFR incident
documentation.
LONGBOW’s position: The FAA should define a standard evidence package schema
for UAFR incidents that includes at minimum:
• Track history with timestamped position data and altitude AGL
• Sensor sources contributing to the track (Remote ID, radar, RF, EO/IR, acoustic,
ADS-B)
• Observed Remote ID serial (or null/missing/mismatch notation)
• Authorization and notification state at time of incursion
(notified/whitelisted/unknown/denied)
• Behavior flags (hovering, loitering, route deviation, RID-missing, RID-mismatch)
• Risk score and rule triggers
• Chain-of-custody metadata including hash of logged sensor records, record
creation timestamp, and custodian identity
• Export format compatible with law enforcement and FAA reporting requirements
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558 | Page 6 of 9

Rationale: Without a standard schema, incident documentation will vary widely across
UAFR sites, making cross-site pattern analysis, multi-agency coordination, and FAA
compliance auditing impractical. LONGBOW has developed a canonical UAFR Event
Object as part of its PEGASUS GUARDIAN product architecture; we recommend the
FAA align the final rule’s evidence requirements with a published minimum data
standard.
For Special UAFRs where criminal penalties may apply, chain-of-custody integrity is
essential. Established federal standards provide the applicable framework. NIST SP
800-86 (Guide to Integrating Forensic Techniques into Incident Response) defines the
principles for acquiring, preserving, and presenting digital evidence in a manner
admissible in legal proceedings — UAFR evidence packages should conform to its chain-
of-custody requirements. Cryptographic timestamps on UAFR event records should
conform to RFC 3161 (Internet X.509 PKI Time-Stamp Protocol) as updated by RFC
5816 (which added SHA-2 algorithm support), providing a legally recognized
mechanism for proving that a record existed at a specific time and has not been altered
since. For prosecutable Special UAFR violations, evidence handling should additionally
conform to ISO/IEC 27037 (Guidelines for identification, collection, acquisition and
preservation of digital evidence), the international standard for digital evidence that
underpins cross-agency and cross-jurisdictional case transfer. The FAA should specify
these standards by reference in the final rule rather than defining a novel evidence
handling framework.
Position 6: Preserve Lawful Airspace Access for Critical Operations
Regulatory context: The NPRM proposes that certain vetted operator categories —
Part 91, 107, 108, 135, 137 — retain access rights within standard UAFRs subject to
notification. This is the correct design.
LONGBOW’s position: LONGBOW strongly supports the FAA’s intent to preserve
lawful access for vetted operators and urges the FAA to ensure the final rule’s
notification and authorization requirements do not inadvertently create access burdens
that prevent operationally critical UAS missions.
Specific applications that must be preserved:
• Public safety and emergency response — law enforcement, fire, search and
rescue, and emergency management UAS operations under jurisdictional
authority must not be delayed or impeded by notification workflows that cannot
accommodate exigent circumstances
• Medical logistics — Level I trauma centers with helipads are specifically
identified as eligible UAFR facilities; the final rule must ensure that medical
drone operations (organ transport, blood delivery, emergency supply) operating
to or through UAFRs can satisfy notification requirements without endangering
patient outcomes
• Infrastructure inspection — utility operators inspecting their own
infrastructure within a UAFR they petition for should not be required to notify
themselves
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558 | Page 7 of 9

• Disaster response and utility restoration — emergency UAS operations
during and after natural disasters must have a defined expedited access process
that does not require full pre-flight notification when the UAFR site itself is the
subject of the response
Rationale: UAFRs should make critical infrastructure more secure, not less
operationally capable. The access framework should be designed with the assumption
that high-frequency legitimate operations will be the norm at most UAFR sites, and that
the notification and whitelist system exists to facilitate — not impede — that access
while documenting the record.
Position 7: Establish a Controlled Information Model for Special UAFR Site Data
Regulatory context: The NPRM creates Special UAFRs for high-security federal,
defense, intelligence, and homeland security sites. The locations and boundaries of
some Special UAFR sites may themselves be sensitive.
LONGBOW’s position: The FAA should establish a controlled information handling
model for Special UAFR site data that allows the rule’s technical requirements —
machine-readable boundaries, notification APIs, monitoring standards — to be
implemented without requiring that sensitive site details be publicly disclosed.
Recommended framework:
1. Public layer — Special UAFRs are designated and charted at a sufficient level to
prevent accidental airspace incursion, with enough geographic specificity that an
operator in preflight planning can identify the UAFR and comply with
notification requirements
2. Controlled layer — detailed site geometry, sensor architecture, monitoring
coverage, and operational terms are accessible only to the UAFR petitioner,
designated monitoring providers, and authorized using-agency personnel,
protected under applicable information-security frameworks
3. Segmented notification — using-agency approval workflows for Special UAFR
access are conducted through secure channels that do not require sensitive site
data to transit public networks or be logged in shared USS infrastructure
Rationale: The value of the Special UAFR framework depends on whether high-
security federal and defense sites actually petition for designation. If the designation
process or the resulting machine-readable data publication exposes sensitive
operational details, security-conscious facilities will decline to petition. A tiered
information model preserves the security value of the designation for those sites while
maintaining the operational benefits of structured UAFR data for standard-site
operations.
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558 | Page 8 of 9

III. CONCLUSION
LONGBOW supports the proposed Part 74 rulemaking. Establishing persistent, legally
enforceable, machine-readable airspace protection around critical infrastructure is the
correct regulatory architecture for low-altitude UAS integration at scale. The NPRM
provides the correct structural foundation.
The seven positions above identify implementation-layer gaps — particularly in operator
notification standards and authorized-operator verification — where additional FAA
specificity in the final rule will determine whether UAFR operational compliance is
achievable at scale or collapses into a patchwork of site-specific, non-interoperable
processes.
LONGBOW is prepared to engage directly with FAA staff on any of the technical
positions above, to share PEGASUS GUARDIAN architecture documentation relevant to
the rulemaking record, or to participate in any advisory or industry working group the
FAA convenes in connection with Part 74 implementation.
Respectfully submitted,

Please preserve the ability to use drones to document conditions and the treatment of animals on industrial farming operations. Independent aerial documentation provides an important tool for transparency and accountability, helping to identify potential animal welfare concerns, environmental impacts, and risks to public health. Maintaining this oversight supports informed decision-making and promotes responsible agricultural practices.

Goodbye facing the truth, goodbye freedom of speech, goodbye democracy.

Welcome dystopia, where unnecessary cruelty has no limits what so ever.

Drone photography serves the public interest by increasing transparency. We have the right to see where our food comes from and in what condition the animals are kept. The FAA should not restrict drones for agricultural facilities.

I strongly oppose these limitations. They restrict freedom, discourage transparency, and set a worrying precedent for the future. We should be working toward greater openness, accountability, and public access to information—not creating unnecessary barriers that make it harder to expose important issues.

Allowing animal agriculture facilities to restrict drone photography would reduce transparency and make it more difficult to document animal welfare concerns, environmental damage, and food production practices. Responsible drone photography serves the public interest, and the public has a right to know what happens behind closed doors. Transparency leads to accountability, and accountability drives progress.

The FAA should not restrict drone usage over agriculture facilities, places that grow our nation’s food.

The public has minimal opportunity to gain awareness of the practices we support through our daily food purchases… And hence little opportunity to participate in an informed way in efforts to improve good practices and move away from harmful practices.

Drones provide vital data to help the public stay engaged and connected with the agriculture that we support and rely upon every day.

I would like to express my concern about any proposed limitations on the use of drones in the airspace above or near agricultural operations.

It is in the public interest to maintain transparency regarding the practices and conditions of agricultural operations in the United States. Responsible drone use can provide valuable information that supports public awareness, accountability, research, and informed discussion.

For these reasons, I respectfully request that any regulations avoid creating broad restrictions on drone operations near agricultural facilities, while continuing to balance legitimate concerns related to safety, privacy, and security.

Thank you for your time and consideration.

Subject: City of Chicago Department of Water Management
Comments Regarding NPRM, Designation: Restrict the Operation of
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
Dear Department of Transportation/ Federal Aviation Administration:
The City of Chicago, Department of Water Management (“CDWM”) owns, operates, and
manages substantial water infrastructure in Chicago. CDWM is responsible for the
purification of safe drinking water for the majority of people in Northeast Illinois. CDWM’s
mission is reliant on secure purification operations. We are keenly vested in ensuring that
there is a practical approach to the recent dynamic growth and development of unmanned
aircraft systems (UAS) balanced against the very real security concerns stemming from
unregulated UAS access to sensitive locations. We applaud the Department of
Transportation/ Federal Aviation Administration for proposing new 14 CFR Part 74 to
bring better parity to both important interests.
UASs are fundamentally changing the security profiles at critical infrastructure facilities.
The low cost of UAS technology has triggered hundreds of thousands of new operators
to enter the aviation community, some of whom have aviation knowledge and experience
and many others who do not. The proliferation of these operations presents significant
safety and security implications of UAS flying in close proximity to critical infrastructure,
such as water supply facilities.
CDWM strongly supports the proposed new regulations to better manage UAS
operations in close proximity to critical facilities such as DWM purification plants,
water cribs, pumping stations, and reservoirs. It is a matter of National and Homeland
security that CDWM water supply facilities are protected from accidental, intentional UAS
attacks or UAS surveillance.
We fully concur with the Department of Transportation/Federal Aviation Administration’s
(DOT/FAA) need to “integrate [UASs] into the airspace of the United States while
balancing the needs of security stakeholders.” (Department of Transportation,
1

“Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed
Site Facility,” Federal Register 91, no. 87 [May 6, 2026]: 24680.)
In relation to the proposed rulemaking, I am further respectfully submitting the following
specific comments for your consideration regarding the subject matter referenced above
on behalf of CDWM Commissioner Randy Conner.
• Lateral Boundaries: Proposed rule §74.58 (b)(1) would require the unmanned
aircraft flight restriction (UAFR) lateral boundaries under §74.58 not to “exceed the
other fixed site facility property boundary.” (ibid, 24687.) For CDWM, a serious
concern with UASs is their ability to perform surveillance operations on our
facilities. This concern emanates from the sensitive nature of CDWM purification,
pumping, and transmission operations. Equally as concerning is the fact that we
take delivery of various substances that are required for our operations. We do not
feel that limiting the UAFR lateral boundaries merely to legal boundaries
meaningfully addresses our surveillance concerns. Without getting into too many
specifics, a distance of at least ¼ mile from our legal boundaries would provide us
with better assurance that unauthorized surveillance opportunities would be
substantively compromised. We agree that DOT/FAA must strike a proper balance
between two compelling interests. Therefore, while we would prefer to see the
UAFR lateral boundary for all of our facilities at ¼, we would adhere to any other
formula developed by DOT/FAA to extend UAFR lateral boundaries for sensitive
fixed sites beyond their legal boundaries. As such, we would recommend
appending the following language at the end of §74.58 (b)(1): “or other lateral
boundary in exceptional cases as determined at the sole and exclusive discretion
of FAA.”
• Altitude Ceiling: Proposed rule §74.60(b)(2) would largely limit the UAFR at a
height not to “exceed 400 feet above ground level….” (ibid, 24687). For CDWM,
our concern is that with the continued rapid evolution of UAS technology, this
height restriction may not be sufficient to limit surveillance of our properties. In
striking a balance, we, therefore, recommend the addition of the following at the
end of §74.60(b)(2): “or other altitude ceiling in exceptional cases as determined
at the sole and exclusive discretion of FAA.”
• Eligible Facilities – Water and Wastewater Systems Sector: To qualify for a
UAFR, a fixed site facility must be an eligible facility under proposed §74.54 (ibid,
24686). As indicated in §74.96, Water systems are one of the sixteen (16) sectors
specifically referenced in the proposed rules as an eligible facility under section
§74.54. (ibid, 24689). Section 74.96 is presently open-ended as “Reserved” with
respect to the eligibility criteria needed for this sector. CDWM offers the following
as possible eligibility criteria under §74.96 following that Section’s preamble:
o “(a) Public water systems that provide water for human consumption
through pipes or other constructed conveyances to at least 15 service
connections or serve an average of at least 25 people for at least 60 days
2

a year. This would include 1) Community Water Systems, 2) Non-Transient
Non-Community Water System, and 3) Transient Non-Community Water
System;”
▪ This broad definition is pulled directly from the Federal Registrar
(ibid, 24672). Aside from air, water is the most important natural
element needed for life. CDWM is among the largest 410 community
water systems that serve more than 100,000 people. However,
CDWM believes that all water providers should be afforded UAFR
protections regardless of their size. As noted by the Cybersecurity &
Infrastructure Security Agency (COSA), “[p]rotecting the systems
that provide water is vitally important to the stability and health of the
nation….” (www.cisa.gov/topics/critical-infrastructure-security-and-
resilience/critical-infrastructure-sectors/water-and-wastewater-
sector.) No community water systems should be penalized because
of their size.
o “(b) Provides purified water to multiple local government jurisdictions;
o “(c) Provides purified water across multiple counties;
o “(d) Provides purified water to a service delivery area greater than 10 miles;
o “(e) Provides purified water to one or more fixed facility sectors as defined
is §74.81 through §74.94;
o “(f) Is located within ¼ mile of any facility that has an annual that met or
exceeded 2,500,00 visitors in the previous calenda year;
o “(g) Where the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)
determines that a public water system has a Risk and Resilience
Assessment (RRA) and an Emergency Response Plan (ERP) that is
compliant with the Safe Drinking Water (SDWA) section 1433 as amended
by America’s Water Infrastructure Act (AWIA) section 2013, that public
water system establishes a prima facie case for eligibility as fixed site
facility.”
If enacted, CDWM would submit our UAFR application and would comply with all UAFR
regulatory requirements. This process should be confidential so that proprietary and
sensitive information is not inadvertently divulged. It is also recommended that there is
clear and consistent accountability for instances of any UAS violations of the proposed
rule.
I am the Assistant Commissioner in charge of Safety and Security at CDWM. I have over
30 years of experience in Law Enforcement and over 10 years protecting the Chicago
