Re: Comment on Docket # FAA-2026-4558, Designation-Restrict the Operation of
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
Dear Administrator:
On behalf of the Boston Red Sox, we are providing comments on the FAA’s Proposed Rule on
unmanned aircraft flight restrictions (“UAFRs”) with respect to fixed site facilities. We applaud
the Administration for proposing this rule and we support the goals, approach and specific
procedures as proposed. We offer the following comments for your consideration.
We agree wholeheartedly with the Administration’s recognition that sports and entertainment
venues should be included in the types of critical commercial facilities that merit protection from
unauthorized unmanned aircraft flights. The language in the proposed rule (§ 74.82) aptly
captures the serious consequences that could stem from a UAV security intrusion at a venue such
as Fenway Park: “The cascading effects of a UAV incident occurring at a facility serving this
large of a community would extend beyond the target facility and negatively affect supply
chains, tourism, and the nation's economy.” We invest significant resources, time and focus to
safeguard our ballparks – both Fenway Park in Boston and our spring training facility, JetBlue
Park in Ft. Myers, Florida – because any type of security breach would not only endanger our
large numbers of fans, employees and players, but would also create major actual and symbolic
damage to the entire outdoor sports and live entertainment industry. Last year, the Department
of Homeland Security recognized Fenway Park for SAFETY Act designation, and our
collaborative efforts with federal, state and local law enforcement underscore the stakes when it
comes to protecting against all types of physical security threats.
With respect to eligibility for limitations on unmanned aircraft flights, we urge the
Administration to modify several proposed criteria in § 74.82 in order to provide for more
flexibility to consider all of the circumstances when adopting UAFRs. Specifically:
• Outdoor sports and live entertainment venues should be eligible to seek permanent
special UAFRs regardless of whether they are able to obtain temporary flight

restrictions as well. Fenway Park, like most other large venues, hosts a wide range of
events and activities in addition to its core offering, Major League baseball games.
Those events include movie nights and amateur high school and college sports events
attended by anywhere from a few thousand people to tens of thousands, as well as
private events in the ballpark for schools, charitable and civic organizations, private
companies and other clients. Temporary flight restrictions would not be available for
this array of smaller events, but a potential UAV incident involving those types of
events could threaten serious direct physical and economic damage (including the
dangers of bodily injury or death), and any breach at any time at Fenway Park would
have serious consequences to our business and the entire industry.
• The criterion for a venue being “primarily outdoors and not enclosed” is vague and not
necessary. Many large sports and entertainment venues include significant spaces that
are covered and/or enclosed areas, and those entire venues merit protection.
• The minimum total attendance requirement of 2.5 million visitors or more in the prior
year is unnecessarily restrictive. Although Fenway Park surpasses this threshold each
year, some Major League Baseball facilities might not, and our organization urges
protection for every venue at which our team participates. In addition, our spring
training facility hosts approximately a full month of Major League spring training
games featuring all of our top players and thousands of fans. JetBlue Park and all other
spring training venues should be eligible for UAFRs even though they do not host 2.5
million fans a year.
• The requirement that a venue be “open to the public” for at least 120 days a year is
likewise unnecessarily restrictive. Does “open to the public” include activities and
events that a venue hosts for charitable organizations and corporate or individual
clients? Does it include smaller events and tours? In any case, Fenway Park typically
has approximately 120 large public entertainment and other events each year, but if in a
given year it was “open to the public” only for 81 or 90 or 100 dates, it would still be a
major economic engine for the city and region. As emphasized above, any security
breach involving Fenway Park could have widespread, long-lasting repercussions, and
an inflexible minimum number of events would artificially limit the effectiveness of the
Administration’s proposed regulatory regime.
In short, we urge the Administration to remove these limiting criteria for eligibility and instead to
allow any outdoor sports and live entertainment venue with seating capacity of at least 5,000
persons to apply for UAFR protections, without including unnecessary limiting requirements.
With respect to the geographic extent of restrictions, we strongly support a program that would
include not only the specific venue property itself within property lines, but also a lateral zone
with a radius of at least 1,000 feet. When venues like Fenway Park host large events with
thousands of people, the large crowds entering and exiting the venue could be vulnerable to
incidents involving UAVs, and an incident outside of the venue could cause as much human,
economic and psychological damage as an event inside the venue.
2

In the same spirit of encouraging flexibility, we urge the Administration not to require formal
security assessments for all venues seeking restrictions. There may be some instances in which a
formal assessment is warranted. There will surely be many cases, however, such as large
professional sports venues, where no formal assessment is needed and it is intuitively obvious
that permanent UAFR protections are appropriate.
As we understand the proposed rule, a venue would be able to request a special UAFR
designation that would still allow for drone flights in its airspace if specifically authorized by the
venue operator and the FAA. We encourage the FAA to make clear in its final rule and
accompanying materials that certain authorized unmanned aircraft flights may be allowed. On
limited occasions, sports and entertainment venues may wish to grant access to drones operated
by reputable, licensed entities to capture video footage or for other safe, valid entertainment
purposes. The UAFR process should accommodate such authorized, safe uses of drone
technology.
As the FAA continues the rulemaking process, we would be glad to speak with your staff to
provide further feedback and guidance based on our extensive experience operating one of
America’s iconic entertainment venues and providing a safe experience to millions of fans and
visitors each year.
We appreciate the opportunity to provide this feedback and again applaud the Administration for
moving forward with these important measures.
Sincerely,
David S. Friedman
Executive Vice President, Legal & Government Affairs
cc: David Thomas, MLB Senior Vice President,
Security & Ballpark Operations
3

Docket No. FAA–2026–4558
Notice No. 26–03
Designation—Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site
Facility
RE: Comments on Proposed Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restriction (UAFR) Rule
To Whom It May Concern:
I appreciate the opportunity to comment on the FAA's proposed rule establishing Unmanned
Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs) around certain fixed-site facilities.
I submit these comments as President of HTS Ag, an agricultural technology company serving
farmers, agricultural retailers, cooperatives, and commercial drone operators throughout the Midwest.
Our company works extensively with precision agriculture technologies, including unmanned aircraft
systems used for crop scouting, mapping, spraying, and agricultural data collection.
I support the FAA's effort to improve aviation safety and protect critical infrastructure while
preserving the continued growth of commercial UAS operations. The proposed rule generally strikes an
appropriate balance between security needs and the integration of drones into the National Airspace
System.
Support for Agricultural Drone Operations
I strongly support the FAA's proposal to allow agricultural drone operations conducted under Part
137 and other approved operating authorities to continue operating within UAFRs, subject to Remote ID
and notification requirements.
Agricultural drones provide significant benefits, including improved input efficiency, reduced
environmental impact, increased worker safety, and greater productivity. Restricting these operations
would create unnecessary burdens for agriculture with limited security benefit.
Food and Agriculture Sector Eligibility
I agree with the FAA's conclusion that broad inclusion of production agriculture facilities would be
inappropriate.
Most farms should not be eligible for UAFRs. Precision agriculture increasingly depends on drone
technology, and expanding restrictions across production agriculture would unnecessarily limit
innovation and adoption.
If the FAA establishes eligibility criteria for the Food and Agriculture Sector in the future, those
criteria should be limited to facilities whose disruption would have significant regional or national
consequences, such as:
• Major grain export terminals
• Nationally significant food processing facilities
• Strategic food reserve facilities
• Critical agricultural supply chain infrastructure
111 4th Street, Neola, IA 51559
(800)741-3305
www.htsag.com

Eligibility should be based on demonstrated economic and food-security impact, not acreage,
ownership size, or production volume alone.
Notification and Remote ID
The proposed reliance on Remote ID is a practical approach that allows lawful operators to remain
identifiable while supporting security objectives.
As UAFRs expand, I encourage the FAA to pursue automated notification systems that leverage
Remote ID and digital authorization tools. Agricultural drone operators often conduct multiple flights
daily across large areas, and scalable compliance solutions will be important for both operators and
facility managers.
Conclusion
Overall, I support the FAA's proposed framework and appreciate the agency's recognition of the
importance of commercial agricultural drone operations.
I encourage the FAA to:
1. Continue allowing Part 137 agricultural operations within UAFRs.
2. Avoid broad inclusion of production agriculture when developing Food and Agriculture Sector
criteria.
3. Limit future agricultural UAFRs to facilities with demonstrable regional or national significance.
4. Develop efficient, technology-based notification solutions that support both security and
operational practicality.
Thank you for considering these comments.
Respectfully,
Adam Gittins
President
HTS Ag
111 4th Street
Neola, IA 51559
111 4th Street, Neola, IA 51559
(800)741-3305
www.htsag.com

To whom it may concern,

I read the FAA's new rule proposal titled "Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility" and I have some concerns. I understand the main objective is to protect privacy and national security by allowing certain sites whose activity should remain secret to apply for this drone restriction. However, the concern is sites not important for national security may nevertheless request the drone restriction to free themselves from much needed accountability to the public. The proposal mentions agricultural sites. Journalists often use drones to investigate reports of animal welfare violations at such locations, for example.

My view is that if you go ahead with this proposed rule, you should carve out exceptions or raise the bar so that most ordinary sites, especially those not closely related to national security and where First Amendment concerns about journalism are more important (such as CAFOs, or Concentrated Animal Feeding Operations), be excluded. Thank you!

comments.commenter: Comment from South Dakota Department of Transportation
comments.comment_text: The South Dakota Department of Transportation (SDDOT) respectfully submits the following comments regarding the Federal Aviation Administration’s proposed rulemaking to establish Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs) around designated categories of critical infrastructure. We appreciate the FAA’s efforts to balance aviation safety, critical facility protection, and the operational needs of public agencies.

I do not agree with allowing certain facilities such as mass producing farms to have drone restrictions. There should be transparency in our food system so people know what is actually happening behind the  marketing scams.

Please don't restrict drone flights over farms. Restricting drone flying further implicitly pushes drone use to more populated areas, possibly increasing accidents which could cause injury to people. Farmland takes up a large percent of land to fly over, and continuing to allow flights will reduce possibly injuries to others.

Comments of Maryland Transportation Authority on Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) Designation – Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility Docket No. FAA-2026-4558

June 30, 2026
Docket Operations
U.S. Department of Transportation
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE, Room W58-213
Washington, DC 20590
Submitted electronically via regulations.gov: https://www.regulations.gov
Re: Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03; RIN 2120-AL33
Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed
Site Facility
Dear Administrator Bedford and Members of the Federal Aviation Administration Rulemaking
Staff:
I. Introduction and Statement of Interest
Concept Solutions, LLC (“Concept Solutions” or the “Company”) is a technology company
headquartered in Reston, Virginia that develops the AvenGIS Virtual Drone Management (VDM)
Platform, a 100% NDAA-compliant, U.S.-engineered software system for real-time unmanned
aircraft system (UAS) airspace detection, tracking, and command-and-control at critical
infrastructure facilities. Concept Solutions respectfully submits these comments in response to the
FAA’s Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) published May 6, 2026, proposing to implement
Section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016 (FESSA) through a new 14
CFR Part 74 establishing Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs).
Concept Solutions has an immediate and direct operational interest in this rulemaking. The
AvenGIS VDM platform is specifically designed to enable critical infrastructure owners and
operators to satisfy the proposed UAFR framework’s requirements, including the Remote ID
sensing mandate proposed in §74.56(d), the operator access notification requirements at proposed
§74.255, the security plan and demonstration-of-need documentation at proposed §74.64 and
§74.66, and the annual UAS traffic reporting obligation proposed in §74.20. We validated our
platform’s core sensor fusion capability during a test conducted at the MITRE Corporation facility
in northern Virginia in February 2026, successfully integrating simultaneous live data from a
commercial Remote ID antenna receiver and a commercial radar unit supplied by one of our
technology and sensor partners, tracking individual UAS contacts in a single display with source-
coded identification and alert classification.
We write to offer a practical perspective from the standpoint of a technology developer actively
building the operational software that will enable fixed site facilities to comply with and benefit
from the UAFR framework as it nears finalization. Our comments are organized as follows:
general support for the proposed rule; specific technical and operational comments on individual

provisions; recommendations for provisions the FAA should clarify, strengthen, or reconsider; and
a discussion of how the AvenGIS VDM platform architecture aligns with and operationalizes the
proposed rule.
II. General Support for the Proposed Rule
Concept Solutions strongly supports the FAA’s proposed implementation of Section 2209 and
commends the Agency for publishing this NPRM in fulfillment of the directive in Executive Order
14305, “Restoring American Airspace Sovereignty” (signed June 6, 2025; 90 FR 24719). The ten-
year gap between the statutory mandate in FESSA Section 2209 (enacted July 15, 2016) and this
NPRM has created significant operational uncertainty for critical infrastructure owners and
operators who lack a formal federal mechanism to designate restricted airspace around their
facilities. The proposed UAFR framework will provide that mechanism and, when finalized, will
create clear legal authority that law enforcement, facility operators, and responsible UAS operators
can rely upon.
We particularly support the following structural choices the FAA has made in this proposal:
● The decision to require Remote ID sensing capability as a precondition for UAFR
eligibility under proposed §74.56(d). This ensures that facilities granted a UAFR will have
the minimum technological foundation to distinguish compliant operators from
unauthorized intrusions and will be better positioned to address the annual UAS activity
reporting required under proposed §74.20.
● The two-tier structure of Standard and Special UAFRs, which appropriately calibrates the
legal and enforcement framework to the threat profile of different facility types, reserving
the more stringent Special UAFR-with potential criminal penalties under 49 U.S.C. 46307
for restrictions issued for national security or homeland security purposes-for facilities with
credible, demonstrated risks.
● The sector-specific eligibility criteria in proposed Subpart C, which reflect meaningful
engagement with Sector Risk Management Agencies (SRMAs) and provide a principled,
defensible basis for determining which of the nation’s approximately 125,000 potentially
high-priority facilities should have access to the UAFR remedy.
● The five-year UAFR term with renewal requirements (proposed §§74.200 and 74.210),
which appropriately builds in periodic review as UAS technology, Remote ID capabilities,
and airspace management tools continue to evolve rapidly.
● The access framework under proposed §74.250, which preserves the public right of transit
for operators who have met higher safety and security standards under Parts 91, 107, 108,
135, and 137, while restricting unauthorized operations. This balance is appropriate and
consistent with the FAA’s statutory responsibilities under 49 U.S.C. 40103.
III. Specific Technical and Operational Comments
A. Proposed §74.56 (Protective Security): Remote ID Sensing Requirement
Proposed §74.56(d) requires that the facility’s security monitoring include the capability to receive
broadcast Remote ID messages from unmanned aircraft operating within or in close proximity to
the requested UAFR, using equipment compatible with the requirements of 14 CFR Part 89.
Concept Solutions strongly endorses this requirement as both necessary and appropriately scoped.

We particularly note that §74.56(d) permits a facility to satisfy this requirement “either directly or
through a contracted service.” Concept Solutions strongly supports this provision. It appropriately
allows critical infrastructure operators-many of whom lack in-house airspace-monitoring
expertise-to meet the requirement through managed service providers, and it reflects the
operational reality of how Remote ID sensing capability will be deployed and sustained at scale
across the eligible facility population.
From our operational experience integrating Remote ID receivers with live UAS traffic, we offer
the following technical observations for the FAA’s consideration:
1. The FAA should clarify the minimum performance standards for §74.56(d) Remote ID
sensing equipment.
The current proposal requires compatibility with Part 89 broadcast requirements but does not
specify minimum detection range, receiver sensitivity, or multi-unit requirements for large
facilities. In our February 2026 testing at MITRE’s National Drone Range, a single commercial
Remote ID receiver successfully captured all six Remote ID-broadcasting aircraft within
approximately 500 meters of the omnidirectional antenna. For facilities such as nuclear generating
stations, large electrical substations, or correctional facilities whose perimeters extend
substantially beyond this range, a single receiver may be insufficient to satisfy the intent of
§74.56(d).
We recommend that the FAA, in the final rule or in the accompanying Advisory Circular, provide
guidance on:
● Minimum detection range requirements as a function of UAFR lateral boundary size;
● Whether multi-receiver deployments are expected for facilities whose property boundaries
exceed the effective range of a single unit;
● The acceptable latency between a drone broadcasting Remote ID and the facility receiving
and logging the broadcast, to ensure the capability functions as a real-time situational
awareness tool rather than a retrospective record only.
2. The FAA should address non-cooperative aircraft - the gap that §74.56(d) alone cannot
close.
The §74.56(d) requirement establishes a meaningful minimum floor, but the FAA should
acknowledge in the preamble to the final rule that Remote ID sensing, by definition, detects only
cooperative operators who comply with Part 89. Our MITRE test simultaneously detected several
radar-tracked contacts alongside only six Remote ID-broadcasting contacts, suggesting that a
meaningful share of UAS activity near a given facility at a given time may be non-cooperative-
whether because the operation was authorized by the Administrator to deviate from the Part 89
Remote ID requirement, the aircraft’s Remote ID module malfunctioned or stopped broadcasting
message elements, or the operation involved a bad actor (the UAS operator itself or a third party
spoofing the Remote ID network) intentionally circumventing detection.
The proposed UAFR framework is not intended to require counter-UAS mitigation capability; that
authority resides elsewhere in federal law. The FAA should nonetheless affirmatively encourage,
in the Advisory Circular and in its SRMA coordination guidance, that applicants seeking UAFRs
for higher-risk sectors consider supplemental sensor layering (radar, RF spectrum analysis,
acoustic detection, and electro-optical and infrared/thermal cameras) as part of their UAS security
and incident response plans under proposed §74.64. The AvenGIS VDM architecture specifically

supports this by integrating Remote ID, radar, RF, and acoustic detection in a unified command-
and-control display, allowing facilities to build toward a more complete detection picture as their
resources and legal authorities permit.
B. Proposed §74.66: Demonstration of Need - UAS Traffic History
Proposed §74.66(a) requires applicants to submit information describing UAS operations over the
facility during the previous 24 months, where available. Concept Solutions supports this
requirement but urges the FAA to address the “where available” qualifier more specifically in the
final rule and Advisory Circular.
For facilities that do not yet have Remote ID sensing capability installed-which will likely include
many applicants at the time of initial UAFR application-the 24-month traffic history requirement
may create avoidable uncertainty about what evidence will satisfy the Agency. We understand that
the FAA does not require automated sensor logs as the exclusive basis for this showing; applicants
may instead provide estimates of UAS activity, including operation type, location, frequency,
whether operators are known to the facility, and the method of observation relied upon, supported
by the underlying data sources used to inform those estimates. We recommend that the final rule
and accompanying Advisory Circular state this expectation explicitly, so that facilities without
pre-existing Remote ID sensing do not mistakenly conclude that §74.66(a) is unsatisfiable in the
absence of a digital traffic log.
We recommend that the FAA:
● Clarify that applicants may satisfy the §74.66(a) requirement through a combination of
formal sensor logs (where available), incident reports filed with law enforcement or
security agencies, security camera footage, sworn statements from security personnel, and
publicly reported incidents, without requiring automated Remote ID logs as the exclusive
or primary form of evidence;
● Create a “pilot period” mechanism-perhaps 90 to 180 days-during which facilities that have
installed §74.56(d)-compliant Remote ID sensing for the first time may accumulate
baseline traffic data and update their UAFR application, with the FAA accepting the initial
conditional application during this window and allowing the applicant to supplement it
with accumulated baseline data, preserving the applicant’s place in the review queue;
● Recognize that the mere installation of Remote ID sensing equipment under §74.56(d) will,
over time, generate the very traffic history data that §74.66 requires, and structure the
program so that compliance with one provision actively builds the evidentiary foundation
for the other.
The AvenGIS VDM platform addresses this gap directly: upon deployment at a facility, the
platform automatically archives all detected UAS contacts, including those detected by Remote ID
and radar, to a time-series data store with full metadata, including source, altitude, speed, heading,
and proximity to user-defined facility boundaries. This log automatically produces §74.66 traffic
history documentation as a byproduct of normal operations, rather than requiring facilities to
reconstruct historical records after the fact. We understand that the §74.20 annual reporting
obligation is intended to draw on access notifications submitted under §74.255, together with Site
Manager records of unauthorized operations; Remote ID sensing data of the kind the AvenGIS
platform captures is well suited to supplement, rather than replace, that reporting framework.
C. Proposed §74.255: UAFR Access Notification

Proposed §74.255 requires operators intending to fly within a UAFR under §74.250 to provide
advance notification to the fixed site facility’s site manager. Concept Solutions supports the intent
of this provision but has several operational observations.
1. The FAA should specify a minimum advance notification window.
The current proposed language requires notification “as soon as reasonably possible in advance of
the operation” without specifying a minimum window. For facility security planning purposes,
this ambiguity may prove unworkable. A facility receiving notification ten seconds before a drone
enters its UAFR has effectively no opportunity to distinguish the notified operator from an
unauthorized intruder in real time.
We recommend that the FAA establish a tiered minimum notification window: for example, one
hour for standard operations, 24–48 hours for critical infrastructure operations, and a shorter
verbal-plus-written protocol (as already proposed) for law enforcement and government operations
requiring operational security. The FAA should also retain flexibility for time-sensitive legitimate
business uses that cannot reasonably meet even a one-hour window-for example, media coverage
responding to an incident at the facility-by allowing notification concurrent with or immediately
following entry into the UAFR in such circumstances. The FAA should also consider specifying
how operators authenticate themselves, and the Advisory Circular could provide additional
guidance on accommodating legitimate time-sensitive business uses.
2. Remote ID serial number whitelisting should be explicitly contemplated.
The FAA seeks comment on “a more automated notification procedure,” including a whitelist of
Remote ID serial numbers that would be made available to fixed site facilities. Concept Solutions
strongly endorses this approach and urges the FAA to include it in the final rule rather than
deferring it entirely to an Advisory Circular.
In our platform architecture, the operator whitelist is a core compliance module: when a Remote
ID-broadcasting aircraft enters the UAFR boundary, the VDM system immediately cross-
references the aircraft’s serial number against the pre-cleared whitelist, classifies the contact as
authorized or unauthorized, and logs the result with a timestamp for the facility’s §74.20 annual
reporting. This automated matching dramatically reduces the operational burden on site managers
and improves the speed and accuracy of the compliant/non-compliant determination that the
UAFR framework depends upon.
We recommend that the FAA create a formal whitelist registry within the UAFR Module web
portal, allowing facilities to pre-register the Remote ID serial numbers of operators they have
authorized, with expiration dates and operation-type fields. This would be consistent with the
FAA’s existing direction of travel toward digital, automated NAS access management.
D. Proposed §74.100: FAA Evaluation and 90-Day Determination
Concept Solutions notes that the proposed rule anticipates the FAA making a final determination
within 90 days of application submission (proposed §74.100). The FAA’s preliminary regulatory
impact analysis models a scenario of more than 9,000 fixed site facilities obtaining UAFRs, drawn
from a universe of approximately 125,000 facilities that SRMAs identified as potentially high
priority for a UAFR. Given application volume of this potential magnitude, we encourage the FAA
to provide transparency into how it intends to manage application volume and sequencing across
the eligible sectors, prioritize applications, and communicate processing timelines to applicants.

We suggest that the FAA consider establishing a public-facing application status tracker within
the UAFR Module and commit to issuing an initial completeness determination-confirming that
the application package is sufficient to proceed to substantive review-within 30 days of
submission. We understand the 90-day window encompasses several discrete stages following
submission: FAA review leading to conditional approval or denial; publication of a proposal in the
Federal Register (absent good cause to forgo notice and comment); a comment period of at least
30 days; and the FAA’s final determination considering comments received and any application
updates-such that applicants will generally know well before the 90-day mark whether their request
has been conditionally approved or denied, and may petition for reconsideration within 30 days of
a denial under §74.160. An early determination of completeness would nonetheless allow
applicants to correct administrative deficiencies before the substantive review and Federal Register
process begins, reducing the likelihood that curable defects delay the conditional approval
decision.
E. Proposed §74.87 (Emergency Services Sector): Correctional Facilities
Concept Solutions commends the FAA for including state prisons and federal correctional
facilities as eligible facility types under the Emergency Services Sector criteria of §74.87 and for
recognizing them as potential candidates for Special UAFR designation. The documented scale of
drone-delivered contraband at correctional facilities-reflected in the FAA’s own record, which
cites 300 drone sightings reported by the Georgia Department of Corrections in 2018 and South
Carolina Department of Corrections reports rising from 193 incidents in 2019 to 262 in 2022, with
the FAA noting that reported incidents likely represent only a fraction of actual incursions-
represents precisely the kind of acute, recurring threat the Special UAFR framework is designed
to address.
We note that correctional facilities present a particular operational challenge for the §74.56(d)
Remote ID sensing requirement: many drone contraband operators deliberately use pre-Part 89
aircraft or modified drones specifically to avoid Remote ID detection. We urge the FAA to state
explicitly in the final rule’s preamble that correctional facilities are among the facility types for
which the Advisory Circular should recommend supplemental non-cooperative detection (radar,
RF) in addition to the §74.56(d) Remote ID minimum.
IV. AvenGIS VDM Platform - UAFR Regulatory Compliance Alignment
The following table summarizes how the AvenGIS VDM platform architecture directly addresses
each major proposed UAFR requirement, demonstrating that the technology necessary to
operationalize this rulemaking at scale exists and is currently under active development.
UAFR Regulatory AvenGIS VDM Capability Status
Provision Requirement
§74.56 Remote ID sensing Tier 1 Remote ID fusion layer: commercial Remote ID Live /
capability at every antenna receiver + commercial radar - live test at MITRE validated

reporting obligation evidence as a byproduct of standard operation development

and audit trail operator classification, whitelist match, notification 2026

V. Summary of Recommendations
For the convenience of FAA rulemaking staff, Concept Solutions’ specific recommendations are
summarized as follows:
● §74.56(d) - Remote ID sensing performance standards: Specify minimum detection
range and receiver sensitivity guidance, either in the final rule or in an Advisory Circular,
as a function of UAFR lateral boundary size. Acknowledge in the preamble that §74.56(d)
establishes a minimum floor that does not address non-cooperative aircraft, and encourage
supplemental sensor layering for higher-risk sectors. Affirm the value of the provision’s
“directly or through a contracted service” option.
● §74.66 - Demonstration of need / traffic history: Clarify acceptable evidence types
beyond digital Remote ID logs, including incident reports, security camera records, and
sworn security-personnel statements. Establish a 90- to 180-day pilot period to allow first-
time Remote ID sensor deployers to accumulate baseline traffic data and update their
UAFR application.
● §74.255 - Operator access notification: Establish a minimum advance notification
window (recommended: one hour for standard operations and 24–48 hours for operations
around critical infrastructure). Include formal Remote ID serial number whitelisting in the
final rule, not deferred to an Advisory Circular only, and establish a UAFR Module
whitelist registry allowing facilities to pre-register authorized operators.
● §74.100 - Application processing: Establish a public-facing application status tracker
within the UAFR Module and commit to a 30-day initial completeness determination to
enable early deficiency correction.
● §74.87 - Correctional facilities: Explicitly recognize in the preamble and Advisory
Circular that correctional facilities face a higher-than-average rate of deliberate non-
cooperative drone operations, and affirmatively recommend supplemental non-cooperative
sensing (radar, RF) in UAFR security plans for this sector.
VI. Conclusion
Concept Solutions commends the FAA for publishing this NPRM and taking the significant
regulatory step of implementing the Section 2209 mandate. The UAFR framework, when finalized,
will provide critical infrastructure owners and operators with the legal foundation they need to
protect their facilities from the growing UAS threat and create a structured market for the

operational software and sensor integration services that make UAFR compliance actionable on
the ground.
The AvenGIS Virtual Drone Management Platform is designed from the ground up to
operationalize this regulatory framework: to satisfy §74.56(d)’s Remote ID sensing requirement,
to generate the §74.66 traffic history documentation automatically, to manage the §74.255 operator
notification and whitelist workflow, and to produce the audit trail that §74.260’s enforcement
framework depends upon. We are committed to working collaboratively with the FAA, SRMAs,
and critical infrastructure owners and operators to ensure the UAFR program achieves its intended
safety and security objectives.
Concept Solutions respectfully requests that the FAA consider the technical and operational
perspectives set forth in these comments as it develops the final rule. We would welcome the
opportunity to discuss our platform’s capabilities and our recommendations with FAA rulemaking
staff at the Agency’s convenience.
Respectfully submitted,
Concept Solutions, LLC
11600 Sunrise Valley Drive, Suite 300
Reston, Virginia 20191
www.concept-solutions.com

I write regarding the FAA seeking comment on the criteria for determining what sites in the Agricultural sector might be considered for a UAFR. Given that the FAA cannot identify any single agricultural site that might have national level debilitating impact; and given that journalists and activists frequently make use of drones to investigate and document animal welfare conditions on farms; I would ask that the FAA not consider individual farms or processing plants as valid applicants for a UAFR.

I am concerned that farms could easily abuse UAFRs as a form of "ag-gag" laws which is surely not the intent of the section 2209 regulations, and would embroil the FAA into First Amendment legal disputes. The FAA has already identified that few sites within the agricultural sector would rise to the level of a national level debilitating threat. Please do not allow farms and processing plants to be granted UAFRs.

I want to express my concern about any limitations to drone footage in the airspace near or above agricultural operations. It is in the public interest to have the ability for trained and licensed drone operators to be able to see these spaces. The public interest in this important source of transparency should outweigh any vague and unsubstantiated risk to national security.
INDIGNE ET CRUEL

comments.commenter: Comment from Northern Kentucky Water District
comments.comment_text:
July 1, 2026
Michelle Ferritto
Office of Rulemaking Federal Aviation Administration
800 Independence Avenue SW
Washington, DC 20591 Submitted via Regulations.gov
RE: Proposed 14 CFR Part 74, Water and Wastewater Systems Sector (§ 74.96)
Docket ID No. FAA-2026-4558
Dear Ms. Ferritto,
Northern Kentucky Water District (NKWD) submits these comments on EPA's proposed National
Northern Kentucky Water District ("NKWD") respectfully submits this comment on the above-
referenced proposed rule. NKWD is a community water system serving approximately 90,000
customer accounts across three counties in Northern Kentucky. NKWD owns and operates water
treatment plants, elevated storage tanks (water towers), pump stations, reservoirs, and raw water
intakes. NKWD also operates unmanned aircraft systems under 14 CFR Part 107 for infrastructure
inspection and operational purposes.
NKWD writes in support of the proposed rule and urges the FAA to develop meaningful eligibility
criteria for the Water and Wastewater Systems Sector under § 74.96, which is currently reserved.
NKWD's comments respond to the five questions the FAA posed for this sector and offer additional
observations from our dual perspective as both a prospective UAFR applicant and a Part 107 drone
operator.
I. Responses to FAA Questions for the Water and Wastewater Sector
1. What types of drinking water and wastewater systems should be considered?
The FAA should include the following facility types as eligible for UAFR designation:
• Water treatment plants, which house chemical storage (chlorine, sodium hydroxide, and
similar substances), process control systems, and exposed operational technology;
• Elevated storage tanks (water towers), which are highly visible, elevated, and cannot be
enclosed or hardened against aerial surveillance or physical impact;
• Pump stations with SCADA or industrial control systems, where proximity-based cyber
intrusion or physical disruption could interrupt service to large populations;
• Raw water intakes, where contamination or disruption at the source would have cascading
effects on treatment and distribution; and
• Reservoirs and impoundments serving as primary drinking water sources.
Water towers merit particular attention. They are among the most recognizable and exposed pieces of
water infrastructure in any community. Their elevated, open-air profile makes them uniquely
2835 Crescent Springs Road P.O. Box 18640 Erlanger, KY 41018
Phone (859) 578-9898 Fax (859) 578-7893

Page 2 of 3
vulnerable to drone-based surveillance, physical collision, or deliberate interference, and they cannot
be enclosed or relocated.
2. What characteristics should be considered in developing gating criteria?
The FAA should consider: (a) whether the facility has exposed operational technology, including
SCADA systems and programmable logic controllers; (b) whether the facility stores or uses hazardous
chemicals; (c) the population and critical customers served; and (d) whether the facility has been
designated as critical infrastructure under America's Water Infrastructure Act ("AWIA") Section 2013,
which requires community water systems serving 3,300 or more people to complete risk and resilience
assessments.
3. Should population served be a factor, and if so, what thresholds?
Yes. NKWD recommends a threshold of community water systems serving 50,000 or more people.
This would capture many of the systems already subject to AWIA risk and resilience assessment
requirements and whose disruption would have clear regional impact.
4. Should critical customers be a factor?
Yes. Water systems that serve hospitals, military installations, other critical infrastructure facilities
(power plants, for example), or large industrial users should be eligible regardless of total population
served. Disruption of water service to a hospital or military base would have consequences
disproportionate to the population count alone.
5. Should a history of unauthorized drone flights be a factor?
A documented history of unauthorized drone activity should strengthen an application, but it should
not be a prerequisite. Most water utilities, including NKWD, do not currently possess Remote ID
sensing or other detection capability sufficient to systematically identify and log drone incursions. The
absence of documented incidents reflects a detection gap, not the absence of risk. Making this a
threshold requirement would penalize utilities that lack the resources to deploy detection technology
before the rule even takes effect.
II. Additional Observations
Chemical Sector Overlap. Many water treatment plants store chlorine, sodium hypochlorite, or
other chemicals in quantities that may independently qualify under the Chemical Sector criteria at §
74.81. NKWD urges the FAA to ensure that § 74.96 independently captures water treatment facilities
so that utilities are not forced to rely on a cross-sector application to protect their core operations.
NKWD as a Part 107 Operator. NKWD operates drones for infrastructure inspection, including
tank inspections, leak detection, and aerial surveying. The proposed access provisions under § 74.250
(allowing Part 107 operators to transit UAFRs with Remote ID and advance notification) are
workable. NKWD requests that the FAA clarify that a facility operator conducting drone inspections
within its own UAFR is subject to streamlined, rather than duplicative, notification procedures. A
utility should not face the same notification burden for inspecting its own water tower that an outside
operator would face.

Page 3 of 3
Remote ID Sensing (§ 74.56(d)). NKWD supports the requirement for UAFR applicants to
maintain Remote ID sensing capability. However, many water utilities will need lead time to procure,
install, and integrate this technology. The FAA should allow phased implementation, permitting
utilities to satisfy this requirement within 12 to 18 months of UAFR approval rather than as a
precondition to applying.
Coordination with EPA. EPA is the designated Sector Risk Management Agency for the water and
wastewater sector. NKWD encourages the FAA to coordinate closely with EPA in developing the §
74.96 criteria, drawing on existing frameworks such as AWIA risk assessments, EPA's cybersecurity
guidance, and the vulnerability data EPA maintains for community water systems.
III. Conclusion
NKWD supports the proposed rule and the FAA's recognition of water and wastewater systems as
critical infrastructure warranting UAFR protection. The current gap in § 74.96 criteria presents both
a risk and an opportunity. NKWD urges the FAA to adopt criteria that reflect the unique
vulnerabilities of water infrastructure, particularly exposed assets like water towers and OT-dependent
treatment facilities, and to set population thresholds that capture the systems whose disruption would
cause regional harm. NKWD also supports AWWA's comments on behalf of the water sector.
NKWD appreciates the opportunity to participate in this rulemaking
Respectfully submitted,
Tom Edge
General Counsel & Director of Compliance, Communications and Regulatory Affairs

comments.commenter: Comment from Association of Air Medical Services
comments.comment_text:
ASSOCIATION OF AIR MEDICAL SERVICES
July 1, 2026
RE: Comments to the Notice of Proposed Rulemaking Designation-Restrict the Operation
of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility, 91 FR 24650, May 6, 2026
(“NPRM”), Docket No.: FAA–2026–4558; Notice No. 26–03.
Dear Administrator Bedford:
On May 6, 2026, the FAA issued in the Federal Register (91 FR 24650) Notice No. 26-03:
Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) Designation-Restrict the Operation of
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility, Docket No. FAA–2026–
4558. Pursuant to Section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety and Security Act (FESSA) of
2016, the NPRM establishes a framework for restricting drone (UAS) operations around
critical infrastructure and select healthcare facilities. The proposed rule
directly impacts the low-altitude airspace where helicopter air ambulances (HAA)
operate every day and provides an opportunity to request an unmanned aircraft flight
restriction (UAFR) for certain fixed site facilities.
The Association of Air Medical Services (AAMS) is a non-profit 501c (6) international trade
association headquartered in the Washington, DC area. Established in 1980, AAMS has
long championed air medical and critical care ground transport and its providers who
serve their communities worldwide. In the US alone, we represent over 90% of the air
medical programs. We hold a shared goal with our members who seek to deliver high
quality, safe, and effective medical care and medical transportation for every patient in
need.
I would first like to thank the FAA for proactively advancing the effort to manage the risk
associated with an increasingly congested airspace. AAMS applauds the FAA’s goal to
improve safety as vertical aviation evolves. While AAMS welcomes this NPRM as a step
forward, this proposed rule does not adequately address a full understanding of the
realities of air medical operations or their hospital and commercial partners who depend
on UAS/UAV as a means to advance healthcare.
In reviewing the NPRM, AAMS obtained feedback from its members and key
stakeholders; these are the companies and their employees who provide 24 hour/day
emergency HAA services, every day, for patients with critical and time-sensitive injuries
and illnesses. Our members recognize and acknowledge the need to share the airspace
safely while allowing for the advancement of UAS/UAV operations. Our members also
represent hospitals and health systems who are utilizing or exploring the use of UAS/UAV
operations to support their missions. These efforts include the use of UAS/UAV to deliver
blood and other critical supplies to and within their hospital systems. It is critical that
the FAA recognize that UAS/UAVs and HAA share the same low-level airspace, and both
may be necessary to save a life. We are aligned in our goal to de-conflict this airspace
and minimize risk.
AAMS respectfully requests that the FAA give full and thoughtful consideration to our
comments, comments provided by the Air Medical Operators Association (AMOA) and
Vertical Aviation International (VAI), and comments provided by the air medical services

and hospitals that provide life-saving and life-changing care to patients on what is likely
their worst day.
OBSERVATIONS
AAMS solicited feedback from its members regarding the NPRM. Our members
emphasized the following observations:
• The NPRM is an important move in the right direction and a welcome step forward in
addressing UAS operations as required by the 2016 FESSA.
• AAMS acknowledges the obligations of the FAA to balance matters of access to the
National Air Space (NAS) and the safety of critical infrastructure in the outlined
sectors.
• The intent to protect critical infrastructure, as defined in the regulation, is an
important consideration.
• The definition of critical infrastructure in the NPRM addresses
several important areas, including Healthcare and the Public Health Sector.
• The NPRM narrowly and inaccurately defines the included fixed facilities within the
Healthcare sector as only Adult and Pediatric Level I Trauma Centers.
• Hospitals and health care facilities are likely to be adopters of UAS technology and
UAS services moving forward. The rules should promote harmony between safe HAA
operations and UAS adoption.
AREAS OF CONCERN
The feedback from AAMS Members regarding the NPRM highlights the following areas
of concern:
• Take off and landing (TOFL) represent the most complicated aspect of HAA
operations. The NPRM does recognize the risk posed by UAS operations during
these critical phases of flight. However, HAA routinely carry out TOFL operations in
rural locations, at rural and community hospital helipads and emergency landing
zones as well as urban hospital helipads.
• The NPRM does not adequately take into account the HAA operating profile and the
realities of the health care delivery system, especially in rural areas.
• The NPRM does not take into account the impact of UAS operations on the most
common points of patient pick-up. These pick-up points are smaller community
hospitals across the US and constitute more than 50% of HAA transports.
• Many HAA operations serve as a critical link between rural communities and
tertiary/quaternary care capabilities that often exist in more urban areas.
• The designation of Level I Trauma Centers recognizes the importance of these
facilities within the health care delivery system; however, there are additional types
of tertiary/quaternary care sites that are excluded. These include designated Stroke
Centers, STEMI (i.e., acute cardiac) Centers, Burn Centers, etc.
• The NPRM does not recognize air medical helipads or emergency landing zones as a
category of critical infrastructure. These helipads and helistops are critical to HAA
operations and need to be protected.

AAMS RECOMMENDATIONS
Based on feedback from AAMS members and key stakeholders representing the HAA
community, AAMS respectfully requests that the FAA take the following actions:
• The FAA should convene a group of key stakeholders to evaluate the impact of
UAS/UAV operations, specific to the effects on HAA operations.
• This group should provide a forum for UAS/UAV services (and major users thereof),
healthcare facilities and hospitals, UAS/UAV healthcare delivery (blood products,
supplies, etc) SMEs, and HAA services/operators to fully consider the scope of
operations and the interactions between UAS/UAV and HAA.
• This group should consider best practices that can be practicably implemented to
mitigate the potential for conflict between UAS/UAV and HAA.
• This group should consider the existing and near-future technologies that are (or will
be) available to enhance deconfliction between UAS/UAV and HAA.
• This group should provide consensus recommendations for the FAA to incorporate as
part of the NPRM.
• The NPRM should be revised to amend regulations such that all inpatient hospitals
and HAA helipads are considered as Critical Infrastructure under the regulations.
• The FAA needs to recognize the importance and critical nature of designated and
undesignated emergency landing zones which are used by HAA every day and revise
the NPRM to include UAFR at or around any HAA emergency landing zone,
designated or undesignated.
AAMS fully supports efforts by the FAA to address ongoing concerns regarding UAV/UAS.
AAMS supports the intent of the NPRM and urges the FAA to continue meaningful action
that will promote dialogue and cooperation between UAS/UAV and HAA operators so that
these two services can co-exist while mitigating the likelihood of a future crash that will
destroy both vital human lives and expensive assets.
With great respect,
Jana Williams Mike Perkins, MBA-HCA, EMT-P, CMTE
President & CEO Board Chair
Association of Air Medical Services COO, MedFlight/MedCare Ambulance
jwilliams@aams.org mperkins@medflight.com

Drone use in and around any LNG plant should be prohibited.  Currently there are no restrictions or prohibitions for members of the public or private operators penetrating the immediate proximate air space of an LNG facility.  An LNG facility is considered Critical Energy Infrastructure (CEI).  Operating non intrinsically safe devices in and around a plant that processes natural gas is highly discouraged.  Current rules in 49CFR193 strictly prohibit electric devices that are non-intrinsically safe within certain classified areas of an LNG plant. In addition, bad actors can cause disruptions or upset processes with targeted or accidental intrusions in the LNG air space.  Its highly recommended the use of private drones be prohibited in, around and above an LNG facility.  The exception would be a purposely hired commercial drone that is intended to be used within the LNG facility by the owner/operator of the facility.

Respectfully, I OPPOSE the implementation of any rule that would allow some U.S. animal agriculture facilities to request restrictions on drone photography, as this would limit a valuable tool for documenting animal welfare, environmental issues and food production. If anything, what is happening in our food system needs more transparency, not less. This is an issue of animal welfare, public health and the health of our environment.

Thank you for considering my feedback.

I wanted to express my concern about any limitations of drone footage in the airspace  near/above agricultural operations. it's in the public's interest to have as much transparency as possible into farming practices in the US

DRONE DEFENSE PROTOCOL
Jansen Griggs · dronedefenseprotocol.com
Public Comment on Proposed Rule
Designation to Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
Docket FAA-2026-4558
Submitted via regulations.gov
Commenter Jansen Griggs, author of The Drone Defense Protocol
Date July 1, 2026
To the Federal Aviation Administration:
I submit this comment in support of the proposed Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restriction (UAFR) framework and to
identify one gap in the protective security requirements under proposed § 74.56 that, if addressed, would materially
improve the rule's effectiveness against the threats it is designed to counter.
My background is roughly fifteen years in unmanned aircraft and counter-unmanned aircraft operations across
critical infrastructure, nuclear security, and public safety environments, including counter-UAS program leadership
at a United States Department of Energy national laboratory. I am the author of The Drone Defense Protocol, an
operational framework for organizational airspace security. I comment here as an individual practitioner, not on
behalf of any agency or employer.
1. Summary of position
The four protective security requirements in § 74.56 establish a sound physical and technical baseline. However, the
framework as written does not address the human observation layer, which is the primary and often the only means
of detecting the non-cooperative drone activity that motivates most UAFR applications. I recommend the FAA
recognize documented workforce observation and standardized reporting competence as a qualifying component of
the § 74.56(3) security monitoring requirement.
2. The Remote ID baseline does not detect the threat the rule targets
Proposed § 74.56(4) requires Remote ID receiving capability. By design, that capability detects only operators who
broadcast Remote ID, which is to say, cooperative and law-abiding operators. The threats that lead a facility to seek
a UAFR are the opposite population: contraband delivery over correctional facilities, surveillance and
reconnaissance of energy and chemical sites, and hostile actors conducting pre-attack observation. These operators
will not broadcast Remote ID. For every non-cooperative contact, the facility's personnel are the primary sensor, and
frequently the only one.
This is not a theoretical concern. A January 2026 Department of Defense guide on the physical protection of critical
infrastructure states directly that training personnel to observe activity, not only aircraft, is critical to countering the
small unmanned aircraft threat. The human observation layer is already recognized at the federal level as an
essential element of airspace defense.
3. The rule references personnel and monitoring but not workforce competence
Sections § 74.56(2) and § 74.56(3) require designated security personnel and security monitoring such as cameras or
patrols. Both requirements speak to the existence of personnel and equipment, but neither addresses whether the
workforce is prepared to recognize, describe, and report anomalous aerial activity in a consistent and actionable
form. A facility can satisfy the literal text of these requirements while its personnel have no shared method for
observing or reporting drone activity, which means inconsistent descriptions, duplicate or missed reports, and
patterns that remain invisible because the underlying data is unstructured.
The gap is compounded by the rule's boundary limits. Because a UAFR's horizontal boundary cannot extend beyond
the applicant's property line, a contact approaching from a stand-off position outside that line is not within the
restricted airspace until it crosses. Trained observers distributed across a facility are what provide early warning of
an approaching contact before it enters the protected zone. Equipment fixed at the perimeter cannot substitute for

that distributed human awareness.
4. Recommendation
The FAA has invited comment on whether additional procedural or technological requirements should apply within
the UAFR framework. The recommendations below are offered directly in that spirit, as low-cost procedural
measures that strengthen the § 74.56(3) security monitoring element. I respectfully recommend the FAA take the
following steps:
(a) Recognize documented workforce observation and standardized reporting training as a qualifying or
complementary means of demonstrating the § 74.56(3) security monitoring requirement. A facility that can show its
personnel are trained to a consistent observation and reporting standard is demonstrably better positioned to
maintain awareness of activity at and around the site than one relying on fixed equipment alone.
(b) For Special UAFRs covering the highest-sensitivity federal and federally sponsored sites, consider requiring
applicants to demonstrate a standardized internal process by which personnel observe and report aerial activity,
including a defined set of observable reporting elements and a designated reporting pathway.
(c) Encourage, through advisory guidance rather than mandate, the adoption of a common, sector-agnostic
observation and reporting vocabulary so that reports from facilities across sectors can be correlated by the FAA,
DHS, DOJ, and law enforcement partners. Consistency of reporting language is what turns isolated sightings into
recognizable patterns.
These measures impose minimal cost. Workforce observation training is among the least expensive protective
measures available to a facility, and it strengthens precisely the detection capability that the technical baseline
leaves unaddressed. Recognizing it within § 74.56 would align the rule with the operational reality that people, not
only equipment, detect the non-cooperative drone threat.
I appreciate the opportunity to comment and welcome any follow-up from FAA staff.
Respectfully submitted,
Jansen Griggs
Author, The Drone Defense Protocol · dronedefenseprotocol.com

American Horse Council
5th Floor, Suite 4110

The American Horse Council (AHC) is grateful for the opportunity to comment on Docket No. FAA-2026-4558
Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
The American Horse Council (AHC) is grateful for the opportunity to comment on Docket No. FAA-2026-4558,
Designation—Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility.
The AHC supports the proposed rule and encourages the Federal Aviation Administration to ensure that
agricultural operations, including equine facilities, are eligible to seek restrictions on unmanned aircraft
operations when they pose a safety risk.
Equine facilities are unique in that they house large animals that are prey species with a strong and instinctive
flight response. Low-flying or unexpected drone activity can startle horses, causing them to panic, run through
fencing, collide with obstacles, injure handlers, or create dangerous situations for riders and spectators.
These risks are especially significant at breeding farms, training centers, boarding facilities, veterinary
hospitals, racetracks, competition venues, and equine-assisted service programs where horses, staff, and
members of the public may be present in close proximity.
Unlike many other agricultural operations, equine facilities frequently host events, lessons, veterinary
procedures, and youth activities where sudden disturbances can create immediate safety concerns for both
animals and people. Drone activity can also interfere with routine animal care and management practices,
resulting in unnecessary stress and welfare concerns.
The AHC encourages the FAA to explicitly recognize livestock and animal safety as considerations
when evaluating requests for unmanned aircraft flight restrictions and to ensure that equine facilities
are eligible to apply for such protections when drone operations present demonstrated safety hazards.
Sincerely,
Julie Broadway
Julie M. Broadway, CAE®
President - American Horse Council & American Horse Council Foundation
jbroadway@horsecouncil.org
The American Horse Council is a Washington D.C.-based advocacy organization representing
individual members and more than 130 equine organizations before Congress and the federal
regulatory agencies. AHC member organizations include breed registries, national and state equine
associations, state horse councils, recreational associations, and organizations representing
racetracks, equestrians, horse shows, veterinarians, farriers, rodeos, and other equine-related
stakeholders.

Terra Vigilis Security Group 1 July 2026
FAA Request for Public Comment
Notice of Proposed Rule Making (NPRM)
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03
RIN 2120-AL33
In response to the FAA, NPRM request for public commentary, Terra Vigilis Security Group
(TVSG) offers the following:
COMMENT
Recent increases in sUAS flights into/around and above nuclear power plants in the U.S.
accelerated the creation of FAA approved Special Security Instructions (SSI) airspace restrictions
(Title 14 of FAR Part 99.7) developed by TVSG in collaboration with security branches at nuclear
power plants across the United States as well as with federal partners including the Department of
Energy DOE (proponent) and the FAA.
As sUAS technology improves, drones are capable of flying faster, remaining airborne longer,
reaching higher altitudes, and carrying larger payloads. The recent elimination of sUAS
manufacturer based “geo-fencing” relative to restricted airspace, is an additional national security
issue for critical infrastructure including the energy and electrical transmission communities.
REFERENCES
1) Title 49, US Code Subtitle 1, Section 106, Subtitle VII-Aviation Programs
COMMENT BASIS
TVES has developed multiple DOE endorsed and FAA approved restricted airspace products
(Special UAFR-Continuous) which are currently in use in the U.S. including facilities in:
• California
• Arizona
• Michigan
• Minnesota
• Louisiana

PUBLIC COMMENTS: Terra Vigilis Security Group Strongly Supports the Following Elements
1) The continued application of “Special UAFR” protections for critical infrastructure
including nuclear power plants and related fixed site energy transmission facilities.
2) The development of “public depictions” via an FAA website.
3) Development and expansion of remote ID sensing capabilities for sUAS operations under
PARTS 107, 108.
4) Consideration for a technology based (monitoring) extension of “Lateral Boundaries” (as a
buffering zone* for sensitive energy sites). *Beyond existing property lines
5) Consideration for increasing “Vertical Boundaries” to 1200’ AGL above Special UAFR
designated sites.
6) Consideration for promotion of active sUAS monitoring of all nuclear power sites relative
to sUAS activity in coordination with appropriate law enforcement agencies which possess
reliable sUAS monitoring and detection systems. This would include Authority to Act in
appropriate situations.
7) Development of a standardized reporting format for sUAS incursion data which can spread
across all critical infrastructure entities to provide a robust baseline for additional protective
authorization mechanisms.
8) Support for Executive Order 13175-Consultation and Coordination with Indian Tribal
Governments. (Multiple nuclear facilities are adjacent to remote tribal lands.)
9) Development of a standardized “Security Appendix” to supplement operational security
plans (OPLANS) at nuclear and electrical transmission facilities and substations specific to
threats posed by sUAS platforms and operators. These sUAS security Appendices would
be designed to account for ongoing sUAS technological advances (speed, battery life etc.).
In addition, the sUAS Appendices should be suitable for easy integration with law
enforcement agencies authorized and capable of sUAS Authority to Act response working
in collaboration with critical infrastructure facilities including nuclear power plants that have
airspace restrictions.
9a) Security Appendix Elements
• Integration Format to existing OPLANs
• Training Module (Threat Assessment) for Identification of sUAS Types,
Capabilities and Intent
• Integration Format for Law Enforcement Agencies with Authority to Act
• Format for Logging sUAS Incursion Data suitable for the FAA Reporting Format

10) Development of an sUAS Identification Summary via:
• Radio Frequency (RF)
o RF Trilateration
o RF Triangulation
o RF Traffic Monitor/Analyzer
o sUAS Operational Drone not Transmitting Remote ID
• Radar Detection and Classification
• Combined Radar/RF based detection
• Authority to Act Capabilities
o Jamming
o RF-Spoofing
o Other
Respectfully Submitted for Review,
CAPT Tim Tyre, PhD, USN (ret)
Certified Flight Instructor (CFI)
Member FAASTEAM
Part 107 Commercial Pilot
Terra Vigilis Security Group
CEO, Emeritus
414-581-1670

comments.commenter: Comment from St Charles Parish Dept of Homeland Security & Emergency Preparedness
comments.comment_text: We at St. Charles Parish Dept. of Homeland Security & Emergency Preparedness support a process for operators and proprietors of certain fixed site facilities to request an unmanned aircraft flight restriction (UAFR). We have 25 critical infrastructures/key resource (CI/KR) including 2 large oil refineries, 9 electrical generation facilities, one being nuclear. Our jurisdiction is split down the middle by the Mississippi river, which is one of the largest tonnage ports in the world. We also have 5 class 1 railroads. Since November of 2020 we have had 341 reported incidents of UAS flights over CI/KR. We have located about 75% of the UAS pilots to determine intent. We have careless, clueless and criminal pilots. We educated most of them on flying over CI/KR and we arrested 14 on state 14:337 charge. Please put something in place to allow for better security above our CI/KR.

We're always told management is going to be out drone testing in the yard to try and find a reason to fire us. So when we see a drone at random times of the day we just assume that it's management. On this occasion I saw a drone flying all over our yard but hours later we were told that management wasn't drone testing that day. So yeah, I don't think drones should be out in our yards testing us. The fact is they're distracting to the yard crews. Us assuming that every drone in the yard is a management drone put us and the safety of the community at risk.The fact that we don't know the exact time and date these drones will be testing us puts us all at risk of bad actors trying to infiltrate us. On the day that I saw the drone that was not from the company, when we reported it, it was hours after we saw it.
I’m a Certified Flight Instructor (3216313), and as an aviation professional, I am opposed this unnecessary regulation meant to restrict our freedoms as pilots and drone operators.

Hello - I strongly disagree with this proposed rule. It is meant to restrict transparency at a time when we need it most. What do they have to hide? Bloody carcasses, animal abuse, and more? Unacceptable in my view. Thank you for your consideration.

This needs to be amended or not passed. I understand some of the benefits, but drones are exceptionally important for monitoring the legal behavior of facilities that do not have inspectors and do not have public access such as Concentrated Animal Feeding Operations. This takes away a tool for citizens to be able to document animal welfare, environmental issues, and food production.

I oppose this proposed rule because it would unnecessarily expand drone flight restrictions and limit lawful recreational and commercial operations without demonstrating that such broad restrictions are needed. Any airspace restrictions should be narrowly tailored, supported by clear evidence, temporary, and designed to minimize impacts on the public.

I disagree with this proposed rule. It unnecessarily expands federal control over public airspace while imposing new burdens on responsible drone operators who have no connection to security threats. The government should rely on targeted enforcement against bad actors instead of creating broad, permanent flight restrictions that diminish lawful access for everyone.

Educate. Advocate. Innovate.
Date: July 6, 2026
To: Michelle Ferritto, Office of Rulemaking
From: Grayson Middleton, Government Affairs Manager
Re: Docket No. FAA-2026-4558: Designation—Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in
Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
Dear Ms. Ferritto,
Delmarva Chicken Association (DCA) is the trade organization representing the meat chicken growers,
companies, and allied businesses on the Eastern Shore of Maryland, the Eastern Shore of Virginia, and
Delaware. We appreciate the opportunity to comment on the notice of proposed rulemaking (NPRM)
“Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility”
published in the Federal Register on May 6, 2026, by the Federal Aviation Administration (FAA).
DCA supports the NRPM which establishes a process for operators and proprietors of certain facilities to
request and maintain an unmanned aircraft flight restriction. Further, we believe that the
NRPM represents an important step toward protecting critical infrastructure, strengthening biosecurity,
and safeguarding the nation's food supply while allowing for the continued responsible use of
unmanned aircraft systems.
Drones have become valuable tools for agriculture. Producers routinely use
unmanned aircraft to monitor crops, inspect infrastructure, assess livestock, and improve operational
efficiency. However, like many emerging technologies, drones can also be misused. As drone
technology has become more affordable and accessible, animal rights activist organizations and other
groups have increasingly incorporated drones into campaigns targeting farms and food production
facilities. These operations often involve capturing aerial footage for so-called investigations,
documentaries, media campaigns, or coordinated activist efforts designed to generate public attention
rather than provide complete context.
The concern extends well beyond privacy. Low-altitude drone activity around livestock and poultry
operations can cause unnecessary stress for animals, disrupt normal farm operations, and raise
legitimate biosecurity concerns, just to name a few. Aircraft moving between multiple agricultural
sites have the potential to contribute to disease transmission risks if not operated responsibly. During a
time when the United States continues to face animal disease threats including Highly Pathogenic Avian
Influenza (HPAI) and New World Screwworm (NWS), we believe that strengthening agricultural
biosecurity and reducing unnecessary aerial activity around sensitive livestock, poultry, and food
facilities is a reasonable precaution.
The NPRM also supports broader food security objectives. America's livestock and poultry farms, along
with meat, poultry and food processing facilities are essential components of the nation's critical
infrastructure, employing hundreds of thousands of dedicated workers and providing protein to millions
of people around the world, and should be facilities considered for a UAFR. Disruptions caused by
16686 County Seat Highway | Georgetown, DE 19947 | 302-856-9037 | www.dcachicken.com |

Educate. Advocate. Innovate.
unauthorized surveillance, repeated drone activity, or security incidents can have consequences that
extend beyond individual farms or food producing facilities and may negatively impact consumer
confidence, supply chains, and the resiliency and safety of the U.S. food system.
Further, the unauthorized use of unmanned aircraft could be used to unlawfully gain
proprietary information on new agricultural innovations or even target the safety of the food supply,
from either domestic or foreign perpetrators. Providing operators of eligible facilities with a clear
process to request unmanned aircraft flight restrictions is a practical way to help reduce these risks
while preserving legitimate commercial, governmental, and authorized drone operations.
The poultry industry has monitored activist tactics for decades and has observed a growing use of
drones alongside other activities such as surveillance, coordinated campaigns, and, in some cases,
trespassing. While the vast majority of drone operators act responsibly and lawfully, the increasing use
of drones by groups targeting agricultural operations underscores the need for a clear regulatory
framework that enables vulnerable facilities to request additional protections when justified.
For these reasons, the Delmarva Chicken Association supports the FAA's NPRM and encourages
the Agency to expedite finalization. Strengthening protections around eligible facilities will improve
agricultural security, support biosecurity preparedness, protect critical food production infrastructure,
and reinforce the resilience of the nation's food system while maintaining responsible access to the
benefits of drone technology.
Thank you in advance for your consideration. Should you have any additional questions, please do not
hesitate to contact me at middleton@dcachicken.com or 410-490-3329.
Sincerely,
Grayson S. Middleton

I. Introduction and Company Background
Red Cat Holdings, Inc. submits these comments in response to the Federal Aviation
Administration’s Notice of Proposed Rulemaking, Designation-Restrict the Operation of
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility, Docket No. FAA-2026-4558.
Red Cat, through its subsidiaries Teal Drones and FlightWave Aerospace, is a U.S. manufacturer
of unmanned aircraft systems supporting military, government, public safety, infrastructure,
and commercial operations. Red Cat’s work sits at the intersection of aviation safety, defense
readiness, UAS manufacturing, operational testing, regulatory compliance, and secure
integration of unmanned aircraft into the National Airspace System.

Red Cat supports the FAA’s effort to implement Section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and
Security Act of 2016 by establishing a process for facility-specific Unmanned Aircraft Flight
Restrictions (“UAFRs”). There are real risks associated with careless, unauthorized, or malicious
unmanned aircraft operations near sensitive fixed-site facilities. Defense industrial base
facilities, critical infrastructure, energy facilities, transportation assets, chemical facilities,
correctional facilities, and other sensitive locations may have legitimate safety or security
needs.
At the same time, unmanned aircraft are not only a risk to be managed. They are also a tool
used to protect, inspect, test, support, and maintain the same facilities this rule is intended to
protect. UAS operations support infrastructure inspection, emergency response, security
awareness, damage assessment, public safety, construction oversight, training, testing, and
government contract execution. The final rule should preserve those lawful and beneficial uses
while addressing the risks created by unauthorized or unsafe operations.
Red Cat therefore supports a narrowly tailored UAFR framework for genuinely sensitive fixed-
site facilities, provided that the final rule preserves FAA authority over the National Airspace
System, avoids creating private control over low-altitude airspace, maintains predictable access
for lawful and vetted UAS operators, and accounts for the operational realities of defense
contractors, public agencies, infrastructure operators, and facility-authorized UAS service
providers.
II. Executive Summary
Red Cat supports the FAA’s objective of protecting national security, homeland security,
aviation safety, people and property, and critical infrastructure through an appropriate fixed-
site UAS restriction framework. Red Cat does not oppose the creation of UAFRs as a general
matter.
The concern is not the existence of a facility-specific restriction process. The concern is how
that process is implemented. If the final rule is too broad, too slow, too dependent on site-by-
site permission, or too rigid in its access conditions, it could unintentionally interfere with
lawful UAS operations that improve safety and security. That includes public safety missions,
infrastructure inspection, defense contractor activity, testing, training, customer support,
facility-requested drone services, and government-support operations.
Red Cat respectfully recommends that the FAA revise the final rule to:
1. Preserve FAA control over all UAFR designation, access, modification, renewal,
suspension, and cancellation decisions.
