2. Make clear that a UAFR does not grant a fixed-site facility operator private control over
airspace.
3. Confirm that site managers may not approve, deny, delay, condition, or prohibit UAS
operations otherwise allowed by the FAA.
4. Clarify that allowed operations within standard UAFRs may include legitimate
operational activity, not only the shortest possible transit through the restricted area.
5. Establish practical access pathways for defense contractors, public aircraft operations,
government-support contractors, infrastructure inspectors, emergency responders, and
facility-authorized UAS operations.
6. Treat Remote ID as one situational-awareness and accountability tool, not as a complete
security solution or an absolute access condition in all circumstances.
7. Protect sensitive operational information through standardized, secure, FAA-managed
notification procedures rather than inconsistent site-by-site contact processes.
8. Require UAFR lateral boundaries, altitude ceilings, and activation periods to be narrowly
tailored to the demonstrated facility risk.
9. Create clear procedures for Special UAFR access, including expedited access for
operators supporting the using agency or conducting necessary public safety, defense,
emergency, infrastructure, or facility-support work.
10. Avoid unintended effects on lawful UAS operations that directly improve facility safety,
security, maintenance, and resilience.
III. Red Cat Supports a Narrowly Tailored UAFR Framework for
Genuinely Sensitive Fixed-Site Facilities
Red Cat supports the FAA’s objective of creating a structured process for the designation of
UAFRs around certain fixed-site facilities where a demonstrated safety or security need exists.
Some facilities present real risk concerns due to national security, homeland security, critical
infrastructure, hazardous materials, public safety, transportation operations, defense
production, or other factors.
A formal FAA-managed process is preferable to ad hoc, inconsistent, or locally improvised
restrictions. A clear federal framework gives applicants, operators, public agencies, and industry
participants better predictability than a patchwork of informal requests, local ordinances,
private security demands, or uncoordinated facility-level expectations.
Red Cat also supports the FAA’s distinction between standard UAFRs and Special UAFRs. A
standard UAFR may be appropriate for facilities with ongoing safety or security concerns. A
Special UAFR may be appropriate when elevated threat conditions, sensitive federal security
needs, or agency-specific concerns justify additional limitations.

The final rule should make clear, however, that eligibility to apply for a UAFR should not
automatically result in approval. A restriction should be based on a demonstrated need. The
applicant should be required to explain why a restriction is necessary, why the requested
boundary is appropriate, why the requested altitude ceiling is necessary, and why the
requested activation duration is justified.
The goal should be a targeted safety and security tool, not a routine method of excluding
unmanned aircraft from broad areas simply because a facility qualifies to submit an application.
Recommendation: The FAA should retain a structured UAFR process, but the final rule should
emphasize that UAFRs are targeted restrictions based on a demonstrated facility-specific safety
or security need. The FAA should require each applicant to justify the scope and duration of the
requested restriction and should approve only the restriction necessary to address the
identified risk.
IV. UAFRs Must Not Become De Facto Private Airspace Control
The FAA should preserve the principle that airspace access decisions remain under federal
authority. Red Cat strongly supports language and implementation procedures making clear
that UAFR designation, operation, and access remain subject to FAA control and are not
delegated to private site managers.
A fixed-site facility operator may have a legitimate need for awareness of UAS activity near its
facility. That does not mean the facility operator should receive practical veto authority over
lawful UAS operations. Site managers may receive notice where required, but they should not
be able to approve, deny, delay, condition, or interfere with operations that are otherwise
permitted under FAA rules or FAA authorization.
This distinction matters. If a UAFR becomes functionally equivalent to private control over low-
altitude airspace, the rule could create a fragmented and unpredictable operating environment.
Public agencies, commercial operators, defense contractors, infrastructure inspectors, and
facility-authorized service providers would face uncertainty regarding who controls access,
what standards apply, and whether FAA-authorized operations can proceed.
That uncertainty would undermine the FAA’s role as the national airspace regulator. It would
also risk inconsistent outcomes between similar facilities, geographic areas, and operators.
Recommendation: The final rule should state clearly that a UAFR does not confer property
rights in airspace, does not delegate FAA airspace authority to a site manager, and does not
allow a facility operator to prohibit operations otherwise allowed by the FAA. Site manager

notification should be for situational awareness unless the FAA separately establishes a specific,
limited access-control role.
V. Allowed Operations Must Include Legitimate Work Within a
UAFR, Not Only Transit
The proposed rule appears to allow certain unmanned aircraft operations inside standard
UAFRs if the operator meets specified conditions, including Remote ID broadcast, operation
under an applicable regulatory framework, and movement through the UAFR in the shortest
amount of time practicable.
Red Cat supports preserving access for lawful operators. However, the “shortest amount of
time practicable” concept may be too narrow if interpreted to allow only transit through a
UAFR. Many legitimate UAS operations require remaining within or near a protected facility for
a defined operational purpose.
Examples include:
1. Facility-requested inspection, mapping, security, or maintenance support.
2. Emergency response, disaster assessment, fire response, law enforcement, or public
safety operations.
3. Infrastructure inspection involving power, water, communications, transportation, port,
rail, or industrial systems.
4. Defense contractor testing, training, demonstration, maintenance, integration, or
customer support.
5. Public aircraft operations or operations conducted in support of a government agency.
6. Damage assessment after severe weather, industrial incidents, cyber-physical events, or
other disruptions.
7. Construction progress documentation or capital project monitoring for critical
infrastructure.
These operations may directly support the safety and security of the facilities the UAFR is
designed to protect. A rule that permits only rapid transit may unintentionally block facility-
authorized or government-supported activity that improves situational awareness, reduces
personnel risk, and improves operational resilience.
Recommendation: The FAA should revise the final rule to distinguish between transient passage
through a UAFR and authorized operational activity within a UAFR. Lawful UAS operations
should be permitted within a standard UAFR when conducted by an eligible operator under

FAA-recognized conditions, including operations requested by the facility, conducted by or for a

VI. Defense Industrial Base and UAS Manufacturing
Operations Require Clear Treatment
Red Cat supports the inclusion of Defense Industrial Base (“DIB”) facilities within the proposed
eligibility framework where the applicant can demonstrate a specific safety, national security,
homeland security, or facility protection need. DIB facilities may involve sensitive design,
production, integration, testing, maintenance, storage, and delivery of systems or components
supporting national defense.
The final rule should also recognize that DIB facilities and defense contractors may need to
operate UAS within or near protected areas. UAS manufacturers, integrators, and government-
support contractors may need to conduct production acceptance testing, payload integration,
maintenance check flights, customer demonstrations, training, validation flights, field support,
incident response support, and government-directed operational support. These activities may
occur at or near defense facilities, test sites, manufacturing locations, customer facilities, or
temporary support locations.
The FAA should avoid placing defense contractors in the position of supporting national security
missions while lacking a clear pathway to conduct necessary UAS operations within or near
facilities protected under the rule. The rule should also account for the reality that many
government missions are carried out with substantial contractor support. A framework that
recognizes agency operations but does not clearly address contractor-supported missions
would create avoidable operational friction.
Recommendation: The FAA should clarify that DIB-related UAFRs must include a pathway for
UAS operations conducted by defense contractors, subcontractors, and government-support
operators when those operations are necessary for testing, production, integration,
demonstration, training, maintenance, customer support, facility security, or performance of a
government contract. The FAA should also clarify that UAS and counter-UAS manufacturers
may qualify as DIB facilities when they meet the applicable criteria and support mission-critical
defense functions.

VII. Remote ID Should Be Treated as a Situational-Awareness
Tool, Not a Complete Security Solution
Remote ID can be a useful tool for accountability, airspace awareness, and differentiation
between cooperative and non-cooperative aircraft. Red Cat supports the role of Remote ID in
the broader UAS regulatory framework.
Remote ID should not, however, be treated as a complete security solution. Operators
intending to conduct malicious, reckless, or unauthorized activity may disable, spoof, obscure,
or ignore Remote ID requirements. At the same time, lawful operators supporting defense,
public safety, research, testing, security-sensitive, or government-directed missions may have
legitimate operational or security reasons why public broadcast of certain information is
inappropriate.
The proposed UAFR framework should account for both realities. Remote ID can help identify
cooperative operators. It does not, by itself, prevent unlawful activity or provide a complete
protective security posture for sensitive facilities. Facility protection may require layered
detection, reporting, coordination, access procedures, law enforcement response, and other
security measures outside the scope of Remote ID.
This issue is especially important for defense-related UAS operations, research and
development flights, electronic warfare-resilient systems, GPS-denied or communications-
degraded environments, law enforcement support, and sensitive government-directed activity.
In those contexts, a rigid Remote ID requirement could conflict with operational security or
mission requirements.
Recommendation: The FAA should retain the Administrator’s ability to authorize operations
without Remote ID where appropriate. The final rule should identify acceptable alternative
compliance pathways, such as FAA authorization, using-agency authorization, secure
notification to an appropriate government point of contact, facility-authorized security
operations, protected mission profiles, or other methods that preserve accountability without
requiring public disclosure of sensitive operational information.
VIII. Notification Procedures Should Be Standardized, Secure,
and Limited to Necessary Information
Notification procedures should provide facility awareness without creating inconsistent access
barriers or unnecessary disclosure of sensitive operational details. Red Cat supports reasonable

notice to a protected facility when lawful UAS operations will occur within a UAFR, but the
process must be standardized, secure, and limited to what is actually needed.
A site-by-site notification system would create avoidable operational burden. Operators may
not know the correct facility contact, contacts may change, response expectations may differ,
and facility personnel may misunderstand notice as a request for permission. This could lead to
inconsistent outcomes and delays, especially for operators conducting time-sensitive public
safety, infrastructure, defense, or facility-support work.
Notification also raises security and privacy concerns. Certain operators may need to protect
details regarding mission purpose, aircraft configuration, payload, personnel, timing, customer
identity, or government support activity. Requiring broad disclosure of operational details
directly to private site managers could create risks unrelated to aviation safety.
Recommendation: The FAA should establish a centralized, standardized digital notification
process through which operators can provide required notice. Site managers should receive
only the information necessary for situational awareness and should not be given discretion to
approve, deny, or delay operations. The FAA should also allow recurring, batch, or standing
notifications for repeated operations, including recurring inspections, facility-contracted UAS
services, public agency operations, and routine defense contractor support.
Emergency operations should be allowed with notice as soon as practicable, including after the
operation when advance notice is not feasible.
IX. UAFR Boundaries, Altitude Ceilings, and Activation Periods
Must Be Narrowly Tailored
Red Cat supports the FAA’s proposal to evaluate UAFR boundaries, altitude ceilings, and
activation periods as part of the designation process. These elements are critical to ensuring
that UAFRs remain proportional to the facility risk and do not unnecessarily restrict lawful UAS
operations.
A facility’s entire property boundary should not automatically become the UAFR boundary
where a smaller security-relevant area would address the demonstrated risk. The maximum
altitude ceiling should not become the default where a lower altitude would be sufficient.
Continuous activation should not be approved where part-time activation would reasonably
address the operational concern.

Overbroad boundaries or activation periods could interfere with lawful operations in adjacent
areas, including public rights-of-way, construction sites, transportation corridors, utility
corridors, waterways, ports, industrial areas, emergency response zones, or nearby properties
with separate operational needs. These concerns are especially important in urban, industrial,
maritime, rail, energy, and utility environments where eligible facilities may be located near
other lawful UAS use cases.
Recommendation: The FAA should require each applicant to justify the requested boundary,
altitude ceiling, and activation duration based on the specific facility risk. The FAA should retain
authority to approve a smaller boundary, lower ceiling, shorter activation period, part-time
activation, or additional access conditions where the requested restriction would unnecessarily
burden lawful UAS activity.
X. Special UAFRs Require Clear Access Procedures for
Government-Support and Time-Sensitive Operations
Special UAFRs may be necessary where a credible safety or security threat exists or where a
federal security or intelligence agency, the Department of Defense, the Department of Energy,
or the FAA determines that additional protection is required. Red Cat supports the availability
of a Special UAFR tool for truly elevated threat conditions.
Special UAFRs should include clear access procedures for operators supporting the using
agency, including defense contractors, subcontractors, public safety partners, infrastructure
operators, and other government-support entities. Many sensitive government operations are
executed through contractor-supported teams. A Special UAFR process that does not clearly
account for contractor-supported missions may delay or prevent necessary operations during
time-sensitive situations.
The FAA should also recognize that certain legitimate UAS operations may need to occur inside
or near a Special UAFR during the period of activation. These may include law enforcement
support, emergency response, fire response, infrastructure restoration, facility security, test
support, and government-directed activity.
Recommendation: The FAA should clarify that a using agency may authorize contractor-
supported UAS operations within a Special UAFR when those operations support the agency’s
mission. The FAA should also establish expedited procedures for non-using-agency operators
that have a legitimate operational need to access Special UAFR airspace, including public safety,
emergency response, critical infrastructure restoration, and facility-authorized operations.

XI. UAFRs Should Not Interfere With UAS Operations That
Improve Facility Safety and Security
UAS operations often reduce risk to people and property. They allow inspections in dangerous
or inaccessible areas, support security awareness, reduce the need for personnel to enter
hazardous environments, improve response after incidents, and provide timely information
during emergencies. For critical infrastructure and defense-related facilities, these capabilities
may be an important part of facility resilience.
The final rule should not treat all UAS activity near sensitive facilities as suspect. The risk profile
of an unidentified, non-cooperative drone near a sensitive facility is different from that of a
facility-authorized inspection aircraft, a public agency aircraft, a government contractor aircraft,
or a trained Part 107 operator conducting documented work under a known mission profile.
A well-designed UAFR framework should improve facility security by distinguishing between
unauthorized or unsafe operations and legitimate, accountable operations. If the process is too
restrictive, slow, or unclear, facilities may lose access to beneficial UAS capabilities that help
them manage the same risks the rule is intended to address.
Recommendation: The FAA should make clear that UAFRs are intended to restrict
unauthorized, unsafe, or security-threatening operations, not to prevent lawful UAS operations
that support the safety, security, inspection, maintenance, testing, or operational resilience of
the protected facility.
XII. The Final Rule Should Account for Existing FAA
Authorization Pathways and Future Part 108 Operations
The UAFR framework should be integrated with existing FAA authorization pathways and future
regulatory structures. Operators may conduct lawful UAS operations under Part 107, Part 91
public aircraft operations, Part 135, Part 137, waivers, certificates of authorization, exemptions,
or future Part 108 authorities. The final rule should not create unnecessary duplication or
conflict between UAFR access requirements and other FAA approvals.
For example, a public agency or contractor operating under a valid certificate of authorization
should not face unnecessary delay because a site manager notification process is unclear. A
Part 107 operator conducting facility-requested infrastructure inspection should have a
predictable pathway to comply. A future Part 108 operator should not be forced into a
Red Cat Holdings, Inc.
15 Ave. Muñoz Rivera, STE 2200
San Juan, Puerto Rico 00901
REDCAT.RED
Page 10 of 12

separate, inconsistent UAFR access process if the operation already meets FAA-recognized risk
controls.
Recommendation: The FAA should harmonize Part 74 access procedures with existing and
future FAA authorization pathways. Where an operator already holds an FAA authorization,
waiver, exemption, certificate, or future Part 108 approval that addresses the relevant
operational risk, the UAFR process should recognize that authorization and avoid duplicative
review except where a specific facility security concern requires additional conditions.
XIII. FAA Should Provide Transparent Procedures for Review,
Renewal, Modification, and Cancellation
UAFRs may have meaningful operational effects on surrounding airspace users. The FAA should
provide transparent procedures for designation, renewal, modification, and cancellation.
Applicants should be required to keep facility information current and to notify the FAA if the
basis for the restriction changes.
The final rule should also include a practical mechanism for affected operators, public agencies,
local governments, infrastructure entities, and other stakeholders to raise concerns regarding
overbroad or outdated restrictions. A UAFR that is no longer necessary, no longer proportional,
or broader than needed should be modified or cancelled.
Recommendation: The FAA should require periodic review of UAFRs and maintain clear
procedures for modification or cancellation when the restriction is no longer justified. The FAA
should also provide a process for affected stakeholders to request review of a UAFR where the
restriction creates unintended operational impacts.
XIV. Conclusion
Red Cat supports the FAA’s work to implement Section 2209 and establish a facility-specific
framework for protecting sensitive fixed-site facilities from unauthorized or unsafe unmanned
aircraft operations. A well-designed UAFR process can support national security, homeland
security, aviation safety, people and property, and critical infrastructure protection.
The final rule should protect sensitive facilities without unintentionally restricting the lawful
UAS operations that support those facilities, improve safety, and advance UAS integration. To
get that balance right, the FAA should preserve federal control over airspace decisions, prevent
private airspace vetoes, maintain predictable access for lawful and vetted operators, and

account for the realities of defense, public safety, infrastructure, testing, facility support, and

Red Cat respectfully requests that the FAA revise the final rule to:
1. Confirm that site managers do not have authority to approve, deny, delay, condition,
restrict, or prohibit UAS operations otherwise allowed by the FAA.
2. Clarify that allowed operations may include legitimate operational activity within a
UAFR, not only rapid transit through the area.
3. Establish access pathways for defense contractors, public aircraft operations,
government-support operators, infrastructure inspectors, emergency responders, and
facility-authorized operators.
4. Treat Remote ID as one situational-awareness tool rather than a complete security
solution or absolute access condition.
5. Require UAFR boundaries, altitude ceilings, and activation periods to be narrowly
tailored to the demonstrated facility risk.
6. Provide clear and expedited access procedures for Special UAFRs, including contractor-
supported missions for using agencies.
7. Use standardized, secure, FAA-managed notification procedures that protect sensitive
operational information while providing appropriate facility awareness.
8. Harmonize Part 74 access procedures with existing FAA approvals, waivers, exemptions,
certificates of authorization, and future Part 108 operations.
9. Provide transparent procedures for review, renewal, modification, and cancellation of
UAFRs.
Red Cat looks forward to continued engagement with the FAA as it develops a framework that
protects sensitive facilities while supporting the safe, secure, and scalable integration of trusted
UAS operations into the National Airspace System.

I am Part 107 certified and use my drone for real estate photography, which is an integral part of my business.
Scheduling Constraints
Real estate photographers like me often receive photography appointments with only one day’s notice. Because these appointments are time-sensitive, delays in approval can create significant scheduling challenges.
Impact of Approval Delays
A typical drone flight to capture the necessary photos of a subject property takes about 10-15 minutes. However, if approval is delayed, the appointment may need to be canceled or rescheduled, resulting in additional costs or lost revenue.

To Whom It May Concern,

Thank you for the opportunity to comment on the proposed rule establishing Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs).

THE FLYING LIZARD, LLC is a small, aviation-driven UAS company conducting commercial operations under 14 CFR Part 107. Our work supports construction, infrastructure, airports, engineering firms, and inspection activities. As a certificated operator, we have a direct interest in regulations that improve aviation safety while preserving the ability of legitimate operators to efficiently serve clients.

We support the FAA's objective of protecting critical infrastructure from malicious or unsafe UAS operations. Protecting facilities of national importance is a legitimate public interest, and we appreciate the FAA's effort to establish a consistent federal framework rather than allowing a patchwork of differing local restrictions.

Our primary concern is whether the proposed rule achieves its stated objective by meaningfully reducing malicious UAS activity, or whether it primarily increases procedural requirements for operators who are already committed to regulatory compliance.

Professional Part 107 operators already invest significant time and resources in complying with FAA regulations. We register our aircraft, maintain certification, conduct mission planning, comply with airspace requirements, operate with Remote ID where required, and accept responsibility for safe flight operations. The proposed notification and coordination requirements would be added primarily to this already compliant population.

By contrast, operators intending to conduct malicious, reckless, or otherwise unlawful activities are unlikely to be deterred by additional notification requirements or operational restrictions. Individuals who already intend to violate existing federal regulations may simply ignore the proposed requirements as well.

Accordingly, we respectfully ask the FAA to address the following operational question:
How does this proposal improve the detection, deterrence, or interdiction of malicious UAS operations, rather than primarily increasing procedural requirements for operators who are already committed to regulatory compliance?

From the perspective of a small commercial operator, this distinction is important.

Many legitimate Part 107 missions are time-sensitive. Construction progress documentation, infrastructure inspections, engineering support, and airport projects frequently require rapid scheduling and coordination among multiple stakeholders. Additional notification requirements may introduce delays and administrative complexity without providing a corresponding improvement in safety or security.

We also encourage the FAA to consider how facility operators will consistently administer these notification requirements.

For example:
What guidance will facility operators receive for evaluating requests?
How will consistency be maintained among facilities across the country?
What constitutes sufficient notice?
What operational expectations exist when an authorized operator is conducting work and an unidentified drone appears in the same area?

These questions affect not only operators but also the facility personnel responsible for implementing the rule.

We respectfully recommend that the FAA continue evaluating whether the proposed framework appropriately balances security objectives with the practical realities of commercial UAS operations. In particular, we encourage consideration of approaches that focus regulatory effort on improving the identification and response to unauthorized or malicious operations while minimizing unnecessary administrative burdens on operators who have already demonstrated a commitment to regulatory compliance.

THE FLYING LIZARD appreciates the FAA's willingness to solicit public input and welcomes efforts that strengthen both aviation safety and the responsible integration of unmanned aircraft into the National Airspace System.

I am submitting this comment as an individual airman. I hold Airline Transport Pilot and Flight Instructor certificates, and I operate under Part 107 also. In general I support the FAA creating a clear national process for Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions near fixed site facilities. This is better than having a lot of different confusing local rules or private restrictions. If a facility has a real safety or security need, there should be a formal FAA process to review it and publish the restriction clearly. But I truly believe UAFRs should be used carefully. Restricting airspace should be the exception, not the default. The final rule should protect truly sensitive facilities while still allowing safe, known, and useful UAS operations to continue.

Please, find the recomendations below:

- On operator credibility, I believe the operator should provide basic information before entering a Standard UAFR: name, contact information, remote pilot certificate number or other applicable certificate/authorization, aircraft registration, remote ID serial number, area of operation, maximum altitude, purpose of flight, and emergency contact information. I support the idea of a “whitelist” system for trusted recurring operators. For example, a utility company, railroad inspection contractor, public safety agency, or approved infrastructure inspection provider should be able to submit their information once, get approval and then use a simpler notification process for repeated operations. This would reduce workload for both operators and facilities. On notification, I recommend one FAA-managed notification system instead of different procedures for every facility. A UAS operator should be able to see the UAFR, understand the rules, submit the required information, and receive confirmation through one system. The process should be simple enough for small operators to use without needing a legal department or special software.

- I recommend that UAFR boundaries stay as narrow as practical. I support the FAA’s general idea that a Standard UAFR should normally stay within the property boundary of the facility. In many cases the restriction should be even smaller than the full property line. A large facility may only have one or two truly sensitive areas. Expanded boundaries beyond the property line should be allowed only in limited cases and only with strong justification. Examples might include explosive hazards, chemical release risk, prison contraband delivery concerns, tall structures, or national security facilities.

- I support using different application windows for different sectors. The FAA should decide which sectors go first based on real safety and security risk, not only on how many applications are expected.

- On Remote ID, I support requiring facilities that request a UAFR to have the ability to receive Remote ID broadcasts. If a facility wants the protection of a UAFR, it should also be able to distinguish a known, compliant operation from an unknown operation. However, Remote ID should be treated as one layer of safety, not the whole solution. Remote ID helps identify compliant operators, but it will not stop every careless or malicious operator.

- I do not recommend creating stronger Remote ID broadcast requirements for UAFR operations until the FAA has more real-world data on cost, reliability, and reception range. Adding special Remote ID requirements could increase cost and complexity for small operators. If additional requirements are added later, they should be phased in and based on actual performance data.

- On Remote ID data retention, I support limited retention, but not unlimited storage. A period of around 6 months seems reasonable for normal monitoring and investigation. Data should be kept longer only when connected to an active investigation or enforcement action. The FAA should clearly state who may access the data, for what purpose, and how privacy will be protected.

- On Special UAFRs, I support a stricter access process, but the process should still be clear. For non-government users, access should require permission from the using agency and approval through an FAA process. The FAA should publish clear instructions explaining who to contact, what information to provide, expected processing time, and whether emergency or urgent access is available.

- A 30-day comment period is reasonable for simple UAFRs, but complex or high-impact UAFRs should have a longer period, such as 45 or 60 days. A shorter period should be used only for urgent safety or security reasons.

Therefore, I support the proposed UAFR process, but I recommend that the final rule be narrow, clear, and practical. UAFRs should protect facilities with real safety or security needs, but they should be easy to find, easy to teach, easy to comply with, and flexible enough to allow safe and legitimate UAS operations.

We need to stop putting corporate interests above the rights of citizens. These companies operate without enough oversight as it is; it’s been proven that this drone photography is a necessary way to understand the impacts that these operators are having on our environment, how our food is being prepared. Why else would they want to remove this right? It can only lead to bad things getting worse. I hope we can put aside corporate interests and do what’s right here.

My biggest concern with your, proposed, new rules and regulations for creating UAFRs that you are potentially creating a more easily searchable and pre-selected database of specific sites that may be vulnerable to unmanned weaponized aircraft which may actually lead to unintended national security concerns. Recreational drone flight being allowed/tolerated is appreciated and should continue however some additional thought is recommended in the structure of UAFR creation as well as allowing UAFRs to capture or signal jam potential UA threats given the creation of a publicly accessible database of potentially vulnerable sites.

There should be an exemption for Chapter 61 Disabled Retired military holding Retired Military ID Cards that are not paid by Congress because they did not make it 20 years to be able to fly in the restricted air space to intercept hostile aircraft. These military members are ordained by Congress and under the Department of War's UCMJ and as such should be trusted to defend National important sites vital to the United States.
There are Congressionally funded Teams to defend such sites but these Retired military members should be given authority to defend such sites not with drones that fly horizontally but with proven in Ukraine drone interceptors that fly vertically for interception. Such drones should be marked with a red tail to signify their Rule Exemption for interception only.

The only Retired Military Force of the United States Military that is not deployable in a future battle vital for the future of the United States needs an exemption for a future battle vital for the survival of the United States. Make the Red Tails Happen. Congress wouldn't even fund it, it would just happen.
'Merica
Trained drone professionals should be able to document activities agricultural facilities. This right to inform the public should not be ignored or dismissed.

As a Part 107 operator , This would impact my job oppertunies. I support the intent of Section 2209 to protect sensitive facilities. However, I have significant concerns about the potential size, scope, and implementation of these restricted areas.

1. Restricted areas must not extend unreasonably far beyond the facility footprint
Many facilities that qualify under Section 2209 are located near neighborhoods, commercial buildings, HOAs, and industrial corridors where legitimate UAS operations occur daily.
If the FAA allows overly large or arbitrary radii, these restrictions will unintentionally block lawful operations that have nothing to do with the protected facility.

I strongly urge the FAA to adopt tight, facility‑bound perimeters rather than broad circular zones that spill into unrelated properties.

2. The rule must not create UAS‑only airspace discrimination
Manned aircraft are not prohibited from flying over these same facilities unless a TFR or FRZ exists.
Creating a new class of permanent, UAS‑only restricted airspace—without equivalent manned‑aircraft limitations—creates an inconsistent regulatory environment.

If a facility is sensitive enough to warrant a federally enforced no‑fly zone, that restriction should apply consistently across all aircraft categories, not solely unmanned systems.

3. Guardrails are needed to prevent misuse or over‑designation
While the intent of Section 2209 is legitimate, history shows that airspace restrictions can be used too broadly or for purposes unrelated to safety or security.
To maintain public trust, the FAA should require:

Clear justification for each requested restricted area

Transparent criteria for approval

Public visibility into the boundaries and rationale

Periodic review to ensure the restriction remains necessary

This prevents the rule from becoming a tool for excessive secrecy or for shielding non‑security‑related activities from public observation.

4. Authorization pathways must be predictable and standardized
Many legitimate UAS operations occur near infrastructure:
roof inspections, solar documentation, insurance assessments, thermal imaging, and construction monitoring.
Operators need a clear, consistent process to obtain authorization from the facility when appropriate.

Without a standardized workflow, operators and clients will face unnecessary delays and confusion.

5. Mapping must be accurate, accessible, and integrated into existing UAS tools
Section 2209 restricted areas must appear in:

FAA UAS Data Delivery System

B4UFly

LAANC provider maps

UAS Facility Maps

Operators cannot comply with restrictions they cannot see.

Summary
I support protecting sensitive facilities, but the FAA must ensure that Section 2209:

Uses narrow, facility‑specific boundaries

Does not discriminate against unmanned aircraft

Includes safeguards against overreach

Provides predictable authorization pathways

Integrates cleanly into existing UAS mapping tools

This rule should enhance safety—not create unnecessary barriers for legitimate, compliant UAS operations.

To Whom It May Concern,

My name is Tony Marino, MBA, and I am an FAA Part 107 Certified Remote Pilot and owner of Aerial Northwest, a commercial drone photography and videography business serving clients throughout Oregon. I appreciate the opportunity to comment on this proposed rule.

First, I fully support the FAA's mission to protect public safety, national security, and critical infrastructure. As a professional commercial drone operator, I understand the importance of responsible flight operations and recognize that certain facilities may require additional security measures.

However, I am concerned that the proposed rule could unintentionally create significant burdens for thousands of legitimate small businesses that rely on lawful access to the National Airspace System.

As written, the proposal could substantially increase the number of permanent or long-term restricted areas across the United States. Many commercial drone missions occur near infrastructure such as railroads, electrical substations, water treatment facilities, communications sites, transportation corridors, industrial properties, and commercial developments. Even when these locations present no operational safety concern, new restrictions could prevent otherwise lawful Part 107 operations.

For small businesses like mine, these restrictions represent more than an inconvenience. Every new restricted area requires additional research, flight planning, client communication, scheduling adjustments, and potentially the loss of projects altogether. Unlike larger corporations, independent drone service providers generally do not have compliance departments or legal staff dedicated to navigating increasingly complex regulatory requirements. These additional administrative burdens directly increase operating costs while reducing our ability to serve clients efficiently.

The proposal may also adversely affect industries that have successfully integrated drone technology into everyday business operations. Commercial real estate professionals, construction companies, engineering firms, insurance providers, utility companies, agricultural businesses, and tourism organizations increasingly rely on FAA-certified drone operators to provide safe, efficient, and cost-effective aerial data collection. Limiting access to large areas surrounding qualifying facilities could reduce the economic benefits these industries have realized through drone technology.

Professional Part 107 operators already operate within one of the most regulated aviation environments in the world. We are required to obtain FAA certification, register our aircraft, comply with Remote ID requirements, understand controlled airspace procedures, maintain visual line of sight, follow operational limitations, and remain subject to FAA enforcement actions. The overwhelming majority of commercial operators work diligently to comply with these regulations and prioritize safety during every flight.

I respectfully encourage the FAA to ensure that any new restrictions remain narrowly tailored to demonstrated security needs. Restriction areas should be limited to the minimum geographic footprint necessary to mitigate identified risks. In addition, each designation should include a periodic review process to determine whether continued restrictions remain justified.

I also encourage the FAA to consider mechanisms that allow qualified commercial operators to conduct legitimate business activities when authorized by facility owners or operators. Infrastructure inspections, engineering documentation, construction progress monitoring, emergency assessments, and commercial property marketing often provide direct benefits to the very facilities these restrictions are intended to protect.

Finally, I respectfully request that the FAA conduct a comprehensive Small Business Impact Analysis addressing how widespread implementation of these restrictions may affect FAA-certified commercial drone operators across the United States. The cumulative effect of numerous individual restricted areas could significantly reduce opportunities for lawful commercial operations and unintentionally hinder continued growth of the domestic drone industry.

Commercial drone pilots are partners in aviation safety. We have invested substantial time, training, equipment, insurance, and regulatory compliance to operate responsibly within the National Airspace System. I respectfully ask the FAA to adopt a final rule that protects critical infrastructure while preserving reasonable access for legitimate commercial drone operations conducted safely under Part 107.

Thank you for considering my comments and for your continued work to ensure both aviation safety and the responsible growth of the commercial drone industry.

Most respectfully,

Tony

Tony Marino, MBA
FAA Part 107 Certified Remote Pilot
Owner, Aerial Northwest, LLC
Salem, Oregon

I am submitting this comment regarding proposed rule Docket No. FAA-2026-4558.

I am concerned that this proposal would allow private facility owners to request FAA-enforced drone flight restrictions over their property without requiring them to demonstrate a specific, credible threat. While I fully support protecting genuinely sensitive facilities and ensuring public safety, the current proposal appears to grant broad authority without sufficient evidentiary standards or procedural safeguards.

As written, the rule could gradually transform public navigable airspace into a patchwork of privately restricted areas. This would have significant consequences for legitimate drone operations conducted under Part 107, including commercial photography, journalism, infrastructure documentation, agricultural work, environmental monitoring, mapping, and other lawful activities that serve both businesses and the public.

The proposal also risks increasing costs, uncertainty, and administrative burdens for small commercial drone operators who already comply with FAA regulations. Operators should not be forced to navigate an expanding network of restrictions that may be based solely on requests rather than documented security needs.

I respectfully urge the FAA to revise the proposal by requiring applicants to demonstrate a clear, specific, and documented security threat before any new flight restriction is approved. The rule should also include transparent standards, a meaningful review process, clearly defined geographic and altitude limits, reasonable expiration dates, and opportunities for reconsideration or appeal to ensure restrictions remain justified over time.

The FAA has long balanced aviation safety with fair access to the National Airspace System. I encourage the agency to preserve that balance by narrowly tailoring this rule so that it protects legitimate security interests without unnecessarily limiting lawful drone operations or setting a precedent for the privatization of public airspace.

Thank you for considering these comments.

comments.commenter: Comment from North Carolina Poultry Federation
comments.comment_text:
Michelle Ferritto
Office of Rulemaking
Federal Aviation Administration
800 Independence Avenue, SW
Washington, DC 20591

Re: Docket No. FAA-2026-4558: Designation—Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility

Dear Ms. Ferritto:

The North Carolina Poultry Federation (NCPF) appreciates the opportunity to submit comments on the Federal Aviation Administration’s Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM), “Designation—Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility.”

North Carolina is one of the nation’s leading poultry-producing states, and our member integrators and producers play a vital role in supporting the state’s economy, rural communities, and the national food supply. NCPF strongly supports the NPRM and the establishment of a clear, practical process for operators and proprietors of eligible facilities—including poultry farms and processing facilities—to request and maintain unmanned aircraft flight restrictions (UAFRs).

Unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) are important tools for modern agriculture, including for monitoring facilities, improving efficiency, and enhancing animal care. However, as the technology has become more widely accessible, misuse has also increased. Poultry operations, in particular, have been targeted by outside groups using drones to capture aerial footage for activist campaigns. These activities often occur without permission and without regard for animal welfare, farm operations, or biosecurity protocols.

Low-altitude drone activity over poultry houses can stress birds, disrupt ventilation systems, and interfere with normal management practices. More importantly, the movement of drones between farms presents a legitimate biosecurity risk. At a time when the industry continues to combat Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI) and other animal health threats, preventing unnecessary and uncontrolled aerial access to poultry facilities is a prudent and necessary measure.

Beyond farm-level impacts, North Carolina’s poultry sector is a critical component of the nation’s food system. Disruptions caused by unauthorized drone activity—whether through repeated surveillance, interference with operations, or security breaches—can have broader consequences for supply chains, market stability, and consumer confidence. Additionally, unauthorized drone use poses risks related to the exposure of proprietary technologies and production practices, as well as potential vulnerabilities in food system security.

The NPRM appropriately balances the continued responsible use of drones with the need to protect sensitive infrastructure. Providing a transparent pathway for eligible facilities to request targeted flight restrictions is a commonsense approach to mitigating risk while preserving legitimate commercial, governmental, and authorized UAS operations.

For these reasons, the North Carolina Poultry Federation supports the FAA’s proposed rule and encourages the Agency to finalize it expeditiously. Strengthening protections around poultry and food production facilities will enhance biosecurity, safeguard animal health and welfare, protect critical agricultural infrastructure, and reinforce the resilience of the U.S. food supply.

Thank you for your consideration of these comments. NCPF stands ready to provide additional information or industry perspective to assist the Agency.

Respectfully submitted,

Summer Lanier

Executive Director
NC Poultry Federation

I submit this comment in my personal capacity as a commercially certificated remote pilot (14 CFR Part 107) employed at an outdoor amusement and theme park facility. A detailed comment is attached as a PDF; this summary presents its principal points.
I strongly support this rulemaking. Unauthorized drone activity over outdoor amusement facilities is a real and growing concern, and a Section 2209 designation would finally provide meaningful legal tools to address it. My recommendations are offered to make the rule workable, and are informed by the FAA's own successful transition from Section 333 exemptions to Part 107: a clear, standardized, risk-based framework that broadened participation while enhancing safety.

1.ELIGIBILITY. The 2.5 million attendance threshold (§74.82) is not a valid proxy for safety risk. According to the BLS QCEW data, 1,407 U.S. establishments are classified under NAICS 71311; at most 15–18 meet the threshold, excluding roughly 98.7% of the industry Congress explicitly named in Section 2209. Even confined to the 437 U.S. parks operating roller coasters (per RCDB), the exclusion rate exceeds 95%. The hazard justifying UAFR protection is defined by the attractions, not gate count: a UAS strike on a coaster train at speed presents the same consequence at a 500,000-visitor park as at a 5-million-visitor park. The FAA should adopt hazard-based eligibility criteria — developed with impartial industry bodies such as IAAPA — or a supplemental pathway based on attraction hazard classification, and should clarify that the absence of historical incident records (§74.66) will not penalize facilities that lacked detection capability.

2.STATUTORY DEFINITION. The "critical infrastructure" definition (42 U.S.C. 5195c(e)) is disjunctive. High-kinetic-energy attractions satisfy the public safety prong directly, and many regional parks satisfy the economic prong as anchor employers in tourism-dependent communities. The 2015 Alton Towers incident demonstrates the mechanism: a single ride incident with no fatalities caused a park closure, sustained attendance decline, and documented regional economic harm.

3-4.AUTHORIZED OPERATIONS. Documented internal coordination protocols exceeding the §74.255 notification requirement should be recognized as satisfying it. The FAA should confirm that facilities may enforce their own third-party operator approval processes (credentials, insurance, lost-link procedures, mission-specific authorizations) as a condition of airspace access.

5.INCIDENT RESPONSE. §74.64 requires response plans without providing the legal framework to build them. The FAA should publish guidance defining what facility security may lawfully do, the role of local law enforcement, and when federal authority is triggered — before or alongside the requirement.

6.SITE MANAGER. The single-site manager model (§74.1) creates a single point of failure. Require a designated alternate, and support authorizations through a DroneZone-style portal.

7-8.OPERATIONS CLARITY. The "shortest practicable time" standard (§74.250) should be clarified as governing transit, not intentionally localized authorized missions. Both integrated and add-on Remote ID solutions satisfying Part 89 should be confirmed as compliant.

9.SENSING INFRASTRUCTURE. Simultaneous procurement by newly designated facilities risks the supply chain delays seen after the Part 89 broadcast mandate. Permit documented procurement commitments at application with a defined deployment window. Limit any Remote ID data retention mandate to 24 months, matching the §74.20 annual reporting cycle.

10.VISIBILITY. UAFRs published only in a federal database will be invisible to the operators most likely to violate them. Integrate boundaries into flight planning applications and build a LAANC-style authorization pathway. Visibility drives compliance.

11–12.ADMINISTRATIVE BURDEN. The facilities needing protection the most are often the least equipped for complex federal applications. Adopt a tiered application process with plain-language templates, streamline three-year renewals into online attestation, and commit to an operational portal before the first renewals come due.

13.AIRSPACE. Clarify that sectional chart literacy, LAANC references, and existing authorizations satisfy airspace documentation (§74.58), with explicit guidance for facilities in complex airspace.

14.ENFORCEMENT ECOSYSTEM. Develop law enforcement education resources and a defined pathway to federal counter-UAS authorities (6 U.S.C. §124n; 10 U.S.C. §130i). A designation is only as effective as the response ecosystem around it.

The FAA has the opportunity to do here what it did with Part 107: solve a complex problem with a clear, workable rule. I hope it will.

Stephen Rutherford
Remote PIC, 14 CFR Part 107

July 2, 2026
Docket Operations
U.S. Department of Transportation
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Washington, DC 20590
RE: Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03
Designation — Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed
Site Facility
To Whom It May Concern:
SKY EYES appreciates the opportunity to submit comments on the FAA’s proposed rule
regarding Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions near certain fixed site facilities.
SKY EYES supports the FAA’s responsibility to protect the safety and security of the National
Airspace System, critical infrastructure, people, and property on the ground. We recognize
that certain fixed site facilities may present unique security concerns and that a clearly
defined federal process is preferable to inconsistent local or private approaches to airspace
restriction.
At the same time, the final rule must preserve practical access for responsible, certificated,
and identifiable UAS operators conducting legitimate commercial, governmental, public
safety, documentation, mapping, and infrastructure-support operations.
SKY EYES is a professional UAS services provider serving commercial, construction,
industrial, manufacturing, real estate, and community sectors. Our work depends on safe,
compliant, and predictable access to low-altitude airspace. Many legitimate UAS operations
occur near or around facilities that could fall within or adjacent to future UAFRs. These
operations may include construction progress documentation, commercial property
documentation, infrastructure support, facility-requested aerial documentation,
emergency response support, mapping capture, and other lawful Part 107 activities.
We respectfully recommend that the FAA ensure the final rule includes the following
principles:
1. Preserve clear access for certificated Part 107 operators.
The proposed rule appropriately recognizes that Part 107 operators may have legitimate
reasons to enter a UAFR. That access should remain clear, practical, and scalable.
Certificated Remote Pilots operating Remote ID-compliant aircraft should not be treated the
same as anonymous, careless, or malicious operators.

The final rule should clearly distinguish between unauthorized UAS activity and lawful
operations conducted by vetted, certificated, and identifiable operators.
2. Use Remote ID as a practical visibility and accountability tool.
SKY EYES supports the use of Remote ID as a key part of airspace awareness around fixed
site facilities. Remote ID can help facility managers and law enforcement distinguish known,
lawful operators from unknown or unauthorized activity.
The FAA should continue developing a framework where Remote ID, operator credentials,
and mission notification tools work together to support security without unnecessarily
excluding compliant operators from the airspace.
3. Clarify that authorized Part 107 operations may include non-transitory activity.
The proposed rule discusses limiting UAS activity within a UAFR to the shortest amount of
time practicable. While this may be appropriate for pass-through operations, many
legitimate commercial and governmental UAS missions are not merely transitory.
Authorized operations may require hovering, orbiting, grid mapping, vertical documentation,
or repeated passes to safely complete the mission. This is especially true for construction
documentation, infrastructure support, facility-requested aerial documentation, public
safety response, and mapping capture.
The final rule should clarify that when a Part 107 operator is operating with appropriate
authorization, coordination, or facility consent, non-transitory flight activity may be
permitted when it is necessary for the approved mission and conducted in accordance with
applicable FAA rules.
4. Establish a practical authorization and notification pathway.
A UAFR system should not become a new, slow, facility-by-facility permission bottleneck
that prevents legitimate operations. The FAA should establish a standardized digital process
for authorized operators to notify, coordinate, or request access where appropriate.
This process should be simple enough for small businesses, public agencies, and facility
operators to use consistently. It should also allow for repeat operators, recurring projects,
and pre-approved vendors to be identified through Remote ID serial numbers, airman
certificate information, or other FAA-approved credentials.
A whitelist or pre-coordination model should be considered for operators who routinely
support a facility or operate in the area for legitimate commercial or public safety purposes.
5. Balance security with continued UAS integration.

The final rule should protect sensitive facilities without creating unnecessary barriers to the
broader integration of UAS into the National Airspace System. Commercial UAS operations
provide meaningful value to construction, infrastructure, emergency management, facility
operations, insurance, real estate, utilities, and public safety.
A broad exclusion-based framework could unintentionally limit exactly the types of
professional operators who help facilities, communities, and agencies use aerial data safely
and responsibly. The FAA should focus on accountability, identification, coordination, and
enforcement against unauthorized operations rather than unnecessary restriction of
compliant operators.
6. Maintain a risk-based approach.
SKY EYES has previously supported FAA rulemaking approaches that are risk-based,
scalable, and built around the existing Part 107 framework. That same principle should apply
here. The final rule should avoid one-size-fits-all restrictions and should scale requirements
based on the sensitivity of the facility, the operational risk, the type of UAS activity, the
credentials of the operator, and the availability of Remote ID or other airspace awareness
tools.
Conclusion
SKY EYES supports the FAA’s goal of improving security around sensitive fixed site facilities.
However, the final rule should preserve practical pathways for lawful, certificated, Remote
ID-compliant Part 107 operators to conduct legitimate commercial, public safety,
infrastructure-support, and facility-authorized operations.
The FAA should finalize a framework that helps distinguish responsible operators from
unauthorized or malicious actors, supports facility security, preserves access to the
National Airspace System, and encourages the continued safe integration of UAS into
American commerce and public service.
Respectfully submitted,
Ken Herman
Founder and Principal Strategist
SKY EYES
FAA Part 107 Remote Pilot
FAASTeam Drone Pro, North Texas FSDO
fly@sky-eyes.com
972-930-1444

comments.commenter: Comment from National Association of State Energy Officials (NASEO)
comments.organization: National Association of State Energy Officials (NASEO)
comments.comment_text:
June 29, 2026

The Honorable Sean Duffy
Secretary of Transportation
U.S. Department of Transportation
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Washington, D.C. 20590

The Honorable Bryan Bedford
Administrator, Federal Aviation Administration
800 Independence Avenue SW
Washington, D.C. 20591

Subject: Comments of the National Association of State Energy Officials (NASEO) on the Notice of Proposed Rulemaking — Designation: Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility [Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; RIN 2120-AL33]

Dear Secretary Duffy and Administrator Bedford:

The National Association of State Energy Officials (NASEO) greatly appreciates the Federal Aviation Administration's (FAA) development of the proposed rule establishing a process for Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs) at fixed site facilities. NASEO represents the governor-designated State and Territory Energy Offices from each of the 56 states, territories, and the District of Columbia. State Energy Offices lead a wide range of energy policies and programs across all energy sectors, and most are engaged in energy sector emergency response and mitigation actions in partnership with utilities and the private sector, as well as other state, local, and federal authorities. In addition, they are responsible for development of State Energy Security Plans as required by the longstanding, bipartisan U.S. State Energy Program established by Energy Policy and Conservation Act.

Given the high importance of protecting our critical energy infrastructure from Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS), NASEO commends the FAA for moving forward with this rulemaking. NASEO supports the establishment of a formal, accessible process, that includes state government engagement opportunities, for energy infrastructure operators, private sector and municipally-owned, to seek airspace protection from UAS threats.

NASEO supports the FAA's proposal to create a clear, enforceable process for restricting unauthorized drone operations near energy sector critical infrastructure under proposed §74.88 and the broader Part 74 framework of the FAA's Notice of Proposed Rulemaking on Unmanned Aircraft Systems Operations in the Vicinity of Critical Infrastructure. The risks the FAA identifies in the proposal, including surveillance of substations, the vulnerability of supervisory control and data acquisition (SCADA) and operational technology systems to UAS-enabled cyber intrusion, and the potential for cascading impacts to the power grid, mirror the threats that many states track and plan against through their State Energy Security Plans. A consistent federal designation process could reinforce and serve as a model for parallel state-level work.

To better align state-federal coordination under the Proposed Rulemaking, NASEO offers the following recommendations:

Under proposed §74.6 of the Proposed Rulemaking, it would make the more protective Special UAFR available to eligible critical infrastructure only when sponsored by the FAA or a federal security or intelligence agency, including the U.S. Department of Energy. Because State Energy Offices develop State Energy Security Plans in direct consultation with the U.S. Department of Energy and hold detailed, jurisdiction-specific knowledge of energy infrastructure risk, NASEO requests that the FAA clarify how State Energy Offices' risk assessments will be utilized to inform the federal sponsorship and evaluation process for energy sector Special UAFRs.
Many states have enacted statutes addressing unmanned aircraft operations over critical infrastructure, and State Energy Security Plans can identify additional state-level protective measures. NASEO recommends that the FAA clarify how the framework would interact with these existing state laws and State Energy Security Plan related measures, so that states and energy infrastructure owners have greater certainty about the respective roles of the federal designation and complementary state actions.

NASEO stands ready to serve as a key partner in advancing this rule and in ensuring that energy security planning frameworks at the state level are coordinated with federal airspace protections.

We appreciate the opportunity to provide these comments.

Best regards,

David Terry
President, NASEO

As a member of Texas Search and Rescue and a Part 107 RPIC I request that restrictions be as minimal as practical to allow us to search for subjects lost or missing from our communities. This life saving mission of UAS search and rescue tools is imperative for better outcomes. Please make exceptions for this critical role.

i dont want to have to be concerned with EVERY move i make cuz there could be a drone in the air. having to worry about a drone watching me could lead to a fatal error being made. as it is with trainmasters sitting in trucks watching us work is enough. we are all professionals and we know how to do our jobs safely and go home to our families. drones are completely unnecessary and can only be distractions whether physically or mentally

What is the proposed way that sUAS pilots will be able to determine if there is a UAFR in place for their area of operation? Will they be able to be seen through any of the LAANC apps? Will we be able to request authorization via the LAANC apps? What will be the criteria that will prevent a sUAS pilot from obtaining access through the UAFR?

There needs to be an explicit carve-out for non-transitory drone operations conducted for newsgathering, documentary production, environmental monitoring, and other First Amendment-protected public-oversight purposes, not just brief transit,  subject to the same Remote ID and notification requirements as other permitted operators.
Good luck nothing

A poorly drawn boundary line under Docket No. FAA-2026-4558, the proposed rule implementing Section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety and Security Act of 2016, could ground me in my own back yard, and I am writing to urge the FAA to close that gap before it becomes final.

While I support the FAA's proposed addition of Section 2209, it must be mentioned that any area granted a no-fly zone must have clearly defined, unambiguous, and sensible boundaries. If boundaries are not clearly defined and limited by the FAA, we risk turning recreational and commercial UAV airspace into a Swiss cheese map of confusing and illogical flight restriction areas. A map peppered with small restricted areas has the potential to punish law enforcement, rescue operations, critical medical supply delivery, infrastructure research and city planning, and many other legitimate uses of small unmanned aircraft.

Facilities who are unfamiliar with the current laws, or who are vehemently opposed to any type of UAV operations, can and will abuse these flight restriction zone requests if they are allowed to do so unchecked. The facility requesting the exception should provide an adequate and legitimate reason for the request, and these requests must be reviewed by a human and should not be granted ad hoc. I note that the NPRM already proposes that lateral boundaries be drawn as "the smallest practicable polygon consistent with documented protection needs" - I strongly support this standard and urge the FAA to apply it rigorously. The outer boundary of any zone should be limited to a specific, reasonable distance from the base facility so as to avoid ambiguity and prevent abuse of the system. The zone and underlying justification should be made publicly available through all FAA channels and maintained in real time.

Furthermore, adding additional restrictions into an already complicated set of rules will further discourage the adoption of a fun hobby, which provides many people a means of experiencing the very human desire for the freedom of flight. As a recreational pilot currently covered under 49 U.S.C. § 44809, I am already hesitant to fly my drone, or even a kite, in my own back yard. I live on the outside edge of Class C airspace, with Class B directly to my south, and my local airport does not support LAANC approvals. I have been severely limited in the amount of hobby time I can spend with my UAV from my own very rural, mostly forested back yard, as the only time I can fly without prior approval is after my local ATC tower has closed for the day. I also live adjacent to my neighborhood's water treatment ponds. If this facility is designated as critical infrastructure by my city, it will completely eliminate my ability to fly my own UAV in my yard unless the restricted zone is explicitly limited to the airspace directly above the ponds themselves, rather than a blanket radius around the facility. Ambiguous no-fly designations of this kind will discourage commercial and recreational flyers alike, whether from genuine confusion around the boundaries or simply a desire to avoid the "red tape" involved in verifying compliance before every flight.

Finally, it should be noted that these restriction zones ultimately will do nothing to discourage any UAV pilots who either already disregard or purposefully defy the existing guidelines. The proposed rule, as drafted, relies almost entirely on voluntary compliance mechanisms such as Remote ID broadcast, advance notification to the facility, and adherence to designated transit corridors, none of which carry any independent means of detection or enforcement absent a pilot's own cooperation. An operator already willing to fly over a prison to drop contraband, conduct unauthorized surveillance of critical infrastructure, or otherwise act with malicious intent has no incentive to broadcast Remote ID, notify the facility, or respect a no-fly zone that exists only as a legal designation on paper. The burden of compliance therefore falls almost exclusively on the those UAV pilots who were never the source of the underlying safety or security concern in the first place; law-abiding users will now face additional notification requirements, narrower transit corridors, and expanded criminal exposure, while bad-faith actors continue operating exactly as they do today. The FAA's own regulatory impact analysis does not appear to model or account for this asymmetry. Absent a corresponding investment in detection capability or a credible enforcement mechanism targeting non-compliant flights specifically, the practical effect of the UAFR framework will be to regulate the operators the FAA can already reach, while leaving the operators the rule is ostensibly meant to stop entirely unaffected.

This rule would allow factory farms to operate in total secrecy. This has the potential to not only result in hideous abuse of the animals, but also in unsanitary conditions that can lead to contamination of food destined for human consumption.
This is a terrible idea which places undue burden on drone operators

As Humanity deals with ever increasing and present noises such as drones by Amazon and Walmart, as well as data center, why would a drone be prohibited above an agricultural site? In Utah, our ranchers and farmers take pride in their compassionate and humane operations with nothing to hide; why is it big Ag is seeking to limit the eyes on and into their remote operations? Why can’t the community and patrons of these companies see for themselves if the claims of “free range” or “open pasture” are as they claim?  In America, buyer beware. We can’t expect the government to make our decisions, so allow we the people the consumers to make our own Independently verified purchases of cattle, chicken, pork, and other normal products we deem worthy of supporting.

Comments on FAA Docket No. FAA-2026-4558
Restricting UAS Operations Near Fixed Site Facilities

Thank you for the opportunity to comment on this proposed rule.

I submit these comments in my individual capacity as an aerospace professional, a lifelong member of the Snoqualmie Indian Tribe, and someone who believes technological innovation and cultural stewardship are complementary goals. Throughout aviation history, the greatest advances have come from anticipating tomorrow's challenges rather than reacting to them after they occur.

Although this proposal addresses unmanned aircraft today, it also helps establish the foundation for the next generation of aviation. Autonomous aircraft, Advanced Air Mobility (AAM), cargo drones, emergency response systems, and personal eVTOL aircraft will increasingly share our skies. The infrastructure we design today will determine how successfully those systems operate tomorrow.

As an aerospace professional, I have learned that the most effective systems solve problems through design rather than enforcement. Retrofitting solutions after technology has matured is almost always more expensive, less efficient, and less effective than building those considerations into the architecture from the beginning.

I encourage the FAA to view this rule as an opportunity to move beyond managing airspace toward designing intelligent airspace.

Modern aircraft increasingly rely on digital navigation, automation, artificial intelligence, and geospatial data. The next evolution of the National Airspace System should enable aircraft to understand not only where they can safely operate, but where they should respectfully operate.

This does not require blanket no-fly zones. Instead, future navigation systems should be capable of recognizing culturally significant landscapes and applying appropriate operational guidance, including altitude management, routing adjustments, hover limitations, noise reduction, or seasonal considerations while preserving emergency and public safety operations.

These are engineering solutions—not barriers to innovation.

I also encourage the FAA to recognize Tribal Nations as infrastructure partners in the evolution of autonomous aviation. Federally recognized Tribes possess generations of knowledge regarding culturally, historically, environmentally, and spiritually significant landscapes. That knowledge should help inform tomorrow's aviation infrastructure rather than being consulted only after conflicts arise.

One opportunity is the development of standardized, machine-readable Digital Cultural Infrastructure that can be incorporated into future navigation databases. Just as aircraft already recognize airports, terrain, obstacles, weather, and restricted airspace, future systems should also recognize designated cultural landscapes. This approach creates predictable operational guidance for pilots, manufacturers, software developers, and autonomous aircraft while reducing future conflicts through intelligent design rather than reactive regulation.

Many Indigenous cultures make decisions by considering the impact on the next seven generations. From an engineering perspective, this reflects the same philosophy that has produced our most successful infrastructure: build systems that remain effective as technology evolves.

The FAA has an opportunity to become a global leader by demonstrating that advanced aviation and cultural stewardship can advance together. Building cultural awareness into aviation infrastructure today will be significantly easier than retrofitting millions of autonomous aircraft tomorrow.

The measure of great infrastructure is not whether it solves today's challenges. It is whether future generations inherit a system that no longer creates them.

Thank you for your consideration.

Respectfully,

Christina "Chrissie" Sparling
Aerospace Professional
Lifelong Member, Snoqualmie Indian Tribe
Comments submitted in my individual capacity.

I am a part 107 sUAS pilot who flies for law enforcement. Please include some type of rule/exemption for law enforcement operations. If law enforcement is called for an incident at a critical infrastructure site, a restriction on drone flight could impact our ability to gain valuable information.

As a Part 107 Remote Pilot, I understand and support the FAA's responsibility to protect critical infrastructure and keep the National Airspace System safe. Public safety should always come first, and I believe there are situations where additional protections are necessary.
My concern is that the proposed restrictions may unintentionally affect responsible commercial drone pilots who use drones to serve their communities and earn a living. As a professional photographer, I use drones to create visual content for clients, and many other operators rely on drones for construction progress, inspections, mapping, agriculture, and real estate. These are legitimate, low-risk operations that are already conducted under FAA regulations.
I encourage the FAA to take a risk-based approach instead of broad restrictions whenever possible. Remote pilots who maintain their certification, comply with Remote ID requirements, and have a history of safe operations should have a clear, practical process for obtaining authorization to fly when operations can be conducted safely. This would help maintain security while allowing professionals to continue serving their clients responsibly.
I also hope the FAA carefully considers the impact these restrictions could have on small businesses. Many commercial drone operators are self-employed or own small companies, and limiting access to large areas could reduce opportunities for legitimate work without meaningfully increasing safety. I believe it's possible to protect sensitive locations while still supporting innovation, responsible drone operations, and the growing drone industry.
Thank you for giving remote pilots the opportunity to provide feedback. I appreciate the FAA seeking input from the community and hope these comments help shape a final rule that balances security with the needs of responsible commercial operators.

I think that including animal agriculture in the “allowed to ban drones near” category is suspect, unethical, and dangerous to both animal and human health and welfare. “Suspect” because an industry that has nothing to hide doesn’t try to make it illegal to be able to see the conditions of the animals and the environment they live in as well as the treatment of those animals. Period. This is an industry that not only avoids transparency but does whatever it can do make sure people can’t see these conditions—housed in giant warehouses, far away from the sight of most people, with the goal of keeping it that way so they can avoid public scrutiny—something the public deserves to be able to see…because it’s the food they eat and can affect their own health and welfare. It’s unethical to keep the health and welfare of these animals hidden. And it makes ot more likely that the public will not know if there are issues with food safety or viruses and other illnesses that could cause another pandemic-type situation.

For these reasons, I believe ethically and health and safety-wise, allowing drones to survey these huge CAFOs is the right thing to do.
