Please don't make this more difficult for us hobbyists,and part 107 drone pilots, ppl will abuse this new rule try to say my house or bu is a no fly zone.

415 LINCOLN ST. 308.995.8601
P.O. BOX 740 CNPPID.COM
HOLDREGE, NE 68949
Docket Operations; U.S. Department of Transportation 7 July 2026
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Room W58-213, West Building 5th Floor
Washington, DC 20590-0001
Re: Comments of The Central Nebraska Public Power and Irrigation District on the Notice of
Proposed Rulemaking, Designation—Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close
Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03; RIN 2120-AL33
91 FR 24650 (May 6, 2026)
Dear Sir or Madam:
The Central Nebraska Public Power and Irrigation District (“Central” or “the District”) submits these
comments in response to the Federal Aviation Administration’s (“FAA”) notice of proposed rulemaking
implementing Section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016. Central is a public
corporation and political subdivision of the State of Nebraska, headquartered in Holdrege, Nebraska, and holds
FERC License No. 1417 for the Kingsley Dam Hydroelectric Project.
Central owns and operates an integrated hydroelectric generation and water-delivery system across south-
central Nebraska. That system includes Kingsley Dam and its Kingsley Hydro plant, which impound Lake
McConaughy, the District’s principal storage reservoir; an approximately 75-mile Supply Canal extending from
Central’s diversion dam near North Platte eastward through the Platte River Valley to the District’s irrigated
service area in Gosper, Phelps, and Kearney Counties; three additional hydroelectric generating plants on the
Supply Canal—Jeffrey, Johnson No. 1 (J-1), and Johnson No. 2 (J-2)—together with their associated dams and
regulating reservoirs, including Jeffrey Lake and Johnson Lake; numerous diversion and regulating dams,
impoundments, and water-control and delivery structures along the canal; high-voltage switchyards and
electrical equipment; and a centralized supervisory control and data acquisition (SCADA) system operated from
the District’s control center in Gothenburg. Central owns all of these critical assets in fee.
Central’s facilities fall squarely within the critical infrastructure that Congress and the FAA seek to protect
through this rulemaking. As the operator of multiple hydroelectric generating facilities, associated energy
infrastructure, and irrigation water delivery systems, the District operates eligible fixed site facilities under both
the Dams Sector (proposed § 74.85) and the Energy Sector (proposed § 74.88). Central therefore writes both
to express its general support for the proposed rule and to offer several constructive recommendations drawn
from its experience operating regulated critical infrastructure and conducting routine unmanned aircraft
operations for compliance and inspection purposes.
I. Central Supports the Proposed UAFR Framework
Central supports the FAA’s creation of a structured process under proposed Part 74 for operators and
proprietors of critical infrastructure to request an unmanned aircraft flight restriction (“UAFR”). The
proliferation of low-cost, highly capable unmanned aircraft has outpaced the tools available to facility operators
and law enforcement to deter, identify, and respond to unauthorized overflight. Central has a direct interest in

protecting its dams, powerhouses, spillways and outlet works, switchyards, water-control structures, and
SCADA control systems—across both Kingsley Dam and the Supply Canal generating plants—from both
careless and malicious unmanned aircraft activity, including surveillance of security-sensitive operations and the
risk of physical or cyber disruption to generation and water-delivery functions.
Central particularly endorses the FAA’s observation that a UAFR functions as a clear legal designation that
helps facility operators and law enforcement distinguish lawful operations from unauthorized or potentially
hostile ones, allowing limited security and enforcement resources to be focused where they are most needed.
For a publicly owned utility that must steward public resources, this differentiation is a meaningful operational
benefit beyond the airspace restriction itself.
II. Recommendations
Central offers the following recommendations to strengthen the rule and ensure it works as intended for
hydroelectric and dam infrastructure:
1. Preserve the facility operator’s ability to conduct its own unmanned aircraft operations within
its UAFR. Central routinely uses unmanned aircraft across its system to satisfy federal compliance
obligations, including riprap and embankment inspections of high-hazard dams and inspections of the
Supply Canal dams, structures, and generating plants, conducted under the FERC-approved Dam Safety
Surveillance and Monitoring Plan. The rule should make explicit—whether through the allowed-
operations provisions of proposed § 74.250, the access-notification process of § 74.255, or the
application itself—that an operator or proprietor may continue to fly its own unmanned aircraft over its
own facilities for safety, dam-safety, security, and regulatory-compliance purposes without burdensome
case-by-case authorization. A restriction designed to protect these facilities should not impede the very
inspections that federal dam-safety regulators require the District to perform.
2. Allow a streamlined or consolidated application for commonly owned, operated, and integrated
facilities. Proposed § 74.52(b) would limit each application to a single fixed site facility even where one
operator owns multiple eligible sites. Central’s system comprises numerous geographically dispersed but
functionally integrated critical assets—four hydroelectric generating plants and a 75-mile Supply Canal
with its associated dams and water-control structures—all owned by Central in fee, operated as a single
coordinated system, and monitored from a common control center. Because Central holds all of these
assets in fee, the coordination and private-property concerns the FAA built into §§ 74.52 and 74.58 do
not arise here; the practical effect of the one-facility-per-application limit is simply to require the District
to prepare and maintain many separate, largely duplicative applications and annual reports. Central
recommends that the FAA permit a single operator to file a consolidated application, or an expedited
application incorporating common information by reference, for multiple eligible facilities that share
common ownership, operational control, and security management. This would reduce administrative
burden on both the applicant and the FAA without diminishing the agency’s ability to evaluate each
requested airspace volume on its own merits.
3. Harmonize Part 74 with existing FERC dam-safety and security requirements. Central is already
subject to comprehensive security and dam-safety oversight under its FERC license. Central encourages
the FAA to coordinate with FERC and the relevant Sector Risk Management Agencies so that Part 74
supplements, rather than duplicates or conflicts with, those existing obligations, and so that
documentation already prepared for FERC may be used to satisfy comparable Part 74 application
elements such as the demonstration of need (§ 74.66) and facility security measures (§ 74.56).

4. Calibrate the Remote ID receiver requirement and any data-retention mandate to be
proportionate for public entities. Central supports the proposed requirement in § 74.56 that a facility
be able to receive broadcast Remote Identification messages, which is a reasonable and proportionate
baseline for situational awareness. On the FAA’s specific request for comment regarding whether to
impose a Remote ID data-retention requirement: Central agrees that retained Remote ID data can have
value for enforcement and for the annual reporting contemplated by § 74.20. Central recommends,
however, that any retention requirement be time-limited (for example, a defined retention period rather
than open-ended retention), that retention remain optional or be paired with the option to rely on third-
party monitoring services, and that the FAA expressly account for the equipment, subscription, and
recordkeeping costs such a mandate would impose on smaller public power and irrigation districts in its
regulatory impact analysis.
III. Conclusion
Central commends the FAA for advancing this long-awaited framework and supports adoption of a final rule
that protects critical energy and dam infrastructure while preserving the efficient and lawful use of the national
airspace. Central respectfully urges the FAA to adopt the recommendations above so that the final rule
effectively protects facilities like the Kingsley Dam Hydroelectric Project and Central’s Supply Canal generating
system without impeding the routine, compliance-driven unmanned aircraft operations on which responsible
operators rely. Central appreciates the opportunity to comment and would welcome the chance to provide
further information.
Respectfully submitted,
/s/ Jim Brown
Jim Brown
Compliance Coordinator
The Central Nebraska Public Power and Irrigation District

I support the FAA’s proposed rule, Designation: Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility, and the creation of a formal process for Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions around qualifying fixed-site facilities.

This rule is an important step toward addressing legitimate safety and security risks created by unmanned aircraft operating near sensitive infrastructure. I also support the FAA’s decision to avoid blanket restrictions and require applicants to demonstrate a specific need based on aviation safety, protection of people and property on the ground, national security, or homeland security.

However, I respectfully request that the FAA consider a narrow eligibility pathway for landowners who have specific fixed-site assets located on their property that may be vulnerable to unauthorized or careless unmanned aircraft activity.

This request is not intended to create a general private right to control airspace over rural land, ranches, farms, residences, or undeveloped property. Land ownership alone should not qualify for a UAFR. However, some landowners have critical, high-value, safety-sensitive, or operationally sensitive assets located on their property. These assets may include infrastructure owned by the landowner, operated by a third party, or placed on the property through easements, leases, rights-of-way, or other legal agreements.

Examples may include pipeline rights-of-way, compressor stations, pump stations, valve sites, oil and gas wellheads, tank batteries, water infrastructure, electrical or communications equipment, livestock breeding facilities, biosecurity-sensitive agricultural operations, concentrated livestock areas, or other fixed-site assets where unmanned aircraft activity could create a credible safety, security, environmental, operational, animal-welfare, or property risk.

In these situations, the concern is not general drone overflight. The concern is low-altitude UAS activity near specific assets located on the landowner’s property where the landowner may have responsibility for site security, animal safety, access control, operational continuity, or protection of property. A landowner may be able to restrict ground access through fencing, gates, signage, and patrols, but those measures do not address low-altitude UAS access to sensitive assets.

I recommend that the FAA allow landowners to request consideration for a UAFR only when the landowner can demonstrate all of the following:

The property contains a specific fixed-site asset with defined geographic boundaries.
The asset is located on the applicant’s property or within a documented easement, lease, right-of-way, or operating area.
The asset has a documented vulnerability to unmanned aircraft activity.
Unauthorized UAS activity could create a credible safety, security, environmental, operational, economic, animal-welfare, or public-impact risk.
The landowner has implemented reasonable ground-based access controls or mitigation measures.
The requested UAFR is limited to the smallest practical lateral and vertical area necessary to protect the asset.
The restriction would not unnecessarily interfere with lawful UAS operations, emergency response, infrastructure inspection, agricultural operations, or public access to the National Airspace System.
The landowner has a process for documenting and reporting unauthorized UAS activity near the asset.

This approach would remain consistent with the FAA’s proposed framework, which already emphasizes documented need, vulnerability, consequence, and proportionality. It would not create blanket airspace restrictions over private land. Instead, it would allow the FAA to evaluate specific land-based asset risks on a case-by-case basis.

Many important assets are located on private land, especially in rural areas. These assets may support energy, water, agriculture, communications, public safety, or other essential operations. In some cases, the landowner is the only party positioned to observe recurring UAS activity, document the risk, coordinate with operators or easement holders, and maintain physical site protections.

I respectfully request that the FAA consider expanding the final rule to include a narrow, evidence-based process for landowners who have specific fixed-site assets located on their property and can demonstrate that a limited UAFR is necessary, justified, and appropriately tailored.

NOTE: This is a summary of my comment. My complete comment — with full legal citations, statutory history, and a formal reference list — is attached as a PDF and constitutes my full submission.

I comment as a private citizen and small business owner. Background: aviation experience since 1996; Commercial Pilot Certificate (Instrument, Multi-Engine); FAA Part 107 remote pilot certificate; B.S., Air Traffic Control, University of North Dakota; M.S., Aviation Safety, Embry-Riddle Aeronautical University; 14 years as an Air Traffic Controller, Memphis ARTCC. I am also a small business owner in Mississippi operating drones (including Apellix pressure-washing UAS) for commercial cleaning and inspection work at utility and government facilities nationwide.

POSITION: I cannot support this rule as written. I would support it if amended as follows.

CORE CONCERN: Proposed Section 74.54 does not limit UAFR eligibility to the facility categories Congress actually enumerated in section 2209 (energy production/transmission/distribution, oil refineries, chemical facilities, amusement parks, railroads, State prisons). Instead it adopts the far broader 16-sector NSM-22 framework, despite Congress affirmatively removing the open-ended "other locations" catch-all from the statute in 2024. Comments already in this docket (e.g., Boston Red Sox, International Association of Venue Managers) confirm this risk is already materializing: stakeholders are actively lobbying to expand "commercial facilities" eligibility well beyond amusement parks, the only such category Congress named.

RECOMMENDED AMENDMENT - a two-tier eligibility structure:

Tier One: automatic/presumptive eligibility for the facility types Congress specifically enumerated.
Tier Two: all other NSM-22 sectors (commercial facilities, financial services, food and agriculture, etc.) should require a heightened "demonstration of cause" - a documented incident or credible threat, a specific national-security nexus, or an explanation of why existing law (49 U.S.C. 46307, 18 U.S.C. 39B, Remote ID under 14 CFR Part 89) is insufficient. Example: a private manufacturer of military hardware is not an enumerated critical-infrastructure category, but could readily qualify under Tier Two by documenting its defense-contractor status and associated federal security requirements - a meaningfully different, individualized standard than automatic inclusion in one of sixteen broadly defined sectors.

FAA already has narrower, incident-driven tools (14 CFR 91.137, 91.141; Special Security Instructions under 14 CFR 99.7) that could inform a more targeted Tier Two standard rather than defaulting to broad, multi-year eligibility for facility types Congress did not name.

ADDITIONAL CONCERNS:

Proposed Section 74.250's "shortest practicable time" transit standard does not accommodate common non-transitory commercial drone work - mapping, inspection, and maintenance operations that require hovering, orbiting, or sustained coverage of an area. This burden is not hypothetical - it directly affects my own business - and the same gap is independently raised in this docket by Aerotas LLC, Avios Media, the Virginia Maritime Association, and the Montana Department of Transportation.
Section 74.255 lacks a defined maximum response time for facility notification. A non-responsive neighboring facility with no relationship to an operator's client can function as an indefinite veto over lawful, time-sensitive commercial work.
Section 74.56's Remote ID data-retention question should be resolved against retention absent a specific, documented need.
Section 74.58's property-line boundary cap and Section 74.60's 400-foot AGL default ceiling should be retained as proposed.

Full statutory analysis and all supporting citations (49 U.S.C. 40103, 44701, 44809, 46307; 42 U.S.C. 5195c; 18 U.S.C. 39B; 14 CFR Parts 74, 89, 91, 99, 107; Executive Order 14305; and comments already filed in this docket) are set out in the attached PDF.

comments.commenter: Comment from Grimmius Cattle Company, Inc.
comments.comment_text:
My name is Austin Flores, Chief Operating Officer of Grimmius Cattle Company, Inc., a cattle operation based in California's Central Valley. I am submitting this comment in strong support of this rulemaking and urge the FAA to explicitly designate agricultural livestock facilities as eligible fixed site facilities with restricted airspace protections comparable to other critical infrastructure.
We are actively dealing with unauthorized drone activity at our facilities on a near-weekly basis. Operators park vehicles on public roads miles away and fly beyond visual line of sight, including at night, in direct violation of existing FAA rules. We have documented this activity and have no legal mechanism to stop it.
A neighboring operation experienced a drone-triggered stampede that injured cattle. Our employees are being filmed without consent, and we are aware of a case where an activist organization submitted over 30 hours of drone footage of a producer's ranch to law enforcement. This footage is being used to file complaints and pressure lawful agricultural operations.
Without enforceable airspace restrictions, we cannot distinguish between recreational intrusion, activist surveillance, and deliberate targeting of food supply infrastructure. That gap is a food security vulnerability.
We urge the FAA to designate agricultural facilities as restricted airspace, apply enhanced penalties for BVLOS and nighttime violations, and coordinate with USDA and DHS to treat this as the critical infrastructure issue it is. Full testimony is attached.

July 2, 2026
United States Department of Transportation
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Docket Operations, Room W58-213
West Building, 5th Floor
Washington, DC 20590-0001
Submitted electronically via regulations.gov
RE: Designation- Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a
Fixed Site Facility (Docket No. FAA-2026-4558)
Reliable Robotics (Reliable) appreciates the opportunity to comment on this Notice of Proposed
Rulemaking (NPRM) regarding the operation of unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) in close
proximity to a fixed site facility. Reliable is currently pursuing a Supplemental Type Certificate
(STC) for the Cessna 208B Caravan (C208), which will enable continuous autopilot engagement
through all phases of flight, including taxi, takeoff, and landing. We are also collaborating with
the FAA on a second STC to add equipment, functionality, and associated elements for the
C208 to be operated by a Remote Pilot in Command (RPIC) as a UAS. The initial concept of
operations is that by leveraging existing airport infrastructure, the C208 UAS will connect small
and rural communities across the United States with regional air cargo services.
The C208 is an 8,000-pound aircraft that is currently in production by Textron Aviation and
certified by the FAA under 14 CFR § 23. More than 3,000 C208s have been delivered to
customers worldwide. The STCs Reliable is pursuing will maintain the aircraft’s existing
airworthiness certification.
The Reliable Robotics C208 UAS will be operated under Instrument Flight Rules (IFR) at
altitudes above 400 ft AGL, meaning that these operations are outside the scope of rules
intended for small UAS, such as the FAA’s proposed Part 108. In addition, the STCs Reliable is
currently pursuing will enable safe operations under both 14 CFR § 91 and 14 CFR § 135.
While Reliable supports the FAA’s efforts in this NPRM to create a single federal framework for
facility-based drone restrictions under Section 2209, we are concerned that this proposal does
not fully consider large UAS operations. Our comments focus on specific sections of the NPRM
that could have unintended consequences as companies such as Reliable Robotics commence
large UAS operations throughout the National Airspace System (NAS).
Proposed Restrictions Do Not Fully Consider Large UAS and AAM Operations
The FAA and DOT are prioritizing large UAS and Advanced Air Mobility (AAM) operations
through pilot programs (eIPP), Executive Orders and other policies. With the rapid progress on
FAA certification of aircraft autonomy and remote piloting technologies, the view that UAS
operations will only involve small aircraft at low altitudes must evolve. As the FAA continues to
develop policy and proposed rules regarding UAS, it is important to address the full scope and
diversity of technologies actively undergoing certification.
____________________________________________________________________________
Reliable Robotics Corporation ■ 950 North Rengstorff Avenue ■ Mountain View, CA 94043

Reliable Robotics
July 2, 2026
Comments to FAA-2026-4558
Page 2
Reliable plans to operate commercial regional air cargo flights using the C208 UAS, meaning
that our flights could require routine access to airspace covered by an unmanned aircraft flight
restriction (UAFR). These flights will generally operate under 14 CFR § 135 and be on an active
IFR flight plan. The remote pilot in command will be in real-time communications with Air Traffic
Control (ATC) throughout all phases of flight. Also, with the aircraft on an active IFR flight plan,
ATC will assign a discrete transponder code which will be transmitted in the same manner as
aircraft with a pilot on board are required to do today.
UAFR Access Provisions Leave Out Large UAS
In proposed § 74.250, the FAA provides a path for certain small UAS operators to access UAFR
airspace over fixed site facilities. However, the proposed rule seems to only envision that small
UAS, which meet certain security and operational requirements, would need to seek access to
this airspace. Reliable believes that as large UAS and AAM operations expand in the coming
years, these aircraft will also need to access UAFR airspace, especially if fixed facilities are
located near existing airports. While the NPRM envisions that UAFRs will not exceed 400 feet
above ground level, large UAS could routinely enter this airspace on takeoff, approach and
landing to an airport, depending on the location of the fixed facility.
To access a UAFR under proposed § 74.250, the UAS must broadcast remote identification in
accordance with 14 CFR § 89. However, large aircraft such as the C208 UAS are not covered
by the remote identification rules, as they are already required to be equipped with transponders
(14 CFR § 91.215; 14 CFR § 135.143). In addition, these aircraft are also required to be
equipped with and transmit ADS-B OUT, which provides precise, near real-time tracking data to
ATC (14 CFR § 91.225). For these reasons, large UAS are generally exempted from remote
identification requirements under 14 CFR § 89 as they are fully conspicuous to ATC. Equipping
these aircraft with remote identification equipment would be duplicative to existing transponder
and ADS-B requirements while providing no added aviation safety or security benefits.
Although large UAS such as the C208 will be in real-time communication with ATC and be
tracked by both their discrete transponder code and ADS-B OUT data, they would not be
allowed to enter UAFR airspace under proposed § 74.250. This significant limitation is
inconsistent with the stated intent of this section as described in the NPRM, “FAA's intent is to
allow established, known, and conspicuous operators to enter and transit UAFRs.”
Suggested Modifications to Proposed § 74.250 and 74.255
Reliable believes that simple modifications to § 74.250 and 74.255 which would retain the FAA’s
stated intent, while expanding the provision to include certain large UAS operations, should be
adopted in the final rule. In addition, the proposed modifications detailed below are consistent
with the goal of allowing UAS operations through UAFR airspace that are "established, known,
and conspicuous” as described in the NPRM.
For Reliable’s planned C208 UAS operations, the aircraft will operate on established air cargo
routes, and be known and conspicuous to the FAA and relevant national security stakeholders
for the reasons described above.

Reliable Robotics
July 2, 2026
Comments to FAA-2026-4558
Page 3
Modification to § 74.250(a)(1):
(1) The unmanned aircraft broadcasts remote identification in accordance with 14 CFR part 89,
or has been assigned a discrete beacon code by an ATC facility that the aircraft is transmitting
and is in two-way communication with ATC.
Proposed new § 74.255(b):
(b) An unmanned aircraft operating under an active flight plan, assigned a discrete beacon code
by an ATC facility that the aircraft is actively transmitting, and in two-way communication with
ATC is not subject to the notification requirements of this section.
—
These proposed modifications recognize that certain large UAS operations, such as Reliable’s
planned cargo flights with the C208, will be known to ATC and fully conspicuous through both
required use of a transponder and ADS-B OUT data. This means that operations through UAFR
airspace, near an airport, should be permitted without a requirement to to separately notify a
facility security desk on every operation. Existing requirements for ATC coordination of these
flights already provides continuous federal situational awareness. A per-flight facility notification
would be duplicative and would impose administrative burden on a class of operations that
presents no monitoring gap for fixed site facilities covered by this NPRM.
Finally, these proposed modifications are narrow, and will only impact fixed site facilities that are
in close proximity to existing airports. These facilities should be familiar with existing aircraft
activity, and the C208 UAS operations planned by Reliable will not appear different to these
facilities from aviation operations occurring today. This proposal also does not require fixed site
facilities to purchase new equipment to monitor large UAS operations, as existing conspicuity to
ATC and communications with controllers is the monitoring mechanism.
Conclusion
Reliable Robotics appreciates the need for facility-based drone restrictions under Section 2209
and the FAA’s significant efforts on this NPRM. With simple modifications to the proposed rule,
security considerations for fixed site facilities can be addressed without introducing potentially
duplicative and burdensome requirements for large UAS operators.
Please contact me with any questions and thank you for considering our comments.
Sincerely,
Scott O’Brien
Vice President, Legislative Affairs

comments.commenter: Comment from Texas Department of Transportation
comments.comment_text:
125 E 11th St | Austin, Texas 78701
512.463.8588
txdot.gov
July 2, 2026
The Honorable Bryan Bedford
Administrator
Federal Aviation Administration
800 Independence Avenue, SW
Washington, DC 20591
RE: Docket Number FAA-2026-4558
Dear Administrator Bedford:
The Texas Department of Transportation is pleased to provide comments to the
proposed rule posted by the Federal Aviation Administration on May 6, 2026:
Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a
Fixed Site Facility. TxDOT offers the following comments on the proposed rule:
FAA proposes that operators or proprietors within the Transportation
sector may apply for a UAFR if the facility meets one of the following
criteria:
Highway bridges or tunnels that serve 50,000 or more vehicles daily and have a
structure length of at least a half mile.
• Comment: TxDOT agrees with the inclusion of highway bridges and tunnels
in the Transportation Systems Sector as eligible critical infrastructure.
However, the setting thresholds (50,000+ Average Daily Traffic AND a half-
mile length) are insufficient. Many structurally critical or economically vital
bridges may fall just short of the half-mile mark or see seasonal traffic
variations, yet their destruction or disruption would still have a debilitating
regional impact.
• Recommendation: Revise the eligibility threshold to allow State DOTs to
designate critical bridges based on structural criticality (e.g., fracture-critical
bridges), lack of viable detour routes, or overall economic impact to the
region, rather than relying solely on vehicle counts and length metrics.
The facility must have designated security personnel... [and] security
monitoring must include the capability, either directly or through a
contracted service, to receive broadcast Remote Identification messages
from unmanned aircraft.
• Comment: TxDOT supports the rule’s inclusion of security measures to
ensure Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs) are actively monitored.
However, the rule does not fully reflect the physical and financial realities of
transportation infrastructure. Highway bridges and tunnels rarely have
dedicated, on-site "designated security personnel". Furthermore, outfitting
Connecting you with Texas
An Equal Opportunity Employer

Administrator Bedford 2 July 2, 2026
multiple bridges with Remote ID (RID) radio-frequency receivers (costing an
estimated $2,800 upfront and $1,000 annually per site) requires a massive
deployment of digital and physical infrastructure by the state, presenting a
significant financial burden.
• Recommendation: Amend the "designated security personnel" requirement
to explicitly allow remote monitoring from a centralized State DOT Traffic
Management Center (TMC). Furthermore, provide federal grant funding or
stipulate that DOTs can satisfy the RID requirement through integration with
regional cloud-based or network-based Remote ID service providers, rather
than requiring localized physical receivers on every bridge.
Unless otherwise authorized by the Administrator, unmanned aircraft may
only operate within an unmanned aircraft flight restriction if the unmanned
aircraft meets the following requirements:
The unmanned aircraft transitions the UAFR in the shortest amount of time
practicable.
• Comment: TxDOT supports the rule’s attempt to prevent unauthorized
hovering, loitering, or surveillance over sensitive sites by transiting aircraft.
However, it does not reflect the reality of internal DOT operations. State DOT
Part 107 and Part 91 pilots conducting infrastructure inspections, surveying,
and 3D mapping inherently require slow, deliberate, non-transitory flight
paths—including hovering, orbiting, and loitering to capture high-resolution
structural data. While the FAA notes this language is meant to be flexible,
"shortest amount of time practicable" is an operational barrier that could be
used to legally penalize state DOT pilots for doing their jobs.
• Recommendation: Add a specific exemption to the entire rule (or at least
the "shortest amount of time practicable") for operators specifically
contracted by, or operating directly on behalf of, the facility
operator/proprietor (i.e., DOT internal UAS programs and contracted
vendors).
The lateral boundary of the unmanned aircraft flight restriction... may not
exceed the fixed site facility property boundary.
• Comment: TxDOT supports preventing private entities from claiming
airspace over their neighbors' property. However, this provision does not
account for the realities of state infrastructure. State DOTs generally own the
right-of-way (ROW), but inspecting the exterior facades, piers, or undersides
of a large bridge safely often requires a drone to fly at a standoff distance
that pushes it outside the strict ROW property line (e.g., hovering over
adjacent water or land). If UAFR is strictly limited to the ROW, TxDOT cannot
legally protect the airspace our own drones need to occupy during an
inspection.
• Recommendation: Allow UAFR lateral boundaries for surface transportation
infrastructure to include a standard buffer zone (e.g., 100 to 200 feet)
Connecting you with Texas
An Equal Opportunity Employer

Administrator Bedford 3 July 2, 2026
beyond the strict right-of-way to allow for safe structural inspection stand-off
distances and account for GPS drift.
An unmanned aircraft may operate within the unmanned aircraft flight
restriction if the unmanned aircraft is operated under 14 CFR part 135 with
a 14 CFR part 119 certificate... [or] proposed 14 CFR part 108.
• Comment: TxDOT supports this provision to ensure that commercial drone
delivery companies operating under Part 135 or the proposed Part 108
beyond visual line of sight rules are not totally blocked from the airspace.
This prevents a patchwork of no-fly zones from suffocating the private sector.
However, the rule does not address deconfliction. If a commercial drone uses
our bridge UAFR as a transit corridor while our DOT Part 107 pilots are
actively flying a bridge inspection, it introduces a mid-air collision risk.
• Recommendation: Mandate that Part 135 and Part 108 operators must use
Unmanned Aircraft Systems Traffic Management (UTM) or strategic
deconfliction protocols when transiting a UAFR to ensure they yield to facility-
sponsored operations such as DOT bridge inspections.
Any person that operates or plans to operate an unmanned aircraft in
accordance with § 74.250 in an unmanned aircraft flight restriction must
provide notice to the fixed site facility site manager.
• Comment: The rule correctly prioritizes situational awareness for facility
owners to differentiate between authorized and unauthorized drones.
However, it does not sufficiently address administrative workflows. If multiple
private delivery companies route over a DOT bridge dozens of times a day,
the volume of individual flight notifications could quickly overwhelm the DOT
site manager. Conversely, requiring internal state DOT UAS pilots to submit
formal, itemized notices (including RID serial numbers, names, registration
numbers, etc.) to our own site managers, to inspect our own bridge, would
create a large amount of redundant, bureaucratic red tape.
• Recommendation: Allow facility operators to establish automated,
programmatic access agreements or "whitelists" for known, high-volume
commercial operators (like drone delivery services) to avoid per-flight
notification fatigue. Additionally, explicitly exempt the facility's direct
employees and internal UAS programs from formal UAFR notification
requirements if their flights are coordinated via internal agency dispatch.
Any person that operates or plans to operate an unmanned aircraft in
accordance with § 74.250 in an unmanned aircraft flight restriction must
provide notice to the fixed site facility site manager in a form and manner
acceptable to the Administrator.
• Comment: TxDOT supports the rule’s attempt to provide situational
awareness to facility managers so they can identify authorized operations.
However, the rule relies on a decentralized, manual, facility-by-facility
notification approach. As commercial drone operations scale up under Part
Connecting you with Texas
An Equal Opportunity Employer

Administrator Bedford 4 July 2, 2026
135 and the proposed Part 108 rules, a fragmented notification system will
become unmanageable for both commercial drone operators traversing the
state and facility managers receiving the notices. Furthermore, without a
centralized system, there is no effective way to ensure strategic deconfliction
between multiple authorized users (like a state DOT inspection drone and a
commercial delivery drone) operating within the same UAFR simultaneously.
• Recommendation: The FAA should mandate the implementation and use of
a state-level UTM system, operated by the state DOT or another appropriate
state-designated entity, to centrally manage UAFR access, automated
notifications, and flight deconfliction. Requiring UAFR access to be
coordinated through a designated state-level UTM would eliminate
notification fatigue for individual facility managers, streamline airspace
access for high-volume commercial operators, and provide state law
enforcement and infrastructure security personnel with a single,
authoritative, real-time picture of authorized airspace activity.

Please find attached the formal comment letter submitted by the California Department of Water Resources (DWR) in response to the Federal Aviation Administration's Notice of Proposed Rulemaking titled Designation – Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility, 91 FR 24650, Docket No. FAA-2026-4558, Notice No. 26-03.

DWR strongly supports this proposed rule to protect critical public infrastructure from drone threats. As the operator of the State Water Project—the nation's largest state-built water and power system—DWR urges the FAA to finalize this framework expeditiously.

We respectfully request that the FAA review our attached 9-page document, which outlines DWR's strong support alongside targeted modifications and technical recommendations designed to strengthen the final rule.

Sincerely,

/Derek Reynolds/
Derek Reynolds, Staff Counsel
California Department of Water Resources

City of Mesa Comments on FAA Docket No. FAA-2026-4558
Designation—Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Critical Facility
General Comments
The City of Mesa appreciates the opportunity to comment on the Federal Aviation Administration's Notice of Proposed Rulemaking regarding Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs).
Mesa supports the FAA's efforts to protect critical infrastructure from unauthorized or malicious unmanned aircraft activity while maintaining public access to the National Airspace System.
As Arizona's third-largest city and a provider of critical municipal services, Mesa owns and operates infrastructure supporting public health, safety, economic security, transportation, emergency response, communications, and regional water and energy reliability. The City encourages the FAA to adopt a risk-based framework recognizing the role local governments play in providing and protecting critical infrastructure.
Municipal Critical Infrastructure Should Be Eligible
The City recommends that the FAA establish eligibility criteria allowing municipal critical infrastructure to qualify for UAFRs when disruption, surveillance, interference, or attack could adversely affect public health and safety, homeland security, economic security, continuity of government operations, or community resilience.
Eligibility criteria should focus on the criticality of a facility and the consequences of disruption rather than whether a facility is publicly or privately owned.
Eligible facilities should include infrastructure within the following sectors:
• Emergency services, including emergency communications centers, emergency operations centers, and critical public safety facilities;
• Communications and information technology facilities, including public safety communications infrastructure, municipal data centers, and operational control systems;
• Water and wastewater systems, including treatment plants, reclamation facilities, pump stations, reservoirs, recharge facilities, utility operations centers, and SCADA systems;
• Energy systems, including electric generation, transmission and distribution facilities, natural gas infrastructure, utility operations centers, and SCADA systems;
• Transportation systems, including airport operational facilities, transportation management centers, transit operations facilities, and transportation communications infrastructure; and
• Government facilities necessary to maintain continuity of essential public services.
Authorized Government and Utility Operations
The City recommends that the FAA expressly allow authorized unmanned aircraft operations conducted by or on behalf of the owner or operator of a protected facility. Municipalities and public utilities rely on drones for infrastructure inspections, utility operations, emergency response, damage assessment, and public safety missions. The City further recommends that the FAA establish a standing authorization or expedited approval process for these operations so that essential governmental and utility functions can continue while supporting the security objectives of the UAFR program.
Recommended Eligibility Criteria
The City recommends that the FAA evaluate eligibility based on objective risk and consequence factors, including:
Population Served
Facilities should receive consideration based on the number of residents and businesses dependent upon their operation.
Critical Customer Impacts
The FAA should consider whether a facility supports:
• Emergency response agencies;
• Hospitals and healthcare facilities;
• Critical utility and infrastructure facilities;
• Essential government operations;
• Airports;
• Defense and aerospace facilities;
• Semiconductor manufacturing and advanced technology facilities; and
• Major employers.
Consequence of Failure
Eligibility should consider whether a drone-related incident could result in:
• Disruption of emergency response or communications capabilities;
• Significant public health and safety impacts;
• Loss or disruption of water, wastewater, reclaimed water, electric, or natural gas services;
• Compromise of drinking water quality or treatment processes resulting from interference, contamination, or disruption of water treatment operations;
• Interruption of transportation operations;
• Loss of operational control systems; or
• Significant economic disruption.
Security and Operational Vulnerability
The FAA should evaluate:
• Documented unauthorized drone activity;
• Exposure of critical infrastructure components;
• Vulnerabilities identifiable through aerial surveillance;
• Cybersecurity risks associated with access to sensitive facilities; and
• Potential disruption of essential public services.

comments.commenter: Comment from Port of Los Angeles, Port of Long Beach, Port of Oakland
comments.organization: Port of Los Angeles, Port of Long Beach, Port of Oakland
comments.comment_text:
July 1, 2026

The Honorable Bryan Bedford
Administrator
Federal Aviation Administration
U.S. Department of Transportation
800 Independence Avenue SW
Washington, DC 20591

SUBJECT: COMMENTS ON THE FAA NOTICE OF PROPOSED RULEMAKING ON UNMANNED AIRCRAFT FLIGHT RESTRICTIONS (DOCKET NO. FAA-2026-4558)

Dear Administrator Bedford:

On behalf of the undersigned California ports, we appreciate the opportunity to comment on the Federal Aviation Administration's (FAA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) establishing a process for fixed-site facilities to request an Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restriction (UAFR). California's ports play a critical role in the national supply chain, international trade, and economic security, and therefore have a strong interest in ensuring that unmanned aircraft system (UAS) protections adequately address the unique risks present in complex maritime environments.

The comments below identify key areas where the proposed rule, as written, does not fully reflect the operational realities of major seaports, particularly those with extensive waterfront infrastructure. We respectfully offer recommendations to ensure the final rule aligns with both aviation-safety priorities and long-established maritime security frameworks administered by the U.S. Coast Guard.

1. Need for UAFR Coverage Over Waterside Areas Adjacent to Critical Waterfront Assets

Under the proposed rule, a Standard UAFR may only encompass the airspace directly above a fixed-site facility owned by an applicant. While this may function adequately for inland fixed-site facilities, it creates significant gaps for seaport facilities, where critical infrastructure often sits on waterfronts, adjacent to open waterways.

Many high-risk waterfront assets, such as passenger terminals, refined-product berths, liquid bulk facilities, container wharves, and security-sensitive utility corridors, are located along water channels, turning basins, slips, and other navigable waterways extending throughout port complexes. These same waterways frequently serve as the operational backbone of maritime commerce and as primary approach paths for vessels, public-safety agencies, and daily industrial marine operations.

Because public port authorities generally own the land but not the adjacent navigable waters, the proposed rule's strict property-line limitation prevents UAFRs from covering waterside approaches. This leaves substantial unprotected airspace in areas where drone incursions often occur, particularly offshore or over open water, where operators may exploit unobstructed approach paths to sensitive facilities.

To ensure meaningful protection of waterfront infrastructure, we respectfully request that the FAA revise the rule to allow UAFRs to extend over:

Water channels, basins, and slips that are controlled, managed, or operationally relied upon by a Port or other fixed-site facility;
Waterside approach zones essential to the security of waterfront critical infrastructure; and
Adjacent harbor or outer-harbor areas where drone threats routinely originate or transit.

Recognizing these waterfront areas within potential UAFR boundaries is essential to aligning the rule with the operational profiles of major seaports and with federal recognition that maritime critical infrastructure encompasses both land-side and water-side elements under the Maritime Transportation Security Act (MTSA).

2. Need for a Single Consolidated UAFR for the Entire Port Complex

Large seaports frequently operate under a landlord model in which a public port authority owns the property while multiple private tenants operate terminal and industrial facilities. Under the proposed rule, it is unclear whether each tenant would need to file separate UAFR applications.

A tenant-by-tenant approach would result in a fragmented patchwork of small, inconsistent, and potentially overlapping UAFR boundaries, despite the fact that seaports function as unified operational systems. Such fragmentation would create enforcement challenges, reduce situational awareness for airspace users, and undermine the clarity the UAFR framework is intended to provide.

We recommend that the FAA allow a single, consolidated UAFR application submitted by a public port authority or comparable facility operator on behalf of all tenants and operators within a port complex. A consolidated UAFR would:

Establish a coherent, clearly defined protected airspace;
Enhance enforceability and situational awareness for aviation and maritime stakeholders; and
Align with existing MTSA frameworks, in which port authorities, not individual tenants, are responsible for comprehensive, area-wide security planning.

3. Recognition of Existing Federally Mandated Maritime Security Assessments as Sufficient for Special UAFR Eligibility

The proposed rule proposes that Special UAFRs require a verified security assessment demonstrating credible threats. Ports already conduct rigorous federally mandated assessments under the MTSA framework, including:

Facility Security Assessments (FSAs) for regulated facilities; and
Area Maritime Security Assessments (AMSAs) overseen by the U.S. Coast Guard's Captain of the Port.

These assessments are detailed, comprehensive, and designed specifically to evaluate risks associated with waterfront critical infrastructure, including aerial-approach vulnerabilities over water and from the maritime domain.

Requiring ports to produce duplicative FAA-specific assessments would increase administrative burden without improving security outcomes. We strongly urge FAA to formally recognize MTSA FSAs and AMSAs as acceptable and sufficient documentation for Special UAFR qualification. This will maintain interagency consistency, reduce redundant work, and ensure that drone-airspace determinations remain aligned with the vetted security posture already established by federal maritime authorities.

Thank you for the opportunity to comment on this important rulemaking. California's ports stand ready to collaborate with the FAA to ensure that drone-related airspace protections are harmonized with existing maritime security practices and remain responsive to the operational needs of the nation's most critical gateways.

We look forward to ongoing coordination as the FAA refines and implements the UAFR framework.

Sincerely,

EUGENE D. SEROKA
Executive Director
Port of Los Angeles

DR. NOEL HACEGABA
Chief Executive Officer
Port of Long Beach

KRISTI MCKENNEY
Executive Director
Port of Oakland

comments.commenter: Comment from Alliance for Telecommunications Industry Solutions
comments.comment_text:
Before the
Federal Aviation Administration
Department of Transportation
Washington, D.C. 20591
In the Matter of )
)
Designation — Restrict the Operation of ) Docket No. FAA-2026-4558;
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a ) Notice No. 26-03
Fixed Site Facility ) RIN 2120-AL33
)
)
COMMENTS OF THE ALLIANCE FOR TELECOMMUNICATIONS INDUSTRY
SOLUTIONS
The Alliance for Telecommunications Industry Solutions (ATIS) respectfully submits these
comments in response to the Federal Aviation Administration's (FAA) Notice of Proposed
Rulemaking (NPRM) on the designation of Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs) near
fixed site facilities.
I. Introduction
ATIS is a leading global standards development and technical planning organization that
advances technical and operational standards for the information and communications
technologies (ICT) sector. Its diverse membership spans key ICT stakeholders, including
wireless, wireline, and VoIP service providers, equipment manufacturers, broadband providers,
software developers, consumer electronics firms, public safety agencies, and internet service
providers. As a founding partner and the North American Organizational Partner of the Third
Generation Partnership Project (3GPP), ATIS plays a pivotal role in shaping global wireless
specifications, including 4G LTE, 5G NR, and now 6G.
ATIS' Next G Alliance (NGA) is the preeminent North American authority on 6G technology,
charged with advancing United States leadership in next-generation wireless networks.1 The
NGA’s membership spans the full ecosystem required to deploy advanced network capabilities at
national scale: carriers, vendors, universities, research institutions, and government partners.
ATIS also convenes the ATIS Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles (UAV) Initiative, a dedicated program
developing technical standards and policy frameworks for unmanned aircraft integration using
mobile network infrastructure.2
1 More information on ATIS’ Next G Alliance can be found at https://nextgalliance.org/.
2 More information on ATIS’ Uncrewed Aerial Vehicles (UAV) Initiative can be found at
https://atis.org/initiatives/uncrewed-aerial-vehicles-uavs/.

ATIS supports the FAA's objective of protecting critical infrastructure from the safety and
security risks posed by unauthorized Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS) operations and
commends the agency for its balanced approach to preserving airspace access for legitimate UAS
operators. These comments identify two active ATIS workstreams with direct relevance to the
questions the FAA has raised in this proceeding. ATIS stands ready to serve as an ongoing
resource to the agency as the outcome of this rulemaking is implemented.
II. The Enforcement Challenge and the Role of the Mobile Network
The NPRM is candid about a fundamental limitation of the proposed framework: the UAFR is a
legal designation, not a physical barrier. Law abiding UAS operators will comply with the
restriction; the safety and security risk will flow from those who do not. The FAA explicitly
acknowledges this and solicits comment on what technological or procedural requirements
should govern UAS access within UAFRs, including how UAS operators should establish their
credibility and whether requirements beyond Remote ID are appropriate.
ATIS submits that the mobile network is uniquely positioned to serve as a technology substrate
for UAFR access management and enforcement support. Mobile Network Operator (MNO)
infrastructure already covers virtually every fixed site facility likely to seek a UAFR in the
United States. It offers network-authenticated identity, two-way communication, and persistent
tracking capabilities that complement and, in key respects, improve upon broadcast-based
identification systems. Remote ID is necessary but not sufficient for the UAFR access-control
problem. As a broadcast protocol dependent on UAS operator compliance and local receiver
infrastructure, it provides no network-authenticated identity verification and no real-time
communication capability between a UAS operator and a facility.
Two ATIS workstreams are developing the standards and technical frameworks to realize the
mobile network's potential in this context. Both are in the initial but meaningful stages, and ATIS
submits them here to inform the FAA's awareness of this emerging work rather than to propose
specific regulatory requirements at this time.
III. Universal Secure Electronic Conspicuity via Mobile Networks
ATIS’ UAV Initiative has begun work on a framework for Universal Secure Electronic
Conspicuity (USEC) using mobile networks. USEC is a dual-mode architecture in which MNO-
based cellular connectivity serves as the primary layer for UAS identification, authorization, and
tracking, with PC5/A2X direct sidelink communication providing a complementary channel
where network connectivity may be limited. This work is aligned with the U.S. Department of
Transportation’s (DOT) Mobile Network Aviation Assessment Program (MNAAP), an effort
assessing cellular technologies as the foundation for next-generation airspace conspicuity and
deconfliction.
USEC is directly relevant to the questions the FAA has raised in this proceeding. The NPRM
asks what information a UAS operator should provide to establish credibility and demonstrate

they are not a security threat, and whether there are additional technological requirements
beyond Remote ID that should govern access through a UAFR. Network-authenticated identity,
confirmed through the UAS operator's MNO, offers a meaningfully higher assurance signal than
broadcast Remote ID alone, with the added benefit of leveraging infrastructure already in place
at or near virtually every eligible facility in the country.
ATIS filed comments in the FCC's related "Unleashing American Drone Dominance" proceeding
(GN Docket 26-74) introducing the USEC framework and will continue to advance it through
standards development and government engagement. The work is promising but still maturing,
and ATIS does not propose specific USEC-related regulatory requirements at this stage. We raise
it here to ensure the FAA is aware of this development as it shapes the UAFR access framework
and companion Advisory Circular guidance, and to signal ATIS' availability as a technical
resource as the standards develop.
IV. ISAC-Enabled Sensing and Airspace Awareness
Integrated Sensing and Communication (ISAC) is a foundational capability of 6G networks that
combines radar-like spatial sensing with wireless communications within a unified system.
Rather than requiring separate sensing infrastructure, an ISAC-enabled network uses its existing
radio access equipment to simultaneously communicate with connected devices and sense the
surrounding environment -- detecting the presence, movement, and characteristics of objects
within its coverage area, whether those objects are network-connected or not.
This capability is directly relevant to the UAFR enforcement challenge. USEC addresses
cooperative UAS whose operators are flying in compliance with network-layer conspicuity
requirements. ISAC addresses the broader airspace picture, including non-cooperative UAS that
broadcast no identity signal or are operating without authorization. A non-cooperative drone is,
by definition, the highest-risk scenario a UAFR is designed to address, and ISAC offers the
prospect of persistent airspace awareness within and around a UAFR boundary without requiring
facility-specific detection hardware.
The Next G Alliance launched the ISAC Data Initiative (ISAC–DI) in June 2026 to address one
of the central practical challenges in realizing ISAC's value: how sensing data generated by 6G-
enabled networks is synthesized, packaged, and delivered to operational decision-making
systems in standardized, usable forms.3 Drone detection and tracking is a key use case for ISAC
and the ISAC-DI will help ensure that the information obtained from MNO ISAC systems can be
delivered effectively to agencies such as the FAA, the Department of Homeland Security, and the
Department of War, who also have requirements for these capabilities.
As with USEC, ATIS raises the ISAC-DI here to inform the FAA's awareness of this work, not to
propose specific regulatory requirements. The initiative has launched, and the standards for data
3 More information on the Next G Alliance ISAC Data Initiative (ISAC-DI) can be found at
https://nextgalliance.org/initiative/isac-data-initiative/.

packaging, delivery interfaces, and interoperability are in development. ATIS anticipates that this
work will become increasingly relevant to the UAFR framework as it progresses, and we
welcome the opportunity to keep the FAA informed.
V. Conclusion and Request for Engagement
ATIS supports the UAFR rulemaking's core objectives and the FAA's balanced approach to
protecting critical infrastructure while preserving legitimate airspace access. The mobile network
represents a significant and underutilized resource for making UAFRs operationally effective.
ATIS is actively working on projects that will make network-based conspicuity and airspace
sensing interoperable and deployable at scale.
Both workstreams are at early stages, and ATIS is not in a position to propose specific regulatory
requirements at this time. We respectfully request two acknowledgements of the FAA:
1. That the FAA take note of the USEC and ISAC-DI workstreams as they develop the
companion Advisory Circular and implementation guidance for the UAFR
framework, recognizing that network-based solutions are likely to become relevant to
both access management and enforcement support as the technology matures; and
2. That the FAA engage with ATIS through the ATIS UAV Initiative and the Next G
Alliance ISAC-DI so that agency staff can remain informed of progress and so that
ATIS can ensure that its work is responsive to FAA's operational and regulatory
requirements.
ATIS stands ready to brief FAA staff on these workstreams and serve as a technical resource as
these standards are developed and mature.
Respectfully submitted,
David Young
Vice President, Technology Policy and Government Relations
Alliance for Telecommunications Industry Solutions
1200 G Street, NW
Suite 500
Washington, D.C. 20005
(202) 628-6380
July 6, 2026

Agricultural transparency is vital in a democratic society. There should be no regulations restricting legally operated drones from accessing footage needed to educate the public. Accountability is also paramount in a democracy and limiting access to how our food supply is being managed is nefarious.

As a certified Part 107 commercial drone photographer specializing in real estate, I am deeply concerned about the proposed Section 2209 flight restrictions.While I respect the need to secure critical infrastructure, this rule creates severe operational and economic challenges for small businesses like mine:Unpredictable No-Fly Zones: Restricting airspace around everyday structures will ground real estate marketing for hundreds of nearby residential homes, hurting homeowners and local economies.Mapping and Visibility Issues: If these restricted zones are not instantly integrated into standard LAANC and B4UFLY apps, safe pilots risk accidentally drifting into restricted areas.No Fast-Track Access: Commercial pilots need a rapid, automated waiver process to safely photograph properties that happen to border these sensitive sites.I urge the FAA to mandate real-time digital mapping for all Section 2209 restrictions and create a fast-tracked access system for certified Part 107 operators before finalizing this rule.

Comments on NPRM: Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (Section 2209)

As an FAA Part 107 certified remote pilot who operates drones strictly for commercial enterprise, I am writing to oppose the "advanced notice" transit requirement in the proposed Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs). Piling administrative hurdles onto vetted professionals is an ineffective security strategy that penalizes those already following the rules while doing nothing to deter bad actors or uncertified hobbyists.

The security concerns driving this NPRM are not caused by Part 107 pilots. Commercial operators invest significant time and money to study airspace, pass federal examinations, and undergo TSA background checks to ensure safe and responsible operations. Our drones are tools for business, not toys for social media fame. The root of the security risk stems from uncertified flyers who can easily purchase highly capable, enterprise-grade hardware without any verification of licensing, training, or intent.

Instead of creating an ambiguous, burdensome pre-flight notification system that disrupts time-sensitive commercial workflows, the FAA should leverage the technology it has already mandated: Remote ID.

Rather than treating every drone silhouette as an anonymous threat, the FAA should implement an extra data transfer protocol within the Remote ID broadcast framework. This protocol would allow a certified pilot’s Remote ID signal to transmit a secure, verified token indicating "Part 107 Certified Status."

When transiting a standard UFR, a facility's security team would instantly see that a vetted, background-checked professional is in the airspace. This solves the facility's security concern through automated data tracking without forcing a business owner to file redundant paperwork for every transit.

I urge the FAA to reject the manual notification model for Part 107 pilots. Instead, the final rule should grant a complete transit exemption to Part 107 operators, utilizing an updated Remote ID protocol to broadcast our verified professional status to ground security in real-time.

I am writing as a recreational drone hobbyist to strongly oppose several provisions in the proposed framework for Section 2209 Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs). While facility security is important, the current proposal creates unsafe, unworkable, and highly discriminatory restrictions on the hobbyist community.Furthermore, the rule is highly ambiguous and open to subjective interpretation by facility managers, creating a fragmented regulatory environment that makes compliance nearly impossible for the average flyer. Discriminatory Airspace RestrictionsThe proposal unfairly targets unmanned aircraft under the guise of security. If a facility represents a legitimate "airspace vulnerability" severe enough to warrant a flight ban, then that risk applies to all aircraft. Singling out drones while allowing manned aircraft to operate freely over the same low-altitude airspace undermines the logic of the security threat. If the airspace is genuinely unsafe or vulnerable, it should be restricted via the existing Temporary Flight Restriction (TFR) framework for all operators, rather than creating a dual-standard system that penalizes drone hobbyists.3. Unacceptable Private Access to Pilot DataI vehemently oppose any mechanism that allows private business entities or facility operators to access FAA databases to retrieve pilot registration or personal data. Granting private corporations access to sensitive federal databases poses severe privacy and security risks for pilots. Hobbyists should not be subjected to tracking, profiling, or harassment by private security personnel simply for operating in public airspace. The FAA must maintain strict data privacy and ensure that pilot information is only accessible to law enforcement agencies under established legal protocols.For these reasons, I urge the FAA to reject the current notification mandates, eliminate private data sharing, and utilize existing, uniform airspace restriction mechanisms that apply fairly to all aviators.

§ 74.250 (a)(2) of the proposed rule, proposes that operations within a UAFR must transit a UAFR in the shortest amount of time practicable.  While there is some comment on the type of operations that would be permitted, it would be important to outline specific operations that would be permitted.  For example, a Part 107 pilot frequently needs to capture images or video of a property or facility, not only from directly above that property, but also at an angle from the property.  This requires that the UAS must hover over an adjacent property, or repeatedly transit the adjacent property in order to do mapping tasks in a grid-like pattern.  If specific operations such as this were included in the rule as acceptable in § 74.250 (a)(2) that would eliminate confusion and stress with the pilot and the facility's security personnel.  A better solution might also be to allow the pilot to specify the type of operations that will be need over the boundaries of the UAFR as part of the notification so that the pilot and the security personnel of the fixed site facility can both be clear about what is taking place.

Drone photography is in the public's interest. It exposes what doors conceal. By limiting drone photography, it emboldens and individuals and especially corporations to carry out cruelty without witnesses or consequence.

I URGE YOU STRENUOUSLY:

Do NOT restrict drone activity for legal certified drone operators, in PARTICULAR as regards to farms of any kind, including animal farms, factory farms, or any other area in which animals are enclosed for commercial profit.

WE THE PEOPLE demand FULL TRANSPARENCY as to where our food comes from - and we are NOT willing to just take a company's word for it. There needs to be unrestricted airways that allow us, the AMERICAN PEOPLE to see what is happening on and around and inside: factory farms, wild public lands, BLM controlled lands where wild horses roam, rodeos and commercial dog breeding facilities.

Re: Docket No. FAA-2026-4558

I am a certificated remote pilot and small-business UAS operator. I support protecting critical infrastructure, but those measures must be balanced against lawful access to the National Airspace System by responsible operators.

I generally agree with the concerns and recommendations expressed by Greg Reverdiau of Pilot Institute. Established and identifiable operators should retain a practical way to operate within or transit a Standard UAFR when otherwise lawful. A pilot using a registered aircraft, broadcasting Remote ID, and providing advance notification should not be treated as an unidentified threat. Recreational operators under 49 U.S.C. § 44809 also should not be categorically excluded if they meet appropriate identification and safety requirements.

I recommend that the final rule include:

1. A trusted-operator and trusted-aircraft whitelist.

The FAA should create a voluntary whitelist allowing operators to verify credentials, aircraft registration, Remote ID serial number, contact information, and applicable waivers or authority. Once verified, an operator should not have to repeatedly provide the same information to each facility. The whitelist would not grant unrestricted access, but would confirm that an aircraft is identifiable and tied to a known operator.

2. An integrated electronic notification system.

UAFR notification should be built into FAA-recognized flight-planning applications in a manner similar to LAANC. When a planned flight intersects a UAFR, the application should identify the restriction, accept the location, date, time, aircraft, and purpose, notify the facility and FAA, and generate a timestamped confirmation.

Pilots should not have to locate a facility manager, call a security desk, leave a voicemail, or send an email without knowing whether it was received. Electronic notification and documented receipt would protect both parties.

Notification must remain notification, not a request for private permission. A facility should not be able to block an otherwise lawful operation by refusing to respond or creating its own approval process.

3. Facility funding for the notification system.

Facilities receiving special protection over portions of the National Airspace System receive the primary benefit and should fund the system through application fees, renewal fees, or another appropriate mechanism. These costs should not be shifted to pilots or small UAS businesses, and pilots should not be charged for each required notification.

4. A clear prohibition on unauthorized interference.

A UAFR must not provide or imply authority for a facility owner, employee, contractor, or private security officer to jam or spoof signals, take control of an aircraft, force it to land, capture or seize it, shoot at it, damage or destroy it, direct another person to interfere with it, or attempt any of those actions.

Such interference can cause a crash into people, vehicles, buildings, roads, power infrastructure, or other property, creating a greater hazard than the flight itself. Every UAFR applicant should acknowledge in writing that the restriction grants no counter-UAS or aircraft-interference authority, and security personnel should be trained accordingly.

5. Meaningful consequences for intentional interference.

The FAA should coordinate with the Department of Justice and Congress, where necessary, to ensure clear criminal penalties for anyone who knowingly and intentionally jams, seizes, forces down, damages, or destroys a lawful unmanned aircraft without specific statutory counter-UAS authority.

Consequences should include FAA civil penalties, criminal referral, restitution for the aircraft, payload, lost data, property damage, and direct losses, possible suspension or cancellation of the facility’s UAFR, preservation of relevant records, and a formal reporting process.

A facility receiving protected-airspace benefits should not be permitted to intentionally endanger an aircraft, its operator, people on the ground, or surrounding property.

6. Narrow, visible, and reviewable restrictions.

Each UAFR should use the smallest reasonable horizontal and vertical dimensions necessary to address a documented threat. Boundaries, altitudes, effective dates, contact procedures, and notification requirements should be published electronically and integrated into commonly used flight-planning applications.

Restrictions should have expiration dates and require periodic review and renewal. They should not unnecessarily prevent lawful work on adjacent properties or create large private exclusion zones over public airspace.

Responsible and identifiable pilots should be treated as partners in aviation safety, not presumed threats. A verified whitelist, automated notification, documented receipts, narrowly tailored restrictions, and strong penalties for unauthorized interference would improve security while preserving lawful aviation access.

As a certified remote pilot, I am concerned that a multi-layer patchwork of regulations is emerging here. The FAA has the authority via the Interstate Commerce Clause and airspace authorities to implement a single standard.

Instead of having 50 different state laws on CIKR plus new federal rules, it would make sense for pilots operating under the federal procedure to be exempt from state laws that may apply to a facility.

If FAA cannot accomplish this, it really makes no sense to add another layer of confusion atop poorly-designed state and local rules.

This was submitted previously, BUT received a notice stating it was not accepted.
------------
​
I am a recreational certified drone operator of only a year, now two years.
I was a freelance-photographer since the late 70s, and am also a military veteran/retiree in my third year of receiving VA care for PTSD among other things. I am a many decades photographer, and discovered droning through a friend, and was and still always envious of his drone imagery. Now I also own a drone, and the perspective it gives me is amazing. I image through my drone many things and subjects: architecture, parks where allowed, and also do a lot of street work of landmarks and interesting structures, etc. I also capture cloudscapes, landscapes, sunsets and more. I don't do any crazy flights and am very respectful of property and people; I fly my drone up, snap my picture, rotate to admire the scenery and perhaps snap a few more, then down. I rarely see anyone else droning while I'm active. Before I bring my drone down, my head rotates like a spinning top, to stay aware of my surroundings, and also not interfere or endanger anyone while landing my drone. This drone hobby gives me a skill, opportunity and perspective that is unlike any other imagery type I get with my still cameras at street level. There are many other droners like me who ARE respectful of property and others.
Thank you for allowing me to express myself and how drone flights calm my mind - I am being treated for PTSD, among other things - and while we [am married] used to stay indoors a LOT, my caring lovely patient wife loves that we finally get out during the day, even if it's just for flying with the drone. I hate studying - didn't even make it through junior high BUT do have a GED - and if I could ever get over my hatred of tests/studying, I will love to take the test for the part 107. There are apparently a lot of droners like me, who use the drone to settle the nerves and to think normally again, and using the drone as an activity to take us back into the social Life we somehow abandoned to easing my Life and mind.
I cannot speak for others, nor for other veterans who might fly a drone for my same reasons, BUT I do know, and my wife can attest, that IF you take away our drones, you'll take away our Lives.

I had a experience where I was switching inhalation hazard cars out and there was a drone flying above me not knowing if it was a intruder watching our operations was very distracting and dangerous it was during the fall in 2025 .
Matt George b164556 of BNSF railway

As seen by FDC 4/3634 allowing commercial operators to apply for a flight restriction will lead to widespread abuse. That flight restriction serves no security purpose and only is to limit commercial exploitation of the airspace.

Any restrictions should be short term in nature and be required to provide proof of a documented threat and not a general security issue. As otherwise we will have a collection of Disney style flight restrictions that provide no security purpose and only limits non-part 107 access to the NAS.

This proposed rule does not go far enough by exempting private citizens from its protections.  Businesses deemed "critical infrastructure" should have access to UAFR protection because of the physical and information-gathering threats UAS systems pose to them and - hence - the threat they pose to our national industry as whole.  Makes sense.  However, private citizens have just as much at stake.  A drone losing power or an operator losing control of a drone can damage the private property they overfly or seriously injure the people on or in it.  A drone equipped with a camera operating over a private citizen's back yard can transmit and record what the property owners feel is for there eyes only, violating their "reasonable expectation to privacy."  The majority of states or municipalities have NOT enacted laws to preclude such activity, but this is a perfect opportunity for Uncle Sam to step up.  This proposed rule also negates the very real vulnerabilities drones pose to our private population (i.e., the consumers) in favor of our "critical infrastructure" (i.e., big business) who will receive all the protection they could want or need.  The legal asymmetry is glaringly obvious and tees up sufficient opportunity for impassioned and unproductive debate in the public sphere.

I am currently a recreational flyer with plans on getting my part 107. I don't think safe recreational flyers with RID should be blocked from flying completely. Maybe have recreational pilot do safety test or something first. Work on the rule to where it's safe and everyone can be included if they fly safely. Or maybe just not sign it into law, because I don't want to be excluded from flying. I already feel like I'm losing the hobby, with everything DJI is being put through is disgusting.

comments.commenter: Comment from National Cattlemen's Beef Association
comments.comment_text:
July 06, 2026
Mr. Bryan Beford
Administrator
Federal Aviation Administration
800 Independence Avenue, SW
Washington, DC 20591
RE: Proposed Rulemaking, “Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in
Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility” (Docket No. FAA-2026-4558)
Dear Administrator Bedford,
On May 6, 2026, the U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT), through the Federal Aviation
Administration (FAA), published a proposed rulemaking entitled “Designation-Restrict the
Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility.”1 This rulemaking
follows President Trump’s Executive Order on June 6, 2025, “Restoring American Airspace
Sovereignty,” to ensure the safety and security of American airspace while also protecting the
critical assets and privacy of domestic operators.2 The National Cattlemen’s Beef Association
(NCBA) urges the FAA to consider the sensitivities and vulnerabilities of beef-producing
operations in food and agricultural production and the threats posed by outsider-operated
Unmanned Aircraft (UA).
NCBA is the oldest and largest trade association representing American cattle producers — the
largest single sector of American agriculture. We have 26,000 direct members and total 178,000
members through our 44 state affiliates. 85 percent of NCBA’s members are farmers and
ranchers in the cow-calf sector, the foundational first step of our supply chain. These operations
are overwhelmingly family-owned and -operated, with an average herd size of less than 50.
