NCBA applauds the Administration’s commitment to securing U.S. property owners' airspace
from foreign and domestic threats as well as protecting the integrity of the Fourth Amendment of
the Constitution and private property rights. Today, beef operations across all 50 states, including
cow-calf farms, backgrounders, feedlots, and processing plants, produce the safest and most
wholesome supply of beef ever experienced, with the lowest cattle inventory recorded in 75
years, but consistently face threats of privacy encroachment and burdensome liability due to the
trespassing of Unmanned Aircraft. While the emergence and innovation of airspace technologies
have proved effective in advancing precision agriculture and resource retention, actors within the
government and public activists are not barred from abusing these technologies, using the intel
gained as a weapon against farms and ranches. Food security is national security, and cattle
country remains at the forefront of providing the safest and most enriching protein for
consumers.
1 Federal Register. Department of Transportation. Federal Aviation Administration. Designation-Restrict the Operation of
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility (2026).
https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2026/05/06/2026-08943/designation-restrict-the-operation-of-unmanned-aircraft-in-
close-proximity-to-a-fixed-site-facility
2 The White House. Restoring American Airspace Sovereignty (2025). https://www.whitehouse.gov/presidential-
actions/2025/06/restoring-american-airspace-sovereignty/

The Fourth Amendment protects private property owners from unlawful searches and seizures by
authorities, requiring that a warrant be issued based on probable cause. Farmers’ and ranchers’
operations are agriculturally developed and should be treated as curtilage that remains protected
under this staple of the Constitution. These facilities should not be ostracized from other critical
infrastructure sites. Federal, state, and local authorities are subject to the same laws of the
Constitution to ensure the privacy and lawful security of all Americans. Therefore, a state’s
authority to inspect property, specifically related to the Clean Water Act, should be exercised
through on-site review and not through inconspicuous or unauthorized aerial surveillance.
Furthermore, protecting these rights and authorities stabilizes producers’ day-to-day operations
and guards their work from burdensome liability and litigation, not scientifically or lawfully
upheld. Upholding a Standard Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restriction (UAFR) for these food and
agricultural properties underscores the President’s directive to protect American airspace
sovereignty domestically and privately.
Intrusion by trespassing is not infrequent or unusual for producers in rural America. However,
the emergence of aerial trespassing significantly increases risks for beef producers throughout
the entire supply chain.3 So-called “activism” has become increasingly threatening, violent, and
harmful by individuals opposing traditional animal agriculture. Failing to protect the security and
privacy of operations opens the doors for bad actors to commit crimes on farms, ranches,
feedlots, and processing plants across the U.S. Aerial trespassing allows individuals to enter the
airspace above operations and capture intel and survey the landscape, which can be used as a
potential weapon for unlawful actions such as releasing, injuring, or burglarizing livestock.4
Moreover, this information can be utilized to target infrastructure and inventory for vandalism,
contamination, or destruction, which can lead to operational and supply chain disruptions.5 These
threats and acts of violence highlight the need to safeguard beef operations and ensure farmers
and processors can continue growing the strongest and safest beef supply.
The free-roaming usage of UA technologies poses serious concerns to cattle safety and welfare,
structural soundness, and emergency management. Consistent aerial flyovers can stress cattle
and negatively alter their behaviors, including movement to preferred grazing and water sources.
Low-flying crafts intimidate and scare cattle that have not been exposed to this specific
technology before, causing cattle to flee through fencing boundaries or other infrastructure,
increasing the opportunity for injury. Moreover, these crafts could damage working site
equipment and sensitive structures involved in daily operations, providing feed, water, and
quality assurance. Any damage to structures or wreckage of UA can be potentially hazardous,
igniting fires that can affect not only the individual operation but also the surrounding landscape
and environment. Usage of this technology in operations should be at the discretion of the owner,
who best understands the risks and layout of all critical infrastructure points.
The current inclusion of food and agriculture facilities as critical infrastructure sites, eligible for
a UAFR, is a proactive step in protecting the privacy of individual owners’ farms and ranches
and larger operations, ensuring government or public interference does not stifle production.
Additionally, this inclusion combats aerial trespass by individuals seeking to harm, deter, or steal
livestock and equipment and shields operation owners from unnecessary liability associated with
3 The Ohio State University College of Food, Agricultural, and Environmental Sciences. Dealing With the Threat of
Intentional Harm to Farm Property (2020). https://farmoffice.osu.edu/blog/thu-12172020-158pm/dealing-threat-
intentional-harm-farm-property
4 Fox News. Animal ‘Rescuers’ Wreaking Havoc By Stealing Farm Animals: ‘These are Criminals, Not Activists’ (2023).
https://www.foxnews.com/media/animal-rescuers-wreaking-havoc-stealing-farm-animals-criminals-activists
5 Farm Progress. Threats, Violence Directed Toward Livestock Farms (2007).
https://www.farmprogress.com/management/threats-violence-directed-toward-livestock-farms
2

these actions. Cattle country is currently operating in a precarious time. Drought and wildfire are
plaguing the industry, and input costs remain elevated. Producers should not be distressed by the
presence of unlawful individuals who threaten their animals, infrastructure, land, and livelihood.
The National Cattlemen’s Beef Association appreciates the consideration provided to the beef
industry regarding UA and encourages the FAA to continue supporting the industry through the
rulemaking process.
Thank you,
National Cattlemen’s Beef Association
3

C C A
ALIFORNIA ATTLEMEN’S SSOCIATION
3841 N. FREEWAY BLVD., SUITE #130, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95834
SERVING THE CATTLE PHONE: (916) 444-0845
COMMUNITY SINCE 1917 FAX: (916) 444-2194
www.calcattlemen.org
Submitted online via regulations.gov
July 6, 2026
Docket Operations
U.S. Department of Transportation
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE, Room W58-213
Washington, DC 20590
RE: Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a
Fixed Site Facility (Docket No. FAA-2026-4558)
To whom it may concern:
The California Cattlemen’s Association (CCA) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments
responsive to the Federal Aviation Administration’s notice of proposed rulemaking to establish a
process for fixed site facilities to request and maintain unmanned aircraft flight restrictions in the
airspace above their operations. CCA is a statewide trade association representing more than 1,700
cattle ranchers and beef producers throughout the state of California. CCA’s purpose is “Nourishing
people, caring for livestock and sustaining the environment for all Californians.”
CCA strongly supports FAA’s establishment of a process by which fixed site facilities may request
unmanned aircraft flight restrictions, and we urge FAA to broadly classify ranching operations as
fixed site facilities eligible to apply for such restrictions. Below, we outline the numerous, significant
threats that unmanned aircraft such as drones pose to ranching operations and provide brief
responses to FAA’s questions regarding criteria applicable to the food and agriculture sector.
Risks of Unmanned Aircraft to Livestock Operations
Unmanned aircraft, such as drones, present a wide range of risks for livestock operations. Drones
directly and indirectly harm animals, can enhance risk of car strikes, can introduce biosecurity
threats, and pose enhanced wildfire risk in remote agricultural areas.
Direct and indirect harm to livestock animals: Collisions with obstacles such as trees, power
lines, or telecommunications poles can damage drones, causing them to fall to the earth.1 When
drones are operated over areas where livestock are heavily concentrated – such as a sale yard or a
confined animal feeding operation – such uncontrolled descent risks direct harm to livestock below.
Additionally, drones are “likely to cause negative stress effects” in livestock.2 Livestock which are
exposed to birds of prey in their natural environment exhibit “a greater unease” when exposed to
1 FRENCH LIVESTOCK INSTITUTE, DRONES IN LIVESTOCK FARMING: OPPORTUNITIES, RISKS AND BEST PRACTICES 26
(2025).
2 Anders Herlin et al., Animal Welfare Implications of Digital Tools for Monitoring and Management of Cattle and Sheep on Pasture,
11 ANIMALS 821 (2021).
RICK ROBERTI BEV BIGGER JAMES HENDERSON STEVE LAMBERT
PRESIDENT TREASURER SECOND VICE PRESIDENT SECOND VICE PRESIDENT
LOYALTON VENTURA BILLY GATLIN WOODLAKE OROVILLE
EXECUTIVE VICE PRESIDENT
MIKE WILLIAMS MIKE SULPIZIO TEMPLETON ANTHONY STORNETTA JACK HOEKSTRA
FIRST VICE PRESIDENT FEEDER COUNCIL CHAIR SECOND VICE PRESIDENT FEEDER COUNCIL VICECHAIR
ACTON BRAWLEY ATASCADERO OAKDALE

drones, which the animals may mistake for predatory birds.3 Drones which move quickly or
erratically may also stress livestock.4 Stress from improperly operated drones could cause livestock to
flee, increasing the risk that animals will injure themselves on rough terrain or even by driving
themselves through infrastructure such as fences. Additionally, increased stress in livestock has been
associated with loss of pregnancies, lower weigh gain, and lower body condition scores.
Indirect risk to humans: As discussed above, a drone pilot inexperienced in animal husbandry may
stress livestock with swift or erratic movements. External stressors often cause livestock to flee, and
animals have been observed knocking down fencing to escape perceived threats. Livestock escaping
a ranch may stray onto adjacent roads and highways, increasing the risk of vehicle strikes which
could catastrophically harm motorists.
More perniciously, drones could be used to intentionally herd animals away from ranches, feedyards,
livestock markets, or slaughter facilities. In recent years, radical animal rights groups have
increasingly used drones as a propaganda tool to cause reputational harm to confined animal feeding
operations.5 These groups have also engaged in the theft of livestock from ranch operations,
euphemistically termed “open rescue.”6 It is foreseeable that these groups could marry these efforts
in the future, using drones to drive livestock through fencelines in a misguided effort to “rescue”
them. Such actions would not only harm ranchers’ operations, they could also pose substantial risks
to motorists on highways adjoining rural ranches. Likewise, it is foreseeable that criminals, terrorists,
or other bad actors wishing to inflict damage upon the populace could use such tactics to create
mayhem, destruction, injuries, and even deaths.
Biosecurity risks: Drones flown above private agricultural operations typically take off from public
land. While farmers and ranchers often institute biosecurity controls for on-the-ground visitors to
the operation, they cannot impose biosecurity controls above their land. As a result, drones flown
above or landing upon private agricultural ground risk biological contamination, such as the
introduction of invasive weeds or pests to the agricultural operation. Drones which move between
multiple ranches also risk disease transmission from one herd to another. At a time when the
USDA, state agricultural agencies, and ranchers nationwide are actively working to combat the
threats of Avian Influenza and New World Screwworm, the FAA should seek to implement
regulations which enable ranchers to better protect the biosecurity of their operations.
Fire and wildfire risk: Most drones are powered by lithium-ion or lithium-polymer batteries. The
FAA recognizes that lithium batteries pose a fire hazard, noting that “Ignition of lithium metal
batteries can be caused when a battery short circuits, is overcharged, is heated to extreme
temperatures, is mishandled, or is otherwise defective.”7 Lithium batteries in drones operated above
rangelands could conceivably combust for a wide variety of reasons. For example, a lithium battery
could become overheated while operating in direct sunlight, or could become damaged after an
3 Id.
4 PURDUE UNIVERSITY EXTENSION, LIVESTOCK REACTION TO UAV FLIGHT,
https://extension.purdue.edu/uav/animal-agriculture/livestock-reaction-to-uav-flight-example.html.
5 Emily K. Upchurch, Comment, Drone on the Farm: The Benefits and Controversies Surrounding the Future of
Unmanned Aircraft Systems in Agriculture, 20 DRAKE J. OF AGRIC. L. 309, 323 (2015).
6 See, e.g., Robert Rodriguez, Animal Rights Activists Charged Criminally for Taking Goats from Kings County Dairy, THE
FRESNO BEE, Mar. 30, 2026.
7 FEDERAL AVIATION ADMINISTRATION, SAFETY ALERT FOR OPERATORS 10017, RISKS IN TRANSPORTING LITHIUM
BATTERIES IN CARGO BY AIRCRAFT (2010).

uncontrolled fall to the ground caused by impact with infrastructure or birds. Rangelands are
comprised of grasses and other forage which are highly-combustible, are often situated in or near
forested environments, and may not be closely and actively monitored at all times. As a result, the
risks to “people and property on the ground” of a lithium battery fire originating on rangelands is
significant. The potential of such fires developing into large conflagrations may foreseeably implicate
national and homeland security, as well.
Response to Solicitations within the Notice of Proposed Rulemaking
The Notice of Proposed Rulemaking poses three questions to aid the FAA in developing criteria for
which “fixed site facilities” within the food and agriculture sector may request an unmanned aircraft
flight restriction. We address each question in brief below, but in short, we believe the potential risks
to agricultural infrastructure and the food supply chain are severe enough that FAA ought to permit
a wide range of food and agricultural sites to apply for unmanned aircraft flight restrictions.
1. What types of facilities within this sector should be considered for a UAFR? Specifically,
those that could have debilitating regional or national-level consequences.
CCA urges the FAA to consider most agricultural operations eligible to apply for UAFRs. While
operations where animals are confined – dairies, feedyards, auction yards, slaughter facilities, etc. –
are more likely to be targeted by bad actors and perhaps carry the greatest risks to the nation’s food
supply chain and food security, improper or nefarious use of unmanned aircraft above any
agricultural operations could pose regional or national threats. Drone-sparked wildfires could quickly
become serious regional emergencies necessitating state and national response, and biosecurity
threats to any ranch could quickly cascade into challenges for the regional and national food supply.
As such, FAA should consider allowing all farms, ranches, dairies, feed yards, auction yards,
slaughter facilities, and other agricultural operations to apply for UAFRs.
2. What characteristics should be considered in the development of gating criteria for those
facilities?
CCA urges the FAA not to impose arbitrary gating criteria to the food and agricultural sector.
Regardless of the size or economic footprint of a farm or ranch, harms from negligent or ill-
intended drone use could quickly spread to regional or national crises.
3. Should the population served or regional or national economic impact be considered in
the development of gating criteria, and if so, what are appropriate thresholds?
No, CCA does not believe that population or economic thresholds should be applied in determining
which fixed site facilities may apply for UAFR.
Conclusion
CCA appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the FAA’s proposal to establish a process
for fixed site facilities to request and maintain unmanned aircraft flight restrictions in the airspace
above their operations. Unmanned aircraft pose a multitude of threats to livestock operations, from
relatively localized impacts to individual livestock to regional and national concerns relating to
wildfire risk and biosecurity. Because these risks exist for virtually all agricultural operations with

potentially-significant impacts on regional and national infrastructure – including the broader food
supply chain – we urge FAA to make UAFR available to all agricultural operators.
Sincerely,
Kirk Wilbur
Vice President of Government Affairs

comments.commenter: Comment from Kentucky Department of Agriculture
comments.comment_text:
Corporate Drive Complex 502-573-0450
Frankfort, KY 40601 www.kyagr.com
July 6, 2026
Submitted via www.regulations.gov
RE: Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03 – Designation—
Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity
to a Fixed Site Facility
To whom it may concern:
Farm security is national security.
America’s food supply is not merely an economic asset—it is a strategic
national asset. The ability of the United States to feed itself, sustain global markets,
and maintain resilient domestic supply chains is inseparable from our national
security.
As Commissioner of Agriculture for the Commonwealth of Kentucky, I
appreciate the Federal Aviation Administration’s efforts to implement Section 2209
of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016 in a manner that balances
innovation in unmanned aircraft systems with legitimate security and safety
concerns. The proposed rule appropriately recognizes that eligibility for Unmanned
Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs) should be limited to facilities whose disruption
would have a demonstrable impact on public safety, national economic security, or
the resilience of critical infrastructure.
For too long, though, agriculture has been viewed solely through the lens of
production and commerce. In reality, agriculture is critical infrastructure every bit
as essential as energy, transportation, telecommunications, and water systems. A
nation that cannot secure its food supply cannot secure its future.
The United States Department of Homeland Security formally recognizes food
and agriculture as one of the Nation’s sixteen critical infrastructure sectors because
disruptions to agricultural production, processing, or distribution can undermine
economic stability, public confidence, and national resilience. America’s adversaries
understand this reality. Foreign animal diseases, cyberattacks against agricultural
supply chains, industrial espionage, intentional contamination, and the exploitation
of emerging technologies—including unmanned aircraft systems—represent evolving
threats to our food system.

Corporate Drive Complex 502-573-0450
Frankfort, KY 40601 www.kyagr.com
Unlike many other critical infrastructure sectors, agriculture encompasses
geographically dispersed production systems that ultimately depend upon a network
of fixed facilities responsible for animal health, food processing, storage,
transportation, chemical manufacturing, and supply chain continuity. A disruption
at a major processing facility, grain terminal, fertilizer depot, veterinary diagnostic
laboratory, or food distribution hub would not simply affect one company or one
community. Such an event could ripple across multiple states, increase food prices,
disrupt exports, threaten animal health, and weaken America’s ability to respond to
emergencies.
Our geopolitical competitors increasingly recognize that strategic advantage is
achieved not only through military strength, but through the ability to disrupt
essential supply chains. Protecting America’s food system must therefore be
understood as a core component of homeland security. Accordingly, the FAA should
ensure that the eligibility criteria for the food and agriculture sector adequately
reflect the modern realities of agricultural production and supply chains.
To that end, I strongly recommend that the FAA partner with USDA to develop
measurable eligibility thresholds for regionally and nationally significant
agricultural facilities and include them in a table similar to the Chemical Sector
portion of the proposed rulemaking. This type of collaborative regulation would
ensure measurable thresholds and would improve consistency, reduce administrative
burden, and limit requests for applicability determinations. Additionally, USDA
involvement would ensure the significance of the national security interest in the
recommended sites below and would eliminate the potential that thresholds set by
the FAA are too high and therefore inapplicable to smaller states or states with
differing agricultural strengths.
I recommend that the FAA adopt the following eligibility criteria for fixed-site
facilities within the food and agricultural sector.
First, eligibility should extend to facilities that represent significant
aggregation points or chokepoints within regional or national food supply chains.
Such facilities should include:
• Grain elevators, inland terminals, export terminals, and feed mills with
annual throughput exceeding a threshold established by the FAA in
consultation with the U.S. Department of Agriculture.
• Livestock transfer points, auctions, and markets, including transportation and
exchange points which may or may not be fixed site locations.
• Livestock breeding and reproduction facilities.

Corporate Drive Complex 502-573-0450
Frankfort, KY 40601 www.kyagr.com
• Meat processing, poultry processing, dairy processing, and food manufacturing
facilities that meet size, production volume, or workforce thresholds indicative
of regional or national significance.
• Cold storage and food distribution centers that serve multi-state supply chains
or support emergency food distribution networks.
Second, eligibility should include fixed facilities whose disruption could
materially affect animal health, plant health, or biosecurity, including:
• State and federal veterinary diagnostic laboratories.
• High-containment animal disease research facilities.
• Livestock quarantine facilities or other sites that are synonymous with animal
disease control.
• Seed repositories, germplasm centers, and facilities maintaining critical
genetic resources.
• Major livestock markets and animal concentration points with established
disease surveillance responsibilities, including sites that serve significant
regional or national agriculture, transportation, or supply-chain functions.
• Facilities supporting foreign animal disease response operations.
Third, eligibility should extend to facilities that manufacture, store, or
distribute essential agricultural inputs where a disruption could impair food
production capacity, including:
• Fertilizer manufacturing facilities and major distribution terminals.
• Crop protection product manufacturing and formulation facilities.
• Large-scale agricultural chemical storage sites.
• Animal feed ingredient processing facilities.
• Fuel terminals dedicated to agricultural operations.
Fourth, eligibility should include any farming operation with a defined
procurement relationship with an institutional purchaser, like a hospital, school or
university, and military installation.
Fifth, eligibility should include irrigation, water and wastewater management,
and agricultural energy infrastructure where the facility serves a substantial
agricultural production area and already satisfies applicable critical infrastructure
definitions.
Relative to all the above recommendations, eligibility should not be limited
solely to facilities under direct federal ownership or operation. Privately owned
facilities that satisfy objective, risk-based criteria should be afforded equal

Corporate Drive Complex 502-573-0450
Frankfort, KY 40601 www.kyagr.com
consideration. Additionally, eligibility should not be tethered to a size requirement.
Rather, the focus should be on the specific purpose of the fixed site facility, regardless
of its size.
The FAA has appropriately chosen a risk-based framework rather than a
blanket approach. By adopting generous but objective eligibility criteria for the food
and agriculture sector, the agency can protect genuinely critical facilities without
unnecessarily restricting legitimate unmanned aircraft operations or innovation in
precision agriculture.
At the same time, I strongly encourage the FAA to maintain clear guardrails
to ensure consistency with the intent of Section 2209. First, eligibility should apply
primarily to fixed-site facilities with clearly defined boundaries. However, in some
instances, targeted exceptions for specific non-fixed operations may be necessary for
full protection of the sector. Also, any boundary should be reasonably limited to
preserve drone instruction and education services that are necessary for ensuring
operators are properly trained for on-farm applications, whether commercially or
personally. Second, restrictions should preserve exemptions for authorized
agricultural aviation activities, emergency response operations, governmental
inspections, and FAA-approved commercial unmanned aircraft operations. Third,
applicants should demonstrate that disruption, sabotage, espionage, contamination,
or interference at the facility would create measurable consequences for food security,
public safety, animal health, or supply chain resilience. And finally, UAFRs should
remain geographically limited to the minimum area necessary to address the
identified risk.
As the FAA finalizes this rulemaking, it should recognize that safeguarding
agricultural infrastructure is not solely about protecting private property—it is about
protecting the American people. When farmers lose access to fertilizer, feed,
processing capacity, or animal health resources, consumers face higher prices and
greater uncertainty. When critical agricultural facilities are disrupted, rural
economies suffer, exports decline, and America’s strategic resilience is diminished.
Food security is homeland security.
I appreciate the opportunity to provide comments and encourage the FAA to
work closely with the U.S. Department of Agriculture, state departments of
agriculture, and industry stakeholders to finalize practical eligibility standards that
recognize agriculture’s indispensable role in securing our nation’s future.

Corporate Drive Complex 502-573-0450
Frankfort, KY 40601 www.kyagr.com
Thank you for your time and your careful consideration of this matter.
Respectfully submitted,
Jonathan Shell
Commissioner
Kentucky Department of Agriculture

July 6, 2026
Hon. Bryan Bedford
Administrator
Federal Aviation Administration
800 Independence Ave., SW
Washington, DC 20591
RE: Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site
Facility [Docket No. FAA-2026-4558]
Dear Administrator Bedford:
On behalf of the Commercial Space Federation (CSF), the leading trade association
representing the American space industry, thank you for the opportunity to comment on the
Federal Aviation Administration's (FAA) Proposed Rule on Designation-Restrict the Operation of
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility [Docket No. FAA-2026-4558].
As the FAA considers updating unmanned aircraft flight restriction (UAFR) eligibility, CSF
strongly encourages the FAA to extend UAFR eligibility to FAA-licensed spaceports, permitted
private space facilities, remote space infrastructure, and related space operations sites.
The space industry is a critical contributor to national security, economic development,
and scientific advancement, and ensuring the operational security of spaceports, space launch
and reentry facilities, and public/privately-owned space infrastructure is in the national interest.
Commercial space activity is expanding rapidly, operational cadence is increasing, and
significant investments are being made into both launch and non-launch ground infrastructure to
support critical national security priorities, such as the National Security Space Launch Program,
Golden Dome for America, and NASA's Artemis Program. These investments deserve
extraordinary protection.
This infrastructure includes not only launch sites but also vehicle manufacturing
facilities, rocket engine test stands, propellant production and storage facilities, spacecraft
integration centers, and research and development complexes—many of which are intentionally
located in remote areas due to safety, security, and operational requirements.
However, unlike similar facilities such as airports and certain military installations,
commercial space infrastructure—whether located at active spaceports or at standalone remote
sites—is only able to utilize temporary flight restrictions (TFRs) during active operations to
secure the airspace. Treating spaceports and private space infrastructure as a regulatory
afterthought to airports, rather than peer categories deserving the same protection, leaves an
increasingly important segment of national security infrastructure exposed to threats posed by
unauthorized unmanned aircraft system (UAS) activity.
Commercial Space Federation | 1301 Connecticut Avenue NW, Suite 250, Washington D.C.

The risk profile of spaceports, launch and reentry infrastructure, and remote private space
facilities makes them attractive, low-friction targets for unauthorized UAS activity. Many of
these facilities sit on remote or sparsely monitored land, hold high-value hardware and
proprietary technology in the open for extended periods, host hazardous materials including
cryogenic propellants and hypergolic fuels, and conduct sensitive testing operations.
Additionally, these sites draw recreational drone operators and photographers seeking close-up
footage of facilities, vehicles, and proprietary systems.
As noted in the Proposed Rule, "…commercial spaceports are susceptible to UAS
operations due to the high impact of drone incidents near these sites." This vulnerability extends
equally to private space infrastructure facilities that may be located hundreds of miles from
traditional spaceports but contain equally sensitive technology, hazardous operations, and
national security-relevant capabilities.
Securing space launch and reentry infrastructure, as well as associated manufacturing,
testing, and integration facilities, from unauthorized drone activity is critical to protecting U.S.
national security priorities while maintaining public safety and the protection of intellectual
property rights. Therefore, CSF respectfully recommends that the FAA designate FAA-licensed
commercial, private, and government spaceports, as well as private space infrastructure facilities
regardless of geographic location, as protected facilities for purposes of Section 2209 eligibility
and related counter-UAS authority.
Thank you for your consideration of this request to provide Section 2209 UAFR
eligibility to commercial space launch and reentry facilities, remote private space infrastructure,
and related operations sites. CSF looks forward to working with the FAA to ensure continued
American superiority in space.
Sincerely,
Dave Cavossa
President
Commercial Space Federation
Commercial Space Federation | 1301 Connecticut Avenue NW, Suite 250, Washington D.C.

comments.commenter: Comment from Associated General Contractors of America
comments.comment_text:
Rick Andritsch, President
Trey Pebley, Senior Vice President
Don Weaver, Vice President
Doug Maibach, Treasurer
Jeffrey D. Shoaf, Chief Executive Officer
Jimmy Christianson, Chief Operating Officer
July 6, 2025
Ms. Caitlin Locke
Associate Administrator, Aviation Safety
Federal Aviation Administration
800 Independence Avenue SW
Washington D.C. 20591
Re: Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed
Site Facility, Docket no. Docket No. FAA-2026-4558.
Dear Ms. Locke,
Thank you for the opportunity to submit comments in response to the Federal Aviation
Administration’s (FAA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) concerning the Designation-Restrict
the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility. The growing use of
unmanned aircraft, or drones, presents significant opportunities for the business community, and we
appreciate the FAA’s diligent efforts to ensure their safe integration into the national airspace
system. In these comments we, the Associated General Contractors of America (AGC), will
highlight issues relevant to the construction industry, who use drones every day.
AGC is the nation’s leading construction trade association. It dates to 1918 and today represents
more than 28,000 member firms including construction contractor firms both union and open-shop,
suppliers, and service providers. Through a nationwide network of 87 chapters in all 50 states, D.C.,
and Puerto Rico, AGC contractors are engaged in the construction of the nation’s highways,
bridges, utilities, airports, transit systems, public and private buildings, water works facilities and
multi-family housing units, among other projects critical to the economy.
Our members don’t typically operate the kind of fixed site facilities that are empowered by the
proposed rule, but they do work on those facilities, and their drone operations could be impacted by
the proposed rule. AGC supports the concept of unmanned aircraft flight restrictions (UAFR) for
sensitive facilities, but we would urge FAA to ensure that the proposal does not disrupt legitimate
construction activity within or near those facilities and that the authorization process is predictable
and efficient.
I. UAFRs Must Accommodate Ongoing Construction Operations
The proposed rule contemplates restricting unmanned aircraft operations within designated areas
unless authorized by the FAA and the using agency. As described, operators may be required to
obtain both agency permission and FAA approval “in a form and manner acceptable to the
Administrator.”
2111 Wilson Blvd., Suite 1000, Arlington, VA 22201 | 703.548.3118 | AGC.org

2
While we understand the need for layered authorizations, this framework risks capturing routine
construction operations, particularly where the project is occurring inside or immediately adjacent to
the fixed site facility.
Contractors rely on drones every day to perform tasks such as topographic mapping, equipment
tracking, land surveying, site security, structural assessments, and safety inspections. These activities
can be completed in a fraction of the time while reducing the need to place personnel in potentially
hazardous situations. The facilities captured by the proposed rule, most of which are government or
military facilities, benefit greatly from the efficiency gains drones bring to the jobsite.
To ensure that this valuable tool is not taken away, FAA should do more to ensure that legitimate
construction operations are allowed under proposed § 74.250. The proposed rule asked for
comment on “whether the language in (a)(2) is broad enough to enable both legitimate transitory
and non-transitory operations within a UAFR, with the appropriate notification to the fixed site
facility, while still preserving the integrity of the airspace restriction.” We don’t think that it is.
As proposed, § 74.250(2) would require a drone to transition the UAFR in the shortest amount of
time practicable. Not only does this not recognize the legitimacy of certain non-transitory drone
operations, it forbids them. Drones that stay within the bounds of a UAFR, such as those in use on
a construction project within or adjacent to the facility, cannot be said to transition the space at all,
much less in the shortest time practicable.
There is no language in proposed § 74.250 that recognizes the legitimacy of certain non-transitory
drone use within a UAFR. We recommend that in the final rule, § 74.250(2) is amended to read
“The unmanned aircraft transitions or operates within the UAFR in the shortest amount of time
practicable.”
FAA should also clarify that non-transitory operations conducted within a UAFR may be authorized
where coordinated with the using agency, that contractors operating under a valid construction
contract within the facility are eligible for such authorization, and that using agencies can rely on an
FAA “whitelist” for operators associated with active projects and pre-approved by FAA.
Lastly, we believe using agencies should be required to provide points of contact for each UAFR
that are either publicly posted or can be reached through FAA. Our members have found that while
working with FAA is very easy to coordinate, other agencies can be less so.
II. Authorization and Notice Requirements Must Be Efficient and Workable
The main impact of the proposed rule on our members is the authorization and notice requirements
in proposed § 74.255. When contractors accept a project in or adjacent to a UAFR, they will need to
provide notice to the fixed site facility in a form and manner acceptable to the Administrator.
AGC supports a coordinated approval process but asks for more clarity regarding timelines for
approvals and whether approvals can be granted on a project-wide or recurring basis.
2111 Wilson Blvd., Suite 300, Arlington, VA 22201 | 703.548.3118 | AGC.org

3
Timelines are critical to any construction job. Without predictable timelines, contractors cannot
sequence work, price risk, or ensure compliance, particularly on federally funded projects with strict
delivery schedules.
Project-based authorizations would allow a single authorization to cover all drone operations for the
duration of a construction process. For example, on large infrastructure projects within secured
facilities, drones are routinely used for daily progress monitoring and safety inspections. Requiring
repeated authorization or prohibiting non-transitory operations would create delays and increase
reliance on more dangerous inspection techniques.
We also want to be sure that contractors are aware of any existing UAFR and the consequent notice
requirements during the contracting process. To that end, the Administrator should consider
requiring that operators of a fixed site facility holding a UAFR to disclose that to any contractors
who are engaged on site.
III. Conclusion
Thank you again for the opportunity to comment. AGC is very supportive of FAA’s efforts to safely
integrate drones into the national airspace system. Please consider our minor recommended
amendment, which would ensure that non-transitory construction operations are authorized by the
proposed rule. Without these changes, the proposed rule risks restricting routine construction
operations and introducing unnecessary delays into critical infrastructure projects. With targeted
revisions, FAA can protect sensitive facilities while preserving the operational benefits of UAS
technology.
Very Respectfully,
Spencer Phillips Deniz Mustafa
Counsel, Regulatory and Litigation Advocacy Senior Director, Construction Advocacy
2111 Wilson Blvd., Suite 300, Arlington, VA 22201 | 703.548.3118 | AGC.org

July 6, 2026
Federal Aviation Administration (FAA)
U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT)
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Room W58-213, West Building 5th Floor
Washington, DC 20590-0001
Re: Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a
Fixed Site Facility; Docket number FAA-2026-4558
To Whom It May Concern:
The Monterey Bay Aquarium (Aquarium) supports the intent of the proposed Federal
Aviation Administration Unmanned Aerial Systems Flight Restriction (UAFR) over
critical infrastructure and public venues. The support is based on the recognized
threats that drones represent to life safety. The proposed restrictions will reduce the
probability of unauthorized drone activity over facilities with high density public
spaces and sensitive biological collections. Additionally, the UAFR restrictions will
provide tools for law enforcement to regulate unauthorized drone flights.
The Aquarium is a world class tourist destination, with over two million visitors
annually, and a facility entrusted with the care of marine wildlife, including species
protected under federal law. And we encourage you to take facilities such as ours into
account as you finalize definitions and criteria for applications. Unauthorized drone
operations near the Aquarium present several safety and security concerns, including
the risk of injury to guests or staff from loss of control, mechanical failure, intentional
misuse, or collisions with structures. Drones may also interfere with emergency
operations, crowd management, law enforcement response, and incident command
during a public safety event.
In addition to life safety concerns, unauthorized drone activity may adversely affect
sensitive and endangered species housed at the Aquarium. Low altitude overflight can
create unnecessary stress to animals. Facilities that care for protected wildlife require
an added level of operational stability, particularly where species recovery,
rehabilitation and public education intersect.
The Aquarium also supports narrowly tailored exceptions within the UAFR for public
safety, emergency response, and authorized scientific research. Public safety agencies
should retain the ability to operate drones when justified by life safety needs,
emergency management, law enforcement, fire response, search and rescue,

hazardous conditions, or other urgent operational requirements. Similarly, scientific
research flights should be permitted when authorized by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife
Service or other appropriate regulatory authority and when such activity supports
protected species research, conservation, recovery, or animal welfare objectives.
These exceptions are essential to ensure that the restriction does not unintentionally
limit lifesaving operations or legitimate federally permitted conservation science.
The Aquarium further encourages the FAA to consider Remote ID, geofencing,
detection capabilities, operator notification tools, and other available technologies as
protective measures. These tools can help identify compliant operators, deter
unauthorized flights, support timely response to unsafe activity, and reduce
unnecessary enforcement burdens. Technology-based protective measures, when
paired with clear flight restrictions and appropriate exceptions, provide a balanced
approach that improves safety without creating unnecessary disruption to lawful UAS
operations.
In summary, the Aquarium supports drone flight restrictions over critical
infrastructure and public venues where unauthorized UAS activity presents
foreseeable risks to life safety, emergency response, and endangered species. We
respectfully urge the FAA to adopt a balanced approach that protects the public and
sensitive wildlife, preserves public safety and scientific research exceptions, avoids
unnecessary impacts to commercial drone operators, and incorporates Remote ID and
other technologies to support effective implementation.
Thank you for your consideration.
Sincerely,
Jeff Field
Director of Security, Safety, Emergency Management
886 Cannery Row Monterey, California 93940 831.648.4800 MontereyBayAquarium.org

July 6, 2026
Submitted via Regulations.gov
Michelle Ferritto
Office of Rulemaking

West Building, 5th Floor

Washington, DC 20590-0001
Re – Designation—Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close
Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility [Docket No. FAA-2026-4558]; 91 Fed. Reg.
24650; (May 6, 2026).
Dear Ms. Ferritto:
The Meat Institute is the nation’s oldest and largest trade association representing
packers and processors of the majority of U.S. beef, pork, lamb, veal, turkey,
chicken, and processed meat products. The Meat Institute provides regulatory,
scientific, legislative, public relations, and educational services to the meat and
poultry packing and processing industry. Meat Institute members play a critical
role in maintaining a safe and continuous food supply chain.
The Meat Institute appreciates the opportunity to comment on the Department of
Transportation’s Federal Aviation Administration's (FAA) proposed rule (proposal)
establishing a process for operators and proprietors of certain fixed-site facilities to
request unmanned aircraft flight restrictions (UAFRs). The Meat Institute supports
the FAA's proposed rule and believes it represents an important and necessary step
toward protecting critical infrastructure, safeguarding the nation's food supply, and
ensuring the continued responsible integration of unmanned aircraft systems into
the National Airspace System.
Unmanned aircraft have become increasingly prevalent throughout the United
States due to their affordability, broad availability, and ease of operation. Today,
virtually any individual can acquire and operate a drone with minimal effort. While

Docket No. FAA-2026-4558
July 6, 2026
Page 2 of 4
unmanned aircraft provide numerous legitimate and beneficial applications across
agriculture and food production, they also present new security, operational, and
safety challenges that warrant careful consideration.
The agricultural sector has adopted drone technology as a valuable tool for
inspecting infrastructure, monitoring crops, evaluating livestock, and improving
operational efficiency1. The Meat Institute recognizes and supports these beneficial
uses of unmanned aircraft systems and agrees that responsible drone operations
can continue to provide meaningful benefits to agriculture and food production.
At the same time, unauthorized drone activity poses growing risks to food
production facilities. Animal rights activists have increasingly incorporated
unmanned aircraft into campaigns targeting livestock and food animal production
operations. Beyond activist activities, drones could also be used by domestic or
foreign adversaries seeking to disrupt food production systems, interfere with
operations, gather proprietary information, or otherwise threaten critical
infrastructure.
America's livestock, poultry, and food processing facilities are essential components
of the nation's critical infrastructure. These facilities employ hundreds of thousands
of workers, produce safe and wholesome food products, and provide protein to
millions of consumers in the United States and around the world. Disruptions to
these facilities can have impacts that extend well beyond a single location, affecting
supply chains, consumer confidence, food availability, and the overall resilience of
the U.S. food system.
Low-altitude drone activity around livestock and poultry slaughter and further
processing operations can create numerous concerns. Such operations may cause
unnecessary animal stress, disrupt normal business activities, create worker safety
concerns, and raise legitimate food safety and food defense issues. Unauthorized
drone activity may also interfere with sensitive operational processes and create
confusion during routine regulatory oversight activities.
The FAA's proposal appropriately recognizes the importance of protecting critical
infrastructure from risks associated with unauthorized drone operations. Within
food production facilities, federal inspectors and other personnel cannot reasonably
be expected to determine in real time whether a drone is being operated by an
activist organization, a malicious actor seeking to disrupt operations, or an
individual attempting to compromise the commercial food supply. The inability to
1 The Department of Homeland Security recognizes the food and agriculture industries (including
plants/establishments) as part of the critical infrastructure. The Bioterrorism Act of 2002 requires domestic and
foreign food companies manufacturing, processing, or holding food to register with the FDA. In addition, both FDA
and FSIS hold food companies to intentional adulteration regulatory requirements to protect against threat agents
and attacks to the U.S. food manufacturing system and its supply chain.

Docket No. FAA-2026-4558
July 6, 2026
Page 3 of 4
quickly determine intent underscores the need for clear regulatory tools that reduce
unnecessary drone activity around sensitive facilities.
The NPRM also advances broader national security and food security objectives.
Unauthorized unmanned aircraft could be used to conduct surveillance of
proprietary agricultural processes, identify vulnerabilities within food production
facilities, or target critical components of the food supply chain. As food production
infrastructure becomes increasingly interconnected and technologically advanced,
protecting these facilities from unauthorized aerial activity becomes increasingly
important.
Providing operators of eligible facilities with a transparent and consistent process to
request unmanned aircraft flight restrictions is a practical and reasonable approach
to reducing these risks. Importantly, the proposal balances security considerations
with the continued availability of legitimate commercial, governmental,
agricultural, and authorized drone operations. The Meat Institute believes the
FAA's approach appropriately protects sensitive facilities while preserving the
many beneficial uses of drone technology.
The Meat Institute particularly supports the FAA's recognition that critical
infrastructure protections should cover facilities whose disruption could
significantly impact public safety, economic security, and national resilience. Food
production and processing facilities play a vital role in each of these areas and
should be eligible for consideration under the framework established in the NPRM.
For these reasons, the Meat Institute and the members it represents strongly
support the FAA's proposed rule and encourage the Agency to move expeditiously
toward finalizing and implementing the program. Strengthening protections around
eligible facilities will enhance agricultural security, protect critical food production
infrastructure, support animal welfare and food safety objectives, and reinforce the
resilience of the nation's food supply while maintaining responsible access to the
benefits of unmanned aircraft technology.
Thank you for your consideration of these comments. The Meat Institute would be
pleased to provide additional information or participate in further discussions that
may assist the Agency as it moves forward with this rulemaking.
Respectfully submitted,
Jesse Fulton, M.S.
Director, Animal Health & Welfare

Docket No. FAA-2026-4558
July 6, 2026
Page 4 of 4
Cc: Julie Anna Potts
Nicole Johnson-Hoffman
KatieRose McCullough, PhD, MPH
Casey Lynn Gallimore

On numerous occasions I have personally witnessed Union Pacific manager testing teams flying drones over chemical plants and refineries in order to conduct efficiency testing of employees in the course of their duties servicing these customers.  When asked if they had permission to fly over these facilities they always reply that they don't need permission from their customers to observe railroad employees. On at least one occasion I witnessed a railroad operated drone flown dangerously close to and through high voltage power lines in order to get a better view of employees working at a customers facility. At Sheldon, Texas and Mont Belvieu, Texas they routinely fly their drones in the same path and altitude that the pipeline patrol planes regularly fly, it's only by sheer dumb luck that there hasn't been a near miss or an outright collision. The use of these drones in the railroad workplace are very distracting to the employees trying to execute their duties. Often times Union Pacific drone operators do not adhere to U.P,'s own policy of landing the drone after it has been spotted by the employees being observed, they just continue to harass you with it until they think they've caught you breaking a rule or procedure.

comments.commenter: Comment from Los Angeles Police Department
Comment from Billings Logan International Airport (BIL)
comments.comment_text: Airports should be allowed to restrict Unmanned Aircraft operations within the vicinity of the airport to those taking-off or landing at the facility, within the Class A through Class E airspace of the airport, unless those unmanned aircraft are under control of the air traffic control facility at that airport. The aviation safety and vital national interest (part of the national airspace system) of the airport facility requires protection of the airspace. This should be a full-time Special UAFR that restricts all other drone operations unless expressly approved by the airport operator.

comments.commenter: Comment from National Congress of American Indians
comments.comment_text:
July 6, 2026

Bryan Bedford, Administrator
Federal Aviation Administration
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Washington, D.C. 20590-0001

RE: Designation—Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility; Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03; RIN 2120-AL33

Dear Administrator Bedford:

On behalf of the National Congress of American Indians (NCAI), I am transmitting the attached formal comment letter, signed by Executive Director Larry Wright, Jr., regarding proposed 14 C.F.R. Part 74. While NCAI supports the FAA’s public safety objectives, the proposed rule contains four regulatory gaps that raise substantial concerns under the Administrative Procedure Act and would leave Tribal Nations unprotected.

Remove the 500-inmate threshold for Tribal correctional facilities. No Tribal facility in the United States meets this threshold due to statutory sentencing caps under federal law. A drone-delivered weapon in a 30-bed Tribal facility creates an immediate security crisis identical to that in a large state prison. The threshold excludes 100 percent of Tribal facilities without any reasoned basis in the administrative record.

Establish objective geographic triggers and require government-to-government consultation. The proposed property-line boundary does not account for the fragmented “checkerboard” land tenure patterns of many reservations. The final rule should adopt a 5-statute-mile radius from Tribal trust lands as an objective trigger for mandatory government-to-government consultation before any Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restriction affecting Tribal lands is approved.

Protect sacred sites and Traditional Cultural Properties in environmental reviews. The rule must account for impacts to Tribal cultural landscapes and incorporate confidentiality protections under Section 304 of the National Historic Preservation Act (54 U.S.C. § 307103), allowing Tribal Nations to engage through confidential government-to-government consultation rather than public dockets.

Issue a Supplemental Notice of Proposed Rulemaking for the Water and Wastewater Systems Sector. Proposed § 74.96 leaves eligibility criteria entirely “Reserved.” Inserting final thresholds without prior notice would violate the APA’s logical outgrowth doctrine.

NCAI looks forward to engaging with the FAA through government-to-government consultation as the agency develops the final rule.

Respectfully submitted,
Viswatej Attili
Policy Associate
National Congress of American Indians

comments.commenter: Comment from National Pork Producers Council
comments.comment_text:
Attached, please find the comments of the National Pork Producers Council on the FAA's propsoed Designation—Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility.

NPPC strongly supports the FAA’s proposal. The rule is a necessary and overdue step toward protecting critical infrastructure, safeguarding the nation’s food supply, defending farm biosecurity, and ensuring the responsible integration of unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) into the National Airspace System. In addition to joining separate comments with other livestock, meat and agricultural groups, NPPC submits these comments to focus the Agency’s attention on a specific and well-documented problem: the biosecurity, worker-safety, operational and physical security threats that low-altitude drone activity poses to swine and other livestock operations.

If you have questions or need additional information, please do not hesitate to contact me at 202-347-3600 or by email at formicam@nppc.org.

July 1, 2026
Michelle Ferritto
Office of Rulemaking
Federal Aviation Administration
800 Independence Avenue SW
Washington, DC 20591
SUBJECT: Proposed Rule regarding Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned
Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558
Submitted via Federal eRulemaking Portal
Dear Ms. Ferritto:
Louisville Water Company provides safe high-quality drinking water to approximately one million
customers in Louisville Metro and the surrounding counties in Kentucky. We play a vital role in
protecting public health and safety and are committed to maintaining the highest quality
drinking water. The security of our fixed-site facilities is critical to the safe and continuous
operation of production, treatment, storage, and transmission of drinking water for
consumption, commercial and industrial production, and fire suppression.
Louisville Water appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the proposed rule and
offers the following for your consideration.
Minimum Requirements for Restriction (Subpart B of Part 74)
FAA proposes a set of baseline conditions that a facility must satisfy independent of any sector-
specific eligibility criteria in Subpart C. Several of these minimum requirements are overly
vague, lack objective metrics, or impose significant burdens that are mismatched to the actual
operational and financial capabilities of public water and systems. In addition, we recommend
that FAA consider expanding its application process to permit consolidated, multi-asset,
community-based applications for UAFRs
Eligible Facilities: Facility Risk and Impact Criteria (§ 74.54)
The proposed rule states the applicant must show that a facility contains critical assets or
components vulnerable to UAS threats, and that the facility's criticality includes the potential for
a regional or national-level impact. Given the lack of authority of water utilities to interdict
drones, we believe that all critical infrastructure assets are vulnerable to unauthorized drone
flights. It is unclear how the FAA might determine the absence of such a risk or what metrics an
applicant can reasonably provide to characterize vulnerability. The FAA also does not define
what level of disruption would constitutes an eligible “regional” or “national” level impact.
This type of information is necessary for regulatory clarity to enable prospective applicants to
understand the metrics by which an application is being reviewed and inhibits applicants’ ability

to properly assess their potential eligibility before investing time and effort in preparing a full
application package. We recommend the FAA provide clearly defined, objective thresholds that
will be applied to this requirement.
Protective Security Measures Requirement (§ 74.56)
The requirement for a facility to have an existing security program with defined perimeter and
designated security personnel is reasonable and consistent with practices most water systems
already have in place. However, the requirement that the facility must have the capability to
receive Remote ID broadcasts warrants further consideration given the technical and economic
burden this can impose, particularly on small and medium-sized water systems.
Louisville Water recommends that FAA establish a community-level application process under
which state, local, Tribal, or territorial (SLTT) governments may apply on behalf of all eligible
critical infrastructure assets within their jurisdiction and may satisfy this requirement through a
shared Remote ID sensing capability covering the full set of participating facilities. Under the
SAFER SKIES Act, Congress has already granted state, local, Tribal, and territorial (SLTT) law
enforcement the ability to detect, track, seize, disable, or even destroy drones that pose a
credible threat to people, critical infrastructure, major public events, or prisons. Requiring each
individual facility operator to separately establish Remote ID sensing capability duplicates a
function already being provided by SLTT law enforcement in many cases. Those are the entities
that should be leading and investigating violations of flight restrictions and interdicting
unauthorized drones when appropriate. A community application approach would allow SLTT
entities to provide coverage to an eligible facility thus lowering the economic burden with shared
services and providing for the collective good intended under FESSA.
Louisville Water is aware of locations where water utilities host SLTT drone-monitoring
installations under existing local arrangements. This mutually beneficial relationship leverages
shared assets and services. This type of model is currently being implemented in communities
hosting the World Cup, which have been collectively granted $250 million in FY26 to implement
programs that “combat the unlawful use of unmanned aircraft systems that pose a threat to the
safety and security of the American people, communities, and institutions.”
Section 2009 provides that applications can be prepared individually or collectively. The
proposed rule states that the “FAA interprets “collectively request” to mean a request by more
than one operator or proprietor for a particular fixed site facility where more than one operator
has an interest or exercises control.” Louisville Water recommends the FAA address a related
issue: the proposed rule would limit each application to a single fixed site facility, even where a
single operator owns multiple eligible assets. Water systems commonly own and operate
numerous geographically separated critical assets (e.g., treatment plants, pump stations,
elevated tanks, wells/well fields, raw-water intakes, etc.). Requiring a separate application, with
separately demonstrated need and security measures, for each individual asset would
substantially increase the compliance burden without delivering a corresponding security
benefit. Louisville Water recommends FAA allow a single consolidated application, supported
by one risk assessment, to cover multiple non-contiguous facilities under common ownership
within a single water system’s service area.

What types of drinking water and wastewater systems should be considered?
Louisville Water Company recommends that eligibility for unmanned aircraft flight restrictions
(UAFRs) in the water sector prioritize fixed-site facilities that are essential to the production,
treatment, storage, and transmission of drinking water.
At a minimum, eligible facilities should include:
 Drinking water treatment plants
 Raw water intake structures and pump stations
 Major finished water pumping stations
 Large storage reservoirs and elevated tanks
 Critical control or operations centers supporting system functionality
Louisville Water recommends that FAA limit eligibility to facilities whose compromise would
materially disrupt service delivery, water quality, or system integrity, rather than all distributed
infrastructure components.
What characteristics should be considered in developing eligibility criteria?
Eligibility criteria should be based on risk, consequence, and system criticality, not simply
facility type and should align with AWIA Risk and Resilience Assessment vulnerability ratings for
drinking water systems as required by the Safe Drinking Water Act § 1433.
Key characteristics should include:
 Operational criticality (ability to maintain continuous service)
 Single-point-of-failure risk or limited redundancy
 Proximity of critical components exposed to aerial surveillance or interference
 Chemical storage or treatment processes that could be targeted or disrupted
 Visibility and accessibility of the site from public areas
 Existing security posture and demonstrated need for enhanced protection
Facilities with high-value assets that are observable or accessible from above should receive
particular consideration, as they present a unique vulnerability not fully addressed by traditional
ground-based security measures.
Should population served be considered, and what thresholds are appropriate?
Yes. Population served should be a primary factor for consideration, as it directly correlates to
consequence of disruption. However, population should be used in combination with other
factors (e.g., critical customers, system redundancy) to avoid excluding high-risk facilities solely
based on size.
What are the characteristics of drinking water and wastewater systems that should be
considered in development of gating criteria?
 Establishing eligibility thresholds based on population served and regional impact,
rather than a single national cutoff. This could include any drinking water,

wastewater or water reuse system that serves a population of 10,000 or more
people, inclusive of wholesale systems and independent of ownership type.
 Population thresholds should be used in combination with other factors (e.g., critical
customers, system redundancy) to avoid excluding high-risk facilities solely based on
size.
 Allow flexibility for smaller systems where disruption would still create significant
localized public health or economic impacts.
 Any drinking water, wastewater and water reuse system that is the primary provider of
water or wastewater treatment service to one or more facilities that independently
quality for a UAFR under the eligibility criteria independently qualify for a UAFR under the
eligibility criteria of another sector in §§ 74.81–74.95. Louisville Water recommends the
following sector-specific dependency criteria:

Critical Infrastructure Recommended Water/Wastewater Criterion
Sector
Any water, wastewater, or water reuse system that uses, stores, or
managers one or more hazardous-release chemicals at or above the
minimum concentration percentages and screening threshold
quantities in Appendix A of the proposed rule, which aligns with the
Chemical criteria in for the chemical sector itself under § 74.81. Based on EPA
Risk Management Program (RMP) data, we estimate that
approximately 2,000 water and wastewater systems would
independently satisfy this threshold.
A water or wastewater system that is the sole-source or primary
Commercial water/wastewater provider to a facility independently eligible under §
Facilities 74.82
A water or wastewater system whose elevated tank, tower, or other
structure hosts a cellular, broadcast, or other communications asset
Communications independently eligible under § 74.83, including those that support
emergency services.
A water or wastewater system that is the sole-source or primary
Critical
provider to a facility independently eligible under § 74.84.
Manufacturing
We recommend that dams providing a drinking water supply function
that constitutes the main source of water to a population exceeding
10,000 be eligible. Notably there are less than 100 water systems that
would satisfy the proposed 500,000 population threshold, while many
Dams drinking water systems own and operate dams that are critical for
service. This change would improve consistency between sectoral
criteria given our proposed threshold of 10,000 people for water
system eligibility.
A water or wastewater system that is the sole-source or primary
Defense Industrial
provider to a facility independently eligible under § 74.86.
Base
A water or wastewater system that is the sole-source or primary
Emergency Services provider to a correctional facility eligible under § 74.87.
A water or wastewater system that is the sole-source or primary
provider to a facility independently eligible under § 74.88 since nearly
Energy all energy production facilities have a dependence on water sector
services.
A water or wastewater system that is the sole-source or primary
Financial Services provider to a facility independently eligible under § 74.89.

Should critical customers be considered?
Yes. The presence of critical customers should be a key eligibility factor.
Facilities serving the following should be prioritized:
 Hospitals and healthcare systems
 Emergency response and public safety infrastructure
 Military installations or national defense-related facilities
 Major industrial or manufacturing operations
 Large regional economic centers
Interruption of service to these customers could create cascading impacts beyond the water
utility, elevating the overall risk profile of the facility.
Should history of unauthorized drone activity be considered?
Yes, it should, but as a supporting factor, not a primary eligibility requirement.
While documented drone activity is useful evidence, reliance on historical incidents alone is not
sufficient because:
 Many critical facilities may be underreported or lack detection capability.
 Emerging drone threats may not yet be reflected in historical data.
 Proactive protection is necessary for facilities with known vulnerabilities and high
consequence potential.
Louisville Water recommends that:
 A history of unauthorized drone activity be considered as additional justification,
and
 Facilities without documented incidents remain eligible if they meet risk and
consequence-based criteria.
Utilities Should Have Primary Authority to Self-Designate Eligible Facilities
Louisville Water Company recommends that the final rule establish a utility self-designation
framework that authorizes drinking water and wastewater systems to identify and designate
their own critical facilities for unmanned aircraft flight restrictions, subject to reasonable federal
standards and notification requirements.
Water utilities are uniquely positioned to make these determinations. Under the America's
Water Infrastructure Act of 2018 (AWIA), community water systems serving more than 3,300
persons are already required to conduct Risk and Resilience Assessments and develop
Emergency Response Plans that identify vulnerabilities, critical assets, and consequence-of-
failure scenarios (SDWA § 1433). These assessments — conducted by trained security
professionals with direct operational knowledge — represent exactly the analytical foundation
needed to support facility-level UAFR designations. Delegating that determination to an outside
agency with no operational role in water system management would duplicate existing work,
introduce delay, and risk misidentification of the assets that most warrant protection.
Louisville Water recommends that the final rule:

 Authorize self-designation by the utility's authorized official (e.g., CEO, General
Manager, or its designee) for facilities that meet the risk and consequence criteria
established in the rule, without requiring pre-approval from FAA or any other federal
agency.
 Establish a notification and certification process under which utilities submit a
standardized designation certification to the FAA, attesting that the designated
facility meets applicable eligibility criteria — without requiring disclosure of
sensitive security information in a public docket
 Protect designation information from public disclosure under applicable FOIA
exemptions and state open record laws, consistent with the treatment of AWIA Risk
and Resilience Assessments and Emergency Response Plans under Safe Drinking
Water Act § 1433(a)(5)
 Allow utilities to add, modify, or rescind designations as their infrastructure evolves,
through the same notification process, without requiring a new rulemaking petition
or agency determination
 Establish a federal backstop under which FAA retains authority to reject a
designation that is facially inconsistent with the eligibility criteria, but with a
presumption in favor of the utility's determination where the facility plausibly meets
the rule's standards
This framework mirrors the approach taken in other critical infrastructure protection regimes —
including the Chemical Facility Anti-Terrorism Standards (CFATS) tiering process and the AWIA
assessment framework itself — where the regulated entity performs the primary vulnerability
analysis and the federal agency establishes standards, oversight, and enforcement rather than
substituting its judgment for the operator's.
Data Protection
The proposed rule does not properly address the need to protect the sensitive information
provided by an applicant. The security sensitive nature of the details requested by FAA to
support the application must be protected from public disclosure. We recommend that the FAA
implement a protocol that protect applications, accepted or denied, that exempts all
information collections from disclosure under section 552 of title 5 of the United States Code.
In addition, the FAA Administrator, in consultation with appropriate Federal law enforcement
and intelligence officials, may develop protocols as may be necessary to protect the information
required to support a flight restriction designation under FESSA. Such protocols shall ensure
that:
 each copy of an application, and all information contained in or derived from the
application, is kept in a secure location;
 only individuals designated by the Administrator may have access to the copies of the
applications; and
 no copy of an application, or part of an application, or information contained in or
derived from an application shall be available to anyone other than an individual
designated by the Administrator.

The Administrator should complete the development of such protocols for the purpose of having
them in place prior to receiving any application from eligible facilities.
Summary Position
Louisville Water Company supports the FAA’s efforts to establish a clear, consistent process for
protecting critical infrastructure from unmanned aircraft risks. We recommend that eligibility
criteria for the water sector be risk-based, scalable, and focused on facilities whose disruption
would create significant public health, safety, or economic impacts.
Given the essential nature of drinking water systems and their increasing exposure to aerial
threats, it is important that the final rule allows utilities to proactively secure their most critical
assets while maintaining operational flexibility.
Thank you for your consideration of these comments. If you have any questions, please contact
Brad Hart at bhart@lwcky.com or (502) 780-2714, or Peter Goodmann at
pgoodmann@lwcky.com or (502) 262-7614.
Sincerely,
Peter Goodmann, Director
Water Quality & Research
Louisville Water Company
c: Brad Hart
Michael Tigue

comments.commenter: Comment from North Carolina Department of Transportation: NC Division of Aviation
comments.comment_text:
STATE OF NORTH CAROLINA
DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION
JOSH STEIN DANIEL H. JOHNSON
GOVERNOR SECRETARY
June 10, 2026
Docket Operations, M-30
U.S. Department of Transportation
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
West Building Ground Floor, Room W12-140
Washington, DC 20590
Re: Comments of the North Carolina Department of Transportation on FAA Notice of Proposed
Rulemaking, Designation—Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a
Fixed Site Facility, Docket No. FAA-2026-4558, Notice No. 26-03, RIN 2120-AL33
To Whom It May Concern:
The North Carolina Department of Transportation (NCDOT) appreciates the opportunity to submit
comments regarding the Federal Aviation Administration’s Notice of Proposed Rulemaking
(NPRM) entitled “Designation—Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to
a Fixed Site Facility.”
NCDOT supports the FAA’s efforts to enhance the security of critical infrastructure and establish a
uniform national framework governing unmanned aircraft system (UAS) operations in sensitive
areas. NCDOT has long been a leader in the safe integration of UAS technology into transportation,
emergency response, infrastructure inspection, and environmental monitoring activities. As one of
the largest state transportation agencies in the nation, NCDOT operates UAS in support of public
safety, transportation system resilience, disaster response, bridge and roadway inspections,
environmental compliance, and infrastructure planning.
While NCDOT supports the overall objectives of the proposed rule, we respectfully request that the
FAA address several significant concerns regarding the rule’s impact on governmental operations,
emergency response missions, environmental enforcement activities, and the relationship between
the proposed federal framework and existing state laws protecting critical infrastructure.
I. Preservation of State and Local Government UAS Operations
NCDOT appreciates that the proposed rule would permit certain operations conducted under Part
91, Part 107, Part 108, Part 135, and Part 137 to access Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions
(UAFRs) subject to notification requirements. However, the proposed framework may create
operational burdens for governmental entities conducting legitimate public missions. For state
transportation agencies, the operational burden is not limited to formal approval. Even a pre-launch
Mailing Address: Telephone: 919-814-0550 Location:
NC DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION Fax: 919-840-9267 1050 MERIDIAN DRIVE
DIVISION OF AVIATION MORRISVILLE, NC 27560
1560 MAIL SERVICE CENTER
RALEIGH, NC 27699-1560
Website: ncdot.gov/aviation

notification obligation can be incompatible with emergency response, incident command, disaster
assessment, and time-critical infrastructure safety missions.
NCDOT conducts UAS missions mainly as civil aircraft operations under 14 CFR Part 107 or in
some cases as Public Aircraft Operations under Public Aircraft Authority, based on the operational
requirements for:
• Bridge inspections;
• Highway and roadway assessments;
• Construction monitoring;
• Traffic incident documentation;
• Emergency response support;
• Disaster recovery operations;
• Flood monitoring;
• Landslide assessments; and
• Infrastructure resilience evaluations.
Many of these missions occur on short notice and require rapid deployment. Requiring advance
coordination or unclear notification procedures before conducting operations within a UAFR may
introduce delays that reduce the effectiveness of governmental missions and potentially jeopardize
public safety. For standard UAFRs, NCDOT requests that the FAA clarify that site-manager
notification is for situational awareness only and does not authorize a facility manager to approve,
deny, delay, or condition qualifying governmental operations.
Accordingly, NCDOT requests that the FAA establish a categorical exemption or streamlined
access process for state governmental agencies conducting official governmental functions,
particularly when operating under Public Aircraft Operations authority.
II. Emergency Response Operations Must Remain Unimpeded
North Carolina regularly experiences hurricanes, flooding events, wildfires, severe weather
incidents, transportation emergencies, and search-and-rescue operations. UAS technology has
become an essential tool for rapidly assessing damage, identifying hazards, locating missing
persons, and supporting incident command operations. The final rule should therefore establish a
