clear emergency-access procedure allowing governmental operators to conduct time-critical
operations within Special UAFRs without prior using-agency or FAA approval when delay would
impair response, subject to post-operation notification and coordination as soon as practicable.
Although the NPRM recognizes governmental and law-enforcement operations and provides
modified notification procedures, NCDOT remains concerned that uncertainty regarding
notification requirements could impede time-sensitive emergency missions.
The need for an emergency pathway is also important for Special UAFRs. Unlike standard UAFRs,
Special UAFRs may require approval from the using agency and, in some circumstances, additional
FAA approval before an operation may proceed. Those approval requirements could be
incompatible with emergency governmental missions where immediate aerial assessment is
necessary to protect life, property, transportation infrastructure, public mobility, incident
responders, or the environment. The final rule should therefore establish a clear emergency-access
procedure allowing governmental operators to conduct time-critical operations within Special
UAFRs without prior approval when delay would impair response, subject to post-operation
notification and coordination as soon as practicable.
NCDOT recommends that the FAA expressly provides that:

1. Emergency response operations conducted by federal, state, tribal, or local governmental
agencies are exempt from UAFR and special UAFR notification requirements when immediate
action is necessary to protect life, property, transportation infrastructure, or the environment;
2. Emergency management agencies may conduct operations within UAFRs without prior
approval when acting pursuant to an emergency declaration or incident command structure; and
3. Post-operation notification should satisfy any reporting requirements associated with
emergency governmental operations.
These revisions would preserve critical response capabilities while maintaining the security
objectives of the proposed rule.
III. Environmental Enforcement and Regulatory Oversight Concerns
NCDOT conducts UAS operations in support of environmental stewardship and regulatory
compliance activities, including monitoring wetlands, waterways, erosion control measures,
stormwater infrastructure, and environmental permit conditions associated with transportation
projects.
In some circumstances, state agencies must conduct investigative or enforcement-related flights
where advance notification to a facility owner or operator could compromise the effectiveness of
the inspection or investigation.
The proposed notification requirements could unintentionally hinder legitimate governmental
oversight and environmental enforcement activities.
NCDOT requests that the FAA establish an exemption or confidential notification process for
governmental agencies conducting regulatory inspections, environmental enforcement actions, or
investigative missions where advance notice would undermine the purpose of the operation.
IV. Federal Preemption and Existing State Critical Infrastructure Laws
NCDOT is concerned by portions of the NPRM that discuss federal preemption of state and local
laws regulating UAS operations.
North Carolina has enacted laws designed to protect critical infrastructure, public facilities,
transportation assets, and public safety interests from unlawful or malicious drone activity. These
laws serve important state interests relating to property protection, public safety, trespass, privacy,
and critical infrastructure security.
NCDOT respectfully requests that the FAA clarify that the proposed rule does not preempt or
invalidate state laws that:
• Protect critical infrastructure from unauthorized access or surveillance;
• Regulate trespass, privacy, nuisance, or property rights;
• Address criminal misuse of unmanned aircraft systems;
• Protect transportation facilities and public assets; or
• Establish penalties for unlawful conduct involving UAS operations.

While NCDOT recognizes the FAA’s authority over navigable airspace and aviation safety, the
final rule should expressly acknowledge the continued role of states in protecting public
infrastructure and enforcing generally applicable laws that do not directly regulate aviation safety or
airspace management.
Without such clarification, the rule could create uncertainty regarding the continued enforceability
of state critical infrastructure protections and potentially invite unnecessary litigation concerning
federal preemption.
V. Transportation Sector Impacts
As the agency responsible for maintaining and operating North Carolina’s transportation network,
NCDOT is concerned that transportation-related facilities could become subject to UAFRs that may
affect routine governmental operations.
Transportation agencies increasingly rely on UAS technology to improve safety, reduce inspection
costs, minimize worker exposure to hazardous environments, and accelerate infrastructure
assessments.
The final rule should ensure that transportation agencies retain the ability to:
• Inspect bridges, highways, tunnels, and transportation facilities;
• Conduct infrastructure condition assessments;
• Monitor construction projects;
• Evaluate disaster-related damage;
• Support traffic management and incident response; and
• Perform transportation planning and engineering functions.
NCDOT recommends that the FAA specifically recognize state transportation agencies as eligible
governmental operators entitled to streamlined access procedures within UAFRs.
VI. Recommendations
NCDOT respectfully requests that the FAA revise the proposed rule to:
1. Establish a categorical exemption or streamlined authorization process for state governmental
agencies conducting official governmental functions;
2. Create an explicit launch-first/post-notification emergency pathway for governmental UAS
operations when immediate action is necessary to protect life, property, transportation
infrastructure, public mobility, incident responders, or the environment. The final rule should
state that prior notification is not required in such circumstances and that post-operation
notification satisfies the UAFR notification requirement;
3. Establish an emergency access procedure for Special UAFRs. When a governmental operator
must act immediately to protect life, property, transportation infrastructure, public mobility, or the
environment, the final rule should allow emergency operations to proceed without prior using-

agency or FAA approval, subject to post-operation notification and coordination as soon as
practicable.
4. Provide an exemption or alternative notification procedure for environmental enforcement and
regulatory investigations;
5. Clarify that the final rule does not preempt state laws governing trespass, privacy, criminal
conduct, property protection, and critical infrastructure security;
6. Recognize state transportation agencies as essential governmental operators requiring reliable
access to UAFR-designated areas; and
7. Develop a standardized electronic notification system that allows governmental operators to
rapidly comply with notification requirements while minimizing operational delays. The system
should include machine-readable UAFR boundaries, current facility contact information,
emergency post-operation reporting, and auditable notification records.
Conclusion
NCDOT supports the FAA’s efforts to strengthen the security of critical infrastructure while
maintaining the safe integration of UAS into the National Airspace System. However, the final rule
must preserve the ability of state agencies to conduct essential governmental missions, respond to
emergencies, protect the environment, and maintain critical transportation infrastructure.
NCDOT appreciates the opportunity to provide these comments and looks forward to continued
collaboration with the FAA as this rulemaking proceeds.
Sincerely,
Riley Beaman
UAS Program Manager
NCDOT: NC Division of Aviation

I am concerned that further regulation will continue to negatively affect commerce for drone businesses.
I have noticed severe staffing shortages of the FAA just to handle Air Space Authorizations. I miss the Wide Area Authorizations that we used to have to immediately fly at 200' AGL any where in a specific controlled airspace. The FAA should bring back the wide area authorization as well as other stream lined methods for commercial businesses to quickly and safely fly in controlled air space. I'm against any further restriction of air space for commercial pilots, until the FAA can have the proper staffing and technology to immediately fulfill the needs of commercial drone pilots. Currently it does not.

Hello, in my opinion, agricultural sites should not be considered for these drone restrictions. No individual farm or processing plant has "national-level debilitating impact". Drones should be allowed to fly over agricultural operations.

As a Part 107 certificate holder, I am fully in favor of this rule.I don't think this rule is going to have any impact on bad actors, but it most certainly will give additional firepower for punishing the stupid actors.

As chief of an agency that employs public safety drone operators, I support the FAA's efforts to protect critical infrastructure through the establishment of Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs). However, I encourage the FAA to provide a clear exemption or standing authorization for bona fide public safety operations conducted under Public Aircraft Operations (PAO) or by governmental Part 107 operators during emergency incidents.

Law enforcement officers, firefighters, and EMS personnel frequently deploy unmanned aircraft in rapidly evolving situations involving threats to life, active crimes, hazardous materials incidents, search and rescue operations, natural disasters, and other emergencies. During these events, drone operators should not be required to determine whether a particular facility has a Standard or Special UAFR or whether additional FAA or facility approval is required before launching in support of an emergency response.

The rule should clearly state that governmental emergency response operations conducted under PAO authority—or governmental Part 107 operations in direct support of emergency response—may operate within UAFRs when necessary to protect life, preserve public safety, or support law enforcement. Notification to the affected facility, when practical, is appropriate, but should not delay time-sensitive operations.

A standardized nationwide exemption would:
•Eliminate uncertainty for public safety drone operators during critical incidents.
•Prevent delays that could adversely affect life safety or officer safety.
•Ensure consistent implementation across jurisdictions.
•Reduce unnecessary administrative coordination during emergencies while preserving the intent of protecting critical infrastructure.

The FAA has appropriately recognized the importance of allowing certain certificated operators within Standard UAFRs. I recommend expanding that concept by expressly exempting governmental emergency response operations from additional approval requirements when acting within the scope of official duties.

Many critical infrastructure facilities—including hospitals, electrical substations, water treatment plants, communications infrastructure, transportation facilities, and government buildings—are themselves frequent locations for police, fire, and EMS calls for service. Public safety agencies routinely respond to crimes in progress, suspicious activity, fires, hazardous materials incidents, medical emergencies, traffic collisions, and search operations at or immediately adjacent to these facilities. The rule should ensure that first responders are not placed in the position of delaying the deployment of a department drone to determine whether a facility is subject to a Standard or Special UAFR or whether additional FAA authorization is required. During emergencies, drone operators should be able to focus solely on protecting life, ensuring officer and public safety, and resolving the incident. Any ambiguity in the rule that causes hesitation or administrative delay undermines the effectiveness of unmanned aircraft as a critical public safety tool and is contrary to the mission of emergency response. This clarification would preserve the FAA's security objectives while ensuring that public safety agencies can rapidly employ drones when every minute matters.

GSK appreciates the opportunity to respond to the Federal Aviation Administration’s (FAA) Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility proposed rule.

We support the intent of FAA’s proposed rule to restrict the operation of unmanned aircraft near fixed-site facilities to enhance the security, safety, and resilience of infrastructure critical to national public health and safety and respectfully offer the following recommendations.

FAA should amend the proposed rule to expand the Healthcare and Public Health Sector eligibility criteria in Subpart C to permit pharmaceutical research and development (R&D) and manufacturing facilities that are critical to national public health and safety to be eligible to apply for Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs).

FAA should consider different types of UAFRs, such as UAFRs that prohibit loitering while permitting flyovers.

Please see our attached comments for additional detail and justification.

comments.commenter: Comment from Great River Hydro, LLC
comments.comment_text:
GREAT RIVER HYDRO
69 Milk Street, Suite 308
Westborough, MA 01581
www.greatriverhydro.com
U.S. Department of Transportation
Federal Aviation Administration (FAA)
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Room W58-213, West Building 5th Floor
Washington, DC 20590-0001
Electronic Submission
July 9, 2026
Subject: DOCKET NO. FAA-2026-4558
Comments on FAA NPRM – FESSA Section 2209 (UAS Restrictions Near Fixed Site
Facilities)
To Whom It May Concern:
Great River Hydro, LLC respectfully submits these comments in support of the FAA’s Notice of
Proposed Rulemaking implementing Section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security
Act of 2016.
Great River Hydro owns and operates hydroelectric generation facilities across New England,
including legacy and historic dam infrastructure that plays a critical role in regional energy
reliability and public safety.
We strongly support the FAA’s effort to establish a formal process allowing fixed-site critical
infrastructure owners and operators to request restrictions on unmanned aircraft systems (UAS)
operating in close proximity to fixed-site facilities. Based on our recent operational experience,
such a process is both necessary and overdue to address an identified and ongoing gap.
Operational Experience with Unauthorized UAS Activity
Over the past several years, we have experienced multiple instances of private drone operators
flying in close proximity to our hydroelectric facilities, including our fixed-site critical
infrastructure hydroelectric Wilder and Moore facilities in Vermont and New Hampshire
respectively. In several cases, operators have recorded and publicly posted high-resolution video
footage depicting sensitive infrastructure components, including dam structures and gate
systems, from vantage points that are not accessible to the general public.
In at least one instance, a local drone operator has repeatedly filmed and disseminated close-up
footage of critical dam features. These activities have occurred without coordination,
authorization, or any viable legal mechanism for restriction or removal. As a practical matter, our
only recourse has been informal requests to the individuals involved, which are not consistently
effective.
1
GRH Comment DOCKET NO. FAA-2026-4558 05142026 v1.

GREAT RIVER HYDRO
69 Milk Street, Suite 308
Westborough, MA 01581
www.greatriverhydro.com
Security Implications
The public availability of those videos significantly increases security risk by reducing the level
of effort required to identify and assess facility vulnerabilities. Hydroelectric dams possess
unique vulnerabilities, particularly at the concrete and structural interface where physical access
from the water or air is possible. Detailed aerial footage of these areas could be leveraged by a
threat actor, at a minimum:
- Identify structural features and access points
- Assess surveillance gaps and response timelines
- Plan the placement of an explosive device or other destructive act
Information of this nature may constitute or relate to Critical Energy Infrastructure Information
(CEII), as defined by the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (FERC), including
engineering, vulnerability, or design details that could be useful in planning an attack and is
exempt from public disclosure under the Freedom of Information Act (5 U.S.C. § 552).
The ability of unauthorized parties to capture and distribute this level of detail via UAS
operations represents a clear gap in current protective frameworks.
Need for Enforceable Regulatory Mechanism
Our experience demonstrates that voluntary compliance and existing legal options are
insufficient to address this issue. Even in cases where operators are known, there is no practical
or timely mechanism to prevent repeated overflight or dissemination of sensitive imagery.
The framework proposed under Section 2209 would provide a much-needed, enforceable
pathway for infrastructure operators to proactively mitigate these risks.
Recommendations
To ensure the effectiveness of the final rule, we respectfully recommend:
1. Inclusion of Hydroelectric Facilities of All Sizes
Eligibility criteria should explicitly encompass hydroelectric facilities, including smaller and
legacy assets, which may present significant safety and security risks despite lower generating
capacity.
2. Protection of Sensitive Information
Petitions, supporting materials, and determinations under Section 2209 should be handled in a
manner consistent with CEII principles, ensuring sensitive infrastructure details are not publicly
disclosed.
2
GRH Comment DOCKET NO. FAA-2026-4558 05142026 v1.

GREAT RIVER HYDRO
69 Milk Street, Suite 308
Westborough, MA 01581
www.greatriverhydro.com
3. Reasonable Restriction Scope
The FAA should establish current and evolving restriction parameters (e.g., lateral distance and
altitude) that meaningfully prevent close-to-medium-range surveillance while remaining
operationally practical to accommodate advances in technologies.
4. Operational Flexibility for Authorized Use
The rule should provide clear and efficient pathways for fixed-site critical infrastructure facilities
owner/operators, authorized fixed-site critical infrastructure facility agents and contractors, and
public safety entities to conduct authorized UAS operations for safety, security and other
reasons, including expedited access during emergencies on or near fixed-site critical
infrastructure facilities.
5. Interagency Coordination
Implementation should align with state and local law enforcement, emergency management, and
dam safety regulators, and other agencies or other authorized entities to ensure awareness,
enforceability, and consistency in application.
Additional Safety and Operational Risks to Fixed-Site Critical Infrastructure Facilities
In addition to security concerns, unauthorized UAS activity presents immediate safety and
operational risks within active fixed-site critical infrastructure hydroelectric facilities. These
environments require constant situational awareness and precise coordination among personnel
managing large volume water flows, critical mechanical systems, and large-scale electric
generation equipment.
The presence of a drone, particularly at low altitude or in close proximity to fixed-site critical
infrastructure facility structures, can create distraction at critical moments, increasing the risk of
operator error in environments where even minor lapses in attention may have serious
consequences.
Further, a malfunctioning or crashed drone has the potential to interfere with fixed-site critical
infrastructure facility operations by impacting hydroelectric dam water-control gates, electric and
mechanical control systems, or other sensitive infrastructure components. Hydroelectric
facilities, like other forms of fixed-site critical infrastructure, depend on the reliable function of
exposed mechanical and structural elements that were not designed to withstand such impacts.
Importantly, these risks are not limited to malicious actors. Even well-intentioned recreational or
commercial drone use can inadvertently create hazardous conditions and disrupt operations.
These safety considerations provide an additional basis for enabling fixed-site critical
infrastructure owners to establish reasonable and enforceable restrictions on close-to-medium-
proximity UAS operations.
3
GRH Comment DOCKET NO. FAA-2026-4558 05142026 v1.

GREAT RIVER HYDRO
69 Milk Street, Suite 308
Westborough, MA 01581
www.greatriverhydro.com
Conclusion
Our direct experience with unauthorized drone activity underscores both the accessibility and the
risk posed by close-proximity UAS operations at hydroelectric facilities. Section 2209 represents
a critical opportunity to close an existing security and safety gap by providing fixed-site critical
infrastructure owners with a structured, enforceable means of limiting such activity.
We appreciate the FAA’s leadership on this issue and encourage adoption of a final rule that is
both protective and practical for fixed-site critical infrastructure operators.
Sincerely,
Patrick Paterson
Regulatory Compliance Specialist
Great River Hydro, LLC
4
GRH Comment DOCKET NO. FAA-2026-4558 05142026 v1.

To: Federal Aviation Administration, U.S. Department of Transportation
Re: Designation, Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to
a Fixed Site Facility; Proposed Rule
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03; RIN 2120-AL33; 91 FR 24650 (May
6, 2026); comments due August 5, 2026
From: Robert J. Green, Independent Economics and National Security Researcher
Executive Summary
I support this rulemaking. Section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security
Act of 2016 has awaited implementation for nearly a decade, and a workable process
for restricting unmanned aircraft operations over fixed site facilities is overdue. I
write to address a design question the proposal raises but does not resolve: what an
unmanned aircraft flight restriction (UAFR) accomplishes against an operator who
does not intend to comply with it, and what follows for how the rule should be built. A
UAFR is a prohibition. It binds the operator who consults the airspace information
systems before flying. It does not bind the operator who has already decided to
violate the law he is standing under, and against that operator a flight restriction
functions after the fact: it enlarges the offense charged, but it does not recover the
imagery collected, unwind the reconnaissance performed, or restore the asset
compromised. Prohibition is not prevention. That observation does not argue against
the rule. It argues for designing the rule around the two functions it can actually
perform against a noncompliant operator: establishing an unambiguous legal
predicate, and improving the detection signal available to the authorities that hold
mitigation authority. I therefore recommend that the FAA (1) state the scope
boundary expressly in the preamble, (2) design the UAFR for detection value rather
than deterrent value, (3) publish UAFR geometries as standardized machine-readable
geospatial data, (4) permit boundary criteria that account for standoff sensing rather
than overflight alone, (5) align fixed site eligibility with the installation lists that
govern the corresponding land-transaction perimeter, and (6) confirm that facilities
manufacturing federally designated critical drugs and their active pharmaceutical
ingredients are eligible under the critical infrastructure sectors the proposal covers.
I. Introduction and Commenter Background
I am an independent researcher specializing in critical supply chain vulnerability,
foreign investment review, and the spatial analysis of national security risk. My
findings derive exclusively from open-source U.S. government data and are
documented and reproducible. I have submitted comments to related dockets,
including the Department of Agriculture's proposed overhaul of the Agricultural
Foreign Investment Disclosure Act (USDA-2026-0001) and the Committee on Foreign
Investment in the United States request for information on the Known Investor
Program (TREAS-DO-2026-0067). I comment here in an individual capacity and hold
no commercial interest in unmanned aircraft, counter-unmanned aircraft systems, or
any product or service affected by this rule.
II. General Position
The proposal implements a statutory mandate that has been outstanding since 2016
and does so in a structured and administrable way. The distinction between a

standard restriction and a special restriction, the requirement that an applicant
demonstrate necessity against enumerated criteria, and the integration of approved
restrictions into FAA airspace information systems are each sound. The FAA has also
been candid, and correct, that this rule creates no counter-unmanned aircraft
authority and authorizes no facility to jam, spoof, or interdict an aircraft. That candor
is a strength of the proposal, and my first recommendation asks the agency to carry it
into the preamble as an express statement of scope.
III. What a Flight Restriction Does, and Against Whom
A flight restriction is an instrument of law addressed to a person who reads it. Its
efficacy is a function of the operator's intent to comply.
Against the careless, the uninformed, and the merely reckless, the UAFR will work
well. Those operators consult airspace information systems, check B4UFLY, register
aircraft at or above the applicable weight threshold, broadcast Remote ID, and hold
remote pilot certification where required. For them, a published restriction is
dispositive: they will not fly there, and the rule will have achieved its purpose before
any enforcement question arises. This population is large, and the safety benefit is
real.
Against an operator conducting deliberate surveillance of a sensitive facility, the
analysis is different, and the difference is structural rather than incidental. Nothing
in the existing regime touches the acquisition of the aircraft: a capable unmanned
aircraft may be purchased over the counter or assembled from components without a
certificate, a registration, or a background check. Registration is an act of
compliance undertaken by a person who intends to comply. Remote ID is a broadcast
that an operator can decline to make, by flying an unregistered aircraft, by omitting
or disabling the module, or by remaining below the registration threshold. Part 107
certificates the pilot who presents himself as a pilot. An operator who has resolved to
conduct unauthorized reconnaissance of a fixed site facility has, by hypothesis,
already accepted the violation of these requirements. A further prohibition adds a
further count to an eventual indictment. It does not stop the flight.
This is not a hypothetical population. Department of Defense officials reported to
Congress more than 350 unmanned aircraft detections at approximately 100 military
installations in 2024, and U.S. Northern Command has since described a sustained
rate of incursions. Over seventeen consecutive nights in December 2023,
unidentified aircraft operated over Joint Base Langley-Eustis. Foreign nationals have
been prosecuted for flying unmanned aircraft over Vandenberg Space Force Base
and near naval shipbuilding facilities. In none of these cases would an additional
published restriction plausibly have altered the operator's decision to fly.
The consequence deserves to be stated plainly, because it bears directly on how the
rule should be written. Against a noncompliant operator, a UAFR operates ex post. It
supplies a clearer basis for prosecution after the collection has occurred. It does not
reduce the information lost or the damage incurred. Deterrence through prohibition
presupposes an audience that weighs the prohibition. Where the adversary has
stepped outside the regulatory system entirely, the rule's value must be sought in
what it enables others to do, not in what it persuades the adversary not to do.

IV. Recommendation 1: State the Scope Boundary Expressly in the
Preamble
I recommend that the final rule state, in the preamble, that a UAFR establishes a legal
prohibition and an airspace designation, that it confers no authority to detect, track,
or mitigate an unmanned aircraft, and that its protective effect against a
noncompliant operator depends entirely on detection and mitigation authorities that
reside outside this rule and outside the FAA. The agency has already said as much in
the proposal. Elevating it to an express statement of scope costs the FAA nothing, is
manifestly accurate, sets appropriate expectations for the facility operators who will
petition for restrictions, and protects the rule against the argument that its
requirements are justified by reference to protections it does not supply.
V. Recommendation 2: Design the UAFR for Detection Value, Not
Deterrent Value
If a UAFR cannot deter a determined operator, it can do something else of
considerable value: it can make that operator visible.
Where lawful traffic is excluded from a defined volume of airspace, any unmanned
aircraft detected within that volume is presumptively unauthorized. That inference is
operationally significant. The recurring difficulty for those responsible for sensitive
facilities is not detecting that some aircraft is present; it is determining, quickly
enough to matter, whether the aircraft is a hobbyist, a news operator, an
infrastructure inspector, or an adversary. A clean airspace designation collapses that
ambiguity. It converts a discrimination problem into a detection problem, and
detection problems are more tractable.
This has a further consequence that I raise with appropriate caution, because the
relevant authorities lie outside the FAA. Officials with mitigation responsibility have
described the requirement to establish hostile intent, a standard drafted for manned
aviation, as a constraint on responding to unmanned surveillance. A published,
unambiguous flight restriction does not resolve that question, and this rulemaking
cannot resolve it. But it does supply a fact that any subsequent determination must
reckon with: the aircraft was operating where no lawful aircraft could be. To the
extent the FAA has discretion in how restrictions are defined, published, and
documented, that discretion should be exercised to maximize the evidentiary clarity
of that fact.
I therefore recommend that the FAA evaluate the design choices in this rule,
including boundary definition, publication format, notice requirements, and the
treatment of permitted transiting operations, against the criterion of detection value:
does the choice make an unauthorized aircraft more clearly distinguishable from an
authorized one? Where a design choice improves deterrence of the compliant while
degrading discrimination of the noncompliant, it should be reconsidered.
VI. Recommendation 3: Publish UAFR Geometries as Standardized
Machine-Readable Geospatial Data
The detection value described above is realized only if the restriction's geometry is
available to the systems that perform detection. I recommend that approved UAFR
boundaries, both horizontal and vertical, be published as standardized, machine-

readable geospatial data in a documented, openly specified format, with stable
identifiers and version history, in addition to their presentation in pilot-facing tools
such as B4UFLY.
A restriction expressed only as a notice legible to a compliant pilot serves the
compliant pilot. A restriction expressed as ingestible geospatial data serves every
detection system that must decide, in seconds, whether a track is anomalous. The
marginal cost to the FAA is small, the data is already being generated to define the
restriction, and the interoperability benefit extends to state and local authorities that
received limited counter-unmanned aircraft authority under recent legislation and
that will otherwise be reconciling airspace designations by hand.
VII. Recommendation 4: Permit Boundary Criteria That Account for
Standoff Sensing
A restriction drawn tightly around a facility's physical footprint addresses overflight.
It does not address collection conducted from outside the boundary by an aircraft
with adequate optics, and modern sensors permit useful collection at meaningful
standoff distances.
I recommend that the criteria for demonstrating that a restriction is necessary permit
an applicant, where the asserted basis is national security or homeland security, to
justify a boundary calibrated to the standoff distance from which the protected
characteristics of the facility can be observed, rather than to the facility's perimeter
alone. I recognize that airspace efficiency imposes a genuine constraint here, that the
FAA must weigh the burden on lawful commercial operations, and that a boundary
drawn without limit would be neither administrable nor lawful. The recommendation
is not for larger restrictions as a general matter. It is that the record permit the
applicant to establish the boundary against the observational threat where that
threat is the stated basis, and that the FAA state the criteria by which it will evaluate
such a showing.
I note explicitly what my own research does not establish. My analysis of Department
of Agriculture data finds that adversary-attributed agricultural landholdings cluster
near military installations at rates well beyond random placement, with the
concentration rising sharply near nuclear-capable installations, and that the signal is
strongest at a scale of twenty-five to fifty miles. That finding demonstrates that
ground near sensitive installations is of demonstrated adversary interest, which
bears on launch-point geography. It does not establish an appropriate radius for an
airspace restriction, which is a function of sensor capability, aircraft endurance, and
airspace burden, and not of land acquisition patterns. I offer the finding for the
proposition it supports and no further.
VIII. Recommendation 5: Align Fixed Site Eligibility with the
Corresponding Land-Transaction Perimeter
The United States protects sensitive installations through instruments operating in
two domains. In the land domain, the Committee on Foreign Investment in the United
States reviews foreign real estate transactions by reference to enumerated
installations listed in Appendix A to 31 CFR part 802, and the Agricultural Foreign
Investment Disclosure Act requires reporting of foreign interests in agricultural land.

In the airspace domain, this rule will define which fixed site facilities may obtain a
flight restriction.
These perimeters presently describe the same national security geography by
different means, developed by different agencies under different criteria, with no
mechanism ensuring that a facility significant enough to warrant land-transaction
review is also eligible for airspace protection, or the converse. I recommend that the
FAA, in coordination with the Department of Defense and the Department of the
Treasury, treat inclusion on the Appendix A installation lists as presumptive evidence
supporting a national security basis for a UAFR petition, and that it invite comment in
the final rule on establishing a standing coordination mechanism so that additions to
one perimeter are considered for the other. Coherence between the land perimeter
and the air perimeter is achievable at low cost, and its absence is difficult to justify to
anyone examining the two regimes together.
IX. Recommendation 6: Confirm Eligibility for Critical Drug and Active
Pharmaceutical Ingredient Manufacturing
The proposal reaches facilities across the recognized critical infrastructure sectors. I
recommend that the final rule confirm expressly that facilities manufacturing drugs,
or active pharmaceutical ingredients for drugs, appearing on an existing federal
critical drug or essential medicines designation are eligible fixed site facilities under
the healthcare and public health and critical manufacturing sectors.
The basis is concentration. My primary-source analysis of the Food and Drug
Administration's Drug Master File system finds that of 7,940 unique active
pharmaceutical ingredients registered globally, 5,980, or 75.3 percent, have a single
registered source, and that eighty-three of the hundred most-prescribed generic
medicines in the United States have no domestic source of active ingredient. These
figures measure registered manufacturing capability rather than commercial volume
and are therefore a floor. Where a single domestic facility represents an irreplaceable
node in the supply of a designated critical medicine, its loss or compromise is a
national security event, and its eligibility for airspace protection should not depend
on an ambiguous sectoral classification. Tying eligibility to designations the
government already maintains, such as the essential medicines list developed under
Executive Order 13944 and the critical drugs identified for the Strategic Active
Pharmaceutical Ingredients Reserve, keeps the category bounded and administrable.
X. Conclusion
Section 2209 should be implemented, and this proposal implements it competently.
The recommendation running through this comment is that the FAA build the rule
around what a flight restriction can accomplish against the operator who ignores it,
which is not deterrence but discrimination: making the unauthorized aircraft visible
as unauthorized, and making that fact legible to the systems and authorities that can
act on it. A rule that is candid about its boundaries, designed for detection, published
in machine-readable form, calibrated where necessary to the observational threat,
and coherent with the land perimeter that guards the same facilities will do more for
national security than a broader prohibition addressed to an audience that has
already stopped listening.

XI. Data Availability
The datasets, replication code, and methodology underlying the findings referenced
above are publicly available through my SSRN author page
(https://ssrn.com/author=10825096), GitHub (https://github.com/rjgreenresearch),
and research website (https://rjgreenresearch.org). The spatial clustering findings
appear in Green, R. (2026), Spatial Clustering of Foreign Agricultural Acquisitions
Near U.S. Military Installations: Evidence from USDA Primary Data, SSRN 6454202.
The findings on the limits of the review perimeter appear in Green, R. (2026), The
Regulatory Perimeter: CFIUS, AFIDA, and the Limits of Foreign Investment Review,
SSRN 6525938. The pharmaceutical concentration findings appear in Green, R.
(2026), Mapping Critical Supply Chain Dependencies: A Sector-by-Sector Analysis of
Single-Source Vulnerabilities in the United States and Major Trading Partners, SSRN
6454618.
I appreciate the agency's consideration of these recommendations and am available
to provide the underlying analysis on request.
Respectfully submitted,
Robert J. Green
Independent Economics and National Security Researcher
Melbourne, Florida
robert@rjgreenresearch.org · ORCID 0009-0002-9097-1021

comments.commenter: Comment from The Ohio Department of Transportation
comments.comment_text:
July 1, 2026
Docket Operations
U.S. Department of Transportation
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE, Room W58-213
West Building, 5th Floor
Washington, DC 20590-0001
RE: Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03; RIN 2120-AL33 - Designation: Restrict the Operation
of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
To the Federal Aviation Administration:
The Ohio Department of Transportation (ODOT), through DriveOhio and its Unmanned Aircraft Systems
(UAS) program, appreciates the opportunity to comment on the Federal Aviation Administration's Notice
of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) to establish a process for designating Unmanned Aircraft Flight
Restrictions (UAFRs) near certain fixed-site facilities. ODOT supports the FAA's objective of protecting
sensitive facilities, critical infrastructure, and public safety. At the same time, the final rule must preserve
the efficient use of the National Airspace System (NAS), avoid over-designation of restricted airspace,
and protect essential state government UAS missions, including infrastructure inspection, emergency
response, disaster recovery, traffic and incident management, and public-safety support.
ODOT operates and supports UAS missions across a broad statewide transportation network. These
missions include bridge and structure inspection, roadway and corridor assessment, traffic and incident
management, construction documentation, asset management, emergency response, disaster recovery,
environmental observation, and public-safety support. Many of these operations are field-driven, time-
sensitive, and conducted in changing conditions where delay can affect infrastructure safety, emergency
response, or public mobility. ODOT is also planning for increasingly automated, remote, and beyond
visual line of sight UAS capabilities that can improve rapid assessment of transportation incidents,
severe-weather damage, and other emergent conditions.
ODOT's comments focus on nine points:
(1) Narrow Eligibility for Transportation Facilities and Preserve True Fixed-Site Critical-Infrastructure
Limits
(2) Clear Notice-Only Access for Standard UAFRs
(3) Emergency Governmental Operations with Launch-First and Post-Notification When Immediate
Action Is Necessary
(4) Expedited And Pre-Coordinated Access Pathways for Special UAFRs
(5) Automated, Centralized, Machine-Readable Notification and Data Exchange
(6) Performance-Based, Validated, And Interoperable Remote ID Sensing Requirements
(7) FAA-Led Information Sharing with State and Local Law Enforcement When UAS Activity May
Involve Unlawful Non-Aviation Conduct
(8) Protection For Low-Altitude Inspection, Emergency Response, And Shielded Public-Service
Operations
(9) Implementation Costs and Federal Funding Support
Page 1 of 6

1. Facility Eligibility Should Be Limited to True Fixed-Site Critical
Infrastructure
ODOT supports FAA's effort to ensure that UAFRs are available only for fixed-site facilities that satisfy
defined sector criteria, have protective security, demonstrate a UAS-specific vulnerability, and show that
damage, disruption, or destruction would create a regional or national-level debilitating impact. This
approach is essential to avoid converting the UAFR program into broad corridor-wide or asset-class-wide
restrictions that would impair the NAS and state DOT operations.
ODOT also supports retaining state prisons within the Emergency Services Sector. Drone-enabled
contraband delivery and surveillance at correctional facilities are well-documented risks, and Ohio has a
direct public-safety interest in ensuring that the final rule continues to recognize the unique security
needs of state correctional institutions. At the same time, any UAFR associated with a correctional
facility should remain narrowly bounded, technically justified, and compatible with lawful public safety,
emergency, and government UAS operations.
For the Transportation Systems Sector, ODOT recommends that FAA retain or further tighten the
proposed highway bridge and tunnel eligibility threshold. The final rule should continue to limit eligibility
to highway bridges or tunnels that meet the proposed threshold of serving 50,000 or more vehicles daily
and having a structure length of at least a half mile.
FAA should also make clear that ordinary grade-separated highway structures, interchange ramps,
underpasses, culverts, roadway segments, highway rights-of-way, maintenance yards, and routine
transportation facilities are not eligible for UAFR designation unless they independently satisfy all fixed-
site, critical-infrastructure, protective-security, demonstrated-need, externality, and regional/national-
consequence requirements. The existence of a bridge, overpass, tunnel, or transportation right-of-way
alone should not justify a federal airspace restriction. Restrictions must be reserved for sites where the
risk and regional or national consequence justify federal airspace action.
2. Standard UAFRs Must Remain Notice-Only System
ODOT strongly supports FAA's proposed statement that site managers do not receive authority to
approve, disapprove, restrict, or prohibit qualifying operations within a standard UAFR. That concept
should remain explicit in the final rule.
For standard UAFRs, qualifying operations under Parts 91, 107, proposed 108, 135, and 137, and
qualifying Public Aircraft Operations, should be able to access the UAFR when the aircraft broadcasts
Remote ID, the operator complies with the applicable access criteria, and the required notice is
provided. Notification should provide situational awareness to the facility and FAA. It should not create a
site-manager veto, a delay mechanism, a local permission process, or a basis for inconsistent site-by-site
operating conditions.
ODOT recommends that FAA include language in the final rule and guidance stating that a facility's
failure to acknowledge receipt of notice does not prohibit a qualifying operation. This clarification is
necessary because, in the field, a notice requirement can easily be interpreted as an approval
requirement if the rule does not expressly say otherwise.
Page 2 of 6

3. Emergency Governmental Operations Should Be Allowed to Launch
First and Notify Later When Immediate Action Is Necessary
ODOT supports the proposed timeline in section 74.255(c) allowing operations conducted by or on
behalf of a government agency or law enforcement acting within the scope of legal authority to provide
verbal notification as soon as reasonably possible and written notification within seven calendar days.
FAA should strengthen this concept in the final rule by making clear that emergency governmental UAS
operations may launch first and notify later when delay would impair emergency response, incident
stabilization, infrastructure safety, disaster recovery, public mobility, or life-safety operations.
State DOT missions are frequently field-driven and time-sensitive. Crews may need to inspect a bridge
after a strike, evaluate flood damage to a roadway, assess a hazardous-materials incident, support law
enforcement at a crash scene, monitor evacuation routes, or document damage after severe weather. In
those circumstances, the operational problem is not only administrative burden; it is the risk that
uncertainty about whom to notify, whether notice was received, or whether a site manager expects
approval authority could delay a mission that protects the traveling public.
FAA should therefore include a clear government emergency pathway with post-operation notification,
auditable documentation, and incident-command compatibility. When multiple public agencies are
operating under a unified command structure, a single incident-command notification or post-operation
report should be sufficient to satisfy the rule for qualifying government operations.
4. Special UAFRs Need Expedited and Pre-Coordinated Access
Pathways for State DOT and Emergency Response Missions
Special UAFRs raise a greater operational risk because they may require using-agency approval and, for
non-using-agency operations, FAA approval. ODOT recognizes the national security and homeland
security circumstances that may justify Special UAFRs. However, the final rule and implementing
guidance should ensure that Special UAFRs do not unintentionally delay emergency response,
transportation incident management, public safety, or disaster assessment conducted by state agencies.
ODOT recommends that FAA establish expedited and pre-coordinated access procedures for state DOTs,
emergency management agencies, law enforcement, fire and rescue, and other public agencies with
recurring or statewide emergency responsibilities. Such procedures could include standing coordination
agreements, agency-level credentials, incident-command access protocols, pre-approved emergency
mission categories, or a rapid approval mechanism available 24 hours per day. FAA should also require
Special UAFR information to identify a current point of contact and an emergency access path that can
be used during degraded communications or disaster conditions. Additionally, FAA should also clarify
how these allowances apply to future authorized BVLOS, automated, dock-based, or remotely
supervised UAS operations conducted by or on behalf of state transportation agencies, emergency
management agencies, or other public entities with statewide operational responsibilities.
Page 3 of 6

5. FAA Should Build a Centralized, Automated, Machine-Readable
UAFR Notification System
ODOT supports FAA's intention to use a web-based module for UAFR access notification. FAA should
design that module to function like a standardized, similar to the Low Altitude Authorization and
Notification Capability (LAANC) enabled notification and coordination capability for UAFRs. The system
should allow operators to identify UAFR boundaries, determine applicable requirements, submit
required notice, receive a time-stamped confirmation, and create an auditable record without relying on
fragmented emails, local forms, or site-specific manual workflows.
ODOT may also be affected as a recipient of UAFR notifications if state-owned transportation facilities or
other public facilities are designated. FAA should account for the administrative burden associated with
receiving, routing, documenting, and responding to notifications, particularly during emergencies or
disasters when state agencies are already managing response operations. The final rule should make
clear that a facility’s receipt of notification is for awareness and recordkeeping only, unless the rule
expressly establishes an approval role. This clarification would reduce the risk that facility personnel,
operators, or local partners mistakenly treat notification as permission.
The system should also make UAFR boundaries, altitude ceilings, activation status, facility contact data,
site identification numbers, notice requirements, emergency notification procedures, and Special UAFR
access instructions available in machine-readable formats and through an application programming
interface (API). State DOTs should be able to integrate this information into GIS tools, mission-planning
applications, dashboards, digital forms, and statewide UAS situational-awareness systems. A machine-
readable approach would reduce misdirected notices, improve field usability, and support consistent
implementation across state and local agencies.
FAA should also clarify that a state-operated tool may support UAFR awareness and routing, but FAA
retains control of the airspace, and no state or facility platform may impose additional approval
requirements beyond the final rule.
6. Remote ID Sensing Requirements Should Be Performance-Based,
Validated, and Interoperable
ODOT supports FAA's proposed requirement that a facility receiving a UAFR have the capability, directly
or through a contracted service, to receive broadcast Remote ID messages from unmanned aircraft
operating within or near the requested UAFR. Remote ID sensing is central to distinguishing lawful and
cooperative operators from potentially non-compliant or suspicious activity.
ODOT recommends that FAA strengthen this requirement using performance-based standards rather
than purely equipment-based requirements. At a minimum, UAFR applicants should demonstrate:
• Documented Remote ID receiving coverage for the requested lateral boundary and relevant
approach areas.
• Conformance with FAA-recognized or DHS validation, testing, or performance criteria for Remote ID
receivers and associated data systems.
Page 4 of 6

• A site-specific RF assessment, coverage study, or equivalent engineering analysis where terrain,
structures, interference, antenna placement, or urban/rural conditions could materially affect
reception.
• Clear data retention, privacy, cybersecurity, and access-control policies for Remote ID data.
• Interoperability with authorized FAA, DHS, state DOT, law enforcement, and emergency
management partners through standardized formats or protocols.
• A process for preserving relevant Remote ID and notification records when an incident is referred to
FAA for enforcement review.
These standards should ensure that UAFR sponsors have a reliable, auditable, and interoperable
awareness capability that supports both facility security and FAA oversight.
7. Information Sharing with State and Local Law Enforcement
ODOT recognizes that the FAA retains authority over the regulation and enforcement of the National
Airspace System. ODOT is not recommending that state or local law enforcement agencies be delegated
FAA airspace enforcement authority or that they serve as the primary enforcement body for UAFR
violations.
However, UAS activity within or near a UAFR volume may also be associated with conduct that falls
within state or local law enforcement jurisdiction, including attempts to introduce contraband,
surveillance of secure facilities, harassment, threats to public safety, interference with emergency
response, or other unlawful activity independent of the airspace violation itself.
For that reason, ODOT recommends that the final rule establish a formal FAA-led information-sharing
and incident referral pathway with appropriate state and local law enforcement agencies. Under this
pathway, Remote Identification data, UAFR notification records, facility reports, and other relevant
operational information available to the FAA or facility owner/operator could be shared with authorized
law enforcement partners when the facts indicate potential non-aviation criminal conduct, public safety
threats, or other unlawful activity within state or local jurisdiction.
Such a framework would preserve FAA authority over airspace matters while enabling state and local law
enforcement to investigate and respond to unlawful activity within their jurisdiction. The final rule
should also address appropriate safeguards for data access, retention, chain of custody, privacy,
cybersecurity, and evidentiary use so that information collected in connection with UAFRs can support
lawful investigations without creating confusion over enforcement roles.
8. The Final Rule Should Protect Low-Altitude Inspection, Emergency
Response, and Shielded Public-Service Operations
ODOT requests that FAA clarify how the standard UAFR access criteria apply to public-service operations
that are not merely transiting the UAFR. Infrastructure inspection, bridge assessment, emergency
response, incident documentation, and disaster assessment may require hovering, orbiting, close-
proximity flight, or repeated passes at low altitude. For bridge and tunnel environments, these
operations may occur close to the structure and within the very area that a UAFR is intended to protect.
Page 5 of 6

FAA should clarify that the requirement to transition a UAFR in the shortest amount of time practicable
does not prohibit necessary low-altitude or close-proximity operations conducted by or on behalf of a
government agency, incident command, or the facility owner/operator when those operations are
otherwise lawful and satisfy applicable notification requirements. FAA should also consider guidance for
shielded operations near structures, including how such missions may be coordinated without creating
unnecessary delay.
9. FAA and DOT Should Address Implementation Costs and Avoid
Unfunded Mandates
ODOT acknowledges FAA's estimate that, under a scenario involving more than 9,000 eligible fixed-site
facilities obtaining UAFRs, annualized costs could range from approximately $21 million to $31 million,
including applicant compliance costs and government review costs. For state agencies, the most
significant costs may include Remote ID sensing hardware or services, validation, RF coverage analysis,
data retention, cybersecurity, notification routing, staff training, maintenance, and lifecycle
management.
ODOT recommends that FAA and the U.S. Department of Transportation identify federal implementation
funding, cost-sharing mechanisms, phased compliance, technical assistance, and model procurement
guidance for state and local government facilities that may seek UAFR protection. Without such support,
implementation could become an unfunded mandate and could result in uneven protection across states
and facility types. FAA should also evaluate whether a phased implementation schedule is appropriate
for state-owned critical infrastructure and correctional facilities.
Conclusion
ODOT supports FAA's effort to create a structured UAFR process for eligible fixed-site facilities. With the
clarifications recommended above, the final rule can improve security around sensitive facilities while
preserving public-service UAS operations, transportation resilience, emergency response, and the
efficient use of the NAS. ODOT appreciates the opportunity to provide these comments and looks
forward to continued collaboration with FAA, DHS, state DOTs, and public safety partners on safe, secure,
and scalable UAS integration.
Sincerely,
Rich Fox
Director, DriveOhio UAS Center
Ohio Department of Transportation
1323 West Blee Road
Springfield, OH 45502
(937) 497-6740
Richard.Fox@dot.ohio.gov
Page 6 of 6

comments.commenter: Comment from State of South Dakota Governor's Office
comments.comment_text:
The state of South Dakota supports the FAA's effort to create a clear framework for Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFR) but offers several recommendations to strengthen the final rule. The key recommendations are:

Expand Eligibility to National Guard Installations: The proposed rule should explicitly include National Guard installations, or a process should be established for them to request flight restrictions.

Framework for Large Public Events: A mechanism for temporary flight restrictions should be created for large, recurring events like the Sturgis Motorcycle Rally, which are not covered under the current fixed-site approach.

State Coordination: The FAA should consult with state agencies before approving UAFRs to ensure local operational awareness and avoid conflicts with emergency response plans.

Rural Infrastructure Pathway: Create a compliance pathway for critical infrastructure in rural and remote areas, allowing for remote monitoring and considering the severity of consequences in petitions.

Strengthen Correctional Facility Eligibility: Allow for special flight restrictions over correctional facilities with documented threats and expedite the process for those with a history of drone-related incidents.

Address Tribal Nation Infrastructure: Clarify the process for tribal governments to petition for UAFRs and establish procedures for consultation and enforcement on tribal lands.

Close the Enforcement Gap: Develop better protocols for notification, evidence preservation, and standard operating procedures to address enforcement challenges, particularly in rural areas.

Address Emerging Detection Challenges: Acknowledge the limitations of current drone detection methods and commit to periodically reviewing requirements as technology evolves.

In conclusion, South Dakota urges the FAA to finalize the rule quickly and to incorporate these recommendations to better protect critical infrastructure, military installations, correctional facilities, tribal infrastructure, and major public events from the threat of malicious drone activity.

July 8, 2026
Ms. Michelle Ferritto
Office of Rulemaking
Federal Aviation Administration
800 Independence Avenue, SW
Washington, DC 20591
Docket Operations
U.S. Department of Transportation
1200 New Jersey Avenue, SE, Room W58-213
West Building 5th Floor
Washington, DC 20590-0001
RE: Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03 – Designation – Restrict the Operation
of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed-Site Facility (Section 2209
Rulemaking)
FAA Office of Rulemaking,
The Canaveral Port Authority appreciates the opportunity to submit public comment regarding
the Federal Aviation Administration’s Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) to establish a
formal process for owners or operators of certain fixed-site facilities to request flight restrictions
of unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) near their sites. The Canaveral Port Authority supports this
proposed rule and recognizes it as a necessary and long overdue step toward preserving
aviation safety, public safety, protection of life and property, and national security.
Port Canaveral is one of the nation’s most strategically significant seaports, operating at the
intersection of maritime commerce, energy distribution, military operations, commercial space
launch operations, homeland security and national defense. The Port annually handles millions
of passenger movements – up to 60,000 passengers in a single day – and more than six million
tons of cargo throughput, including critical commodities, such as transportation fuels and high
value cargoes for military and government operations. The Port also regularly supports
hazardous cargo operations, including liquefied natural gas (LNG) ship-to-ship fueling. Port
Canaveral facilities and operations are in proximity to U.S. Navy, U.S. Army and U.S. Air Force
facilities and their related surface and marine assets. These interdependent operations create a
uniquely complex operating environment in which unauthorized UAS activity presents elevated
safety, security, and operational risks.

As UAS technology continues to rapidly proliferate, Port Canaveral has experienced sustained
drone activity within its airspace, including hundreds of incursions in a single year. These
incidents illustrate the growing gap between current aviation regulations and the operational
realities of critical maritime infrastructure. Unauthorized drone activity in this environment
introduces risks ranging from surveillance and intelligence collection to operational disruption
and, in extreme scenarios, catastrophic consequences in proximity to hazardous materials and
high-density passenger operations.
While the Port Authority strongly supports the FAA’s efforts to implement Section 2209, we
respectfully offer the following considerations to ensure the rule fully accounts for the unique
operational, security, and regulatory environment of U.S. seaports.
Recognition of Seaports as Unique Critical Infrastructure Environments: Unlike traditional
fixed-site facilities, seaports operate as multi-domain environments that include waterside
assets, navigation channels, intermodal transportation corridors, and multi-tenant industrial
infrastructure. Port Canaveral encompasses cruise terminals, energy operations, cargo facilities,
military activities, and space-related operations functioning within a unified but non-contiguous
security footprint.
The FAA should recognize port authorities as eligible owners and operators of fixed-site facilities
to request UAS flight restrictions across the full extent of their jurisdictional operational areas,
including both upland (landside) and waterside infrastructure. Limiting eligibility to individual
facility operators or discrete parcels does not reflect the operational realities of modern ports
and may result in fragmented or ineffective airspace protections.
Port authorities should be permitted to apply for and manage UAS restrictions on a port-wide
basis, without requiring individual tenants or parcel-level operators to submit separate
applications. In a multi-tenant environment such as Port Canaveral, requiring separate
applications for each facility would create unnecessary administrative burden, introduce
inconsistencies in coverage, and delay implementation of needed protections.
A single, coordinated application by the port authority should be sufficient to establish
restrictions across the broader port environment, regardless of tenant participation, where the
port authority retains jurisdictional responsibility for safety and security.
Alignment with Existing Maritime Security Frameworks: Port Canaveral operates under a
comprehensive federal security framework, including Maritime Transportation Security Act
(MTSA) requirements and United States Coast Guard-approved Facility Security Plans (FSPs).

These programs incorporate extensive risk assessments, layered security controls, and
interagency coordination.
The FAA should formally recognize MTSA-regulated facilities and existing Facility Security Plans
as satisfying, or substantially fulfilling, application and documentation requirements under
Section 2209.
Specifically, the FAA should:
• Allow MTSA-regulated facilities and port authorities to submit existing security
documentation in lieu of duplicative analyses.
• Accept Facility Security Plans and associated assessments as sufficient evidence of risk
and operational sensitivity.
• Ensure facilities operating under approved Facility Security Plans should be presumed to
meet the security and risk justification requirements associated with UAS restriction
requests.
• Establish a streamlined or prioritized review process for federally regulated maritime
facilities.
Aligning the Section 2209 process with existing federal maritime security frameworks will reduce
administrative burden, eliminate duplicative review, and accelerate implementation without
reducing security effectiveness.
Flexibility in Defining Airspace Boundaries: Strict adherence to property-line boundaries for
UAS restrictions will create security gaps in the port environment. Critical infrastructure,
including berths, navigation channels, fueling zones, and vessel operations, often extends
beyond fixed parcel boundaries and operates within shared or interconnected spaces.
Modern unmanned aircraft systems also possess surveillance capabilities, including high-
resolution optical sensors and thermal imaging that allow operators to collect sensitive
information from outside traditional facility boundaries.
The FAA should permit applicants to define UAS restriction areas that reflect the full operational
and security footprint of port environments:
• Include waterside operational zones and navigation channels
• Reflect interconnected infrastructure systems
• Provide reasonable buffer areas where necessary to address surveillance and security risk

Consideration of Vertical Airspace Limitations and Surveillance Capabilities: The Canaveral
Port Authority urges the FAA to evaluate whether proposed altitude limitations adequately
reflect the operational realities of port facilities. Port facilities and port operations equipment
such as mobile harbor cranes, ship-to-shore cranes and other industrial equipment create
scenarios where UAS may legally operate above restriction ceilings while maintaining visibility
into sensitive operations.
The FAA should allow applicants to justify expanded vertical protections based on facility
specific infrastructure and demonstrated operational risk.
Operational Impacts and Authorized UAS Activities: Port Canaveral routinely conducts
authorized drone operations in support of inspection, emergency response, environmental
monitoring, and maritime safety missions. These activities are essential to safe and efficient port
operations.
The FAA should establish a clear and practical mechanism that allows authorized operations to
continue within restricted areas, including streamlined or programmatic approvals for
governmental and operational use.
Implementation, Coordination, and Resourcing Considerations: The Port Authority shares
concerns regarding the potential volume of applications and the FAA’s capacity to process
requests in a timely manner.
The FAA should:
• Prioritize applications for critical infrastructure supporting national defense, energy, and
supply chain operations
• Coordinate closely with federal partners, including DHS and the United States Coast
Guard
• Ensure sufficient staffing and technical expertise to support timely review
Detection and Mitigation Considerations: While the establishment of restricted airspace is an
important step, it does not prevent unauthorized or malicious UAS activity on its own. Effective
risk reduction requires complementary detection and mitigation capabilities. Continued federal
coordination to expand detection and response authorities remains essential to ensuring the
effectiveness of the Section 2209 framework.

Closing
Port Canaveral strongly supports the FAA’s implementation of Section 2209 as a necessary
advancement in protecting critical infrastructure within the National Airspace System. The Port
Authority urges the FAA to adopt a flexible, risk-informed approach that fully leverages existing
federal security frameworks to support the strategic significance of U.S. seaports and mitigate
the real-world risk to maritime operations posed by uncontrolled UAS flights.
Sincerely,
John Murray (Jul 8, 2026 22:29:13 EDT)
Capt. John W. Murray
Chief Executive Officer
Canaveral Port Authority

comments.commenter: Comment from City of Maricopa, Arizona
comments.comment_text: See uploaded file with comments from the Chief of Police for the City of Maricopa, Arizona.

Censys Technologies Corporation
1808 Concept Court, Suite 200
Daytona Beach, FL 32114
13 July 2026
Docket Operations, M-30
U.S. Department of Transportation (DOT)
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Washington, DC 20590-0001
RE: Censys Technologies Corporation Comments on the Federal Aviation Administration’s
Notice of Proposed Rulemaking – Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close
Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility (Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03)
To Whom It May Concern,
Censys Technologies Corporation (Censys Technologies) appreciates the opportunity to
comment on the Federal Aviation Administration’s (FAA) Notice of Proposed Rulemaking
(NPRM) concerning the establishment of Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs)
around certain fixed site facilities. Censys Technologies is an American drone manufacturer and
operator that develops and deploys Unmanned Aircraft Systems (UAS) in support of critical
infrastructure inspections and other commercial operations throughout the United States.
Censys Technologies supports the FAA’s efforts to implement Section 2209 of the FAA
Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016 and believes the proposed rule represents an
important step toward protecting critical infrastructure while maintaining access to the National
Airspace System (NAS) for legitimate and safe UAS operations. Censys Technologies is
encouraged by the FAA’s recognition that a UAFR can help facility operators and law
enforcement distinguish between authorized and unauthorized operations in the vicinity of a
protected site. The ability to quickly determine whether a UAS is permitted to operate within a
UAFR will improve situational awareness, enhance security, and allow resources to be focused
on genuinely suspicious activity rather than legitimate commercial operations.
Censys Technologies and many of its customers routinely conduct operations over and around
fixed-site infrastructure that may qualify for Standard UAFRs under the proposed rule, including
facilities within the energy and transportation sectors. Accordingly, Censys Technologies has a

strong interest in ensuring that the final rule both protects critical infrastructure and preserves the
ability of authorized operators to conduct necessary inspections, monitoring, and maintenance
activities.
Section 74.250(a)(2) – Allowed Operations
Censys Technologies appreciates the FAA’s proposed approach to allowed operations within a
UAFR and specifically supports the Agency’s decision to allow certain operations conducted
under Parts 91, 107, 108, 135, and 137 to access UAFR airspace, provided applicable
requirements are met. Censys Technologies is also pleased to see that the FAA has already
contemplated future operations conducted under Part 108 and has incorporated those operations
into the proposed framework. This approach promotes regulatory certainty and avoids the need
for future rulemaking solely to accommodate Part 108 operators.
However, Censys Technologies recommends additional clarification regarding proposed §
74.250(a)(2), which states that:
“The unmanned aircraft transitions the UAFR in the shortest amount of time practicable.”
Censys Technologies appreciates the flexibility provided by the phrase “shortest amount of time
practicable.” However, the final rule should explicitly distinguish between operators that are
merely transiting through a UAFR and operators that have a legitimate operational need to
conduct work within the UAFR itself.
The NPRM acknowledges that some UAS operations may require non-transitory access to
UAFR airspace. Censys Technologies agrees with this concept and recommends that the FAA
clearly state in the final rule that the requirement to transit a UAFR in the shortest practicable
time applies only to operators whose purpose is to pass through the airspace. Operators that have
been authorized by the fixed site owner or operator to conduct inspections, surveys, monitoring
activities, emergency response, or other legitimate operations within the UAFR should be
permitted to remain within the UAFR for the duration reasonably necessary to complete the
operation safely.
Many critical infrastructure inspections require aircraft to maneuver slowly, conduct detailed
imaging, perform repeated passes, or remain within a specific area for extended periods.

Imposing an expectation that such operations be completed as rapidly as possible could create
uncertainty for operators and potentially incentivize behavior that is less efficient or less safe.
The FAA should make clear that authorized operations may remain within a UAFR as long as
necessary to safely accomplish their intended purpose.
Such clarification would preserve the FAA’s objective of preventing unnecessary loitering while
ensuring that infrastructure owners and their approved operators can continue to obtain the data
required to maintain and protect critical assets.
Section 74.255 – Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restriction Access Notification
Censys Technologies supports the FAA’s proposal to require notification and coordination
mechanisms that enable facility operators to identify authorized aircraft operating within a
UAFR. The ability to distinguish legitimate operators from unauthorized operators is one of the
primary benefits of the proposed rule.
However, Censys Technologies strongly recommends that the FAA adopt the alternative
notification method discussed in the NPRM and allow trusted and approved operators to utilize a
standing approval or “whitelist” process.
Under such a process, an operator could submit identifying information once, including relevant
airman certificate information and a list of associated unmanned aircraft registration numbers or
Remote ID identifiers. Once approved by the facility operator, those aircraft and operators could
be recognized as authorized participants for future operations without the need to submit a
separate access request before every flight.
Censys Technologies believes this approach would provide the facility operator with the same
practical security benefit while substantially reducing administrative burden for both the facility
and the UAS operator.
Many infrastructure inspection programs involve routine and recurring operations over the same
assets. Requiring a separate notification submission for each individual flight may be workable at
low volumes but becomes increasingly burdensome as operations scale. As the FAA continues to
enable more advanced operations, including those conducted under Part 108, scalable
authorization processes will become increasingly important.

UAS Enforcement and Interdiction
Censys Technologies also encourages the FAA to emphasize throughout the final rule and
accompanying guidance that a UAFR is an airspace designation and not an authorization for
private entities to interdict, interfere with, disable, or otherwise take action against unmanned
aircraft.
The NPRM correctly notes that the establishment of a UAFR does not independently authorize
operators or proprietors of fixed-site facilities to use equipment designed to take control of,
destroy, or otherwise interfere with unmanned aircraft. Censys Technologies strongly supports
this position.
Authority to interdict aircraft should remain limited to entities specifically authorized by federal
law. Any enforcement response to unauthorized operations should continue to be conducted
through appropriate federal, state, and local law enforcement channels.
Censys Technologies recommends that the FAA prominently reiterate this principle in the final
rule, guidance materials, and public outreach efforts to reduce the potential for misunderstanding
and ensure consistent implementation across affected industries.
Conclusion
Censys Technologies supports the FAA’s efforts to establish a practical and scalable framework
for protecting critical infrastructure through the use of UAFRs. The proposed rule appropriately
balances security concerns with the need to preserve access to the NAS for legitimate and
beneficial unmanned aircraft operations.
To strengthen the final rule, Censys Technologies recommends that the FAA:
1. Clarify that the “shortest amount of time practicable” requirement in § 74.250(a)(2)
applies to operators transiting a UAFR and does not restrict authorized operators from
conducting legitimate operational activities within the UAFR for the time necessary to
complete those activities safely.
2. Adopt a standing authorization or “whitelist” approach under § 74.255 that allows trusted
operators and associated aircraft to be pre-approved for recurring operations within a
UAFR.

1. Emphasize that private entities are not authorized to interdict, disable, or otherwise
interfere with unmanned aircraft solely because a UAFR exists, and that enforcement
authority remains with appropriately authorized government entities.
Censys Technologies appreciates the FAA’s continued engagement with industry on these
important issues and looks forward to supporting the safe integration of UAS operations within
and around protected infrastructure environments.
Respectfully submitted,
Pete Frank
Senior Regulatory Affairs Specialist
Censys Technologies Corporation

This regulation is going to add a significant burden to UAS operators, the FAA, and local law enforcement without having any benefit of stopping a bad actor. This regulation will never stop a person that going to use a UAS for criminal activity as they will simply disregard the law. In fact, this probably has a negative security consequence in that this regulation we require the "publishing" of a list of critical infrastructure locations that might otherwise go unnoticed. These proposed rules if moved forward into regulation would force a public list of security sensitive locations so that legitimate UAS operators know where they are, and this could be exploited by bad actors. A much better security model is to conceal our critical infrastructure as much as possible not publish these locations.
