In addition there will be many operators that inadvertently fly into one of these restricted locations if thousands of these locations are created. This will ultimately waste the time of the FAA and law enforcement and divert their attention away from other safety matters where they would be able to provide a much greater public benefit. The waste of time due to inadvertent UAS operations is already happening regularly with TFRs. This proposed regulation would be increasing UAS airspace restrictions more than 100 times as compared to TFRs. The FAA currently does not have enough staff to enforce most UAS incursions into TFRs now, and will have even less of an ability to investigate and enforce TFRs if they have thousands of other sites they must also investigate.

In addition if the data were to be analyzed I do not believe that this proposed rule if in place now would have reduced, prevented, or stopped any drone security problems that we have had in the USA. Is there even a single event in the past that this regulation would have prevented? I am highly confident the answer is no, because this rule is not going to stop someone that is not going to follow the rules.

comments.commenter: Comment from U.S. Poultry & Egg Association
comments.comment_text:
July 6 ,2026
U.S. Department of Transportation
Federal Aviation Administration
800 Independence Ave. S.W.
Washington, DC 20591
Re: Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03; Designation—Restrict the Operation of
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
To whom it may concern.
Attached you will find comments on the Federal Aviation Administration’s (FAA) proposed rule
“Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site
Facility” published in the Federal Register on May 6, 2026, by the Federal Aviation
Administration (FAA).
We appreciate the opportunity to provide comments on this proposed rule. If you should have
any questions about our comments or need additional information, please contact Paul
Bredwell at 678-514-1973 or via email at pbredwell@uspoultry.org.
Sincerely,
U.S. Poultry & Egg Association

Introduction
US Poultry & Egg Association (USPOULTRY) is the world's largest and most active poultry
organization. Membership includes producers and processors of broilers, turkeys, ducks, eggs,
and breeding stock, as well as allied companies. Formed in 1947, the association has affiliations
in 26 states and member companies worldwide.
The U.S. Poultry & Egg Association is writing to formally submit comments regarding the
Federal Aviation Administration’s (FAA) proposed rule to restrict unmanned aircraft systems
(UAS) operations over critical fixed-site infrastructure. We strongly urge the FAA to explicitly
include commercial poultry and egg production facilities under the definitions of protected
critical infrastructure. Restricting unauthorized drone flights around these farms is vital to
maintaining strict on-farm biosecurity and defending the national food supply.
Threat to Strict Biosecurity and Disease Mitigation
Commercial poultry operations operate under intense biosecurity protocols designed by
industry and the USDA Animal and Plant Health Inspection Service (APHIS) to prevent the
introduction of devastating pathogens. Low-flying, unauthorized UAS present a multi-faceted
biosecurity threat to these sensitive ecosystems:
• Fomite and Cross-Contamination Vector: Drones that move between different areas or
properties can act as mechanical vectors (fomites). They risk transporting microscopic
viral particles on their hulls and propellers directly onto clean farm perimeters.
• Disruption of Wildlife Barriers: Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza (HPAI), Newcastle
Disease, and other zoological viruses are primarily carried by wild migratory waterfowl.
Poultry facilities often use various practices to keep wild birds away from barns. Low-
altitude drone operations can panic wild birds, flushing flocks of migratory waterfowl
into immediate contact with poultry exhaust fans, feed lines, and facility structures.
Protection of National Food Security and Economic Stability
Food and agriculture are recognized as critical federal infrastructure sectors. A single positive
detection of HPAI, Newcastle or numerous other diseases requires immediate responses. In the
case of HPAI, mandatory depopulation of the entire facility's flock is required to contain the
outbreak.
• Market Devastation: Outbreaks lead to localized shortages, skyrocketing consumer
prices, and massive financial losses for producers.
• Trade Impacts: International trading partners frequently shut down borders to U.S.
poultry products upon disease detection. This inflicts widespread damage on national
economic security.

• Supply Chain Vulnerability: Protecting these localized manufacturing centers from
external operational hazards is fundamentally a matter of national security.
Proposed Regulatory Solutions
To ensure the rule achieves its intended goal without placing an undue burden on the
agriculture sector, we recommend the FAA integrate the following into the final rulemaking of
14 C.F.R. Part 74:
1. Critical Infrastructure Status for Food Production: Classify commercial egg-laying
facilities, broiler farms, and turkey operations as high-risk, fixed-site facilities eligible for
explicit flight restrictions.
2. Standard Horizontal and Vertical Buffers: Implement a mandatory minimum lateral and
vertical buffer zone (e.g., 400 feet) around the physical boundaries of animal housing
facilities.
3. Strict Operator Exceptions: Restrict airspace exceptions exclusively to agricultural
drones operated by the farm owner, contracted operators with clear written
authorization, or emergency government response teams.
Conclusion
The poultry and egg industries appreciate that the Federal Aviation Administration has
requested comments on restricting unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) operations over critical
fixed-site infrastructure. Without question, commercial poultry and egg farms are critical
infrastructure as they provide a wholesome, economical and more importantly reliable source
of protein to the citizens of the United States. We thank the FAA for its proactive approach to
handling the safety and security risks associated with integrating UAS into the national airspace.
Protecting the biosecurity of our agricultural facilities is essential to keeping our national food
supply resilient, safe, and stable.
Additionally, the proposed Rule’s ability to protect these operations from intended and
unintended consequences of disrupting the animal husbandry operations that take place on US
poultry and egg operations is paramount to protecting the security of our nation’s food supply.
The U.S. Poultry & Egg Association welcomes the opportunity to provide these comments and
look forward to working with the FAA. If you have any questions regarding these comments or
would like additional information, please contact Paul Bredwell at pbredwell@uspoultry.org.
Respectfully submitted,
U.S. Poultry & Egg Association

The dual objective articulated in this NPRM—protecting critical infrastructure while integrating
unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) into the National Airspace System (NAS)—is both necessary
and important. While much of the proposal appropriately focuses on the mechanics of
establishing Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs), I encourage the FAA to consider a
broader design principle: that as new regulatory tools are introduced, the overall airspace
regulatory framework should become more comprehensible, not less.
Although regulations governing UAS (such as Part 107) necessarily differ in important respects,
UAS operators, traditional aircraft, and air traffic services ultimately share a single National
Airspace System. Where operational objectives are similar, regulatory frameworks should
converge rather than evolve into parallel systems with different terminology, logic, or
operational expectations.
Decades of experience show that complexity imposes its own costs, including to the paramount
goal: safety. Ongoing NOTAM modernization efforts reflect the reality that even critical
operational information becomes less effective when it is difficult to identify amidst excessive
volume and complexity. Similarly, Temporary Flight Restrictions (TFRs) are vital for pilots to
be aware of and comply with, yet the cumulative complexity of how they are disseminated and
communicated has long been a source of frustration and operational error. Even conscientious
operators can struggle when critical restrictions are fragmented across multiple systems or
presented inconsistently.
While the NPRM does not contemplate changes to the typology of Special Use Airspace (SUA)
that pilots are taught from day one, there are design principles that could be more explicitly
integrated into the UAFR concept that promote clarity, integration, and harmonization. For
example:
• Consistent terminology across existing airspace restrictions
• Common logic for activation, publication, and depiction
• Integration into existing pilot and remote pilot briefing tools and workflows
• Clear and predictable operational expectations for transit versus prohibited activity
• A bias towards standardized, predictable implementations instead of facility-specific
variations
From a human factors perspective, every new category of airspace restriction, publication
mechanism, or operational distinction increases cognitive demands on operators. While each
individual addition may be justified on its own merits, the cumulative effect should also be
considered as part of the rulemaking process.
As advanced air mobility, increasingly autonomous aircraft, and beyond-visual-line-of-sight
operations mature, distinctions between traditional aircraft and UAS are likely to become
progressively less operationally significant. The regulatory architecture adopted today should, to
the greatest extent possible, facilitate that future integration rather than entrench separate
conceptual frameworks. Even if rulemaking processes are necessary to meet narrowly targeted
regulatory needs – as in this case, with the imperative of protecting sensitive airspace around

sensitive infrastructure – each should be taken as an opportunity to drive this coherence and
vision of a unified NAS.
It is easy to argue that some concerns, such as the imperatives of national security, warrant new
tools and frameworks such as UAFRs, with new complexities being an unfortunate side effect.
Regulatory simplicity is therefore not merely a matter of administrative convenience. It is an
enabling condition for regulatory compliance, operator situational awareness, public trust, and
ultimately the safe integration of increasingly diverse aircraft into a single National Airspace
System.
I encourage the FAA to incorporate these principles into the final rule and into any associated
implementation guidance, recognizing that the long-term success of UAFRs will depend not only
on their legal authority, but also on their clarity, predictability, and ease of integration into
existing workflows.
Submitted by:
Theodore Waddelow
FAA Commercial Pilot, Flight Instructor, Former Senior Director for Corporate Affairs, Visa
Inc.

comments.commenter: Comment from ClearView Environmental Engineering LLC
comments.comment_text:
After reviewing the proposal and randomly reading submitted comments, I do not agree with this unmanned aircraft flight restriction UAFR. I own a small environmental engineering company that operates sUAS in Class -D airspace, around military installations with 0 AGL altitudes. Have part 107 and a real fixed-wing pilot certification, retired Air Force. All my mission sorties have Airspace Authorization from the FAA before missions and full contact with responsible ATCT during missions; I’m a “good actor”.

My simple reason for no … FAA can have 100s of rules/fines on UAS operations, but none of them discourage “bad actors” or even ignorant UAV operators from following them. I encounter kids flying UASs at 0 altitude Class-D airspace. I have parents tell me they buy UASs for their kids so they can take pictures for their business, no clue what Part 107 is or FAST knowledge, or even know they are in Class-D airspace. Anyone (child, foreigner, felon, anyone with money) can buy a UAS and fly it. No FAA rule prevents the purchase of a UAS in the USA. FAA UAS rules are equivalent to Federal and state gun rules, “bad actors” don’t care about them, and it does not prevent misuse. The average person still thinks drones and RC planes are just toys because kids fly them.

The FAA has a hard enough time enforcing GA/commercial aviation rules. NTSB has cited the FAA numerous times for lack of enforcement. UAS operations are another layer of rules added to FAA’s already stretched-thin manpower. My nearest Flight Standards District Office (FSDO) is 4.5 hrs away in a different state. The next closest, in my state but in a different time zone, is 6 hrs away. The manning that handles UAS as an additional duty is about 3, sometimes 4 people. Last thing I want is to increase any federal agency funding/manning.

FAA needs to realize that current UASs usage, in the USA, is a consumer “good or bad actor” free-for-all. The availability of very tech-capable UASs for very few dollars is astonishing. If one wants protection from UASs flying over them, one will need to adapt to active denial of flight means. Ukraine’s usage of UASs, in the last few years, are real lessons learned for UAS applications in the USA.

Finally, as noted by others. When the FAA starts listing hundreds of small, restricted airspaces around the USA, on FAA UAS Data on a Map (arcgis.com), that will let “bad actors” know that there is something critical in that area. This rule will make things worse.

July 13, 2026
Electronic Submission: Regulations.gov
U.S. Department of Transportation
Docket Operations
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Room W58-213, West Building, 5th Floor
Washington, DC 20590
Re: Comments in Response to FAA Section 2209 NPRM: Designation - Restrict the
Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility [Docket No.
FAA-2026-4558]
Dear Sir or Madam:
The Fertilizer Institute (TFI), on behalf of its member companies, appreciates the opportunity to
comment on the Federal Aviation Administration's Notice of Proposed Rulemaking, Designation
– Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility. TFI
supports the FAA's efforts to implement Section 2209 and establish a framework that addresses
the risks posed by unauthorized unmanned aircraft operations near critical infrastructure facilities.
Statement of Interest
TFI represents the nation’s fertilizer industry, and its members are engaged in all aspects of the
fertilizer supply chain. Fertilizer is a key ingredient in feeding a growing global population, which
is expected to surpass 9.5 billion people by 2050. Half of all food grown around the world today
is made possible through the use of fertilizer.
Over the past three years, the U.S. fertilizer industry invested an average of $2.4 billion annually
in capital infrastructure projects. These investments create jobs, increase worker and community
safety, and help conserve energy, land, water, and air resources. The U.S. fertilizer industry is one
of the world’s largest. The United States is the world’s fourth largest producer of nitrogen-based
fertilizers and the fifth largest producer of phosphate fertilizers. The U.S. fertilizer industry
generates more than $130 billion in economic benefit annually and provides approximately
500,000 U.S. jobs.
TFI supports the FAA's efforts to establish a regulatory framework that addresses unmanned
aircraft system (UAS) risks to critical infrastructure while supporting the safe integration of UAS
into the National Airspace System (NAS). TFI supports implementation of Section 2209 and
recognizes the importance of a risk-informed, practical, and operationally achievable process for
establishing unmanned aircraft flight restrictions (UAFRs). TFI also appreciates the FAA's
coordination with Sector Risk Management Agencies (SRMAs), industry stakeholders, and
security partners throughout development of the proposed rule.
TFI believes several aspects of the proposed rule would benefit from additional clarification,
refinement, and operational flexibility to ensure the framework can be implemented effectively
1

across the fertilizer supply chain, including manufacturing facilities, storage and distribution
terminals, blending operations, and transportation-related infrastructure. In particular, the final rule
should reflect the diverse nature of fertilizer operations and the important role these facilities play
in supporting U.S. agriculture, food production, and supply chain resilience, while avoiding
unintended impacts to lawful UAS operations.
TFI's comments are intended to support a practical implementation approach that enhances
aviation safety, security, and protection of people and property without creating unnecessary
regulatory burdens, operational uncertainty, or barriers to participation for critical infrastructure
owners and operators.
Discussion
TFI encourages the FAA to recognize that the primary risk posed by unauthorized UAS
operations at fertilizer facilities is not limited to surveillance or intentional attack, but includes
the potential for accidental or intentional release of toxic inhalation hazard (TIH) materials.
Drone incursions involving collision, payload delivery, or interference with critical systems
could result in significant off-site consequences, posing unacceptable risks to workers,
surrounding communities, and the environment.
I. Key Areas of Support
A. Establishing a Section 2209 Implementation Framework
a. TFI supports the FAA's efforts to establish a workable regulatory framework for
implementing Section 2209 and addressing UAS-related risks to critical
infrastructure. A final rule that is clear, administrable, and risk-informed would
provide an important tool for facility owners and operators while supporting the
continued safe integration of UAS into the NAS.
b. Fertilizer manufacturing, storage, blending, distribution, and transportation
facilities play a critical role in supporting U.S. agriculture, food production, and
supply chain resilience. This rule represents an important step toward enhancing
UAS security for critical infrastructure facilities while supporting future efforts to
address evolving UAS threats.
c. The final rule should recognize the importance of facilities that support agricultural
production, domestic manufacturing, and critical supply chains. Protecting
infrastructure essential to food and agricultural systems will strengthen national
resilience while advancing broader objectives related to economic security and
critical infrastructure protection.
B. Risk-Informed Protection of High-Consequence Fixed Site Facilities
a. TFI supports the concept of targeted UAFRs for facilities where unauthorized
UAS operations may create safety, security, environmental, operational, or supply
chain risks. Fertilizer facilities often handle high-risk products and processes that
are essential to agricultural production and regional distribution networks. The
final rule should preserve the FAA oversight while allowing eligibility and
implementation decisions to account for facility-specific vulnerabilities,
2

consequences, and operational realities. This risk is particularly acute for facilities
handling anhydrous ammonia and other hazardous chemicals, where even a small-
scale drone interaction could initiate a release event. The consequences of such an
incident extend well beyond the facility boundary and should serve as a primary
consideration in determining eligibility, boundary design, and operational
restrictions.
C. Coordination with SRMAs, Security Partners, and Industry Stakeholders
a. TFI appreciates the FAA's coordination with SRMAs, security partners, and
stakeholders during development of the proposed rule. Continued coordination will
be essential to ensure that final Part 74 requirements protect sensitive information,
minimize unintended impacts to lawful operations, and remain practical for
fertilizer manufacturing, storage, and distribution facilities. TFI encourages the
FAA to continue outreach with both critical infrastructure owners to promote
understanding of the program's requirements, application process, and operational
expectations.
D. Transparency with Critical Infrastructure
a. TFI supports appropriate notification procedures and transparency throughout the
UAFR designation and implementation process. Facility owners and operators
require timely information regarding authorized UAS operations that may occur
within or near designated restricted airspace. Effective coordination among the
FAA, facility operators, authorized UAS operators, and other stakeholders will help
improve situational awareness, reduce confusion, and support the safe and secure
operation of both critical infrastructure and legitimate UAS activities.
b. TFI also notes that many fertilizer facilities rely on internal UAS programs to
support safety-critical operations, including hazardous gas detection, emergency
response, inspections, and maintenance planning. Requirements that delay, restrict,
or create additional administrative steps for these internal operations may introduce
unintended safety and compliance risks and should be carefully minimized.
II. Modifications Required to Enhance Critical Infrastructure Security and Resilience
A. Section IV.B: Lateral boundaries, risk-based and beyond the fence line
Section IV.B of the NPRM provides that the lateral boundaries of a UAFR must lie
entirely within the applicant's property lines. TFI proposes that the FAA instead establish
lateral boundaries based on a facility-specific risk analysis rather than fixing them to the
perimeter fence or property line. For example, for many fertilizer facilities, the
consequences of an incident, including a release of an inhalation hazard, extend past the
fence line, and the restricted area should be sized accordingly. The FAA should also allow
boundaries to extend beyond the property line in defined circumstances, such as covering
an adjacent logistics point of departure. Multiple TFI members interact with hazardous
chemicals such as anhydrous ammonia, including ground-based and marine access
operations. Such operations face a sustained threat during vessel loading, which can take
up to 36 hours and present stationary, high-consequence targets. For these reasons, a risk-
based justification for a specific site would grant beyond-fenceline boundaries to protect
such unique assets.
3

B. Section IV.B: Vertical boundary clarity
Section IV.B of the NPRM prescribes the ceiling of UAFR and SUAFR zones. TFI
proposes that final rule also detail the offset or rounding method to determine the ceiling
above a qualifying structure so facilities can determine the applicable ceiling without
ambiguity.
C. § 74.250: Asset-owner contribution to standard UAFR overflights
Proposed § 74.250 allows operators under Parts 91, 107, 108, 135, and 137 to transit a
Standard UAFR if they broadcast Remote ID, transit in the shortest practicable time, and
notify the facility under proposed § 74.255, which leaves the facility with notice but no
opportunity to provide input in the approval process.
TFI proposes a defined opportunity for the facility to comment on or request a delay of
the proposed overflight during hazardous operation. A set window, for example 72 hours
to accept or respond, would give facilities a voice without creating a blanket prohibition.
This mirrors how the FAA already coordinates with owners under existing national security
airspace restrictions, where the agency seeks input before approving an overflight while
keeping final authority.
Additionally, the FAA should explicitly recognize that credible threats are not limited
to larger commercial platforms. Small, low-cost UAS, including hobbyist and FPV drones,
have demonstrated the ability to carry payloads and cause damage with minimal resources.
This reality reinforces the need for strict overflight controls and minimized ambiguity in
transit provisions.
D. § 74.250, Section IV.B: Remote ID needs an industry standard
Proposed § 74.250 and Section IV.B require operators to broadcast Remote ID and
facilities to deploy Remote ID receivers under 14 CFR part 89, without identifying a
standard for the receiving technology. TFI supports using Remote ID to separate authorized
operators from non-compliant aircraft, and recognizes the FAA intends to treat any aircraft
not broadcasting Remote ID as non-compliant. The rule’s gap is that it requires facilities
to acquire Remote ID receiving capability without identifying a standard for that
technology, so facilities are asked to invest in equipment with no defined performance
baseline. The final rule should establish or reference a performance-based capability
standard.
Facilities also face uncertainty regarding the cost and technical requirements associated
with Remote ID implementation, including procurement of compatible receiving systems
and potential transition to approved (“blue-listed”) UAS platforms. The final rule should
provide clarity to avoid premature or misaligned investments.
E. § 74.250: Transit definition and standoff buffer
Proposed § 74.250 permits authorized operators to transit a UAFR “in the shortest
amount of time practicable,” without defining the path or geometry. The allowance for
authorized operators to transit a UAFR in the shortest practicable time leaves room for
ambiguity. This will create disputes between facility security teams and lawful operators.
TFI proposes that the final rule include a requirement for the operator to define
authorized transit as a direct, straight-line path, prohibit loitering or zigzag routes, and
provide a risk-based lateral buffer where warranted. Where risk warrants, the FAA should
4

require an absolute lateral standoff buffer that prevents any overflight of critical process
areas, rather than relying solely on behavioral restrictions within the UAFR. For high-
consequence facilities handling TIH materials, Standard UAFRs should default to a no-fly
posture except for explicitly authorized operations. Allowing routine commercial transit
over sensitive assets introduces unnecessary and avoidable risk.
F. Section I.E: Protect confidential security information
Section I.E of the NPRM requires applicants to describe how a UAFR would be
integrated into the facility's security plans, and Section IX.B governs treatment of
Confidential Business Information (CBI). TFI proposes that the final rule clarify that an
applicant may submit its drone response plan and only a high-level, non-descriptive
summary of its overall security plan rather than the full security plan, and that the FAA
rely on the established CBI procedures to protect any sensitive information submitted in
support of a UAFR application.
G. Sections V.C, V.D: Eligibility criteria
Sections V.C and V.D of the NPRM set the minimum and sector-specific eligibility
criteria for part 74, and Section I.D confirms the Administrator's retained authority to weigh
facility-specific circumstances. TFI proposes that the sector-specific eligibility criteria for
the chemical and food-and-agriculture sectors remain flexible and be updated as risk
profiles evolve, rather than fixed to a static list.
TFI recommends building on the security-focused CFATS Schedule A approach while
keeping it adaptable as chemicals and risk profiles change and asks the FAA to apply its
“at the discretion of the Administrator” authority to weigh facility-specific circumstances
case by case rather than against rigid thresholds.
H. Recognition of Small UAS Threats
The final rule should explicitly account for the demonstrated capability of small, low-
cost UAS platforms to deliver payloads or cause physical damage. Regulatory frameworks
that focus primarily on larger or commercial UAS risks underestimate the evolving threat
landscape.
III. Other Opportunities to Improve 14 CFR Part 74
A. Sections V.C, V.D: Definition of “like facilities”
Sections V.C and V.D of the NPRM screen eligibility through sector-specific minimum
criteria, and Section I.E requires applicants to describe existing unmanned aircraft activity
over the facility. TFI proposes clarification on how these criteria treat facilities of the same
type that present materially different actual UAS activity. Facilities of similar type can
show very different confirmed UAS activity, and a clear definition is needed to know how
the term applies to individual applications.
B. Section I.E: Designation notice process shouldn’t create a target list
Section I.E of the NPRM would publish each proposed UAFR in the Federal Register
for a minimum 30-day comment period and would post granted UAFRs on a public
website. TFI proposes that the final rule adopt a notice process that does not create a public
inventory of facilities seeking or receiving protection, as such a list could identify sensitive
5

facilities and increase security risks. The process for noticing UAFR designations should
not produce a public list that functions as a target map of protected facilities. If an approved
designation proceeds directly to a final determination, that approach would be acceptable.
However, if the FAA elects to include a notice-and-comment process, the comment period
should be limited to no more than 30 days and should incorporate safeguards to protect
sensitive information, such as providing direct notification to registered stakeholders rather
than broadly publishing facility identities
C. Sections I.B, IV.B: Counter-UAS authority
While TFI recognizes that counter-UAS authority is outside the scope of this
rulemaking, the absence of detection and mitigation authority leaves a critical gap between
regulatory designation and real-world risk reduction. A UAFR establishes a legal boundary
but does not physically prevent unauthorized drone incursions. TFI encourages continued
coordination across federal agencies to address this gap and develop practical response
capabilities for critical infrastructure operators and law enforcement
D. Section V.G: UAFRT/SUAFR recertification
Section V.G of the NPRM governs the term and renewal of a UAFR under Subpart F.
TFI proposes a straightforward recertification process where the facility's footprint and risk
profile have not materially changed since the original designation. In those cases,
applicants should be permitted to demonstrate continued eligibility through a renewal
process that focuses on the effectiveness of the restriction rather than a full re-justification
of the need for the UAFR. This approach would reduce unnecessary administrative burdens
on both applicants and the FAA while ensuring that UAFRs continue to serve their intended
security purposes.
E. Annual reporting
TFI proposes that any annual reporting remain within the facility's own drone
response policy, and encourages the FAA to avoid prescriptive annual reporting that adds
burden without a clear security benefit.
F. Enforcement and Response Limitations
TFI notes that local law enforcement agencies may have limited resources and capabilities
to respond to unauthorized UAS incursions in real time. The effectiveness of UAFRs
depends not only on designation but also on enforcement. The FAA should consider how
enforcement mechanisms, coordination, and penalties can support the deterrence
objectives of the rule.
IV. Cost Analysis Considerations
A. Section IV.A: Environmental assessment
Sections IV.A and I.E of the NPRM build an environmental review into the UAFR
process. TFI respectfully urges the FAA to eliminate or narrow these requirements except
in the rare circumstance where a proposed UAFR presents a clearly identifiable
6

environmental concern that is not already addressed through the agency’s programmatic
review. A UAFR is fundamentally a security and airspace management measure, not a
physical construction, facility operation, or other environmentally affecting process. The
proposed approach could impose substantial costs and delays on applicants seeking
security protections. As a result, these provisions would likely discourage applicants from
pursuing a UAFR despite the security risks that are currently present.
B. Section I.E: 24-month data requirement
Section I.E of the NPRM requires applicants to document existing unmanned aircraft
activity over the facility as a precondition to designation. TFI proposes that this historical
activity data be developed after designation rather than as a barrier to entry, since a facility
facing a current threat is left unprotected while trying to meet this requirement.
C. Section IV.A, I.E: Overall burden and a fast-track pathway
Sections IV.A and I.E of the NPRM place the full evidentiary burden on applicants
across the application, environmental review, and supporting documentation. TFI
recommends a streamlined, fast-track Standard UAFR pathway under which initial
designation would require only a facility description, confirmation of sector eligibility, and
a basic statement of security need, with additional information provided after designation.
The current application, recordkeeping, renewal, and consultant requirements may impose
unnecessary burdens that could deter participation by facilities the rule is designed to
protect.
D. Operational and Technology Cost Uncertainty
TFI recommends the FAA further evaluate the potential cost burden associated with
compliance, including investment in Remote ID infrastructure, UAS platform replacement,
and integration of detection technologies. These costs may be significant and are currently
difficult for facilities to forecast.
V. Conclusion
TFI represents the companies that manufacture, store, blend, distribute, and transport the fertilizer
that sustains American agriculture. The United States is the world's fourth largest producer of
nitrogen-based fertilizers and the fifth largest producer of phosphate fertilizers, and the industry
generates more than $130 billion in annual economic benefit while supporting approximately
500,000 American jobs. Half of all food produced worldwide depends on fertilizer, which ties the
security of these facilities directly to the security of the nation's food supply.
TFI's member facilities sit within several of the sixteen critical infrastructure sectors identified in
National Security Memorandum 22 and defined under 42 U.S.C. 5195c(e), including the Chemical,
Food and Agriculture, and Critical Manufacturing sectors. Many of these same facilities already
carry high-consequence designations under other federal security frameworks, including the
Maritime Transportation Security Act, the Environmental Protection Agency's Risk Management
Program, and the chemicals of interest identified in CFATS Appendix A before that program
lapsed. These overlapping classifications reflect a simple fact: The potential for a drone-related
7

incident to trigger a toxic release with off-site consequences underscores the importance of a
strong, clearly defined, and enforceable framework.
For these reasons, TFI urges the FAA to adopt a final rule that is risk-informed, operationally
workable, and accessible to the critical infrastructure owners it is meant to protect. The refinements
described in these comments would strengthen the framework's security value while avoiding
unnecessary burdens that could deter participation by the very facilities Section 2209 was enacted
to safeguard. TFI appreciates the opportunity to comment and stands ready to serve as a resource
to the FAA as it works toward a final rule.
Please contact me by email at kcryan@tfi.org with any questions regarding our comments.
Sincerely,
Kaytlin Cryan
Director, Government Affairs
8

I am a Survey Technician and FAA Part 107 Certified Remote Pilot. I routinely use unmanned aircraft systems (UAS) to collect surveying and mapping data for engineering, land development, transportation, ALTA surveys, and infrastructure projects. My work includes gas power plants, solar power plants, electrical transmission lines and substations, water towers, railroad corridors, wastewater treatment facilities, and adjacent properties where accurate aerial data is essential for design and construction.
I oppose the proposed rule as currently written because it risks unnecessarily restricting access to the National Airspace System for legitimate commercial operations performed by trained and certificated remote pilots.
My primary concern is that UAS Facility Restriction (UAFR) boundaries could extend beyond the actual critical infrastructure site and encroach upon neighboring properties or public rights-of-way. Surveyors and engineers are frequently hired to perform work on adjacent parcels, roadway projects, utility improvements, or site developments requiring aerial data near existing infrastructure. Overly broad UAFRs could prevent the collection of essential survey information or force inefficient and potentially less safe flight operations simply to avoid restricted airspace.
The proposed rule should establish a clear, standardized, and timely authorization process for legitimate commercial operators. Critical infrastructure owners routinely hire surveying and engineering firms to perform aerial mapping of their own facilities, while neighboring property owners often require surveys that cannot reasonably be completed without operating nearby. Certificated Part 107 operators should have a practical means of obtaining authorization for legitimate commercial work.
I am particularly concerned about requests to designate entire railroad corridors, transmission corridors, pipelines, or other linear infrastructure as UAFRs. Such restrictions would extend far beyond what is necessary and unnecessarily interfere with surveying, engineering, construction, agriculture, utility inspection, and infrastructure projects. Where a demonstrated security concern exists, restrictions should be narrowly tailored to the specific facility or location rather than extending for miles along an entire corridor.
The National Airspace System should remain a national system managed by the FAA. Airspace restrictions should remain rare, evidence-based, geographically limited, and justified by demonstrated security needs. The proposed process should not allow private or non-federal facility operators to create broad drone exclusion zones that result in a patchwork of permanent restrictions across the country.
The FAA has historically regulated airspace, not individual classes of aircraft. If an area presents such a significant security risk that aircraft should not operate overhead, that determination should be based on the sensitivity of the airspace itself rather than imposing restrictions that apply only to unmanned aircraft while manned aircraft remain free to overfly the same area. A helicopter or fixed-wing aircraft operating between approximately 400 and 1,000 feet AGL may present a similar or greater risk than a small UAS, yet those operations would remain permissible. Any permanent restriction should be supported by evidence, narrowly tailored to the identified risk, and applied consistently unless the FAA can clearly demonstrate why different treatment is necessary.
If UAFRs are established, they should be fully integrated into existing FAA airspace systems, including FAA data products, LAANC-enabled applications where appropriate, FAA facility maps, Air Aware/B4UFLY applications, and common commercial flight planning software so remote pilots can identify restrictions during normal preflight planning.
Finally, I encourage the FAA to determine whether permanent UAFRs are the least restrictive means of addressing security concerns. Professional Part 107 operators are already subject to FAA certification, operational rules, and enforcement. Additional restrictions should not unnecessarily impede legitimate surveying, engineering, and infrastructure work.
I respectfully request that the FAA revise the proposed rule to ensure that any UAFRs are narrowly defined, supported by a demonstrated security need, integrated into existing FAA authorization systems, and accompanied by a practical authorization process for legitimate commercial operators.

Unmanned drones should not be flown over railroad yards by anyone for safety purposes. As a railroader I don’t know who is operating those and what their intent is. With so much sensitive and high security materials traveling thru, how am I to know if it’s just taking video or plotting out an attack. I feel unsafe wether it’s the railroad flying the drone or a regular civilian.

I am deeply concerned about the FAA’s proposal to make it more difficult to use drones for documenting industrial animal agriculture facilities. These facilities often cover vast areas and intentionally conceal the treatment of farmed animals. Drone photography has been crucial in revealing the hidden realities of these operations. Restricting drone use would hinder transparency and accountability, making it harder for the public to understand and address the issues within this industry. I urge the FAA to reconsider this proposal and maintain the ability for drone use to ensure ethical practices in animal agriculture.

Re: Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a
Fixed Site Facility
Dear Secretary Duffy and Administrator Bedford,
On behalf of the undersigned organizations dedicated to transparency in the animal
agriculture industry, we write to express our opposition to the Federal Aviation
Administration’s (FAA) proposed rule as it applies to photojournalism about the food and
agriculture industry and to share our concerns about the development of gating criteria for
food and agriculture fixed site facilities to obtain unmanned aircraft flight restrictions
(UAFRs). We thank the FAA for the opportunity to provide public input on these questions.
The proposed rule threatens to shield food and agriculture fixed site facilities from
needed public oversight. If finalized, it would chill First Amendment-protected
newsgathering activities, depriving the public of vital information about the nation’s food
system without furthering any legitimate safety interests.
Photojournalists and animal advocates who collect images and video of food and
agriculture fixed site facilities from unmanned aircraft systems (UAS, or drones) engage in
newsgathering and speech protected by the First Amendment. Drones are a vital tool that
farmSTAND.org | 712 H Street NE, Suite 2534 Washington, DC 20002 | (202) 630-3095

allow photojournalists to gather compelling and communicative images of large
agriculture operations—footage that would be impossible to obtain from a roadside
vantage and prohibitively costly to capture with other aerial technology.
These images provide the public with critical information and visual context about
large-scale animal farming, furthering public debate on the role and impacts of corporate-
controlled concentrated animal feeding operations (CAFOs) in our food system.
Photojournalists and advocates use drone-captured images to, for example, educate the
public about the scale and natural resource demands of industrial animal farming, monitor
and enforce environmental and animal care regulations, protect consumers and ensure
fair market competition, and document the impact of natural disasters and disease
outbreaks. Such speech and newsgathering on issues of public concern lies at the heart of
the First Amendment.
The photojournalists within the undersigned commenters’ network of U.S.-based
drone operators are experienced, Part 107-certified operators who take care to follow all
local, state, and federal drone regulations. There is no evidence that they or any other
drone operators pose any legitimate safety or security threat to persons, animals, or
property on food and agriculture fixed site facilities. And shielding such facilities from
drone-captured imagery and videos is not a valid state interest.
Allowing very large facilities that confine thousands of animals to obtain UAFRs,
under the agency’s suggested gating criteria for the sector, would shut down an important
avenue of public accountability without any attendant benefit to safety or security.
Facilities that supply a significant portion of a region’s or the country’s meat, milk, or eggs
should be subject to greater public oversight of their operations—including through aerial
photojournalism—not allowed to shut down that oversight.
Furthermore, the FAA’s narrow proposed allowance of drone operations within
UAFRs would not alleviate the chill on protected speech and newsgathering. Allowing
operations in a UAFR only if the drone transits the restricted airspace in the shortest time
practicable, and if the operator provides advanced notice to the manager of the fixed site
facility, would make effective visual newsgathering impossible.
The undersigned thus urge the agency not to develop gating criteria for or freely
grant UAFRs to food and agriculture fixed site facilities.

I. Interests of Commenters
We Animals is an international organization that advocates for animals through
photojournalism. It provides strong visual storytelling tools to help animal advocates
communicate effectively. We Animals has an interest in ensuring open access to airspace
over food and agriculture fixed site facilities for all means of gathering aerial imagery and
recordings of those facilities. We Animals uses the aerial imagery it collects from its
protected newsgathering activities in the airspace over food and agriculture fixed site
facilities to engage in protected free speech, encouraging public debate about animal
farming and animal welfare. Airspace access for newsgathering drone operations is
essential to We Animals’s visual advocacy.
Animal Outlook is a national animal advocacy organization. It challenges animal
agribusiness through undercover investigations, legal advocacy, corporate and food
system policy reform, and disseminating information about the many harms of animal
agriculture. Animal Outlook uses drone imagery to further its mission to collect and
disseminate information about the harms of animal agriculture, and to conduct
investigations of animal production facilities. Animal Outlook relies on open airspace
access above food and agriculture fixed site facilities to work towards its mission.
FarmSTAND is a nonprofit legal advocacy organization dedicated to dismantling the
corporate-controlled industrial animal agriculture system and building a food system that
is accountable to communities, workers, independent farmers and ranchers, animals, and
the environment. FarmSTAND has a particular interest in promoting transparency in the
food system and a history of challenging restrictions that punish or impede journalists and
whistleblowers from documenting corporate agriculture’s ill effects. FarmSTAND seeks to
preserve open airspace over food and agriculture fixed site facilities for drone
photojournalists to document conditions at large-scale industrial facilities and to ensure
such facilities are held accountable for their social and environmental impacts.
II. Drones are a vital and unique photojournalistic tool that serves the public
good by allowing the safe and timely collection of information of public
concern about food and agriculture fixed site facilities.
Farming communities, eaters, and animal advocates around the country are deeply
concerned about the environmental and social impacts of large-scale animal agriculture.
Animal foods—meat, milk, and eggs—overwhelmingly and increasingly comes from large,
farmSTAND.org | 712 H Street NE, Suite 2534 Washington, DC 20002 | (202) 595-8816

each passing year.1 As illustrated below, aerial and drone photography provides the public
with visual context and timely information about the impact of animal agriculture in a way
that cannot be replicated by observation, photography from the ground, or non-visual
media.
Drone photography and videography are an accessible and affordable means of
creating compelling imagery and videos that communicate the vast scale and impact of
industrial animal agriculture. Indeed, as legacy media companies reduce budgets and
coverage in the face of increasing financial struggles in the digital media age,2 independent
journalists and non-profit news organizations are increasingly taking on important subjects
of national interest like food and agriculture.3 These organizations often lack the resources

and government enforcement of environmental laws—matters of significant public

Indeed, drone photography serves as a critical tool communities can use to monitor
the runoff from large-scale CAFOs, slaughterhouses, and other agriculture fixed site
facilities, because aerial imagery can more easily show water flow patterns and water
quality impact over a relatively large geographic area. This type of information is highly
useful to public debate when officials are considering necessary enforcement measures or
regulatory changes, and is most easily and affordably produced by drone.10
c. In the aftermath of natural disasters like hurricanes, floods, heatwaves,
and wildfires, photojournalists use drones to safely collect and share
timely health and safety information about food and agriculture fixed
site facilities.
When a natural disaster strikes, drone imagery has also been critical to
understanding the impacts on and of large-scale animal agriculture sites. CAFOs
commonly hold liquid animal waste in massive cesspools (euphemistically called
“lagoons”), which can overflow or breach during extreme weather events. Lagoon spills are
an immediate public health and environmental threat, adding huge amounts of pathogen-
and chemical-laden waste to local waterways that provide drinking water to the
surrounding community.11 Manure lagoon spills also cause significant environmental

e. Drones allow photojournalists to provide compelling imagery depicting
living conditions for the cows, pigs, chickens, turkeys, and other animals
raised at industrial agriculture facilities.
The American public is highly interested in the treatment of animals on food and
agriculture facilities, with some surveys reporting that nearly 80% of respondents are
farmSTAND.org | 712 H Street NE, Suite 2534 Washington, DC 20002 | (202) 595-8816

concerned about the negative impacts of industrial animal agriculture on animal welfare.18
However, food and agriculture fixed site facilities are often closed to the public and provide
very limited public information about the conditions in which animals are housed and
raised.19 Members of the public cannot easily observe animal care practices at such
facilities, which are often located far away from large towns and cities, can span hundreds
of acres, and are difficult or impossible to observe from public roads.
Food and agriculture corporations generally prefer this lack of public transparency
and oversight, because they are aware that consumer understanding about common—and
cruel—animal raising practices, like intensive confinement of animals, could harm their
reputation and sales.20

g. Drone photojournalism can help ensure market integrity in government
certifications of food and agriculture facilities such as the USDA’s
Certified Organic Program.
Drone photography can promote market integrity in other ways, such as in the U.S.
Department of Agriculture (USDA) National Organic Program. The Program sets practices
and criteria that meat, dairy, and egg producers must meet to market their products with
farmSTAND.org | 712 H Street NE, Suite 2534 Washington, DC 20002 | (202) 595-8816

the USDA Organic seal.24 The USDA Organic label is protected by law; producers who
misrepresent their products as organic may be subject to civil or criminal penalties.25 But
while certified organic farms must be inspected annually, inspections are generally
planned and paid for by the farm operator.26 This makes outside accountability important,
as “even large, prominent players can fall short of standards without detection,” as a
Washington Post investigation explained.27
In that investigation, aerial photojournalism raised questions about whether a large,
certified organic Colorado dairy was providing the grazing and outdoor access required by
the USDA Organic program. The USDA opened an investigation into the dairy after Post
journalists used aerial imagery, including drone photography and videography, to show
that the 15,000-head operation was not actually providing its cows with the minimum
required grazing access during the summer grazing months.28 After the Post’s reporting,
the dairy reportedly changed its practices to allow more of its herd access to grazing.29

III. The FAA should not adopt gating criteria for food and agriculture fixed site
facilities because there is no justifiable or demonstrated need for an
airspace restriction over such facilities.
As the FAA states in the proposed rule, it considers “restricting airspace [to be] an
extraordinary remedy,”30 “to be applied only when necessary.”31 Thus, “UAFR eligibility”
must be “limit[ed] . . . to balance the safety and security concerns Congress identified . . .
with the national policy objectives of ensuring the public right of transit, preserving
airspace efficiency, and integrating UAS into the [national airspace].”32 Accordingly,
owners and operators of animal food and agriculture fixed site facilities must
“demonstrate that there is an important need to justify the remedy of restricting
airspace.”33
They cannot do so. Indeed, drones pose no documented or legitimate security—
including biosecurity—risks to animal food and agriculture fixed site facilities. In the U.S.,
the vast majority of drone use is for news and information-gathering purposes, which does
not present any significant safety risk to persons and property on the ground.34
Professional photojournalists, including those with whom We Animals and Animal Outlook
work, typically operate with a Part 107 drone pilot certificate, and already meet the “higher
bar for safety and security” that the Agency proposes to use to allow certain excepted
operations in a UAFR.35
Responsible drone pilots, including those who work with We Animals and Animal
Outlook, also conduct their newsgathering activities at heights distant enough to protect

pt. 74) (“Proposed Rule”).

(last accessed June 12, 2026) (showing that the substantial majority of drones in the United

Animal Outlook has captured drone video of thousands of cattle at CAFOs around the

Moreover, the undersigned can find no evidence of any incidents of national food
supply safety issues caused by drones. In fact, drones can support agricultural biosecurity
by identifying diseased plants, signs of pests, and invasive species in real time.38 Neither
could any documented instance of accidental drone damage or interference with
infrastructure or other property on food and agriculture fixed site facilities be located.39 In
fact, the undersigned found only one instance of reported—indirect—harm from a drone to
a food and agriculture fixed site facility in the U.S.: a farm reported that its equipment was
stolen after the perpetrators allegedly use a drone to scout the property.40
