This single incident of indirect harm is insufficient to justify developing gating
criteria that could enable food and agriculture fixed site facilities to effectively ban
protected speech and newsgathering about their facilities, when conducted using a drone.
To the extent that facility owners claim that drones could damage their facilities or
introduce biosafety threats, such speculative concerns would not rise to the level of an
“important need” that would justify a UAFR, especially when, as documented here, access
to airspace over these facilities provides important public benefits like consumer
protection, fraud investigation, and accountability for environmental and animal care
noncompliance.
a. Large-scale agriculture operations with an outsized economic impact
should not be prioritized for UAFRs
The undersigned are particularly concerned about the proposed rule’s suggestion
that large-scale agriculture facilities supplying a disproportionate share of meat, milk, and
eggs to various regions or to the country as a whole should be considered for UAFRs. They
should not. These facilities need greater scrutiny, including through visual journalism, not
less. This is particularly true given past industry attempts to punish journalists who access
air space to engage in protected First Amendment activity. For example, National
Geographic photojournalist George Steinmetz was arrested in 2013 after photographing a
Kansas feedlot from a hang glider.41 While law enforcement officers disavowed any intent
to prevent or punish the journalist’s legitimate speech activities, free speech advocates
worried that the trespass charges were “an indirect method to obstruct Steinmetz’s work”
where the “the real motivation for the arrest [was] to frustrate photojournalism.”42
Given this and other industry-driven efforts to silence or criminalize whistleblowing
and investigations of CAFOs and slaughterhouses,43 the undersigned fear the primary
interest of food and agriculture fixed site facilities in UAFRs is limiting public scrutiny of
41 Paragliding Journalist Arrested After Photographing Kansas Feedlot, REPORTER’S COMM.
FOR FREEDOM OF THE PRESS (July 15, 2013), https://www.rcfp.org/paragliding-journalist-
arrested-after-photographing-kansas-feedlot/ (last accessed June 10, 2026).
42 Id.
43 Ag-Gag Litigation, FARMSTAND https://farmstand.org/case/ag-gag-litigation/ (last
accessed Jun. 30, 2026).
farmSTAND.org | 712 H Street NE, Suite 2534 Washington, DC 20002 | (202) 595-8816

their operations. This background provides context to the proposed rule’s questions about
developing gating criteria for this sector, which commenters here address.
1. What types of facilities within this sector should be considered for a UAFR?
Specifically, those that could have debilitating regional or national- level
consequences.
Given the importance of drone photojournalism to transparency, accountability,
and public debate about the food and agriculture industry, and the lack of any documented
drone-related threat to fixed site facilities in this industry, no food and agriculture fixed site
facilities should be considered for a UAFR, and the FAA should not develop gating criteria
for the sector. Instead, facilities in the sector should be considered for a UAFR on a case-
by-case basis only, upon a “demonstrat[ion]” of “an important need” for such restrictions,
such as specific, credible threats from drones.
2. What characteristics should be considered in the development of gating criteria
for those facilities?
Because UAFRs should be granted to fixed site facilities in this sector solely based
on demonstrated need, there are no common characteristics that merit developing gating
criteria. In particular, as noted below, size and economic output should not be considered
relevant gating criteria, as the need for transparency and accountability is at its zenith for
the largest food production facilities. Very large agriculture operations have outsized
environmental and social impacts that make drone-facilitated oversight critical.
3. Should the population served or regional or national economic impact be
considered in the development of gating criteria, and if so, what are appropriate
thresholds?
A great number of people served by a particular facility and the facility’s economic
impact are factors that should weigh against granting a UAFR. The larger a facility’s role in
our industrial food system, the more important public access to airspace for
accountability and transparency is. If any gating criteria are established for this sector, the
farmSTAND.org | 712 H Street NE, Suite 2534 Washington, DC 20002 | (202) 595-8816

FAA should apply a presumption against granting a UAFR for facilities that meet the
USDA’s definition of a medium or large CAFO.44
IV. Enabling large-scale food and agriculture fixed site facilities to freely obtain
UAFRs would violate the First Amendment.
The proposed rule has significant constitutional infirmities. Allowing large,
economically powerful farms to obtain UAFRs and close off their airspace to virtually all
drone operation would bar First Amendment-protected aerial photography that enriches
public debate on issues of public interest. And it would do so without furthering any
significant governmental interest in safety or security.
a. Aerial photojournalism about food and agriculture fixed site facilities is
protected speech and newsgathering.
Newsgathering via aerial videography and photography is protected under the First
Amendment. Multiple federal circuit courts have found a “First Amendment right to film
matters of public interest.”45 Indeed, “the act of making an audio or audiovisual recording
is necessarily included within the First Amendment’s guarantee of speech and press rights
as a corollary of the right to disseminate the resulting recording. The right to publish or
broadcast an audio or audiovisual recording would be insecure, or largely ineffective, if the
antecedent act of making the recording is wholly unprotected.”46 As the Supreme Court
44 See EPA Administered Permit Programs: the National Pollutant Discharge Elimination
System, Concentrated Animal Feeding Operations, 40 C.F.R. § 122.23(b) (2026).
45 Animal Legal Def. Fund v. Wasden, 878 F.3d 1184, 1203–05 (9th Cir. 2018) (quoting
Fordyce v. City of Seattle, 55 F.3d 436, 439 (9th Cir. 1995)) (striking down provision of Idaho
law banning audio and video recording of an agricultural production facility’s operations);
People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals, Inc. v. N.C. Farm Bureau Fed'n, Inc., 60 F.4th
815, 829 (4th Cir. 2023) (“PETA”) (“scores of Supreme Court and circuit cases apply the
First Amendment to safeguard the right to gather information as a predicate to speech”);
Fields v. City of Phila., 862 F.3d 353, 359–60 (3d Cir. 2017) (affirming the public’s right to
record police activity under the First Amendment); Am. Civ. Liberties Union v. Alvarez, 679
F.3d 583, 607–08 (7th Cir. 2012) (finding Illinois eavesdropping law likely unconstitutional
as applied to open audiovisual recording of police activity).
46 Alvarez, 679 F.3d at 595 (emphasis in original).
farmSTAND.org | 712 H Street NE, Suite 2534 Washington, DC 20002 | (202) 595-8816

has stated, “the creation and dissemination of information are speech within the meaning
of the First Amendment.”47
Courts have followed this logic to find a First Amendment interest in using a drone
to take images.48 The FAA appears to agree, recognizing that “some UAS operations,”
including “first amendment activity, may require non-transitory operations” over fixed site
facilities, such as hovering and repositioning to document conditions below.49 And courts
have held that “recording—and even more specifically, recording of animals or the
conditions in which they live—is speech-creation” protected under the First Amendment,
“not mere conduct.”50
“Citizens’ gathering and disseminating newsworthy information,” including taking
photographs and video recordings with drones, is also protected First Amendment activity
because it “leads to citizen discourse on public issues.”51 The free flow of information
supports “uninhibited, robust, and wide-open” debate on public issues, considered the
“highest rung of the hierarchy of First Amendment values.”52 Photojournalism promotes
such public debate because “[f]irst-hand accounts, buttressed by video evidence,
enhance accuracy and credibility in reporting and increase transparency and [audience]
trust.”53 This type of reporting allows the public “to appreciate the full scope of the societal
issues” covered by the photographs and recordings.54
47 Sorrell v. IMS Health Inc., 564 U.S. 552, 570 (2011); see also Brown v. Entm't Merchs.
Ass'n, 564 U.S. 786, 792 n.1 (2011) (“Whether government regulation applies to creating,
distributing, or consuming speech makes no difference”).
48 Nat'l Press Photographers Ass'n v. McCraw, 90 F.4th 770, 783, 789–90 (5th Cir. 2024)
(“NPPA”); Yoder v. Bowen, 146 F.4th 516, 523–24 (6th Cir. 2025).
49 Proposed Rule at 24676.
50 Animal Legal Def. Fund v. Kelly, 9 F.4th 1219, 1228 (10th Cir. 2021) (stating holding of W.
Watersheds Project v. Michael, 869 F.3d 1189, 1194 (10th Cir. 2017)).
51 Fields v. City of Phila., 862 F.3d 353, 359–60 (3d Cir. 2017).
52 Snyder v. Phelps, 562 U.S. 443, 452 (2011) (quoting New York Times Co. v. Sullivan, 376
U.S. 254, 270 (1964) and Connick v. Myers, 461 U.S. 138, 145 (1983)).
53 PETA, 60 F.4th at 829.
54 Id.
farmSTAND.org | 712 H Street NE, Suite 2534 Washington, DC 20002 | (202) 595-8816

Moreover, food and agriculture issues, including “food safety and animal cruelty[,]
are of significant public importance.”55 As the Tenth Circuit noted, “[a]n individual who
photographs animals or takes notes about habitat conditions is creating speech” that they
can later use “to further public debate.”56 As such, photojournalists’ right to capture
imagery of food and agriculture fixed site facilities, including information about the animal
conditions, environmental impact, and public health issues on CAFOs, to foster public
engagement on such issues, is protected under the First Amendment.
b. The suggested UAFR gating criteria and sanctions for unauthorized
operations within a UAFR will chill protected speech.
If finalized as the FAA proposes, the rule would burden and chill the undersigned’s
and others’ protected speech and newsgathering activities. The suggested gating criteria
raise the specter of the largest and most economically powerful agriculture operations
being granted UAFRs. Once granted, the regulations would impose civil sanctions on any
person who conducts unauthorized drone operations in a UAFR. The threat of such
sanctions will chill speech by discouraging or preventing drone operators from legitimate
newsgathering activity.
For example, We Animals has deferred investigation of specific animal agriculture
sites in states that impose restrictions on drone use over private property for fear of
criminal consequences, despite having reason to believe that its investigations would
produce reliable information about issues of substantial public concern. Photojournalists
in a challenge to Texas laws criminalizing drone photojournalism similarly described losing
“thousands of dollars” in freelance photography assignments they had cancelled or
foregone out of fear of liability.57 These concerns are not unfounded or speculative: as
55 Wasden, 878 F.3d at 1190.
56 W. Watersheds Project, 869 F.3d at 1196–97 (adding that such photography is “creating
speech in the same manner as an individual who records a police encounter,” the basis on
which the First Amendment right to film matters of public interest was first affirmed).
57 NPPA, 90 F.4th at 779.
farmSTAND.org | 712 H Street NE, Suite 2534 Washington, DC 20002 | (202) 595-8816

detailed above, photojournalists have been arrested for trespass after photographing
agricultural sites from the air.58
Civil sanctions for unauthorized operations within a UAFR include the suspension or
revocation of a pilot’s certificate, rating, or authorization—measures that could
permanently bar photojournalists from collecting audio or visual recordings by drone in
any location, even those without airspace restrictions. Given such steep penalties,
granting UAFRs to large, economically powerful farms would thus significantly chill the
undersigned’s and others’ speech.59
c. Granting UAFRs to food and agriculture fixed site facilities, as the
proposed rule would enable, would fail First Amendment scrutiny.
Because granting UAFRs for food and agriculture fixed site facilities would burden
and chill protected speech, the FAA’s action must survive First Amendment scrutiny. At a
minimum, imposing UAFRs over such facilities would impose a time, place, and manner
restriction on speech, by defining a place and manner in which photojournalism activities
are restricted: over food and agriculture facilities, using a drone.60 Alternatively, given the
fact that in the U.S., drones are overwhelmingly used for photography and videography,61
imposing UAFRs could be understood as a restriction of a non-expressive activity (the act
58 See, e.g, Paragliding Journalist, supra n. 41. The same journalist received another
criminal citation and had his photography drones seized by the New York City Police
Department in April 2020 while documenting mass burials on an uninhabited island in New
York City during the COVID-19 pandemic. Kara Murphy, Case Dismissed Against George
Steinmetz, Whose Drone Was Confiscated for Documenting Mass Burials, DIGITAL
PHOTOGRAPHY REV. (Aug. 17, 2020), https://www.dpreview.com/news/7618724976/case-
dismissed-against-george-steinmetz-drone-confiscated-for-documenting-mass-burials
(last accessed Jun. 11, 2026). As this article notes, the criminal case was later dismissed,
but the reporter was forced to purchase new equipment to continue working during the
several months his equipment was held by the NYPD. Id.
59 PETA, 60 F.4th at 831 (holding that a state law with the effect of criminalizing undercover
investigations failed even intermediate scrutiny under the First Amendment because “all
four challenged provisions chill an alarming amount of speech without any ‘actual
evidence’ in the legislative record that lesser restrictions will not do”).
60 Turner v. Lieutenant Driver, 848 F.3d 678, 690 (5th Cir. 2017).
61 MILLER, supra n. 29.
farmSTAND.org | 712 H Street NE, Suite 2534 Washington, DC 20002 | (202) 595-8816

of flying a drone) that nonetheless “has the inevitable effect of singling out those engaged
in expressive activity.”62 Precisely how the restriction burdens protected speech is less
important, however, because regardless of how they are analyzed, adopting gating criteria
for food and agriculture fixed site facilities and granting UAFRs based on the criteria would
fail to meet even intermediate scrutiny and accordingly violate the First Amendment.
Content-neutral restrictions on speech are permissible only when narrowly tailored
to serve a substantial government interest.63 The government may not “burden
substantially more speech than is necessary to further the government's legitimate
interests” such that “a substantial portion of the burden on speech does not serve to
advance its goals.”64 Regulations allowing large farms to obtain UAFRs fail to meet this
standard because they burden substantially more speech than is necessary to ensure the
security of these facilities.
As the FAA notes, “restricting airspace is an extraordinary remedy” that should be
granted “only in those limited circumstances in which there is a clearly articulated need
that justifies the remedy of restricting airspace, and where narrower measures cannot
adequately address a clearly documented security risk.”65 There are thus two distinct
problems with restricting airspace over food and agriculture fixed site facilities. First, as
detailed above, there is no “clearly articulated need” or a “clearly documented security
risk” of drones harming people, animals, or property on food and agriculture fixed site
facilities, either purposefully or accidentally. Second, to the extent the food and agriculture
industry or the FAA fears such security risks could be latent or could develop, the agency
cannot show that narrower measures would be inadequate to address them.
If “low barriers to entry” for drone use and some drone operators’ “limited aviation
knowledge” are, as the FAA states, the root cause of many of the accidental unmanned
aircraft incidents giving rise to the need to develop gating criteria for airspace
62 Arcara v. Cloud Books, Inc., 478 U.S. 697, 706-07 (1986) (citing Minneapolis Star &
Tribune Co. v. Minn. Comm'r of Revenue, 460 U.S. 575 (1983)).
63 Turner Broad. Sys., Inc. v. F.C.C., 512 U.S. 622, 662 (1994) (citing U.S. v. O'Brien, 391 U.S.
367, 382 (1968)).
64 Ward v. Rock Against Racism, 491 U.S. 781, 799 (1989).
65 Proposed Rule at 24656.
farmSTAND.org | 712 H Street NE, Suite 2534 Washington, DC 20002 | (202) 595-8816

restrictions,66 those could be addressed by a narrower rule implementing more stringent
knowledge or experience requirements for drone operators. We Animals and Animal
Outlook already require the drone operators they work with to follow applicable FAA rules
and to operate safely and professionally, and the organizations would be prepared to meet
reasonable further licensing, certification, safety, and operating requirements.
Moreover, to the extent the concern is intentional harm to agricultural operations—
such as introducing biosecurity threats or sabotage—those harms could be addressed
with narrower restrictions targeting drone operation for such illicit purposes. The agency
has not considered such narrower measures and found them inadequate to address
security risks.67 Adopting gating criteria that would enable large CAFOs to freely obtain
UAFRs and shut down aerial photojournalism about their operations would thus burden far
more speech than is necessary and would fail intermediate scrutiny.68
d. The proposed regulations could even enable unconstitutional content or
viewpoint discrimination.
While the rule, if finalized, would fail even intermediate scrutiny, aspects of the
suggested gating criteria for food and agriculture fixed site facilities, and the agency’s
explanation of how such facilities could obtain UAFRs, raise the specter of potential
content-based discrimination on protected newsgathering. If the regulations facilitated
that, they would be subject to—and would necessarily fail—strict scrutiny.69
66 Id.
67 PETA, 60 F.4th at 832 (before restricting speech, government must “produce evidence
demonstrating that it seriously undertook to utilize existing laws or attempted to use less
intrusive tools readily available to it to achieve the proffered aims”) (internal quotation and
citation omitted); McCullen v. Coakley, 573 U.S. 464, 496 (2014) (“Given the vital First
Amendment interests at stake, it is not enough for [the government] simply to say that
other approaches have not worked.”).
68 PETA, 60 F.4th at 832 (government failed to show that North Carolina Property Protection
Act restricted less speech than was actually necessary to meet state interests, and thus
Act failed intermediate scrutiny).
69 Free Speech Coal., Inc. v. Paxton, 606 U.S. 461, 471 (2025) (“‘Content-based laws —
those that target speech based on its communicative content — are presumptively
farmSTAND.org | 712 H Street NE, Suite 2534 Washington, DC 20002 | (202) 595-8816

First, the FAA’s questions about gating criteria for food and agriculture facilities, in
proposed 14 C.F.R. § 74.90, suggest that only or primarily large-scale food and agriculture
fixed site facilities—those whose impairment “could have debilitating regional or national-
level consequences,” that produce food for a large segment of the population, and/or that
have a significant “regional or national economic impact”—could obtain UAFRs.70 This
could have the effect of restricting protected drone photojournalism if the subject is a
large, economically powerful dairy or feedlot, while allowing it if the subject is a small
family farm. In other words, it would allow aerial photography of small herds of animals
grazing outside, while prohibiting aerial photography of the enormous feedlots, manure
lagoons, and animal confinements that typify CAFOs.71
This concern is exacerbated by the agency’s suggestion that agricultural facilities
requesting a UAFR could make the requisite showing of vulnerability by identifying
“weaknesses or gaps that may be intentionally or unintentionally exploited by unmanned
aircraft to the detriment of the facility’s operation or mission.”72 If the FAA granted a UAFR
to a food and agriculture fixed site facility based on the facility’s showing that drone
photography of its operations has generated or could generate negative public opinion or
economic consequences for the facility, to the detriment of the facility’s mission, the
regulatory framework would effect unconstitutional viewpoint discrimination.73 Courts
unconstitutional and may be justified only if’ they satisfy strict scrutiny” (quoting Reed v.
Town of Gilbert, 576 U.S. 155, 163 (2015)).
70 Proposed Rule at 24670.
71 Wasden, 878 F.3d at 1204 (provision barring audiovisual recording of “the conduct of an
agricultural production facility's operations” was an “obvious example of a content-based
regulation of speech because it define[d] regulated speech by particular subject matter,”
enabling, for example, “a videographer [to] record an after-hours birthday party among co-
workers, a farmer's antique car collection, or a historic maple tree but not the animal
abuse, feedlot operation, or slaughterhouse conditions”) (cleaned up).
72 Id. at 24687.
73 Wasden, 878 F.3d at 1196–97 (Idaho law that meant “to quash investigative reporting on
agricultural production facilities” by criminalizing gaining access by means of
misrepresentation was a content-based restriction that failed strict scrutiny); Kelly, 9 F.4th
at 1233 (finding state law “viewpoint discriminatory in operation” when “text of the statute
alone show[ed] it is meant to protect animal facilities,” by criminalizing one “who lies to
acquire or exercise control over an animal facility intending to expose wrongdoing,” but not
farmSTAND.org | 712 H Street NE, Suite 2534 Washington, DC 20002 | (202) 595-8816

have struck down restrictions that similarly function to silence or punish critical speech
about animal operations while privileging neutral or positive speech, such as drones
conducting mapping or academic research.74
e. The narrow allowance for drone operations within UAFRs would not
alleviate the unconstitutional chill on drone-facilitated photojournalism
about food and agriculture fixed site facilities.
The FAA is aware that implementing blanket airspace restrictions could impact
“first amendment activity, [which] may require non-transitory operations” over fixed site
facilities.75 The agency asks whether its proposed allowance of certain drone operations
within UAFRs is broad enough to accommodate legitimate activities, including First
Amendment activity.76 The answer is no. Under the proposed rule, once a fixed site facility
has been granted a UAFR, only Part 107-licensed operations that “transit the UAFR in the
shortest practicable time” and provide advanced notice to the fixed site facility’s manager
of the date, time, and identity of the drone operator will be permitted.77 These conditions
will effectively shut down, or at the very least undermine the integrity, utility, and
communicative effect of, photojournalism about food and agriculture fixed site facilities.
First, requiring drone operators to transit the UAFR in the shortest practicable
time—allowing only uninterrupted, direct fly-overs—will prevent newsgathering.
Newsgathering requires more: operators often need to hover, reposition, and record long
enough to clearly capture visual documentation of vital information about food and
agriculture fixed site facilities. These are necessarily non-transitory operations.
one “who tells the same lie to gain the same control intending to laud the facility or for
neutral reasons”).
74 See, e.g., Kelly, 9 F.4th at 1233; PETA, 60 F.4th at 830–31 (state law triggered strict
scrutiny—but failed even intermediate scrutiny—as applied to PETA’s investigative
activities, as provisions prohibiting using data to breach employee’s duty of loyalty “do not
merely target speech, but speech critical of the employer”).
75 Proposed Rule at 24676.
76 Id.
77 Proposed Rule at 24690–91.
farmSTAND.org | 712 H Street NE, Suite 2534 Washington, DC 20002 | (202) 595-8816

Second, providing advanced notice to the food and agriculture fixed site facility
could allow the site operator to obscure or remove any conditions they did not want to be
photographed from above, also undermining protected newsgathering. Such a condition
would undercut drone photography’s utility as a tool for environmental enforcement,
consumer protection, and preservation of market integrity. For example, if the egg
producer in the Alderfer Farms consumer protection case78 or the dairy operation in the
organic certification integrity investigation79 cited earlier had advanced notice of drone
overflights, they could have adjusted the animals’ outdoor access temporarily and evaded
accountability.
In sum, the language in 14 C.F.R. § 74.250(a)(2) is not “broad enough to enable both
legitimate transitory and non-transitory operations within a UAFR.”80 While no gating
criteria to grant UAFR for food and agriculture fixed site facilities are warranted, if the
agency proceeds to develop them, at a minimum, the FAA must clarify that
photojournalists and other drone pilots engaged in protected First Amendment speech are
not restricted to transitory overflights and need not give advanced notice of their drone
activity to facility operators. Further, while the proposed rule specifically states that fixed
site facility operators are not permitted to electronically interfere with or intercept
drones,81 the agency should amend § 74.250 to state that facility managers may not
harass, interfere with, threaten, or otherwise seek to enforce a UAFR against drone
operators engaged in legitimate First Amendment activities.
Respectfully submitted,

comments.commenter: Comment from United Dairymen of Arizona
comments.comment_text:
United Dairymen of Arizona appreciates the opportunity to comment in support of the FAA’s proposed rule to designate and restrict unmanned aircraft operations near critical fixed-site facilities.

Arizona’s dairy farms are an essential part of the nation’s food supply, providing safe, nutritious milk while caring for thousands of animals every day. Unauthorized drone activity over dairy operations creates significant concerns for animal welfare, employee safety, biosecurity, operational security, and business continuity.

Dairy cattle can be sensitive to unexpected disturbances, particularly during milking, calving, and handling activities. Low-flying drones have the potential to startle livestock, increasing the risk of injury to both animals and employees. They may also interfere with routine management practices and emergency responses.

In addition, dairy farms maintain biosecurity protocols to protect animal health and the food supply. While drones may not directly transmit disease, unauthorized surveillance of facility layouts, security measures, and operational practices creates unnecessary vulnerabilities for agricultural operations that are part of our nation’s critical infrastructure.

Arizona dairy producers recognize the many beneficial uses of drones in agriculture, including crop management, infrastructure inspection, and precision agriculture. However, these legitimate uses differ significantly from unauthorized drone operations conducted without the knowledge or consent of facility owners.

As a cooperative representing dairy producers across Arizona, United Dairymen of Arizona is committed to advancing animal well-being, employee safety, environmental stewardship, and a secure food supply. This proposed rule supports those shared objectives.

We support the FAA’s efforts to provide a mechanism for restricting unauthorized drone operations over designated agricultural facilities where appropriate. Doing so will help protect livestock, employees, food production, and critical agricultural infrastructure while preserving the ability for responsible and authorized drone use.

Thank you for considering these comments.

Submitted on behalf of the dairy producers of United Dairymen of Arizona.

comments.commenter: Comment from National Press Photographers Association (NPPA)
comments.comment_text:
Comments of the National Press Photographers Association (NPPA) regarding the designation of airspace to restrict the operation of unmanned aircraft
in close proximity to a fixed site facility

As the Manager of Physical Security for North Carolina's Electric Cooperatives, I support the FAA's effort to establish drone flight restrictions around critical infrastructure, but I encourage expanding eligibility beyond CIP Medium and High Impact facilities.

The current proposal creates an artificial distinction between assets that are considered critical for regulatory purposes and assets that are critical for maintaining electric service to communities. Threat actors do not select targets based on CIP classifications, and our protective measures should not be limited by them. The Moore County substation attack demonstrated that facilities outside the highest regulatory impact categories can still have significant consequences for public safety, reliability, and resilience.

There is little legitimate reason for unauthorized recreational drone activity directly over critical infrastructure. Asset owners should have the ability to request airspace protections for all critical infrastructure facilities, regardless of CIP designation, based on operational importance and risk.

This rule should also be viewed as a first step toward a broader critical infrastructure protection framework. As drone threats continue to evolve, policymakers should consider pathways that ultimately enable qualified critical infrastructure operators to deploy appropriately regulated counter-UAS detection and mitigation capabilities to protect essential services.

Protecting only a subset of critical infrastructure leaves unnecessary gaps in our national security and resilience posture.

I oppose this proposal because it shifts too much discretion toward creating new drone-restricted areas without sufficient public accountability. Drones are used for photography, education, agriculture, infrastructure inspection, and countless other legitimate purposes. Broad flight restrictions should be the exception, not the rule. Before limiting access to public airspace, the FAA should require a stronger demonstration that existing measures are inadequate and that the proposed restrictions are the least burdensome option available.

As a drone enthusiast, documentary maker, and reader of the position statement by the professional journalist of the NPPA (national press photographers association, https://petapixel.com/2026/07/15/nppa-raises-constitutional-concerns-over-faas-proposed-drone-limits/), I wish to register my criticism of the proposed restrictions on drone use for giving members of the public a visual context and bigger picture of the subject. I acknowledge the concept of critical infrastructure and its protection, although, satellite views and streetview on Google gives an potential terrorist plenty of visual information already. So barring card-carrying journalists from reporting breaking news is gratuitous and does little to improve security. Therefore, I oppose drone cameras restrictions in use by professional journalists as it is written in the current proposal.

I urge the FAA to withdraw this proposal. Permanent or long-term drone restrictions should not be adopted based on speculative risks. Every new no-fly zone reduces access to public airspace for responsible operators who follow the rules. The FAA should focus on enforcing existing regulations against bad actors instead of imposing additional restrictions that primarily affect law-abiding recreational and commercial drone pilots.

comments.commenter: Comment from Articles In Common LLC
comments.comment_text:
I oppose this proposed rule as drafted because it risks creating a broad and expanding system of drone exclusion zones without demonstrating that existing authorities are insufficient.

The FAA correctly acknowledges that the National Airspace System is a public resource and that restricting access is an extraordinary remedy. However, this proposal would allow thousands of facilities across sixteen critical infrastructure sectors to seek long-term flight restrictions, potentially resulting in a patchwork of permanent no-fly zones that significantly reduce lawful access for responsible operators.

The FAA already has extensive tools available, including Remote ID, aircraft registration, Part 107 certification, Temporary Flight Restrictions, and civil and criminal penalties for unlawful operations. The proposal does not provide sufficient evidence that expanding geographic restrictions will be more effective than enforcing existing laws against those who actually misuse drones.

The economic impacts also deserve greater consideration. Small businesses, photographers, surveyors, infrastructure inspectors, researchers, conservation organizations, search and rescue volunteers, journalists, and recreational pilots all rely on predictable access to public airspace. Additional restrictions and permitting requirements create burdens that fall primarily on law-abiding operators, while individuals with malicious intent are unlikely to be deterred by new no-fly zones.

I am also concerned about the impact this proposal could have on transparency and public accountability. Drones have become an important tool for journalists, documentary filmmakers, researchers, and watchdog organizations to document pollution, environmental damage, wildlife impacts, and conditions at industrial agriculture facilities. The public has a legitimate interest in understanding what occurs at facilities whose operations may affect surrounding communities, public health, the environment, or animal welfare. Restricting lawful aerial access around broad categories of facilities could unintentionally reduce independent oversight and make it more difficult to expose misconduct or abuse that is otherwise hidden from public view.

If the FAA proceeds with this rule, applicants should be required to meet a high evidentiary standard demonstrating a specific, documented, and recurring threat that cannot reasonably be addressed through existing laws or less restrictive alternatives. Every restriction should be narrowly tailored, publicly justified, subject to periodic review, and automatically expire unless renewed based on current evidence.

The National Airspace System belongs to the public. Restrictions should remain the exception, not the rule. The FAA should preserve lawful access wherever possible while focusing enforcement resources on operators who violate existing laws rather than creating increasingly broad restrictions that diminish transparency, innovation, and responsible use of public airspace.

RE: Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; Notice No. 26-03; RIN 2120-AL33 — Designation—
Restrict the Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site
Facility
I respectfully submit the following comments regarding the FAA’s proposed 14 CFR
Part 74 framework for Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions.
I am an international UAS operations and aviation safety professional with more
than 4,000 flight hours and practical experience in BVLOS infrastructure
inspection, Safety Management Systems, operational risk management, flight
crew standardization, and airspace coordination across Europe and Latin America.
My comments are submitted in my individual professional capacity and are
focused primarily on the operational scalability, safety assurance, and digital
integration of the proposed UAFR framework.
I support the FAA’s objective of establishing a risk-based mechanism to protect
eligible fixed-site facilities. Critical infrastructure must be protected from careless,
unauthorized, and malicious UAS activity. I also support the principle that a UAFR
should not automatically be granted merely because a facility belongs to a critical
infrastructure sector. The requirements proposed under §§ 74.54 through 74.68
appropriately recognize that an applicant should demonstrate eligibility,
vulnerability, potential consequences, existing protective measures, the expected
effect of the restriction, and the externalities imposed on other users of the
National Airspace System.
However, the final rule must ensure that UAFRs operate as an integrated airspace-
management capability rather than as a fragmented collection of static, facility-
specific restrictions. The long-term success of Part 74 will depend not only on
where the FAA establishes UAFRs, but also on how those restrictions are digitally
published, activated, accessed, monitored, and incorporated into routine Part 107
and future Part 108 operations.
1. UAFR information should be authoritative, machine-readable, and operationally
consumable
Publication in FAA Order JO 7400.12 and depiction on a public website may
establish legal notice, but those mechanisms alone are not sufficient for scalable
flight planning and real-time operational compliance.
The FAA should provide every UAFR through a single authoritative digital data
service containing, at minimum:
A unique UAFR identifier.
Standard or Special UAFR status.

Precise horizontal geometry.
Vertical limits and altitude reference.
Effective dates and activation periods.
Current operational status.
Applicable access category and conditions.
Notification requirements.
Revision history and effective timestamp.
Cancellation, suspension, or modification status.
The data should be available through a documented application programming
interface using an aviation-compatible geospatial schema. It should be
consumable by UTM service suppliers, flight-planning applications, fleet-
management platforms, and operators’ operational control systems without
manual transcription.
A PDF, map image, or website intended primarily for human interpretation cannot
be the sole operational source for a restriction carrying civil or potentially criminal
consequences. The authoritative legal publication and the operational digital data
service should be synchronized, version-controlled, and traceable.
2. The FAA should establish a centralized notification mechanism under § 74.255
The notification process should not require operators to discover and
communicate through different facility-specific portals, email addresses,
telephone numbers, or locally developed procedures. Such a model may be
manageable for an isolated VLOS operation, but it will not scale to recurring BVLOS
routes crossing multiple UAFRs.
The preamble refers to notification to both the FAA and the fixed-site facility, while
the proposed regulatory text of § 74.255(a) refers specifically to notice being
provided to the site manager. The final rule should clarify this point and establish
one official FAA-controlled submission channel.
The operator should submit the required information once through an FAA system.
The system should validate the operator’s credentials, registration and Remote ID
information, generate a transaction or authorization-verification token, and
automatically provide the appropriate situational-awareness information to the
facility.
This would preserve the FAA’s exclusive airspace-management authority and
prevent notification from evolving into a de facto private approval process. As the

FAA correctly states in the preamble, the site manager should not have authority to
approve, reject, delay, or impose additional conditions on an operation that
qualifies under § 74.250.
The information disclosed to private facility personnel should also follow a data-
minimization principle. Requiring disclosure of an individual’s mailing address and
complete airman certificate number to every affected private facility may create
unnecessary privacy, identity-security, and data-retention risks. The FAA could
verify the credentials and transmit confirmation that the operator is eligible
without disclosing the complete underlying identifier.
3. Remote ID should be treated as one identification layer, not as proof of
authorization
The proposed relationship between § 74.56 and § 74.250 is conceptually sound: an
allowed aircraft broadcasts Remote ID, while the protected facility maintains the
capability to receive Broadcast Remote ID messages.
Nevertheless, Broadcast Remote ID is a local radio-frequency transmission using
technologies subject to propagation limitations, masking, interference, terrain,
structures, antenna placement, receiver performance, congestion, and equipment
configuration. Industrial sites containing metal structures, electromagnetic
sources, complex terrain, or large property areas may present particularly difficult
reception environments.
Failure by a facility receiver to detect a compliant aircraft must not, by itself,
establish that the operator failed to broadcast Remote ID or operated unlawfully.
The FAA should define performance objectives for facility Remote ID reception
systems, including:
Required coverage of the UAFR volume.
Minimum availability and system-health monitoring.
Time synchronization.
Handling of duplicate, erroneous, or spoofed identifiers.
Receiver testing and maintenance.
Cybersecurity and access control.
Data-retention limitations.
Procedures for reporting degraded or unavailable coverage.
A static whitelist of Remote ID serial numbers would also present lifecycle,
cybersecurity, and operational-control difficulties. Aircraft may be replaced,

leased, reassigned, maintained, or operated by different authorized personnel. A
dynamic FAA validation service linked to the specific operation would be more
reliable than distributing permanent lists of serial numbers to multiple facilities.
4. Section 74.250(a)(2) should distinguish transit from authorized operational
presence
Proposed § 74.250(a)(2) requires the unmanned aircraft to transit the UAFR in the
shortest amount of time practicable. This is appropriate for an aircraft merely
crossing the restricted volume. It may not be appropriate for an authorized
inspection, emergency response, public safety mission, mapping activity,
infrastructure assessment, or other operation whose purpose requires
maneuvering or remaining within the UAFR.
The preamble recognizes that certain operations may require non-transitory
activity within the restriction, but the regulatory text does not clearly establish an
exception for those operations.
The provision should be revised along the following lines:
“The unmanned aircraft must remain within the UAFR only for the time and within
the area reasonably necessary to complete the authorized transit or operation,
including any maneuvering required for safety of flight, traffic deconfliction,
contingency management, or compliance with an FAA authorization.”
This would preserve the principle of minimizing unnecessary exposure while
avoiding a conflict between the rule and the operational purpose of a legitimate
UAS mission.
The FAA should also ensure that operations conducted under applicable waivers,
exemptions, certificates of authorization, or other FAA approvals are addressed
clearly. The transition between Part 107 and the future Part 108 framework should
not create an unintended access gap for operations that remain validly authorized
by the FAA.
5. UAFRs should be tailored in three dimensions and in time
I support the requirement in § 74.58 that lateral boundaries be narrowly tailored
and not exceed the facility’s property boundary. The FAA should go further and
require the smallest practicable polygon around the vulnerable asset or
operational area rather than routinely accepting the entire property boundary.
The same proportionality should apply vertically. Where a property contains one
tall structure, applying the height of that structure plus 100 feet across the entire
UAFR may unnecessarily restrict lower-altitude routes over other portions of the

site. Where technically feasible, the FAA should permit segmented or graduated
vertical limits.
The proposed definition of a part-time UAFR also remains operationally coarse. A
restriction active 24 hours per day for as many as 290 consecutive days may be
“part-time” on a calendar basis but function as a continuous restriction
throughout an entire operational season.
The final rule should permit published daily, weekly, event-based, or operational
activation windows when the underlying risk does not exist continuously. Dynamic
activation should only be used where reliable, timely publication and deactivation
can be guaranteed.
The FAA should also evaluate the cumulative effects of nearby UAFRs. An
individual polygon may appear proportionate when assessed in isolation while
multiple adjacent restrictions may collectively obstruct a BVLOS corridor, create
route choke points, concentrate aircraft over populated areas, increase flight time,
reduce energy reserves, or create new contingency-management risks.
Externality analysis under § 74.68 should therefore consider both the individual
UAFR and its cumulative regional interaction with existing and reasonably
foreseeable restrictions.
6. Approval and renewal should be based on a documented risk case and
measurable effectiveness
The application should demonstrate more than the existence of a vulnerable asset
and a theoretically credible consequence. It should establish a structured
relationship between:
The UAS threat scenario.
The facility vulnerability.
The potential consequence.
Existing preventive and responsive controls.
The residual risk.
The expected risk reduction attributable specifically to the UAFR.
The operational and economic effects imposed on other airspace users.
The proposed requirement to describe UAS activity observed during the preceding
24 months should be interpreted carefully. Many facilities will not have had
continuous or technically reliable UAS detection during that period. Absence of
recorded activity may reflect an absence of surveillance rather than an absence of

UAS operations. Conversely, an unidentified Remote ID signal should not
automatically be classified as a hostile, unlawful, or facility-threatening operation.
The final rule should establish minimum data-quality and classification criteria for
incident information used to justify a UAFR.
During the five-year term, the facility should be required to monitor whether the
UAFR is achieving its intended safety or security outcome. Renewal should
consider reported incursions, lawful access activity, detection-system
performance, false or unverified alerts, operational externalities, changes to the
facility, and whether narrower mitigations have become available.
A UAFR should not become permanent merely because it was previously
approved.
7. Special UAFRs require predictable access and emergency procedures
I recognize that Special UAFRs address credible and potentially sensitive security
threats and therefore require stricter access controls. Nevertheless, the final
framework should define a secure and expedited method for emergency response,
disaster assessment, utility restoration, search and rescue, law enforcement
support, and other time-critical operations not conducted directly by the using
agency.
The rule or associated guidance should identify:
The authority responsible for access decisions.
A continuously available contact mechanism.
Expected response times.
Emergency or immediate-access procedures.
Credential-verification methods.
Procedures during communications or system failure.
Conditions for revoking or terminating access.
Without predictable procedures, legitimate time-critical operations may be
delayed precisely when UAS capabilities are most valuable.
8. The FAA should retain a hybrid notice process
I recommend retaining Federal Register publication as the legal mechanism for
proposed and final designations while using an FAA website, subscription service,
API, and targeted digital notifications to improve operational awareness.

A minimum 30-day comment period may be adequate for a simple, isolated UAFR.
More complex proposals—particularly those affecting established commercial
routes, multiple facilities, densely used airspace, or regional BVLOS networks—
should receive a longer review period.
Affected operators should be able to subscribe to geographic areas and receive
automatic notifications when a UAFR is proposed, modified, activated, renewed,
suspended, or cancelled.
Public notice must provide enough information for meaningful airspace-impact
analysis while protecting facility vulnerability information, security plans, and
sensitive technical details from unnecessary disclosure.
9. Technical cross-references should be reviewed before publication of the final
rule
Proposed § 74.62(c) appears to state that eligibility for a part-time UAFR depends
on meeting the relevant industry criteria in § 74.60. Section 74.60 addresses the
altitude ceiling rather than sector eligibility. The intended reference may be §
74.54, the applicable provision in Subpart C, or another eligibility provision.
Similarly, proposed § 74.215(a)(2) appears to require cancellation when a facility
no longer meets the eligibility criteria in § 74.60, which again concerns altitude
rather than eligibility.
The FAA should review and correct these cross-references to avoid ambiguity in
implementation and enforcement.
Conclusion
The proposed Part 74 framework can become an important component of low-
altitude airspace safety and critical infrastructure protection. Its effectiveness,
however, should not be measured only by the number of UAFRs created or by the
number of compliant flights displaced.
A successful framework should enable security personnel to distinguish known
and lawful operations from activity requiring further investigation, while preserving
predictable access for certificated, permitted, emergency, public-interest, and
infrastructure-support operations.
For this reason, I respectfully recommend that the final rule require digitally
interoperable UAFR data, centralized and automated notification, privacy-
protective credential validation, performance-based Remote ID reception,
mission-appropriate access provisions, proportionate spatial and temporal
boundaries, cumulative airspace-impact analysis, and periodic evidence-based
review.

Critical infrastructure protection and safe UAS integration are not competing
objectives. A properly designed Part 74 framework should achieve both.
Respectfully submitted,
Manuel Ignacio Pérez Pan
UAS/BVLOS Operations and Aviation Safety Professional
Spain

I oppose this proposed rule. It creates a broad framework for expanding permanent drone restrictions without requiring strong evidence that each restriction is truly necessary. The FAA already has tools to address legitimate security concerns. This proposal risks creating a patchwork of no-fly zones that will burden recreational and commercial drone operators while providing little measurable safety benefit. Any restrictions should be narrowly tailored, temporary whenever possible, and based on a clear showing of need. Please withdraw or substantially revise this proposal.

I am the founder of UUAS, a small UAS business in Murfreesboro, Tennessee, and a graduate student (M.S. Aviation, Middle Tennessee State University). On July 19, 2026, I published an open-access research series and a complete reconstruction of the FAA UAS Sighting Reports — 21,765 records, November 2014–June 2026 (DOI: https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.21444168). These are unverified pilot/ATC reports, not confirmed incidents; read with that caveat, the record shows no significant national trend over 11 years and tracks manned-traffic exposure, not operator geography.

I support a workable Section 2209 process and recommend that the final rule:

Adjudicate "aviation safety necessity" on site-specific evidence, not an assumed national escalation (no significant trend 2015–2025; p = 0.814; Paper 1).
Reject operator/registration density near a facility as hazard evidence — the per-capita association is negative (Spearman ρ = −0.35; Paper 2).
Treat registration and certificate counts as cumulative tallies, not activity measures, and specify allowable quantitative evidence and its limitations (Paper 4).
Restore the complete sighting record to the FAA's public page and publish granted/denied 2209 designations in machine-readable form.
Weigh the burden on low-risk small operators.
As a drone operator and journalist, I oppose this proposition because it impedes our ability to report factual, accurate news.

July 20, 2026
Submitted electronically via Regulations.gov
United States Department of Transportation
1200 New Jersey Avenue, SE
West Building Ground Floor, Room W12-140
Washington, DC 20590
Re: Comments in Response on Restricting the Operations of Unmanned Aircraft in Close
Proximity to Fixed Site Facilities [Docket: FAA-2026-4558]
To Whom It May Concern:
Percepto appreciates the opportunity to submit comments in response to the Federal
Aviation Administration’s (FAA) proposed rulemaking (NPRM) titled “Designation – Restrict the
Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility.”
I. ABOUT PERCEPTO
Percepto is both a UAS manufacturer and operator with global operations, serving clients that
range from Fortune 500 companies to smaller enterprises. Our primary focus is critical
infrastructure inspection, where we provide safe and reliable BVLOS operations. While some
customers operate our drone-in-a-box systems themselves, most rely on Percepto to manage
and operate missions on their behalf. In addition to infrastructure inspection, our systems are
also used for applications such as site surveillance and security. By combining our advanced
autonomous aircraft with Percepto’s proprietary AIM software, we deliver actionable insights and
operational efficiency at scale which helps organizations improve performance, safety, and
sustainability.
Percepto has worked closely with the FAA over many years through countless waiver approvals,
participation in the Partnership for Safety Program with Florida Power & Light, and they type
certification process. We’ve proven our ability to meet the highest regulatory standards. Most
recently, Percepto received formal recognition from the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency
(EPA) for meeting the criteria under §60.5398b(d)(2) and was granted approval to use our drone
system as an alternative test method for emissions monitoring. These milestones underscore
our commitment to compliance, safety, and innovation.

II. PERCEPTO SUPPORTS REASONABLE SECURITY MEASURES
Percepto supports reasonable measures to protect critical infrastructure and other
sensitive sites from unauthorized or malicious drone operations. As a company that
conducts routine commercial drone operations in and around critical infrastructure, we
understand the need for effective security requirements. At the same time, those
requirements should allow authorized operators to continue providing the safety,
efficiency, and operational benefits that commercial drones deliver. Strong security and
commercial drone operations go hand in hand. Clear, practical regulations help protect
the National Airspace System, people and property on the ground, and public confidence
in the safe use of drones.
Percepto appreciates the FAA's continued commitment to protecting both national
security and access to the National Airspace System. The NAS is a public resource, and
we support the FAA's approach of strengthening security while preserving access for
authorized operators. Commercial and public safety UAS operators flying under FAA
approvals already operate in a structured and accountable environment. Requirements
such as Remote Identification, operational approvals, and airspace authorizations
provide transparency and traceability. We appreciate that the proposed rule builds on
this framework by helping distinguish authorized operations from unauthorized or
malicious activity, rather than broadly restricting compliant operators.
III. SPECIFIC AREAS OF SUPPORT IN THE NPRM
Overall, Percepto believes the NPRM establishes the right framework for protecting
critical infrastructure while preserving access to the National Airspace System for
legitimate operations. We support the FAA's decision to limit UAFRs to facilities that can
demonstrate a legitimate security need, require applicants to implement layered security
measures, and distinguish authorized operators from unauthorized activity rather than
broadly restricting access to the airspace. In particular, we support:
● Risk-based approach. The proposed rule focuses restrictions on facilities that
demonstrate a legitimate security need, rather than broadly restricting access to
low-altitude airspace or whole categories of sites. We support this targeted
approach.
● Facility-specific eligibility criteria. Applying sector-specific eligibility criteria
helps ensure that UAFRs are reserved for facilities where disruption could have
significant security, economic, or public safety consequences.
● Demonstrated need for restrictions. Requiring applicants to demonstrate why
a UAFR is necessary helps ensure that restrictions are based on identifiable
security risks rather than applied by default.

● Layered security. The NPRM appropriately requires facilities seeking a UAFR to
implement layered security measures, including restricted access, designated
security personnel, security monitoring, and the ability to receive Remote ID
broadcasts. Percepto supports these requirements because they help ensure
that UAFRs are reserved for facilities that have already implemented reasonable
physical and operational security measures. Airspace restrictions should
complement a comprehensive security program rather than serve as the primary
means of protecting a facility.
● Property boundaries. Percepto supports limiting UAFRs to the property
boundaries of the protected facility. Expanding UAFRs beyond the facility
footprint could unnecessarily restrict access to surrounding airspace used for
legitimate commercial operations while providing limited additional security
benefit.
● Use of Remote Identification. We support the proposal to use Remote
Identification as a tool to improve situational awareness and help distinguish
authorized operations from unauthorized activity.
● Transparent designation process. We support the FAA's commitment to a
public notice and comment process and a centralized application process. These
steps promote transparency, improve stakeholder participation, and help ensure
that UAFRs remain narrowly tailored and supported by the record.
IV. AREAS FOR CLARIFICATION
Percepto notes a few areas of interest that require further clarification in the final rule.
● Notification Process. Percepto supports the proposed notification process as a
way to distinguish authorized operations from unauthorized activity. However, the
process should be designed to support routine commercial UAS operations at
scale. Many operators conduct recurring BVLOS flights at the same facilities
every day under established FAA approvals. Requiring individual notifications for
each flight would create unnecessary administrative burden for both operators
and facility owners without improving security. The notification process should
therefore be automated to the greatest extent possible and should support
standing or recurring notifications for routine operations. This approach would
maintain facility awareness of authorized activity while allowing operators to
conduct recurring operations efficiently.
● UAFR Application Process. The final rule would benefit from additional detail
regarding the UAFR application process. In particular, applicants should have
clear expectations for the information required, the review process, expected
timelines, opportunities to address incomplete applications, and the

circumstances under which a designation may be modified or renewed. A
predictable process will help facilities obtain appropriate protections while
allowing operators to understand where and when restrictions may be
established.
The final rule should also clearly describe the process for modifying, renewing, or
removing a UAFR when the underlying security need changes.
● Detection and Identification of Authorized Operations. Percepto supports the
proposed requirement for facilities to monitor Remote ID broadcasts but
recommends that the FAA also provide a standardized method for facilities to
quickly identify authorized operators. As commercial UAS operations continue to
scale, facilities should not be expected to manually evaluate every compliant
drone operating near a UAFR. The FAA should establish a centralized process
that allows authorized operators to be identified through an FAA-managed or
FAA-approved whitelist. Detection systems could then automatically distinguish
known, authorized operations from unknown or potentially unauthorized aircraft,
allowing facility personnel to focus their attention on activities that warrant
investigation. This approach would improve security while reducing unnecessary
administrative burden for both facilities and operators.
● Demonstration of Need. We support requiring applicants to demonstrate the
need for a UAFR using objective information rather than anecdotal observations
alone. The final rule should provide additional guidance on what information is
sufficient to demonstrate need and encourage the use of verifiable operational
data wherever practical. This will promote consistent application of the rule and
help ensure that UAFRs are established where they are supported by
demonstrated risk.
● Special UAFR Designations. Percepto supports the availability of Special
UAFRs for facilities facing elevated security risks. However, the final rule should
more clearly define the eligibility criteria, required supporting information, and
decision-making process for these designations. Clear, objective standards will
help ensure that Special UAFRs remain limited to the narrow, high-risk
circumstances they are intended to address. The FAA should also clarify that
federal sponsorship, while an important consideration, does not by itself establish
eligibility for a Special UAFR and should be evaluated alongside the applicant's
demonstrated security need.
V. CONCLUSION
Percepto appreciates the FAA's thoughtful, risk-based approach to this rulemaking and
believes the proposed framework appropriately balances national security with continued
access to the National Airspace System for compliant operators. We appreciate the

opportunity to provide these comments and look forward to supporting implementation of
the final rule.

I oppose allowing food and agriculture facilities to obtain Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs). Drone photojournalism is one of the only tools the public has to see inside factory farms and hold them accountable. There is no documented evidence of drones threatening safety or security at these facilities, but there is a documented history of drone footage exposing false marketing claims, environmental violations, and animal welfare abuses. This rule could let the largest, most powerful operations shield themselves from scrutiny — the opposite of what the public interest requires. I urge the FAA not to develop gating criteria for this sector and not to grant UAFRs to food and agriculture facilities. Please keep the sky open.
Don’t ground the drones that expose factory farming, and it’s evil and horrors. Animals suffering horrendously should be exposed!
PLEASE HELP!!!!

Factory farms are inhumane, foster illness in the animals and those that eat them, and are built to keep the public out. But they have no more right to privacy than the rest of us. They have miles of fencing, no roadside view, no way, from the ground, for you to see how the animals inside are actually treated. Just like fences and walls around our back yards prevent views, but a drone would pick up if someone was being beaten in them. In recent years, drones have been one of the tools that have helped volunteer investigators see over that wall — capturing what these facilities don't want anyone to know.
The Federal Aviation Authority (FAA) is corruptly considering taking action that would ground these drone flights, but as long as realtors, hobbyists, photographers can fly their drones over our neighborhoods, houses, parks where we gather, there's no reason to put factory farms off limits.
The agency has proposed a rule that could let the largest, most powerful factory farms in the country ban drones from flying anywhere near their facilities. It's being sold as a safety measure. It is not. There is no documented case of a drone harming a person, an animal, or a piece of equipment at one of these facilities in the US. What drones have done is expose the repeated lies, cruelty, and pollution companies would rather keep hidden.
We've seen firsthand what's at stake. In one case, drone footage from a number of Pennsylvania egg facilities helped prove a company's "free roaming" hens had no real outdoor access at all — evidence that led to a $287,500 settlement in a federal consumer protection case. That kind of accountability only happened because the airspace over that facility was still open. If this rule passes, such investigations could become impossible. No more aerial footage exposing false marketing claims. No more evidence of manure lagoons breaching after a hurricane. No more documentation of mass culling during a bird flu outbreak. No more way to check a company's story against reality. The bigger and more damaging the facility, the more protection it would get.
There is absolutely no valid reason to restrict the drone flights over these facilities as long as citizens are permitted to fly drones over unrestricted airspace above our homes, parks, roads, and wild lands.

This rule could let the largest, most powerful operations shield themselves from scrutiny — the opposite of what the public interest requires. I urge the FAA not to develop gating criteria for this sector and not to grant UAFRs to food and agriculture facilities.

I strongly oppose allowing food and agriculture facilities to obtain Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs). Drone photography and videography have become one of the few ways the public can gain insight into what happens inside large-scale factory farms—places that are otherwise inaccessible. Transparency is essential, especially when it comes to industries that directly impact public health, animal welfare, and the environment.

To my knowledge, there is no documented evidence showing that drones have posed a legitimate safety or security threat to these facilities. What drone footage has done, however, is reveal misleading marketing practices, environmental violations, and instances of animal mistreatment that the public deserves to know about. Without this technology, many of these issues may never have come to light.

Granting UAFRs to food and agriculture facilities would allow some of the largest and most powerful agricultural operations to operate with even less public oversight. Restricting lawful aerial documentation would reduce transparency and accountability at a time when consumers are increasingly seeking honest information about where their food comes from and how it is produced.

For these reasons, I respectfully urge the FAA not to develop gating criteria for the food and agriculture sector and not to grant UAFRs to these facilities. Protecting the public's ability to document and understand the practices of these operations is an important part of ensuring accountability and maintaining public trust.

Michelle Ferritto
