Federal Aviation Administration
800 Independence Avenue SW, Washington, DC 20591
RE: Public Comment on the Notice of Proposed Rulemaking, Designation, Restrict the Operation of
Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
Docket No. FAA-2026-4558; RIN 2120-AL33; 91 FR 24650 (May 6, 2026)
To the Office of Rulemaking:
I. Interest of the Commenter
Raad Labs Inc. ("Raad") operates a generalized aerial intelligence platform. Through a network of more than
6,000 FAA-certificated and internationally credentialed remote pilots operating across 65 countries, Raad
conducts recurring, high-frequency aerial data collection for industrial, energy, telecommunications, real
estate, and other infrastructure sectors. Our clients include asset owners, insurers, engineering firms,
environmental and compliance stakeholders, government and disaster-response agencies, and institutional
research organizations. Raad also operates a self-serve tasking platform at raad.com through which customers
commission this work.
Raad's operations are precisely the profile of operation the proposed rule describes as trusted: United States
operations are conducted under 14 CFR Part 107 by TSA-vetted certificate holders, broadcast Remote
Identification in accordance with 14 CFR Part 89, and are flown by identified parties subject to FAA
enforcement. We therefore have a direct and substantial interest in how proposed Part 74 defines the
operations that may access Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restriction (UAFR) airspace.
We support the FAA's core objective. The kinetic and reconnaissance threats that motivated Section 2209 and
Executive Order 14305 are real, and a coherent federal framework is preferable to the fragmented patchwork
of state critical-infrastructure statutes that has developed in the decade since the statute was enacted. Our
comments are directed at a narrow but consequential set of drafting choices in Subpart G that, as written,
would burden the exact operators the rule identifies as trustworthy while doing little to address the threat
the rule targets.
II. Summary of Recommendations
Raad does not argue that no restriction should exist. Section 2209 directed the FAA to establish this
mechanism, and settles that restrictions must be available; it does not settle how much of the National
Airspace System should be enclosed, or which operators must be excluded to achieve the security objective.

That prior question governs everything below. If the objective is to reduce the risk of a terrorist attack on
critical infrastructure, is excluding vetted, identified, federally certificated operators from a public asset the
highest-impact and lowest-downside way to achieve it? Raad submits that it is not, and that the following
seven changes would deliver the security Section 2209 seeks at a small fraction of the cost to the airspace.
1. Categorically exempt certificated operations. Operations under Parts 91, 107, 108, 135, and 137
should be exempt from Standard UAFR operating restrictions where the operator broadcasts Remote
ID, holds current federal certification and TSA clearance, provides advance notice through a single
standardized FAA-operated system (see Recommendations 2 and 3), and does not physically interfere
with facility operations. These four conditions fully satisfy Section 2209's purpose without conditioning
access on flight geometry, altitude, or case-by-case facility consent. This question has largely been
settled at the state level already. Most states that have enacted critical-infrastructure drone statutes
exempt operations conducted in compliance with FAA rules, including Texas, whose regime is the most
restrictive to survive judicial review (see Section IV). The FAA should adopt federally the carve-out the
leading state models have already validated. If the FAA declines a categorical exemption, it should at
minimum define "transit" and add an express observation category to § 74.250. (Section V)
2. Mandate a standardized, LAANC-style notification system. Any notification requirement under §
74.255 should be satisfied by a single automated digital submission with defined effect, not bespoke
per-facility procedures. Fragmented notification is an anticompetitive burden that favors large,
vertically integrated operators over small businesses. (Section VI)
3. Condition access on operator trust, not facility permission. Access should hinge on Remote ID, federal
certification, and TSA vetting, not on a facility's and its owners' preferences regarding public airspace.
Raad affirmatively supports a notification requirement, one operators do not face today, provided it
runs through a single FAA-operated system and confers no power on the facility to deny, delay, or
obstruct a compliant flight, and provided that neither the FAA nor the notified facility may condition
access on the purpose or subject matter of the operation. (Section VII)
4. Weigh the public-interest value of aerial observation. A transit-only regime forecloses a channel of
lawful public-interest observation that no other platform can replace at comparable cost and
resolution, spanning independent verification (journalism, environmental and regulatory monitoring,
insurance, and research) and governmental functions that commercial operators routinely support
under contract (municipal GIS and land-use planning, right-of-way and corridor mapping, and
post-disaster damage assessment). The FAA should weigh this expressly; the governmental component
in particular stands apart from the contested and, in the Fifth Circuit, adverse constitutional question.
(Section VIII)
5. Weigh the network and administrative cost of restriction density. The Regulatory Impact Analysis
counts only applicant and government costs. It assigns no cost to enclosing route-contiguous airspace,
though restrictions will cluster in the corridors where the UAS economy is densest, and no cost to
committing the FAA's scarce rulemaking capacity to thousands of site-specific proceedings. A
categorical exemption makes the marginal cost of each UAFR, to the network and to the agency, near
zero; a transit-only regime makes both compound. (Section IX)
6. Sort access by operator trust, not flight geometry. The rule's security rationale is an identity
distinction; its drafting is a physics distinction. These should be reconciled in favor of the identity-based
logic the FAA itself articulates. (Sections III and IV)

7. Place the burden of justification on the applicant. An applicant seeking to enclose public airspace
should be required to identify a specific and articulable threat, to explain why existing authorities are
inadequate to it, and to explain why the requested boundaries are no larger than that threat requires.
Eligibility is not entitlement. (Section IX)
III. A Restrictive Reading Would Contradict the Administration's Own Drone-Dominance Policy
This rulemaking does not stand alone. Section 2209 of the FAA Extension, Safety, and Security Act of 2016 is
the statutory mandate for this rule, but for nearly a decade the FAA did not act on it. What broke that
impasse was a deliberate pair of executive orders. On June 6, 2025, the Administration issued Executive Order
14305, Restoring American Airspace Sovereignty, which directed the FAA to promptly submit this
long-overdue Section 2209 rule and finalize it as soon as practicable, and, the same day, the companion
Executive Order 14307, Unleashing American Drone Dominance. The two orders were designed to operate in
tandem: the first to secure the airspace, the second to expand the domestic UAS economy within it. Even the
sovereignty order itself calls for scaling up domestic production and expanding exports of American-made
drones. A faithful implementation of the first cannot be allowed to defeat the express purpose of the second.
Executive Order 14307 declares that drones enhance United States productivity, create high-skilled jobs, and
are reshaping the future of aviation, and it names infrastructure inspection, among other fields, as an
industry that drones are already transforming. Its pillars are the integration of UAS into the National Airspace
System through risk-based rulemaking, the commercialization of UAS at scale within the domestic economy,
and the strengthening of the domestic drone industrial base. Drone dominance, in the Administration's own
framing, is not achieved by manufacturing alone. It requires a thriving domestic economy of operators putting
American-made drones to productive use across inspection, data collection, and monitoring.
The mechanism the rule uses is also not risk-based. Executive Order 14307 instructs the FAA to integrate
unmanned aircraft into the National Airspace System through risk-based rulemaking. Proposed § 74.250
instead sorts operations by their geometry and altitude, which does not track the risk the companion
sovereignty order targets: a function of operator identity and intent, not of flight path. Section IV sets out why
a vetted, identified, Remote-ID-broadcasting operator presents the same negligible security profile whether it
crosses a facility or collects data above it.
To the extent a broader restriction is driven not by the risk of disruption but by a general unease at being
observed from above, that discomfort, however understandable, is not among the purposes Congress
assigned the FAA. The agency's charge is the safe and efficient use of the navigable airspace, supplemented
by Section 2209's protection of fixed sites from kinetic and reconnaissance threats; neither reaches insulating
a facility from the fact of lawful observation. Frontier technologies have provoked this unease before, and
giving in to it has proved costly: Britain's 1865 Red Flag Act, which required every self-propelled vehicle to be
preceded by a man on foot carrying a red flag, is credited with ceding a generation of automotive
development to other nations.
The FAA should therefore read and implement Part 74 in the way that reconciles both orders: secure the
airspace against genuine threats through the Special UAFR tier and the exclusion of operators who are
anonymous to the federal government, having undergone no identity verification and no security threat
assessment and holding no revocable certificate, while preserving, through the categorical exemption
recommended below, the domestic commercial and public-interest operations that the Administration has

made it national policy to expand. Where two companion orders can be harmonized, the FAA should not read
one so as to nullify the other.
IV. The Access Carve-Out Rests on Operator Trust, Not on the Shape of the Flight
Proposed § 74.250 permits operations conducted under Parts 91, 107, 108, 135, and 137 to access Standard
UAFR airspace. The preamble explains why: these operators have met rigorous FAA safety requirements and
TSA security threat assessments where applicable, and are, in the FAA's words, established, known, and
conspicuous. The rule then attaches three conditions: broadcast of Remote ID under Part 89, transit in the
shortest practicable time, and notification to the facility under § 74.255.
The justification for admitting these operators is entirely about who they are. A Part 107 remote pilot has
surrendered their identity to the federal government, been screened against terrorist watchlists, holds a
revocable certificate that gives the FAA direct enforcement leverage, and broadcasts their identity and
position in real time throughout every flight. That trust profile does not change based on whether the aircraft
crosses the facility in a straight line or orbits above it. The security analysis is identical in both cases. Yet the
"shortest practicable time" condition makes the lawful, trusted operator's access depend on the geometry of
the flight rather than on the trust that justified admitting them in the first place. The rule builds the correct
whitelist and then attaches the wrong condition to it.
The vetting behind that certificate deserves to be named precisely, because the premise of this rule is
counterterrorism. A Part 107 applicant is screened by the Transportation Security Administration against the
federal terrorist watchlist before the certificate issues, and remains subject to that screening as long as the
certificate is held. This is not a paperwork formality or a general background check. It is the same
counterterrorism instrument the government applies to airline passengers and to certificated airmen, and it is
the government's own considered answer to the question whether a given individual presents a terrorism risk
in aviation. Section 2209 exists to protect fixed sites from precisely that risk. The operators § 74.250 would
bar from observation are therefore the single population the federal counterterrorism apparatus has already
screened and cleared for this exact purpose.
The restriction inverts the government's own risk hierarchy
The consequence of conditioning access on flight geometry is a set of outcomes that no threat model can
justify. Consider a single individual who holds a Part 107 certificate and has passed a TSA security threat
assessment. That same person is trusted by the federal government to:
• board a commercial airliner carrying scores of passengers;
• hold a remote pilot certificate after federal security vetting; and
• charter a general-aviation aircraft and loiter over the very same facility, at altitude, for hours, carrying
a high-magnification camera, with no Remote ID, no advance notification, and no UAFR applicability
whatsoever, because Section 2209 reaches only unmanned aircraft.
The proposed rule would nonetheless prohibit that same vetted individual from flying a three-pound,
Remote-ID-broadcasting quadcopter over the facility to collect data, after having notified the site in advance.
If the threat model is a projectile, the rule restricts the smallest airframe available while leaving untouched
the manned aircraft that carries orders of magnitude more kinetic energy and fuel. If the threat model is

reconnaissance or espionage, the rule restricts the one platform that continuously broadcasts its operator's
identity while leaving unrestricted the manned aircraft that may broadcast nothing. Under either threat
model, the ordering of restrictions is inverted. The rule constrains the transparent and the accountable and
exempts the opaque.
This is the familiar and long-recognized failure of airspace restrictions generally: they bind the compliant, not
the hostile. A person intending to fly an explosive-laden drone into a refinery is already committing multiple
federal felonies and is wholly undeterred by a Part 74 airspace designation. Such a person does not first
obtain a certificate, register the aircraft, broadcast their identity, and email the facility a notice of intent. The
only operator the rule can actually reach with precision is the one who shows up, announces themselves, and
complies. Excluding that operator from observation missions delivers essentially no security benefit, because
the excluded profile has no overlap with the threat profile the rule was written to address.
The question the record does not ask
Raad does not dispute that Congress directed the FAA to establish this mechanism, and does not argue that
no restriction should exist. Section 2209 requires a petition process and the FAA is obliged to build one. But
the mandate settles only that restrictions must be available. It does not settle how much of the National
Airspace System should be enclosed, or which operators must be excluded to achieve the security objective.
The NPRM and the Regulatory Impact Analysis are both framed around the second-order question: what
process should govern designation. The prior question is never asked. If the objective is to reduce the risk of a
terrorist attack on critical infrastructure, is excluding vetted, identified, federally certificated operators from a
public asset the highest-impact and lowest-downside way to achieve it?
The record does not support an affirmative answer, and the preceding analysis explains why: the excluded
population has no overlap with the threat population, and the platform left entirely unrestricted carries more
kinetic energy and better sensors with no identification requirement at all. The security gained is not merely
small. On the rule's own threat model it is difficult to identify at all. The cost, by contrast, is concentrated,
compounding, and borne by parties with no voice in the proceeding that imposes it.
Raad asks the FAA to apply to this instrument the test it applies to any other: does the marginal exclusion buy
marginal security, and at what price to the public asset being enclosed? Where the answer is that a vetted
operator's exclusion buys nothing, the exclusion should not be made.
V. Recommendation 1: Categorically Exempt Certificated Operations, Conditioned on Identity,
Notice, and Non-Interference
The logical structure of the rule points to a cleaner remedy than a transit definition. If the harms Section 2209
targets are the kinetic threat, targeting, and espionage, and if the FAA's own basis for admitting Part 91, 107,
108, 135, and 137 operators is that they are vetted, certificated, and conspicuous, then the security objective
is fully achieved by ensuring four things about any operation in Standard UAFR airspace. It does not require,
and is not advanced by, a restriction keyed to the geometry or altitude of the flight.
Recommendation. FAA should categorically exempt operations conducted under Parts 91, 107, 108, 135, and
137 from Standard UAFR operating restrictions, subject to the following four conditions:

• the drone is identifiable: the aircraft broadcasts Remote ID in accordance with 14 CFR Part 89;
• the operator is known and vetted: the operator holds current federal certification and has cleared the
applicable TSA security threat assessment;
• the facility is notified: the operator provides advance notice through the standardized digital system
described in Recommendation 2; and
• operations are not disrupted: the operation does not create a physical hazard to, or an operational
conflict with, the facility's own activities, including its own aviation or unmanned aircraft operations.
These four conditions map directly onto the aviation-safety, national-security, and homeland-security factors
Congress enumerated. A known drone, flown by a known and vetted operator, whose presence the facility
has been told of in advance, and which does not interfere with the facility's operations, presents none of the
threats the rule was written to address, whether the aircraft crosses the site in a straight line or observes it
from above. Restricting that operation by reference to flight geometry adds no security and subtracts lawful
commerce and lawful observation.
For clarity: the fourth condition concerns physical and operational interference only. It is not a license for a
facility to treat lawful observation as "interference" because it prefers not to be observed. Observation of a
facility from the public navigable airspace, by an identified and vetted operator, is not disruption of that
facility's operations, and the final rule should say so.
The navigable airspace is a public asset, and restrictions on it must be tailored
The navigable airspace is not private to the facility beneath it. It is a public asset over which the United States
has exclusive sovereignty under 49 U.S.C. 40103(a)(1), and through which the public holds a statutory right of
transit under 40103(a)(2). The Supreme Court confirmed as much in United States v. Causby, 328 U.S. 256
(1946), which discarded the ancient common-law rule that a landowner owned the airspace up to the
heavens, held that doctrine has no place in the modern world, and described the airspace as a public highway
in the public domain. A facility owner no more owns the airspace above its fence line than a landowner owns
the interstate that passes its property, and a transit-only reading that lets a facility exclude observation would
quietly reintroduce, for drones, the up-to-the-heavens ownership Causby rejected. Because the airspace is a
public asset held in trust, a restriction on it must be justified by, and narrowly tailored to, the security interest
it serves. A restriction that binds identified, vetted, non-interfering operators while leaving anonymous
manned aircraft untouched is not tailored to a security interest; it is tailored to nothing the record supports.
The most restrictive tested state regime still exempts compliant commercial operators
This is not a novel request. In the decade the FAA took to implement Section 2209, numerous states enacted
their own critical-infrastructure drone statutes. The most detailed of them, Texas Government Code Chapter
423, makes it an offense to operate a drone below 400 feet over a critical-infrastructure facility (Tex. Gov't
Code § 423.0045), and it was upheld by the Fifth Circuit against facial First Amendment and preemption
challenges in National Press Photographers Ass'n v. McCraw, 90 F.4th 770 (5th Cir. 2024). Even that statute,
the most restrictive critical-infrastructure regime to survive judicial review, exempts an operator using a drone
for a commercial purpose so long as the operation complies with all applicable FAA rules, restrictions,
exemptions, and authorizations (Tex. Gov't Code § 423.0045(c)(5)).
The lesson for this rulemaking is direct. The jurisdiction that has restricted critical-infrastructure overflight
most aggressively, and prevailed in court, nonetheless concluded that its security objectives were fully served

while letting FAA-compliant commercial operators through. If Texas's interest in protecting its refineries,
substations, and pipelines is satisfied by a compliant-commercial-operator exemption, the FAA's identical
interest is satisfied by one as well.
We ask the FAA to adopt federally the carve-out the leading state models have already validated, and to do so
in regulatory text. The courts have declined to find that federal law occupies this field, so a uniform national
standard will come only from the FAA writing one.
The deference already runs the right way. States exercising police powers over their own refineries,
substations, and pipelines concluded that FAA compliance is a sufficient answer. A facility's interest in the
airspace above its fence line is narrower still. If the sovereigns closest to these assets declined to make access
turn on their permission, no facility can claim it should turn on theirs.
A categorical exemption does not weaken Section 2209
Exempting certificated operations from Standard UAFR restrictions leaves the rule's security architecture
intact. Recreational operators under 49 U.S.C. 44809 remain fully excluded, as the NPRM proposes. The
Special UAFR tier remains available, unchanged, for the genuinely sensitive federal, defense, and intelligence
sites where a heightened restriction and an express-permission regime are warranted. What the exemption
removes is only the geometry-based limitation on the one population the rule already identifies as
trustworthy. It narrows nothing that protects against the actual threat; it removes only the part of the rule
that burdens the compliant.
Fallback: if FAA declines a categorical exemption, it must at least define transit
Should the FAA decline to adopt a categorical exemption, it should not leave the current draft as written. The
word "transit" appears nowhere in the proposed definitions at § 74.1, and "shortest practicable time" is
undefined, exposing every certificated operator to enforcement risk on every flight and handing facilities a
colorable basis to allege that any given operation was not a bona fide transit. At minimum, then, the FAA
should (a) define "transit" and "shortest practicable time" in § 74.1, measuring reasonableness against the
operation's legitimate purpose rather than bare traversal, and (b) add to § 74.250 an express category
permitting aerial data collection, inspection, survey, mapping, and observation by certificated operators. The
FAA solicited exactly this input, asking what additional operations it should allow and what the economic
impact would be if it does not. Permitting a Part 137 operator to spray adjacent to a facility and a Part 135
operator to carry cargo across it, while forbidding a certificated operator from photographing it, is not a
coherent security distinction.
VI. Recommendation 2: Standardize Notification, or Entrench the Largest Operators
The § 74.255 notification requirement, as proposed, specifies no mechanism, no lead time, and no format.
The FAA estimates that as many as 9,000 UAFRs may be designated over five years. Absent standardization,
that is a prospect of thousands of individually defined notification procedures: one facility wanting a
telephone call, another a web form, another a fixed number of hours of advance notice, another an email
address that no one monitors.
This fragmentation is not a neutral inconvenience. Its cost falls almost entirely on small operators. A large,
vertically integrated operator with a dedicated regulatory-compliance function and in-house engineering can

absorb bespoke, multi-facility notification at negligible marginal cost, and can build automated pipelines to
handle it at scale. The sole proprietor and small business, who together make up the overwhelming majority
of the hundreds of thousands of certificated remote pilots and who form the backbone of the commercial
drone economy, cannot. For them, the overhead of researching and satisfying dozens of idiosyncratic
per-facility procedures is prohibitive, and the rational response is simply to stop bidding on any work near
critical infrastructure.
The result would be an outcome the market itself could never produce: administrative friction, rather than
competition on price or quality, clearing small operators out of the most valuable industrial airspace in the
country, which is exactly where infrastructure-inspection demand concentrates. A rule promulgated under an
Executive Order whose stated purpose includes unleashing American innovation should not, in its
implementation, hand the most valuable segment of a growing domestic industry to its largest incumbents
through paperwork. The remedy is straightforward and inexpensive for the FAA to adopt.
Recommendation. FAA should require that any notification obligation under § 74.255 be satisfied through a
single, standardized, automated digital system modeled on the Low Altitude Authorization and Notification
Capability (LAANC), with a nationally uniform format, a defined and short lead time, and automatic effect
upon submission. Notification should be a machine-to-system message, not a discretionary interaction with
each facility's security office. This preserves the facility's awareness interest while eliminating the
anticompetitive drag of fragmentation.
VII. Recommendation 3: Condition Access on Operator Trust, Not Facility Authorization
The proposed rule already reflects the right principle in part: an operator using the transit carve-out does not
request permission from the facility. The final rule should preserve that principle for every allowed operation,
including observation and data collection. Remote ID is what serves the FAA's legitimate interest in knowing
who is operating and being able to identify a non-compliant operator; it is passive, continuous, real-time,
verifiable, and involuntary, and any receiver in range, including the facility's own required receive capability,
logs it. Each of these safeguards is already a condition of lawful operation rather than a new obligation
created by this rule: TSA security threat assessment is a prerequisite to issuance of the remote pilot certificate
under Part 107 and of the airman certificates underlying Part 91, 135, and 137 operations, and Remote ID
broadcast is already mandatory under Part 89. The rule would therefore be exempting operators who have
already satisfied every identity and vetting condition the federal government imposes. Conditioning access
instead on facility consent would hand the observed party a veto over lawful operations, which is precisely
the wrong party to hold that power.
Raad supports a notification obligation and welcomes it. This is worth emphasizing, because proposed §
74.255 would create a federal notification obligation that does not exist in the current regulations, and Raad
is not asking to be relieved of it. A trusted operator identifies itself and announces its operations; it does not
ask permission. Raad supports notifying every affected facility, provided that notification runs through a
single, standardized, FAA-operated system or interface, as recommended in Recommendation 2, rather than
through thousands of bespoke, facility-specific procedures. Standardized notice delivers the facility's
awareness interest in full while remaining administrable for operators of every size.
One guardrail must survive in the final rule: notice is notice, not permission. The proposal correctly provides
that notification is not approval and that the facility cannot deny a compliant operator access. The final rule

should state this expressly and ensure that facilities cannot convert the notification requirement into a de
facto authorization, a delay or obstruction mechanism, or a record marshaled to suppress lawful operations.
Notification should ask what, not why
A notification requirement should collect operational facts and nothing else: the location, altitude, and time
window of the operation, the aircraft, and the identity of the certificated operator.
A purpose inquiry would do real damage. For journalists it would require disclosure of the story being
pursued, the entity being examined, and the information sought, before the flight that would obtain it, which
converts an operational filing into advance notice to the subject of the reporting.
The party best positioned to act on the disclosure is the facility receiving the notice. A requirement that
operators tell a facility why they are flying, in advance, hands that facility exactly the information needed to
prepare for, contest, or preempt the observation, and it does so without any corresponding security benefit.
The final rule should provide expressly that neither the FAA nor a notified facility may condition access on the
purpose or subject matter of the operation.
Recommendation. FAA should require notification through a single, standardized, FAA-operated system
rather than bespoke facility procedures, and should state expressly that notification confers no authority on
the facility to deny, condition, delay, or obstruct a compliant operation, and no authority to inquire into the
purpose or subject matter of the flight.
VIII. Recommendation 4: The Public-Interest Value of Aerial Observation
To be precise about what the rule does and does not restrict: a facility that holds a UAFR controls the
operations conducted within its own restricted airspace and can authorize its own inspections, whether flown
by internal pilots or by contractors it engages. Facility-commissioned self-inspection therefore continues. The
burden of a transit-only reading of § 74.250 falls elsewhere. It falls on the inspection, mapping, and
documentation of adjacent and linear infrastructure, a transmission line or pipeline running past a protected
site, which requires operating over or across the UAFR even when the linear-asset owner commissioned the
work. And it falls on aerial data collection performed for parties other than the site owner, the insurers,
lenders, regulators, engineers, journalists, and researchers who depend on it. Both are legitimate and growing
parts of the UAS economy that Executive Order 14307 seeks to expand.
The value of data collected for parties other than the site owner lies precisely in its independence. Insurance
and lending assessment, environmental and regulatory compliance verification, safety and engineering review
of neighboring assets, journalism, and research exist to provide an account that does not depend on the site
owner's cooperation. If the only observation the airspace permits is observation the facility itself authorizes,
the facility becomes the gatekeeper of all information about itself, and independent verification, which serves
insurers, lenders, regulators, communities, and the public, becomes impossible. That is a significant
consequence for the FAA to weigh, and the proposed rule does not appear to have weighed it.
Aerial observation by unmanned aircraft is not solely a commercial convenience. It is, increasingly, the only
scalable instrument by which the public can independently verify conditions on the ground at industrial and
infrastructure sites. Commercial satellite imagery, at the resolution generally available, cannot resolve the
site-level detail needed to verify environmental, safety, and regulatory compliance; low-altitude aerial data

collection can. Journalism, environmental monitoring, insurance documentation, academic research, and
market transparency all depend on this capability.
Journalism is the clearest illustration. Unmanned aircraft have become a routine instrument of visual
reporting, used to document natural disasters, wildfires, flooding, environmental releases, industrial
accidents, infrastructure failures, transportation incidents, search and rescue operations, publicly funded
construction, and governmental activity conducted in public view. In many of these situations low-altitude
aerial imagery is the only practical means of establishing conditions on the ground accurately, independently,
and while the event is still unfolding. The resulting record serves interests the FAA itself is charged with
advancing: it alerts the public to environmental hazards, infrastructure failure, and emergency conditions, and
it supplies the factual basis on which regulators, insurers, and communities act. A framework that forecloses
that record does not trade public safety against press access. It trades one instrument of public safety for
another.
Unmanned aircraft also substitute for a more dangerous platform. Aerial newsgathering was historically
conducted by helicopter, at real and recurring cost in crew lives. A small unmanned aircraft performs much of
the same work at a fraction of the risk, a fraction of the expense, and with a far smaller acoustic and
operational footprint over the community below. A rule that restricts the unmanned platform while leaving
the crewed one untouched does not merely misallocate security attention, as Sections III and IV explain. It
also pushes the same missions back toward the more hazardous alternative.
The distributional consequence deserves separate attention, for the same reason set out in Recommendation
2. A news helicopter and a small unmanned aircraft routinely document the same event from comparable
vantage points, but only the unmanned aircraft is subject to a UAFR. The organizations that can absorb the
cost of rotary-wing coverage are the largest ones. Local broadcasters, regional newspapers, nonprofit
newsrooms, documentary producers, and independent operators cannot, and for them the unmanned
platform is not a cheaper option but the only option. A restriction drawn so that it binds only the affordable
platform therefore falls hardest on the smallest participants while leaving the largest comparatively
unaffected. That is the same asymmetry that unstandardized notification would produce among commercial
operators, arising here from the scope of the restriction rather than from its administration, and the FAA
should ask in both instances whether the disparity is necessary to the security objective it has stated.
Critically, Raad does not rest this recommendation on the First Amendment, and the FAA need not resolve the
constitutional question to act on it. The Fifth Circuit held in National Press Photographers Ass'n v. McCraw, 90
F.4th 770 (5th Cir. 2024), that operating an unmanned aircraft is not inherently expressive and that a
low-altitude restriction over critical infrastructure does not, on its face, implicate the First Amendment,
reversing a district court that had concluded otherwise, 594 F. Supp. 3d 789 (W.D. Tex. 2022). Whatever the
eventual resolution of that question, the public-interest value described here is a matter the FAA is obliged to
weigh as a policy matter under its own rulemaking obligations, and the governmental and public-safety uses
identified above carry that weight independently of any constitutional interest.
Whatever the constitutional line, a transit-only regime would foreclose a channel of lawful, public-interest
observation for which no substitute exists at comparable resolution. That channel includes journalism,
environmental and regulatory verification, insurance documentation, and academic research. It also includes
core governmental functions that commercial operators routinely deliver under contract for jurisdictions that

do not operate their own fleets, including municipal GIS and land-use planning, right-of-way and make-ready
mapping, and post-disaster damage assessment. This last category matters precisely because it carries
public-interest weight wholly apart from any First Amendment interest: even if the constitutional question is
resolved against drone flight, these governmental and public-safety uses remain lawful, valuable, and
foreclosed by a transit-only reading.
IX. Economic Impact: The Cost of Restriction Scales With Density, Not With Sites
The FAA requested data on the economic impact to commercial operators excluded from UAFR airspace. The
most important economic features of this rule are two that the Regulatory Impact Analysis does not examine.
First, low-altitude airspace is network infrastructure, and the cost of restricting it scales with the density of
restrictions, not with their number. Second, the rule encloses not only airspace but the FAA's own
administrative capacity, which is the scarcest resource in the drone economy. A per-facility analysis, however
carefully performed nine thousand times, cannot see either cost, because both arise only in the aggregate.
The Regulatory Impact Analysis counts one side of the ledger. The FAA estimates annualized costs of $21
million to $31 million under a scenario of more than 9,000 facilities obtaining UAFRs. That figure comprises
applicant compliance and government review costs; it assigns no cost to the operators excluded from the
airspace or to the industries downstream of the data they collect, and the analysis acknowledges that
benefits would depend on the ultimate scope of the restrictions granted. The only quantified cost, in other
words, is the paperwork of enclosure. By that method, enclosing the entire national airspace below 400 feet
would appear nearly costless.
Airspace is a network, and networks fail at their weakest segment. The economic unit of low-altitude
aviation is not the point but the route: the transmission corridor inspected end to end, the pipeline right of
way flown in sequence, the survey grid flown without interruption, the beyond-visual-line-of-sight networks
contemplated by proposed Part 108, whose economics depend on route contiguity. The proposal does not
close those routes to passage, and Raad does not suggest otherwise: § 74.250 permits a certificated operator
to transit a Standard UAFR. The route-level cost arises in three other ways. The segments where the work
itself must occur over the UAFR are lost rather than slowed, and a linear inspection with a gap at every
protected crossing is worth a fraction of a complete one. The transit permission is conditioned on the shortest
practicable time, a term the rule does not define, so an operator cannot know in advance whether a slow
inspection pass or a survey turn will later be characterized as something other than transit, and that
ambiguity is priced as risk even where the flight would have been lawful. And notification stacks per facility,
so a corridor crossing many UAFRs carries a compliance burden that scales with the number of restrictions on
the route rather than with its length. Each of these costs lands on parties absent from the individual UAFR
proceeding, which concerns one applicant and one parcel, and the proceeding in which the route-level cost
could be weighed does not exist anywhere in proposed Part 74. The effect concentrates where the drone
economy is densest: the 16 eligible sectors cluster in industrial corridors and metropolitan areas, and in a
dense city, where a hospital, substation, data center, water facility, and telecom hub may occupy adjoining
blocks, the composite of individually reasonable restrictions is a low-altitude airspace that is available for
passage but largely unavailable for work. The record confirms the scale. The agency acknowledges the eligible

sectors could encompass millions of facilities, narrowed by SRMA criteria to a candidate pool on the order of
125,000, of which the 9,000-facility scenario is only the projected five-year uptake. Nothing in the rule prices,
caps, or even measures cumulative density.
Nothing disciplines the demand for enclosure. An applicant captures the full perceived benefit of a UAFR
while bearing only a modest application cost; the cost of the enclosure falls entirely on third parties and on
the public whose right of transit under 49 U.S.C. 40103(a)(2) the airspace embodies. When excluding is
privately valuable and privately near-free, applications will systematically exceed what any aggregate welfare
analysis would support. The FAA itself describes restricting airspace as an extraordinary remedy. A remedy
that is nearly free to request, evaluated in isolation, and never audited in aggregate will not remain
extraordinary.
The burden of justification belongs to the applicant. An applicant seeking a UAFR is asking to enclose a
portion of a public asset, and the question before the FAA on each application is therefore not whether an
operator can justify its use of public airspace, which requires no justification, but whether the applicant can
justify removing it from public use. The FAA should not treat eligibility as establishing entitlement. An
applicant should be required to identify a specific and articulable threat rather than a generalized concern
about unmanned aircraft, to explain why existing authorities are inadequate to it, including Part 107
operating limitations, Temporary Flight Restriction authority, Remote ID, geofencing, and the criminal statutes
that already reach attacks on infrastructure, and to explain why the requested boundaries are no larger than
that threat requires. Absent that showing, the restriction is not an extraordinary remedy, which is the FAA's
own characterization of it, but a default. And the mere prospect that a facility may be photographed from
lawful public airspace is not a threat.
The FAA's institutional throughput is the scarcest resource in the entire drone economy. Section 2209 itself
is the proof: Congress gave the agency 180 days in 2016, and the rule arrived in 2026. That decade reflects
the genuine scarcity of the agency's rulemaking capacity, for which every UAS priority competes, and the
proposed rule would commit that capacity to an open-ended docket of site-specific proceedings. Each UAFR is
an individual adjudication: intake, evaluation coordinated with Sector Risk Management Agencies, an
individual Federal Register comment period of at least 30 days, a final determination, a reconsideration path,
and a permanent obligation to administer amendments, renewals, and cancellations on five-year terms, along
with charting and integration into LAANC and public awareness tools. The FAA's own scenario implies several
completed proceedings every working day, sustained for five years, against a 125,000-facility candidate pool.
The workforce that would process that queue is the same workforce charged with the priorities of Executive
Order 14307: finalizing Part 108, expanding LAANC, and integrating unmanned aircraft through risk-based
rulemaking. Every analyst-hour spent adjudicating whether an individual substation warrants a flight
restriction is an hour not spent on the work that grows the airspace economy, and the pending queue is itself
a harm, casting legal uncertainty over exactly the corridors where operators must plan recurring work.
A categorical exemption for certificated operators resolves both problems; a transit-only regime guarantees
them. Both costs share one origin: the rule restricts by geometry, so every added restriction subtracts usable
airspace from every operator regardless of trust, and every application carries total stakes and therefore
demands full adjudication. The categorical exemption proposed in Recommendation 1, under which
operations conducted under Parts 91, 107, 108, 135, and 137 pass through any Standard UAFR subject to

Remote ID, standardized notice, and non-interference, inverts that arithmetic. The marginal cost of each
additional UAFR, to the airspace network and to the agency, approaches zero. Routes remain contiguous at
any restriction density. The stakes of each application fall, evaluation standardizes, and the FAA ceases to be
the bottleneck on both the restriction process and the industry it regulates. Sort access by operator trust, and
the quantity of restrictions stops mattering; sort by geometry, and it becomes the only thing that matters.
X. Support for Aligned Stakeholder Positions
Raad endorses the comments submitted by the Drone Service Providers Alliance to the extent they align with
the recommendations above, in particular: that access to Standard UAFRs should not be limited to transit
operations; that notification should follow a LAANC-style standardized model; that Standard UAFR boundaries
and any buffer or altitude standards should be uniform and clearly defined rather than facility-specific; that
applicants should be required to demonstrate a genuine, documented operational need rather than mere
concern; and that Part 74 should expressly disclaim any authorization for private-party UAS mitigation or
interdiction, which must remain within federal authority. We also support the call for clear federal
preemption language, so that the national framework Section 2209 promises actually displaces, rather than
layers atop, the existing patchwork of state and local restrictions.
XI. Conclusion
The proposed rule identifies the right population of trusted operators and then, through a geometry-based
restriction, an undefined term, and an unstandardized process, risks excluding them from the very work that
makes them valuable. The navigable airspace is a public asset, the threat the rule targets is defined by
operator identity, and even the most restrictive state critical-infrastructure regime to survive judicial review
still exempts FAA-compliant commercial operators. The cleanest path is therefore a categorical exemption for
certificated operations, conditioned only on identity, notice, and non-interference. On the designation side,
the burden of justifying an enclosure of public airspace should rest on the applicant seeking it. That approach
lets the FAA achieve its security objective and deliver a genuine uniform national standard without
suppressing a lawful, transparent, and publicly valuable domestic industry, and without weakening the Special
UAFR tier that protects the sites that actually warrant it. The test the FAA should apply to this instrument is
the one it applies to any other: does the marginal exclusion buy marginal security, and at what price to the
public asset being enclosed? A vetted, identified, TSA-screened operator is the one population the federal
counterterrorism apparatus has already examined and cleared. Excluding that operator buys no security, and
an exclusion that buys none should not be made. We appreciate the opportunity to comment and welcome
the chance to provide operational data or further detail to the Office of Rulemaking.
Respectfully submitted,
Teej Ragsdale
Founder and Chief Executive Officer
Raad Labs Inc.

•There is no documented evidence of drones threatening safety or security at these facilities, but there is a documented history of drone footage exposing false marketing claims, environmental violations, and animal welfare abuses.

Please keep drones flying to stop animal abuse in factory farming areas. It is possible to spot lies and cruelty.

Keep the drones flying. companies are taking advantage of loop holes and sell their eggs as a free range where actually it is all just a larger cage. Dont let them get away with this by banning drones flying and exposing their lies.

This rule could allow the largest and most powerful agricultural operations to avoid public oversight, undermining transparency and accountability. The public deserves greater scrutiny of these facilities—not less.

As a member of the public, I have learned about many of the realities of factory farming through drone footage. Those images have provided a rare and important window into practices that would otherwise remain hidden from view. Seeing the scale of animal confinement and the conditions at some industrial farming operations has profoundly shaped my understanding of the need for transparency and independent oversight. Restricting access to this type of documentation would make it even harder for the public to understand what is happening inside these facilities and to hold powerful industries accountable.

I strongly urge the FAA not to create gating criteria for this sector and not to grant UAFRs to food and agriculture facilities.

To Whom It May Concern,
I am a professional nature photographer, filmmaker, and FAA Part 107 Remote Pilot based in western Colorado. For more than a decade, I have used drones to document America's public lands, including national forests, Bureau of Land Management lands, wildlife habitat, wildfires, drought, floods, and other environmental changes throughout the western United States.
I support reasonable security measures around sensitive infrastructure. However, I am concerned that the proposed rule, as written, could unnecessarily restrict lawful aerial photography, journalism, scientific documentation, and commercial operations far beyond what is required to protect these facilities.
Many of the facilities covered by this proposal are located within, adjacent to, or visible from public lands. These landscapes belong to the American people and are frequently documented by photographers, filmmakers, researchers, conservation organizations, tourism agencies, and news organizations. A broad flight restriction around a single facility could unintentionally prevent aerial documentation of surrounding public lands that have no connection to the protected site.
As someone who regularly documents wildfire impacts, drought, forest health, public land management, and changing landscapes, I am particularly concerned that these restrictions could make it more difficult to document issues that are clearly in the public interest. Responsible drone operators provide valuable perspectives that are often unavailable through any other practical means. For many documentation projects, a small unmanned aircraft can accomplish the work more safely, more quickly, at substantially lower cost, and with far less disruption to nearby communities than a manned aircraft. It also avoids the significantly greater fuel consumption, emissions, and noise associated with helicopters and fixed-wing aircraft, while often reducing the need for personnel to enter hazardous or difficult terrain.
I am also concerned that these restrictions could unintentionally become a mechanism for reducing public transparency. Much of the infrastructure covered by this proposal exists on or adjacent to public lands and was built, funded, or is operated with public resources. Lawful aerial photography conducted from public airspace plays an important role in documenting environmental change, land management, natural disasters, infrastructure projects, and other matters of legitimate public interest. While security concerns are important, restrictions should be narrowly tailored so they do not unnecessarily limit the public's ability to observe and document publicly significant places and events.
If the FAA proceeds with this proposal, I encourage several modifications:
Restrict only the minimum area necessary to protect the identified security concern rather than establishing unnecessarily broad exclusion zones.
Require every designation to include a clear public justification explaining why the restriction is necessary.
Establish automatic expiration dates so restrictions are periodically reviewed rather than remaining in place indefinitely.
Maintain a publicly searchable database showing the exact boundaries, effective dates, expiration dates, and legal justification for every restricted area.
Provide a transparent process for affected parties to request review or appeal of newly designated restrictions.
Create a permitting or waiver process for legitimate commercial operators, journalists, documentary filmmakers, researchers, and others performing work that serves the public interest while maintaining appropriate security safeguards.
The FAA has worked hard over the past decade to integrate drones safely into the National Airspace System. The overwhelming majority of certificated Part 107 pilots operate responsibly, comply with Remote ID requirements, and understand their obligations. Drones have become an important tool for environmental monitoring, journalism, infrastructure inspection, emergency response, scientific research, and commercial photography because they often provide a safer, lower-impact alternative to traditional aircraft. Regulations should continue to target genuine security threats without unnecessarily limiting lawful commercial activity, journalism, environmental documentation, scientific research, or public access to information.
Thank you for considering these comments.
Sincerely,
Jason H
FAA Part 107 Remote Pilot
Colorado

This rule could let the largest, most powerful operations shield themselves from scrutiny — the opposite of what the public interest requires. I urge the FAA not to develop gating criteria for this sector and not to grant UAFRs to food and agriculture facilities.

I strongly oppose allowing food and agriculture facilities to obtain Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs). There is no documented evidence that drones pose a safety or security threat to these facilities. I strongly oppose allowing food and agriculture facilities to obtain Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs). Drone photography and videography have become one of the few ways the public can gain insight into what happens inside large-scale factory farms—places that are otherwise inaccessible. Transparency is essential, especially when it comes to industries that directly impact public health, animal welfare, and the environment.

To my knowledge, there is no documented evidence showing that drones have posed a legitimate safety or security threat to these facilities. What drone footage has done, however, is reveal misleading marketing practices, environmental violations, and instances of animal mistreatment that the public deserves to know about. Without this technology, many of these issues may never have come to light.

Granting UAFRs to food and agriculture facilities would allow some of the largest and most powerful agricultural operations to operate with even less public oversight. Restricting lawful aerial documentation would reduce transparency and accountability at a time when consumers are increasingly seeking honest information about where their food comes from and how it is produced.

For these reasons, I respectfully urge the FAA not to develop gating criteria for the food and agriculture sector and not to grant UAFRs to these facilities. Protecting the public's ability to document and understand the practices of these operations is an important part of ensuring accountability and maintaining public trust.

Safety is a transparently disingenuous pretense for businesses seeking federal approval to ban drones anywhere near their facilities. Indeed, transparency and public and consumer awareness of their routinely horrific practices, are precisely what they're afraid of happening, because of the ability of safe, regulated, and responsibly operated drones to inform the public about what is actually taking place on vast swaths of land, in ways that actually do greatly impact public health and safety. This is at the heart of photojournalism in the public interest!

These enterprises, as routine business practices, massively pollute the air and water, gravely impacting many millions of Americans where we live. They pack living beings so densely that the spread of dangerous zoonotic diseases, which pose very significant threats to public health, are also routine. Simultaneously, these same companies spend massive amounts in advertising to outright lie to the public, misrepresenting their facilities as spacious, clean, humane, and somehow, bucolic and wholesome. In these ubiquitous ads, animals happily wander hills and fields and just happen to have some extra milk/meat/fur/skin to give to us. But whenever someone tries to show the public what's actually happening, these companies lie, again, and pretend any transparency is what threatens "biosecurity", and thus, supposedly, all our lives. When the real threat is the aforementioned and empirically endemic spread of zoonotic diseases, between animals grotesquely crammed together, and then, all-too-often transmitted to human beings. The real threats are from the business practices these companies wish to keep hidden: like the massive amounts of antibiotics and hormones they pump into the animals they breed to kill, which also end up in our bodies, affecting our health. And, like the blood, pus, urine, tumors, and feces which end up all over and in our food. This kind of public scrutiny is nothing new or dangerous! It's instead all about protecting public health, safety, and environmental standards, in the public interest. This is right out of Upton Sinclair's famous muckraking work, The Jungle, well over a hundred years ago. This kind of scrutiny is how our country achieved any basic health and safety standards to begin with!

The business practices these companies wish to keep hidden from public knowledge, at any cost, of course extend far beyond environmental devastation, like horribly polluted water and air; antibiotics, hormones, and bodily waste and disease all over our food; and, the conditions which make zoonotic contagion inevitable. These companies also keep their routine business practices far from where most people live, behind high walls and fences, and even guarded by armed, violent security guards, for another reason: They know the public simply does not and will not accept the degree and scale of cruelty and suffering they inflict, which these companies consider normal and fine. They are simply apoplectic at the idea that any of their customers should see how they actually house, confine, and abuse these many many millions of thinking, feeling animals. And they most certainly are determined to prevent the public from seeing the kind of recreationally sadistic abuse that's common among employees and supervisors at these facilities. Of course they don't want the public to see helpless baby animals crying in pain, being crushed underfoot. Of course they don't want the public to see workers beating and torturing and mocking animals already in despair, who are about to be killed. But the public has every right to know these things.

The public safety claim is bogus. These companies simply want to be able to do whatever they wish, our laws and regulations be damned. They want no public awareness of their actual practices. A picture is worth a thousand words, and they want to censor every accurate image, in advance, so the public will never know. That's flat-out Un-American.

Thank you sincerely for your time and consideration!

I am absolutely opposed to banning these drones. There is no evidence they have caused any harm to the property people or animals. But they have been very effective in finding cases of animal abuse. 'Corruption' by any other name. The general public has a right to know how our fellow creatures are treated and what is being done in their name.

To Whom It May Concern,

I strongly oppose this proposed rule. It is being presented as a safety measure, yet there is no documented evidence that drones have harmed people, animals, or equipment at industrial livestock facilities in the United States. What drones have done is provide transparency and accountability where the public would otherwise have none.

Drone footage has exposed misleading marketing claims, documented environmental pollution, revealed failures in animal welfare, and provided critical evidence during disasters and disease outbreaks. Investigations using aerial footage have already led to meaningful legal accountability, including settlements over false "free-roaming" claims.

Restricting drone access would shield the largest factory farms from public scrutiny while making it harder to document pollution, animal welfare concerns, and the real-world impacts of industrial agriculture. Transparency builds public trust; secrecy erodes it. If companies are operating responsibly, they should not fear lawful observation from public airspace.

Please reject this proposal and preserve the public's ability to use drones as a lawful tool for documenting environmental conditions, protecting consumers, and holding powerful industries accountable.

Michelle Ferritto
Director, Airmen and Airspace Rules Division
Office of Rulemaking
Federal Aviation Administration
800 Independence Avenue, SW
Washington, DC 20591
Submitted electronically to www.regulations.gov
Re: Notice of Proposed Rulemaking to Designation-Restrict the Operation of Unmanned
Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility
Docket FAA-2026-4558
Dear Ms. Ferritto,
ASTM International is pleased to offer the following comments in response to the Federal Aviation
Administration’s May 6, 2026, Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (NPRM) “Designation—Restrict the
Operation of Unmanned Aircraft in Close Proximity to a Fixed Site Facility”.
ASTM International (ASTM) is a U.S.-headquartered non-profit organization and one of the world’s
largest standards development organizations. Over 13,000 ASTM standards, which address health,
safety, and the environment, operate globally. ASTM standards, and related technical information,
are widely used in the U.S. and throughout the world as a basis of purchasing and other contracts,
codes, and regulations. ASTM’s standards development process is aligned and consistent with the
principles for international standards development agreed by members of the World Trade
Organization Committee on Technical Barriers to Trade and cited by the Office of Management and
Budget in its guidance to federal agencies.
ASTM Committee F38 on Unmanned Aircraft Systems develops international voluntary consensus
technical standards that underpin the safe integration of unmanned aircraft systems (UAS).
Accordingly, ASTM F38 took note of FAA’s effort and proposal to implement section 2209 of the FAA
Extension, Safety and Security Act of 2016 and give operators and proprietors of eligible fixed-site
facilities a clear, predictable process for requesting an unmanned aircraft flight restriction (UAFR).
ASTM Committee F38 offers the following observations for consideration as FAA desires to
strengthen the technical basis of one specific element of the proposal.
The NPRM states that the UAFR framework “requires the deployment of Remote Identification
(Remote ID) receivers as described in 14 CFR part 89 to monitor UAS activity,” while clarifying that
a UAFR does not by itself authorize detection or mitigation technologies that interfere with
unmanned aircraft, and that any such activity remains subject to Title 18 and Title 49 U.S.C. As
currently drafted, however, the proposed rule and preamble do not reference any voluntary
consensus standard that applicants, facility operators, or equipment manufacturers could use to
specify, procure, install, or verify the performance of compliant Remote ID detection systems.

ASTM to FAA re UAFR Proposal
July 21, 2026
Page 2
In a final rule or accompanying guidance material, such as an advisory circular, the agency could
consider for reference the following existing, FAA-relevant consensus standards:
• ASTM F3411-22a, Standard Specification for Remote ID and Tracking
o This standard defines the technical means by which unmanned aircraft broadcast
and/or network Remote ID messages, including identification, location, velocity,
and control station information. It is the underlying technical specification on which
FAA’s Remote ID rule (14 CFR part 89) and the FAA-accepted Means of Compliance
process are built and is widely implemented by UAS manufacturers and by
commercially available Remote ID detection equipment.
• ASTM F3586-22, Standard Practice for Remote ID Means of Compliance to Federal Aviation
Administration Regulation 14 CFR Part 89
o This standard provides the structured process by which a Remote ID means of
compliance is documented, evaluated, and submitted to FAA for acceptance,
ensuring that equipment used to broadcast or detect Remote ID does so in a
consistent, verifiable, and FAA-recognized manner.
Citing voluntary consensus standards as a means of compliance in the UAFR rule or accompanying
guidance material could give fixed-site facility applicants and Remote ID detection equipment
manufacturers a single, recognized technical baseline for system performance and
interoperability, reduce the risk of inconsistent or non-interoperable detection deployments across
UAFR sites, and align the UAFR program with the means-of-compliance framework FAA already
uses under Part 89. In the case of ASTM F3586-22, it would also reinforce, rather than duplicate,
FAA’s existing regulatory structure, since F3586-22 governs how compliance with Part 89 is
demonstrated, rather than creating a new or separate compliance pathway.
FAA could also consider clarifying, either in the rule text or preamble, that Remote ID detection
systems deployed to satisfy the UAFR requirement would be expected to process Remote ID
messages broadcast in conformance with ASTM F3411-22a, so that detection system performance
expectations are not left solely to individual equipment vendors to define.
ASTM appreciates the opportunity to provide technical information to FAA as the agency advances
this long-anticipated rulemaking and seeks alignment with related regulations to improve
interoperability and avoid duplicative or conflicting technical requirements across the growing
number of UAS detection deployments nationwide.
ASTM would welcome the opportunity to discuss this comment further with FAA staff and to make
members of ASTM Committee F38 available as a resource during the rulemaking process.
If you have any questions, please contact Mary Mikolajewski, Manager of Committee F38, at
mmikolajewski@astm.org, or Craig Updyke, Senior Director, Global Policy and International Trade,
at cupdyke@astm.org.
Sincerely,
Jeff Grove – Vice President, Global Policy, Cooperation and Communication

To Whom It May Concern:

I respectfully submit this comment in opposition to allowing food and agriculture facilities to obtain Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs).

Drone photojournalism serves an important public interest by providing one of the few independent ways to document conditions at factory farms and other large agricultural operations. This reporting has helped expose false marketing claims, environmental violations, and animal welfare abuses that otherwise would have remained hidden from the public.

At the same time, I am not aware of documented evidence showing that drones have posed a safety or security threat that would justify creating a new category of flight restrictions for food and agriculture facilities. Any limitation on access to public airspace should be supported by clear, evidence-based safety or security concerns.

Granting UAFRs to food and agriculture facilities would risk allowing some of the largest and most powerful agricultural operations to avoid public scrutiny rather than addressing legitimate aviation safety concerns. Such restrictions would reduce transparency and diminish the public's ability to hold these operations accountable.

For these reasons, I urge the FAA not to develop gating criteria for the food and agriculture sector and not to grant UAFRs to food and agriculture facilities.

Thank you for considering my comment.

I oppose allowing food and agriculture facilities to obtain Unmanned Aircraft Flight Restrictions (UAFRs). Drone photojournalism is one of the only tools the public has to see inside factory farms and hold them accountable.

There is no documented evidence of drones threatening safety or security at these facilities, but there is a documented history of drone footage exposing false marketing claims, environmental violations, and animal welfare abuses.

This rule could let the largest, most powerful operations shield themselves from scrutiny — the opposite of what the public interest requires. I urge the FAA not to develop gating criteria for this sector and not to grant UAFRs to food and agriculture facilities.

Factory farms are built to keep the public out. Miles of fencing, no roadside view, no way for you to see how the animals inside are actually treated. In recent years, drones have been one of the tools that have helped us see over that wall — capturing what these facilities don't want anyone to know.

Now the Federal Aviation Authority (FAA) is considering taking action that would ground these drone flights.


Tell the FAA to keep drones flying
The agency has proposed a rule that could let the largest, most powerful factory farms in the country ban drones from flying anywhere near their facilities. It's being sold as a safety measure. It is not. There is no documented case of a drone harming a person, an animal, or a piece of equipment at one of these facilities in the US. What drones have done is expose the repeated lies, cruelty, and pollution companies would rather keep hidden.

We've seen firsthand what's at stake. In one case, drone footage from a number of Pennsylvania egg facilities helped prove a company's "free roaming" hens had no real outdoor access at all — evidence that led to a $287,500 settlement in a federal consumer protection case. That kind of accountability only happened because the airspace over that facility was still open.
Please don't stop the drones flying over factory farms.  You will only allow more suffering and abuse.
